Anatomy & Physiology

Anatomy & Physiology

Complete edition · all 28 chapters · by Dr. Vidya Aggrawal · Nightingale College
Anatomy & Physiology · Chapter 1

An Introduction to the Human Body

Before we can study any single organ, we need two things: a map and a compass. This chapter gives you both. You will learn how the body is built up in nested levels from atoms to a whole person, meet the eleven organ systems that share the work of staying alive, and discover the one idea that runs underneath all of physiology — homeostasis, the body's relentless drive to hold its internal world steady. Then we will learn the precise language anatomists use to describe where things are, so that for the rest of the book, "left," "above," and "in front" never mean the wrong thing.

By the end of this chapter you can

  • Distinguish anatomy from physiology and explain why structure and function are always studied together.
  • Order the six levels of structural organization from chemical to organism, and give an example at each level.
  • Name the eleven organ systems, state the main job of each, and predict which system a given organ belongs to.
  • Define homeostasis and diagram a negative feedback loop using its three parts — receptor, control center, and effector.
  • Contrast negative and positive feedback and give a real example of each.
  • Describe the anatomical position and use directional terms in correct pairs (superior/inferior, proximal/distal, and the rest).
  • Identify the three body planes and the body cavities, and locate a structure by region or quadrant.

Section 1The levels of organization

Two questions, always asked together

Everything in this book grows out of two words. Anatomy asks what is it and where is it — the shape, the parts, the position of a structure. Physiology asks how does it work — the function, the process, the job that structure performs. The two are inseparable, because in the body structure always fits function. The wall of an air sac in the lung is thin as tissue paper precisely so that oxygen can cross it in a fraction of a second; the wall of the heart's main pumping chamber is thick and muscular precisely because it must launch blood to the whole body. Change the structure and you change what the tissue can do — a rule you will see over and over.

Small parts build bigger parts

The body is organized like a set of nesting dolls, each level built from the one below it. There are six levels, and it is worth being able to recite them in order:

LevelWhat it isExample
1. ChemicalAtoms bond into moleculesHydrogen + oxygen → a water molecule; amino acids → a protein
2. CellularMolecules assemble into the smallest living unitA muscle cell, a neuron, a red blood cell
3. TissueSimilar cells working together on one taskCardiac muscle tissue; nervous tissue
4. OrganTwo or more tissue types forming a structure with a jobThe heart, the stomach, the skin
5. Organ systemOrgans cooperating on a large functionThe cardiovascular system (heart + vessels + blood)
6. OrganismAll systems together = one living personYou

Read the ladder in either direction and it tells a story. Going up, tiny parts combine into ever-larger, more capable structures until they add up to a living human. Going down, any disease can be traced to a lower level: a heart attack is an organ problem that begins with cells starved of oxygen because a molecule (cholesterol) built up in a vessel wall. Learning to move up and down this ladder is one of the most useful habits in all of A&P.

Cells are the pivot point. Everything below the cell is chemistry; everything at or above it is alive. The cell is the smallest thing that can be called living, which is why the entire middle of this course — from membranes to metabolism — lives at the cellular level.

Zoom in and out through all six levels in the interactive below.

Interactive Zoom from a single atom out to a whole human, one nested level at a time. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Anatomy = structure and position; physiology = function and process; structure always fits function.
  • The six levels, low to high: chemical → cellular → tissue → organ → organ system → organism.
  • The cell is the smallest living unit and the dividing line between chemistry and life.
  • Disease can be traced down the ladder to the tissue, cell, or molecule where it began.

Section 2The organ systems

Eleven teams, one goal

Keeping a human alive is too big a job for any one organ, so the work is divided among eleven organ systems. They are not really separate — they overlap and depend on one another constantly — but grouping them by their main function makes the body learnable. A helpful way to hold them is to sort them by the kind of job they do: covering and support, control and communication, moving substances, and continuing the species.

SystemMain jobSignature organs
IntegumentaryCovers and protects; temperature, sensation, vitamin DSkin, hair, nails
SkeletalSupport, protection, movement levers, blood-cell formation, mineral storageBones, cartilage, joints
MuscularMovement, posture, heatSkeletal muscles
NervousFast control and communicationBrain, spinal cord, nerves
EndocrineSlow, hormonal controlThyroid, pancreas, adrenal, pituitary
CardiovascularTransports blood, O₂, nutrients, wastesHeart, blood vessels, blood
Lymphatic / ImmuneFluid return and defense against pathogensLymph nodes, spleen, thymus, vessels
RespiratoryGas exchange — O₂ in, CO₂ outLungs, airways
DigestiveBreaks down food, absorbs nutrients, removes wasteStomach, intestines, liver, pancreas
UrinaryFilters blood, balances water/salts/pH, makes urineKidneys, ureters, bladder
ReproductiveProduces offspring and sex hormonesOvaries/uterus; testes

No system works alone

The magic is in the cooperation. Take one breath and follow it: the respiratory system pulls in oxygen, but it is the cardiovascular system that carries that oxygen to the tissues, the muscular system (the diaphragm) that did the pulling, the nervous system that set the breathing rate, and the skeletal system (the rib cage) that gave the lungs a frame to expand inside. When you meet a disease later in the course, ask which systems it touches — the answer is almost never just one.

The pancreas is a double agent. A few organs belong to two systems at once: the pancreas is digestive (it makes enzymes) and endocrine (it makes insulin); the pharynx and the diaphragm serve both breathing and other jobs. Don't be surprised when an organ shows up in two chapters.

Tap each system in the interactive to watch its organs light up on the body.

Interactive Tap a system and watch its organs light up on the body. Open full ↑
Key points
  • There are eleven organ systems; know each system's main job and a signature organ.
  • Systems constantly cooperate — a single act like breathing draws on five or more at once.
  • Some organs serve two systems (the pancreas is digestive and endocrine).

Section 3Homeostasis & feedback

The single big idea of physiology

If you remember one word from this whole course, make it homeostasis — the maintenance of a stable internal environment despite a changing outside world. Your body temperature hovers near 37 °C whether you are in a snowstorm or a sauna; your blood glucose, blood pH, and fluid volume all stay penned inside narrow ranges. They do not stay perfectly still — they wobble gently around a set point, like a tightrope walker making constant tiny corrections. That dynamic balance is health. Push a value too far outside its range and cells stop working; push it further and the person dies. Nearly every disease in the rest of this book is, at heart, a failure of homeostasis.

The negative feedback loop: three parts

The body keeps values steady mainly through negative feedback, and every negative feedback loop has the same three components. Learn them once and you can read any loop in the body:

  • A receptor (a sensor) detects the change and reports it.
  • A control center (usually the brain or an endocrine gland) compares the reading to the set point and decides on a response.
  • An effector (a muscle or gland) carries out the response, which pushes the value back toward normal.

It is called negative feedback because the response opposes (negates) the original change — if the value went up, the loop brings it down, and vice versa. The classic example is body temperature. Step into the cold: temperature receptors in your skin fire; the control center (the hypothalamus) reads "too cold"; and it drives effectors — blood vessels constrict to keep heat in, and muscles shiver to make heat. Temperature climbs back to the set point, and the loop quiets down. A home thermostat works exactly the same way: sense, compare, correct.

Negative = corrective. Don't read "negative" as bad. Negative feedback is the body's autopilot, gently canceling out every drift from normal. It is the mechanism behind stable temperature, glucose, blood pressure, calcium, and pH — the whole idea of a healthy steady state.

Positive feedback: the rare amplifier

Occasionally the body wants the opposite — a signal that amplifies itself until an event is finished. This is positive feedback, and it is rare precisely because it is explosive. In childbirth, the baby's head stretches the cervix, which triggers oxytocin, which strengthens contractions, which stretches the cervix more — a snowballing cycle that builds until delivery ends it. Blood clotting works the same way: the first platelets to stick release chemicals that recruit more platelets, which recruit more, sealing the wound fast. Positive feedback always needs a built-in stop (birth, or a finished clot); without one it would run away.

FeatureNegative feedbackPositive feedback
Effect on the changeOpposes / reverses itAmplifies / continues it
ResultReturns to set point (stability)Drives toward a completion event
FrequencyThe rule — most loopsThe exception — a few
ExamplesTemperature, glucose, blood pressure, pHChildbirth, blood clotting, lactation

Knock a value off its set point in the interactive and watch a negative feedback loop pull it home.

Interactive Throw the body off balance and watch a negative feedback loop pull it home. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Homeostasis is a stable internal environment held near a set point; losing it is the root of most disease.
  • A negative feedback loop has three parts — receptor → control center → effector — and its response opposes the change to restore normal.
  • Positive feedback amplifies a change until a completion event stops it: childbirth, clotting, milk let-down.

Section 4The language of position

Start from the anatomical position

To describe where things are without confusion, everyone agrees to start from the same pose: the anatomical position. The body stands upright, facing forward, feet slightly apart, arms at the sides with the palms turned forward. Every directional term is defined from this pose, so a structure's description never changes even if the real patient is lying down or upside down. One more rule that trips up students: "right" and "left" always mean the patient's own right and left, not yours as you look at them.

The directional pairs

Directional terms come in opposing pairs, which makes them easy to learn two at a time:

TermMeaningExample
Superior / InferiorAbove / below (toward head / toward feet)The heart is superior to the stomach
Anterior / PosteriorFront / back (ventral / dorsal)The sternum is anterior to the heart
Medial / LateralToward the midline / away from itThe nose is medial to the eyes
Proximal / DistalNearer to / farther from the trunk (limbs)The elbow is proximal to the wrist
Superficial / DeepToward the surface / away from itThe skin is superficial to the muscle

Two of these pairs deserve extra care. Proximal and distal are used mainly for the limbs and always measured from where the limb attaches to the trunk — so the shoulder is proximal, the fingertips distal. Medial and lateral are measured from the body's imaginary midline, so structures near the center are medial and structures out toward the sides are lateral.

Terms are relative, not absolute. A structure is only superior or proximal compared to something else. The wrist is distal to the elbow but proximal to the fingers. Always finish the sentence: "X is [term] to Y."

See each pair drawn on a body in anatomical position below.

Interactive Superior, medial, proximal, deep — each pair drawn on a body. Open full ↑
Key points
  • All terms are defined from the anatomical position (upright, facing forward, palms forward).
  • Right and left always mean the patient's right and left.
  • Learn the pairs: superior/inferior, anterior/posterior, medial/lateral, proximal/distal, superficial/deep — and use them relatively ("X is [term] to Y").

Section 5Planes & sections

Three ways to slice

To look inside the body — on a cadaver table or on a CT scan — we imagine cutting it along flat surfaces called planes. There are three, and they are perpendicular to one another:

  • The sagittal plane divides the body into left and right. A cut exactly down the midline is a midsagittal section; an off-center one is parasagittal.
  • The frontal (coronal) plane divides the body into front and back (anterior and posterior).
  • The transverse (horizontal) plane divides the body into top and bottom (superior and inferior) — the "slice" view you see on a CT.

Why does this matter to a nurse or clinician? Because medical images are always taken in one of these planes, and reading a scan means knowing which slice you are looking at. A transverse CT of the abdomen shows organs in cross-section; a sagittal MRI of the spine shows the vertebrae stacked front-to-back. Naming the plane is the first step in orienting yourself to any image.

Mid-sagittal is the mirror line. Only the midsagittal plane splits the body into two roughly equal, mirror-image halves. Any other sagittal cut (parasagittal) gives unequal left and right pieces.

Watch each plane sweep through the body in the interactive below.

Interactive Sagittal, frontal, and transverse planes sweeping through. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Sagittal = left/right; frontal (coronal) = front/back; transverse = top/bottom.
  • A midsagittal cut makes equal mirror halves; other sagittal cuts (parasagittal) do not.
  • Medical images (CT, MRI) are read by first naming their plane.

Section 6Cavities & regions

Where the organs live

The organs are not loose inside you — they are housed in enclosed spaces called body cavities that protect them and let them move. There are two main groups. The dorsal (posterior) cavity runs along the back and holds the nervous system: the cranial cavity (the brain) and the vertebral cavity (the spinal cord). The larger ventral (anterior) cavity holds most other organs and is split by the diaphragm into the thoracic cavity above (heart and lungs) and the abdominopelvic cavity below (digestive organs, then the pelvic organs).

Serous membranes: the slippery linings

Organs in the ventral cavity are wrapped in thin, double-layered serous membranes that secrete a slick fluid so organs can glide without friction. Each has a two-part name: the pleura around the lungs, the pericardium around the heart, and the peritoneum around the abdominal organs. When one of these membranes becomes inflamed, you get the "-itis" diseases — pleuritis, pericarditis, peritonitis — and the friction or fluid buildup causes real trouble.

Mapping the abdomen: regions and quadrants

Because so much can go wrong in the belly, clinicians divide the abdominopelvic surface into a grid to describe exactly where a pain or a mass is. The simple version uses four quadrants (right and left, upper and lower — RUQ, LUQ, RLQ, LLQ); the detailed version uses nine regions (a tic-tac-toe grid: the central umbilical region, the epigastric above it, the hypogastric below, and flanking regions on each side). Saying "RLQ pain" instantly narrows the suspects — the appendix lives there.

Location is a diagnosis clue. RUQ pain points to the gallbladder and liver; RLQ pain classically means appendicitis; epigastric pain suggests the stomach or pancreas. Learning the map now pays off in every clinical chapter later.

Explore the cavities, membranes, and the region/quadrant grids in the interactive.

Interactive The body cavities and membranes, plus the 9 regions & 4 quadrants. Open full ↑
Key points
  • The dorsal cavity (cranial + vertebral) holds the CNS; the ventral cavity splits at the diaphragm into thoracic and abdominopelvic.
  • Serous membranes — pleura, pericardium, peritoneum — reduce friction; their inflammation gives the "-itis" diseases.
  • The abdomen maps into 4 quadrants or 9 regions; location narrows the diagnosis.

The chapter in one breath

  • Anatomy is structure, physiology is function, and structure always fits function.
  • The body is built in six nested levels: chemical → cellular → tissue → organ → organ system → organism, with the cell as the smallest living unit.
  • Eleven organ systems share the work of survival and constantly cooperate; know each one's main job.
  • Homeostasis is the stable internal environment that defines health; most disease is a failure of it.
  • Negative feedback (receptor → control center → effector) opposes change to restore a set point; positive feedback amplifies change to complete an event (childbirth, clotting).
  • Anatomical language starts from the anatomical position; directional terms are used in relative pairs, and right/left mean the patient's.
  • Three planes (sagittal, frontal, transverse) slice the body; organs sit in dorsal and ventral cavities lined by serous membranes; the abdomen maps into quadrants and regions.

ReferenceWords worth knowing

A quick reference for the terms in this chapter — skim it now, or circle back whenever a word trips you up.

Anatomical position
The standard reference pose — standing upright, facing forward, arms at the sides with palms forward — from which all directional terms are defined.
Anatomy
The study of the body's structures and their physical relationships (what a part is and where it lies).
Anterior (ventral)
Toward the front of the body.
Control center
The part of a feedback loop (often the brain or an endocrine gland) that compares a sensed value to the set point and directs a response.
Distal
Farther from the point where a limb attaches to the trunk (the fingers are distal to the elbow).
Effector
The muscle or gland in a feedback loop that carries out the corrective response.
Frontal (coronal) plane
A vertical plane dividing the body into anterior (front) and posterior (back) portions.
Homeostasis
The maintenance of a relatively stable internal environment despite external change, kept near a set point by feedback.
Medial
Toward the midline of the body.
Negative feedback
A control loop whose response opposes and reverses the original change, restoring the value to its set point; the body's main homeostatic mechanism.
Organ
A structure made of two or more tissue types that performs a specific function (for example, the heart or stomach).
Physiology
The study of how the body's structures function and the processes that keep it alive.
Positive feedback
A control loop whose response amplifies the original change until a completion event stops it (childbirth, blood clotting).
Proximal
Nearer to the point where a limb attaches to the trunk (the elbow is proximal to the wrist).
Sagittal plane
A vertical plane dividing the body into left and right portions; a midline cut is midsagittal.
Serous membrane
A thin, double-layered membrane (pleura, pericardium, peritoneum) that lines a ventral cavity and secretes fluid to reduce friction around organs.
Set point
The target value a homeostatic variable is regulated around (for example, ~37 °C for body temperature).
Superior
Toward the head or the upper part of the body.
Transverse (horizontal) plane
A plane dividing the body into superior and inferior portions; produces a cross-sectional view.

Check yourself

1. A student says the thin wall of a lung's air sac and the thick wall of the heart's left ventricle prove a core principle of A&P. What is the principle, and what does each wall illustrate?
Structure fits function (form follows function). The alveolar wall is paper-thin so gases can diffuse across it almost instantly; the left-ventricular wall is thick and muscular so it can generate the high pressure needed to pump blood to the entire body. The shape of a structure is always matched to the job it does.
2. Place these in order from smallest to largest and name the one that marks the boundary between non-living and living: tissue, organism, molecule, cell, organ system, organ.
Molecule (chemical) → cell → tissue → organ → organ system → organism; the cell is the boundary. Everything below the cell is chemistry; the cell is the smallest unit that is alive, which is why it is the pivot of the whole course.
3. To which organ system does each belong: the kidney, the thyroid gland, a lymph node, and the diaphragm?
Kidney = urinary; thyroid = endocrine; lymph node = lymphatic/immune; diaphragm = muscular (and it powers respiration). Assigning organs to systems by their main job is a habit worth building early.
4. Name the three parts of a negative feedback loop and map body-temperature regulation onto them.
Receptor → control center → effector. Skin temperature receptors sense cold; the hypothalamus (control center) compares this to the ~37 °C set point; and effectors respond — blood vessels constrict and muscles shiver — raising temperature back toward normal.
5. Classify each as negative or positive feedback, and justify: (a) rising blood glucose triggers insulin, which lowers it; (b) cervical stretch in labor triggers oxytocin, which strengthens contractions.
(a) Negative feedback; (b) positive feedback. In (a) the response opposes the change (glucose goes up, insulin brings it back down) — the corrective, stabilizing pattern that runs most of the body. In (b) the response amplifies the change (more stretch → more oxytocin → more contraction → more stretch) until delivery ends the loop.
6. Describe the anatomical position, and explain why "the patient's left" can differ from "your left."
Upright, facing forward, feet slightly apart, arms at the sides with palms forward. Directional terms are defined from this fixed pose so descriptions stay consistent. Right and left always refer to the patient's own sides; when you face a patient, their left is on your right.
7. Fill in the correct directional term: the wrist is ____ to the elbow; the nose is ____ to the ears; the skin is ____ to the muscles; the lungs are ____ to the diaphragm.
Distal; medial; superficial; superior. The wrist is farther down the limb (distal), the nose is nearer the midline than the ears (medial), the skin is nearer the surface than muscle (superficial), and the lungs sit above the diaphragm (superior).
8. A CT scan shows the abdomen as a circular cross-section, like looking down through a slice. Which plane produced this image, and what two portions does that plane create?
The transverse (horizontal) plane, which divides the body into superior and inferior portions. Transverse sections are the standard cross-sectional "slice" view of CT imaging.
9. Which single plane divides the body into equal, mirror-image left and right halves, and what is a cut off to one side of it called?
The midsagittal plane; an off-center sagittal cut is parasagittal. Only the exact midline (midsagittal) cut yields two equal mirror halves; parasagittal cuts give unequal left and right pieces.
10. The ventral cavity is separated into two smaller cavities by one muscle. Name the muscle, the two cavities, and the major organs in each.
The diaphragm separates the thoracic cavity (heart and lungs) from the abdominopelvic cavity (digestive organs and, below, the pelvic organs). The dorsal cavity, by contrast, holds the brain (cranial) and spinal cord (vertebral).
11. Match each serous membrane to its organ, and name the disease when it becomes inflamed: pleura, pericardium, peritoneum.
Pleura → lungs (pleuritis); pericardium → heart (pericarditis); peritoneum → abdominal organs (peritonitis). These double-layered membranes secrete fluid so organs glide without friction; inflammation of any of them produces pain and can cause fluid buildup.
12. A patient reports sharp pain in the right lower quadrant (RLQ). Which classic condition should come to mind, and why does naming the location matter clinically?
Appendicitis — the appendix lies in the RLQ. Because organs occupy predictable regions and quadrants, the location of a pain immediately narrows the list of suspects (RUQ → gallbladder/liver; epigastric → stomach/pancreas; RLQ → appendix), guiding assessment and imaging.
Anatomy & Physiology · Chapter 2

The Chemical Level of Organization

Every heartbeat, every thought, every drop of sweat begins as chemistry. Long before a cell can divide or a nerve can fire, atoms must bond, reactions must run, and water must do its quiet, essential work. This chapter takes you down to the chemical level — the foundation the whole rest of the book is built on. You will meet the atom and the handful of elements that make up almost all of you, learn the three bonds that hold molecules together, watch reactions rearrange matter and see how enzymes speed them up, discover why water is the perfect medium for life, learn to read pH and the buffers that guard your blood, and finish with the four great families of molecules that build your body. Get comfortable here — everything upstairs depends on this floor.

By the end of this chapter you can

  • Describe the structure of an atom — protons, neutrons, and electrons — and explain how the atomic number defines an element.
  • Name the major elements that make up most of the human body and distinguish them from trace elements and isotopes.
  • Compare ionic, covalent (polar and nonpolar), and hydrogen bonds, and explain the octet rule that drives bonding.
  • Classify chemical reactions as synthesis, decomposition, or exchange, and explain activation energy and how enzymes lower it.
  • Explain how water's polarity makes it the body's solvent, temperature buffer, and lubricant.
  • Interpret the pH scale, distinguish acids from bases, and describe how buffers hold blood near 7.4.
  • Identify the four organic macromolecule families — carbohydrates, lipids, proteins, nucleic acids — plus ATP, and give a job for each.
  • Contrast dehydration synthesis with hydrolysis as the way polymers are built and broken.

Section 1Atoms & the elements

Matter, elements, and the atom

Anything that takes up space and has mass is matter — your bones, your blood, the air in your lungs. All matter is built from elements, pure substances that cannot be broken down into anything simpler by ordinary chemistry. There are 118 known elements, but life is surprisingly economical: only about two dozen appear in the body, and just six — oxygen, carbon, hydrogen, nitrogen, calcium, and phosphorus — account for roughly 99% of your mass. The smallest particle of an element that still behaves like that element is the atom. Split an atom apart and you no longer have the element; you have its raw ingredients.

ElementSymbol~% of body massA job it does
OxygenO65%Part of water; final electron acceptor in respiration
CarbonC18%The backbone of every organic molecule
HydrogenH10%Part of water; sets pH as the H⁺ ion
NitrogenN3%In every protein and nucleic acid
CalciumCa1.5%Bone mineral; muscle and nerve signaling
PhosphorusP1%Bone; the energy bonds of ATP and DNA

Inside the atom: three particles

An atom has a dense center, the nucleus, surrounded by a cloud of moving electrons. Only three subatomic particles matter for this course. Protons carry a positive charge and sit in the nucleus. Neutrons carry no charge and share the nucleus with the protons, adding mass but no charge. Electrons carry a negative charge and are almost weightless, whirling around the nucleus in layered shells (energy levels). Because protons and electrons carry equal and opposite charges, a neutral atom has exactly as many electrons as protons, and the whole thing balances out to no net charge.

ParticleChargeLocationRelative mass
Proton+1Nucleus1 (heavy)
Neutron0Nucleus1 (heavy)
Electron−1Shells around the nucleus~1/1800 (negligible)

Atomic number, isotopes, and the outer shell

One number is the atom's fingerprint: the atomic number, the count of protons in the nucleus. Change it and you change the element itself — 1 proton is always hydrogen, 6 is always carbon, 8 is always oxygen. The mass number adds protons and neutrons together. Atoms of the same element that carry different numbers of neutrons are called isotopes: carbon-12 and carbon-14 are both carbon (6 protons each), but carbon-14 has two extra neutrons and is radioactive, which is exactly why radioactive isotopes are so useful in medical imaging and dating. What drives an atom's chemistry, though, is not its nucleus but its outermost electrons — the valence shell. Atoms are “happiest” (most stable) when that outer shell is full, which for most biological atoms means eight electrons — the octet rule. An atom with a full outer shell is inert; an atom short a few electrons is reactive and ready to bond.

Isotopes at the bedside. Because isotopes behave chemically like their normal counterparts but announce themselves with radiation, clinicians use them to see the invisible: iodine-131 traces the thyroid, technetium-99m lights up bone scans, and carbon isotopes drive PET imaging of active tissue. Same element, extra neutrons, enormous diagnostic power.

Pick an element and watch its atom assemble — nucleus in the center, electrons filling their shells — in the interactive below.

Interactive Pick an element and watch its atom build — nucleus in the center, electrons whirling in shells. Open full ↑
Key points
  • An atom has a nucleus of positive protons and neutral neutrons, orbited by negative electrons in shells.
  • The atomic number (proton count) defines the element; a neutral atom has equal protons and electrons.
  • Isotopes are the same element with different neutron counts; some are radioactive and medically useful.
  • The valence (outer) shell drives chemistry — atoms bond to reach a stable, full shell (the octet rule).

Section 2Chemical bonds

Why atoms bond at all

Atoms bond for one reason: to reach a stable, full outer shell. An atom with a lonely, half-empty valence shell will do almost anything to fill it — give electrons away, take them, or share them — and each of those strategies produces a different kind of bond. The noble gases (helium, neon, argon) already have full shells, so they drift through the world refusing to react. Everything else is looking for a partner. Understanding the three main bonds — ionic, covalent, and hydrogen — lets you predict how nearly every molecule in the body behaves.

Ionic bonds: give and take

Sometimes the cleanest way to fill a shell is to hand an electron over completely. When one atom donates an electron and another accepts it, both walk away charged: the donor becomes a positive cation, the acceptor a negative anion. Opposite charges attract, and that electrostatic pull is the ionic bond. Table salt is the classic case: sodium hands its lone outer electron to chlorine, becoming Na⁺, while chlorine becomes Cl⁻, and the two cling together as NaCl. In water, ionic compounds pull apart into their separate ions — these dissolved, charged particles are your electrolytes (Na⁺, K⁺, Ca⁺⁺, Cl⁻), and they run nerve signals, muscle contraction, and fluid balance.

Covalent bonds: sharing — polar and nonpolar

More often, two atoms simply share a pair of electrons so that both count it toward a full shell — a covalent bond, the strongest and most common bond in the body. Carbon builds all of life on covalent bonds. But sharing is not always fair. When both atoms pull equally, the shared electrons sit in the middle and the bond is nonpolar (as in O₂ or the carbon–hydrogen bonds of fats). When one atom pulls harder, it hogs the electrons and the bond becomes polar, leaving one end slightly negative and the other slightly positive. Water is the great polar molecule: oxygen pulls the shared electrons away from its two hydrogens, so oxygen carries a partial negative charge and the hydrogens a partial positive — a lopsidedness that, as you will see, makes life possible.

Hydrogen bonds: weak but mighty

A hydrogen bond is not a bond within a molecule but a gentle attraction between molecules (or between distant parts of one big molecule). It forms when the slightly positive hydrogen of one polar molecule is drawn to the slightly negative oxygen or nitrogen of another. Any single hydrogen bond is feeble — a fraction of the strength of a covalent bond — but in the millions they become mighty. Hydrogen bonds hold liquid water together, zip the two strands of DNA into a double helix, and fold proteins into their working shapes. They are strong enough to give structure yet weak enough to break and re-form, which is exactly what living molecules need.

BondHow it formsStrengthExample
IonicOne atom donates an electron to another; opposite charges attractStrong (weak once dissolved in water)Na⁺Cl⁻ (table salt)
Covalent (nonpolar)Electrons shared equallyStrongestO₂; C–H bonds in fats
Covalent (polar)Electrons shared unequally — partial chargesStrongWater (H₂O)
HydrogenAttraction between partial + and partial − on polar moleculesWeak (strong in numbers)Between water molecules; DNA strands
Ionic bonds dissolve; covalent bonds don't. Drop salt in water and it splits into Na⁺ and Cl⁻ — the ionic bond gives way. Drop sugar in water and it dissolves but stays a whole molecule, because its atoms are held by covalent bonds that water cannot break. This one difference explains why blood is full of free electrolytes yet its glucose stays intact.

Flip between ionic, covalent, and hydrogen bonds and watch how each one forms in the interactive.

Interactive Give electrons away, share them, or feel a gentle attraction — flip between the three bonds. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Atoms bond to fill their valence shell; a full shell is stable and unreactive.
  • Ionic bonds transfer electrons, making charged ions (electrolytes) that separate in water.
  • Covalent bonds share electrons — equally (nonpolar) or unequally (polar); they are the body's strongest bonds.
  • Hydrogen bonds are weak individual attractions between polar molecules that add up to hold water, DNA, and proteins together.

Section 3Chemical reactions & enzymes

Reactants, products, and three ways to rearrange atoms

A chemical reaction is simply the making or breaking of bonds — atoms let go of old partners and grab new ones. The starting materials are the reactants; what you end up with are the products. No atoms are created or destroyed; they are only rearranged. Almost every reaction in the body fits one of three patterns. Synthesis reactions join smaller parts into a larger molecule (A + B → AB) — the building, or anabolic, reactions. Decomposition reactions break a large molecule into smaller pieces (AB → A + B) — the demolishing, or catabolic, reactions, like digesting a meal. Exchange reactions do both at once, swapping partners (AB + CD → AC + BD). Many reactions are also reversible, running either direction depending on conditions.

TypePatternWhat it doesBody example
Synthesis (anabolic)A + B → ABBuilds larger moleculesAmino acids joined into a protein
Decomposition (catabolic)AB → A + BBreaks molecules apartDigesting starch into glucose
ExchangeAB + CD → AC + BDSwaps partnersBuffers trading H⁺ in the blood

Energy: the hill every reaction must climb

Chemical bonds are little reservoirs of energy, so rearranging them always involves an energy change. Reactions that release energy (usually as heat) are exergonic — these power the body, like breaking down glucose. Reactions that absorb energy are endergonic — building big molecules costs energy. But here is the catch: even a reaction that releases energy overall must first be nudged over an initial hump called the activation energy — the energy needed to get the reactants unstable enough to react at all. Think of a boulder resting in a small dip near the top of a hill: it will roll down and release a lot of energy, but only after something lifts it over the lip first. That lip is activation energy, and at body temperature most reactions would sit stuck behind it for hours or years.

Enzymes: shrinking the hill

The body cannot wait hours, and it cannot simply crank up the heat — so it uses enzymes. An enzyme is a biological catalyst (nearly always a protein) that lowers the activation energy of a reaction, letting it run thousands or millions of times faster at a safe 37 °C. Crucially, an enzyme is not consumed — it does its job and is freed to do it again. Each enzyme is exquisitely specific, with an active site shaped to fit one particular substrate like a key in a lock (or, more accurately, a glove that molds around the hand). Because enzymes are proteins, anything that unfolds a protein — a high fever, the wrong pH — can cripple them, which is one reason your body guards its temperature and pH so fiercely.

The “-ase” giveaway. Most enzymes are named for their substrate plus the suffix -ase: lactase digests lactose, lipase digests lipids, protease digests protein, and sucrase digests sucrose. See a molecule's name with “-ase” on the end and you are almost certainly looking at the enzyme that takes it apart.

Watch a reaction run over its energy hill, then switch on an enzyme and see the hill shrink in the interactive below.

Interactive Watch a reaction climb its energy hill, then add an enzyme and see the hill shrink. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Reactions rearrange atoms from reactants into products as synthesis, decomposition, or exchange.
  • Exergonic reactions release energy; endergonic reactions absorb it; every reaction must first clear its activation energy.
  • Enzymes are protein catalysts that lower activation energy, are reused, and are highly specific to their substrate.
  • Enzymes depend on their shape, so heat and pH extremes can disable them.

Section 4Water, the solvent of life

Polarity: the source of all water's tricks

You are roughly 60% water, and that is no accident — nearly every remarkable thing water does traces back to a single feature: it is polar. Recall from Section 2 that oxygen hogs the shared electrons in each O–H bond, so a water molecule (H₂O) carries a slightly negative pole at the oxygen and slightly positive poles at the two hydrogens. Because opposite partial charges attract, every water molecule clings by hydrogen bonds to the water molecules around it. That constant, shifting web of hydrogen bonds is the hidden machinery behind water's role as a solvent, a temperature buffer, and a lubricant.

The universal solvent

Water is called the universal solvent because its charged poles surround and pull apart other charged or polar substances, scattering them evenly into solution. The dissolving liquid is the solvent, the dissolved substance the solute. Molecules that mix into water — salts, sugars, amino acids — are hydrophilic (“water-loving”), while nonpolar molecules like fats are hydrophobic (“water-fearing”) and refuse to dissolve. This split does real anatomical work: it is exactly why cell membranes, made of hydrophobic lipids, form watertight barriers in your watery body. And because so much of the body's chemistry happens in solution, water is the medium in which nutrients travel, wastes dissolve, and reactions occur — blood plasma is, at heart, water carrying dissolved cargo.

Heat, cushioning, and chemistry

Water's hydrogen bonds also give it a very high heat capacity: it takes a lot of energy to raise water's temperature, so your water-rich body resists sudden swings in temperature and stays near 37 °C. Better still, evaporating water carries away enormous heat, which is why sweating cools you so effectively on a hot day. The same cohesive stickiness lets water form protective, cushioning films — the slippery fluid in serous membranes, the synovial fluid in joints, the mucus lining your airways all rely on it. Finally, water is not just a bystander to chemistry; it is a participant. The body builds big molecules by removing water (dehydration synthesis) and breaks them down by adding water (hydrolysis) — the two reactions you will meet again in Section 6.

PropertyWhy (from polarity)What it does for you
SolventCharged poles pull solutes apartTransports nutrients, wastes, and ions in blood
High heat capacityHydrogen bonds absorb heatBuffers body temperature near 37 °C
Evaporative coolingBreaking H-bonds carries off heatSweating cools the body
Cohesion / lubricationMolecules stick to one anotherSlippery films in joints, membranes, airways
ReactantCan be added or removed in reactionsBuilds and breaks macromolecules
“Like dissolves like.” Polar and ionic substances dissolve in polar water; nonpolar substances dissolve in nonpolar fats. This simple rule predicts a lot of physiology — why oil and water separate, why fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) need fat to be absorbed, and why the hydrophobic core of a cell membrane keeps the watery inside and outside apart.

Explore what water's lopsided charge buys you — dissolving, sticking, and buffering heat — in the interactive.

Interactive Water is polar — lopsided in charge. Explore what that one fact buys you. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Water is polar, so its molecules hydrogen-bond to one another — the source of all its special properties.
  • As the universal solvent, water dissolves hydrophilic solutes but not hydrophobic ones, a split that shapes membranes.
  • High heat capacity and evaporative cooling let water stabilize body temperature and enable sweating.
  • Water is a reactant — removed in dehydration synthesis, added in hydrolysis.

Section 5pH, acids, bases & buffers

What pH actually measures

pH is a measure of how many free hydrogen ions (H⁺) are floating loose in a fluid. The more H⁺, the more acidic the fluid; the fewer, the more basic (alkaline). The scale runs from 0 to 14, with 7 as neutral (pure water). Below 7 is acidic, above 7 is basic. The one twist students must respect is that the scale is logarithmic: each whole step is a tenfold change in H⁺. A fluid at pH 4 is not slightly more acidic than pH 5 — it holds ten times more hydrogen ions. That is why a drift of even a few tenths of a pH unit in the blood is a genuine emergency.

Acids, bases, and the number that matters most

An acid is a substance that releases H⁺ into solution, driving pH down — stomach acid sits near pH 2. A base accepts H⁺ (or releases OH⁻), driving pH up — household bleach and pancreatic secretions are basic. The number that matters most in all of physiology is the pH of blood, which must stay locked in the razor-thin range of 7.35 to 7.45 (about 7.4). Slip below 7.35 and you are in acidosis; rise above 7.45 and you are in alkalosis. Push past roughly 6.8 or 7.8 and proteins denature, enzymes fail, and the situation becomes life-threatening. Compared with the wild pH swings of the world outside, the body keeps its blood almost eerily steady.

FluidApprox. pHAcidic / basic
Stomach acid~2Strongly acidic
Urine~6Slightly acidic
Pure water7Neutral
Blood7.35–7.45Slightly basic
Pancreatic juice~8Basic

Buffers: holding the line at 7.4

How does blood hold its pH so steady while acids pour in from every meal and every muscle? The answer is buffers — chemical partnerships that soak up excess H⁺ when a fluid turns too acidic and release H⁺ when it turns too basic. The body's front-line buffer is the bicarbonate system: carbonic acid (H₂CO₃) and bicarbonate ion (HCO₃⁻) sit in equilibrium, trading hydrogen ions back and forth to blunt any swing. Buffers do not make acid disappear; they cushion the blow long enough for the lungs (which blow off CO₂) and kidneys (which excrete H⁺) to restore true balance. It is homeostasis at the molecular level — and a preview of the acid–base chapters ahead.

A little number, a big deal. Because pH is logarithmic, blood at 7.1 holds roughly twice the H⁺ of blood at 7.4 — a change that sounds tiny on paper but can slow the heart, cloud consciousness, and threaten life. When a clinician frets over a pH of 7.2, remember it is not a rounding error; it is a tenfold-scale alarm.

Push the pH up and down and watch buffers fight to pull it back toward neutral in the interactive.

Interactive pH measures free H⁺; blood must stay near 7.4. Push the pH around and watch buffers respond. Open full ↑
Key points
  • pH measures free H⁺: below 7 is acidic, above 7 is basic, and the scale is logarithmic (each unit = 10×).
  • Acids release H⁺; bases accept it; blood must stay in the narrow range 7.35–7.45.
  • Straying gives acidosis (too low) or alkalosis (too high), both dangerous.
  • Buffers — chiefly the bicarbonate system — absorb or release H⁺ to hold pH steady.

Section 6The molecules of life

Organic molecules and the carbon backbone

Turn from the small inorganic molecules — water, salts, gases — to the big organic ones, and you meet the true building blocks of the body. Organic molecules are, by definition, built on a skeleton of carbon. Carbon is the perfect construction atom because it can form four covalent bonds at once, letting it chain into long backbones, branch, and close into rings. The largest of these — macromolecules — are usually polymers: long chains snapped together from small repeating units called monomers. The body assembles a polymer by dehydration synthesis (removing a water molecule to bond two monomers) and takes it apart by hydrolysis (adding water to split the bond). Nearly everything you are made of belongs to just four families — plus one special molecule that powers the whole enterprise.

The four families

Carbohydrates are sugars and their chains — monomers called monosaccharides (glucose) link into polysaccharides (glycogen, starch). They are the body's quick, preferred fuel. Lipids are the oily, water-fearing molecules: triglycerides store energy and insulate, phospholipids build every cell membrane, and steroids like cholesterol become hormones. Their hydrophobic nature is exactly what makes membranes work. Proteins are the body's workhorses, built from twenty kinds of amino acids strung together and then folded into precise three-dimensional shapes — and because shape determines function, a protein that unfolds (denatures) from heat or wrong pH stops working, like an egg white turning solid in a hot pan. Proteins serve as enzymes, structural fibers, transporters, and messengers. Nucleic acids — DNA and RNA — are chains of nucleotides that store and carry the genetic instructions for building every protein you own.

FamilyMonomer / unitExampleMain job
CarbohydrateMonosaccharide (glucose)Glycogen, starchQuick energy; short-term fuel storage
LipidFatty acids + glycerolTriglyceride, phospholipid, cholesterolEnergy storage, insulation, membranes, hormones
ProteinAmino acid (20 kinds)Enzymes, keratin, hemoglobinStructure, catalysis, transport, signaling
Nucleic acidNucleotideDNA, RNAStore and express genetic information

ATP: the cell's energy currency

One small molecule deserves its own spotlight: adenosine triphosphate, or ATP. It is the universal energy currency every cell spends. ATP holds three phosphate groups on a tail, and the bond to the last phosphate is a coiled spring of energy. When the cell needs power — to contract a muscle, pump an ion, or build a molecule — it snaps off that last phosphate (ATP → ADP + phosphate) and the released energy drives the work. The cell then recharges ADP back into ATP using energy from food. You are, in a very real sense, rebuilding your body weight in ATP every day, spending and remaking it billions of times a second.

Shape is everything for a protein. A protein's job is written in its folded shape, so anything that unfolds it — a high fever, an acid or base, some poisons — can destroy its function even though the amino acids are all still there. That is denaturation, and it is why a fever above about 41 °C is dangerous: the body's own enzymes begin to lose their shape.

Pick a macromolecule family and watch it snap together from its monomers in the interactive below.

Interactive Four families of organic molecules, each built from small monomers — pick one and watch it build. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Organic molecules are built on carbon, which forms four bonds; big ones are polymers of repeating monomers.
  • Polymers form by dehydration synthesis (removing water) and break by hydrolysis (adding water).
  • The four families: carbohydrates (fuel), lipids (membranes, storage, hormones), proteins (workhorses whose shape is their function), and nucleic acids (genetic information).
  • ATP is the cell's energy currency, releasing energy when its last phosphate is removed.

The chapter in one breath

  • All matter is built from elements, and the smallest unit of an element is the atom — a nucleus of protons and neutrons orbited by electrons; the atomic number (protons) defines the element.
  • Six elements — O, C, H, N, Ca, P — make up about 99% of the body; atoms bond to fill their outer shell (the octet rule).
  • Three bonds hold molecules together: ionic (transfer electrons → ions), covalent (share electrons, polar or nonpolar), and weak hydrogen bonds.
  • Reactions rearrange atoms by synthesis, decomposition, or exchange; each must clear an activation energy hill that enzymes lower.
  • Water is polar, making it the body's solvent, temperature buffer, and lubricant, and a reactant in building and breaking molecules.
  • pH tracks free H⁺ on a logarithmic scale; blood is held at 7.35–7.45 by buffers such as bicarbonate.
  • Life is built from four organic families — carbohydrates, lipids, proteins, nucleic acids — plus ATP, assembled by dehydration synthesis and broken by hydrolysis.

ReferenceWords worth knowing

A quick reference for the terms in this chapter — skim it now, or circle back whenever a word trips you up.

Acid
A substance that releases hydrogen ions (H⁺) into solution, lowering pH below 7.
Activation energy
The initial energy hump that reactants must overcome before a reaction can proceed; enzymes lower it.
Atom
The smallest unit of an element that retains the element's properties; a nucleus of protons and neutrons orbited by electrons.
Atomic number
The number of protons in an atom's nucleus, which defines the element.
ATP (adenosine triphosphate)
The cell's energy currency; it releases usable energy when its terminal phosphate is removed to form ADP.
Base
A substance that accepts hydrogen ions (or releases OH⁻), raising pH above 7.
Buffer
A chemical system that resists changes in pH by absorbing or releasing H⁺, such as the bicarbonate buffer of blood.
Carbohydrate
An organic family of sugars and their polymers (glucose, glycogen) used mainly for quick energy.
Covalent bond
A bond in which atoms share electrons; equal sharing is nonpolar, unequal sharing is polar.
Dehydration synthesis
Building a polymer by removing a water molecule to link two monomers.
Electron
A negatively charged, nearly massless particle that orbits the nucleus in shells and drives chemical bonding.
Element
A pure substance that cannot be broken down chemically; the body is built mostly from six of them.
Enzyme
A biological catalyst (usually a protein) that lowers a reaction's activation energy without being consumed.
Hydrogen bond
A weak attraction between the partial-positive hydrogen of one polar molecule and a partial-negative atom of another.
Hydrolysis
Breaking a polymer apart by adding a water molecule across the bond.
Ionic bond
A bond formed when one atom donates an electron to another, creating oppositely charged ions that attract.
Isotope
An atom of an element with the usual number of protons but a different number of neutrons.
Lipid
A hydrophobic organic family including triglycerides, phospholipids, and steroids; used for storage, membranes, and hormones.
Nucleic acid
A polymer of nucleotides (DNA or RNA) that stores and expresses genetic information.
pH
A logarithmic measure of free hydrogen-ion concentration; 7 is neutral, lower is acidic, higher is basic.
Polar molecule
A molecule with an uneven charge distribution, such as water, where one end is slightly negative and the other slightly positive.
Protein
A polymer of amino acids folded into a specific shape; its shape determines its function as an enzyme, structure, transporter, or messenger.

Check yourself

1. An atom has 8 protons, 8 neutrons, and 8 electrons. What element is it, what is its net charge, and what is its mass number?
Oxygen; net charge zero; mass number 16. The atomic number (8 protons) makes it oxygen; equal protons and electrons make it neutral; protons plus neutrons (8 + 8) give a mass number of 16.
2. Two atoms of the same element have different numbers of neutrons. What are they called, and does their chemical behavior change?
They are isotopes, and their chemistry is essentially the same. Chemical behavior is set by electrons (and thus proton count), not neutrons, so isotopes react alike — but the extra neutrons can make one radioactive and useful for imaging.
3. Why do atoms form chemical bonds at all, and why are the noble gases like neon inert?
Atoms bond to fill their outer (valence) shell and become stable — the octet rule. Noble gases already have full outer shells, so they have no reason to gain, lose, or share electrons and stay unreactive.
4. Classify each bond: (a) sodium gives an electron to chlorine; (b) two oxygen atoms share electrons equally; (c) a partial-positive H is drawn to a partial-negative O on a neighboring molecule.
(a) Ionic; (b) nonpolar covalent; (c) hydrogen bond. Ionic bonds transfer electrons to make ions, covalent bonds share them (equally here, so nonpolar), and hydrogen bonds are weak attractions between polar molecules.
5. Salt (NaCl) and sugar both dissolve in water, but only one splits into charged particles. Which one, what are those particles called, and why does the other stay whole?
Salt splits into ions (electrolytes: Na⁺ and Cl⁻); sugar stays a whole molecule. Salt's ionic bond gives way in water, freeing charged ions, while sugar's atoms are held by covalent bonds that water cannot break.
6. Name the three types of chemical reaction and match each to an example: digesting starch into glucose; joining amino acids into a protein; buffers trading H⁺.
Decomposition = digesting starch; synthesis = joining amino acids; exchange = buffers trading H⁺. Decomposition breaks molecules apart (catabolic), synthesis builds them up (anabolic), and exchange swaps partners.
7. What is activation energy, and how does an enzyme let a reaction run faster at body temperature without being used up?
Activation energy is the initial energy hump a reaction must clear; an enzyme lowers that hump. By lowering activation energy the enzyme speeds the reaction dramatically at 37 °C, and because it is a catalyst it emerges unchanged and can act again.
8. Water's most important feature is that it is polar. Explain what “polar” means for a water molecule and name two properties that result.
Polar means uneven charge: oxygen is slightly negative, the hydrogens slightly positive. This lets water hydrogen-bond to itself and to solutes, giving it its solvent power, high heat capacity, cohesion, and role in reactions — any two of these count.
9. Why do fat-soluble vitamins need fat to be absorbed, phrased using the rule “like dissolves like”?
Because they are nonpolar (hydrophobic), they dissolve in nonpolar fats, not in polar water. “Like dissolves like” means polar substances dissolve in water and nonpolar ones dissolve in fat, so fat-soluble vitamins ride along with dietary fat.
10. The pH scale is logarithmic. How much more acidic is a fluid at pH 4 than one at pH 6, and why is a blood pH of 7.1 a serious concern?
pH 4 has 100 times more H⁺ than pH 6 (two units = 10 × 10). Because each unit is a tenfold change, even the small drop from 7.4 to 7.1 roughly doubles blood H⁺ — enough to disrupt enzymes and threaten life (acidosis).
11. Blood is constantly flooded with acids yet holds its pH near 7.4. What chemical system prevents wild swings, and does it remove the acid permanently?
Buffers — chiefly the bicarbonate system (H₂CO₃ / HCO₃⁻) — and no, they only cushion the change. Buffers absorb or release H⁺ to blunt swings, buying time for the lungs (exhaling CO₂) and kidneys (excreting H⁺) to restore true balance.
12. Name the four organic macromolecule families and give the monomer of each. Then explain why a high fever endangers proteins.
Carbohydrates (monosaccharides), lipids (fatty acids + glycerol), proteins (amino acids), nucleic acids (nucleotides). A protein's function depends on its folded shape; a high fever denatures proteins — unfolds them — so enzymes lose their shape and stop working even though the amino acids remain.
Anatomy & Physiology · Chapter 3

The Cellular Level of Organization

If the last chapter zoomed out to the whole body, this one zooms all the way in — to the single smallest thing that can still be called alive. The cell is where physiology actually happens: where oxygen is burned for energy, where genes are read into proteins, where the decision to grow or divide is made. In this chapter you will meet the membrane that wraps every cell and chooses what crosses it, tour the tiny organelles that split the work inside, follow a gene as it becomes a working protein, and watch one cell become two. Master the cell and the rest of the body starts to make sense — because every tissue, organ, and system is just cells, cooperating.

By the end of this chapter you can

  • Describe the fluid mosaic model and explain the jobs of the phospholipid bilayer, membrane proteins, cholesterol, and the sugar coat.
  • Distinguish passive from active transport, and place diffusion, facilitated diffusion, osmosis, the Na⁺/K⁺ pump, and bulk transport in the right column.
  • Name the major organelles and state the specific function of each.
  • Explain how the nucleus stores DNA and how that DNA is packaged into chromatin and chromosomes.
  • Trace protein synthesis from gene to protein through transcription and translation.
  • Order the phases of the cell cycle and describe what happens in each stage of mitosis.
  • Predict what happens to a cell in isotonic, hypotonic, and hypertonic solutions and connect it to the choice of an IV fluid.

Section 1The cell membrane

A wall that thinks

Every cell in your body is wrapped in a plasma membrane — a boundary so thin it is invisible under a normal light microscope, yet smart enough to decide what enters and what leaves. Without it a cell would simply dissolve into its surroundings. The membrane does three big jobs at once: it separates the cell's inside from the outside, it controls traffic across that border (it is selectively permeable, letting some things through and blocking others), and it communicates, carrying receptors and ID tags that let the cell sense and be recognized. Everything else in this chapter depends on this one structure holding the line.

The fluid mosaic model

We picture the membrane using the fluid mosaic model, and both words matter. Mosaic means it is a patchwork of many different pieces — lipids, proteins, and sugars set side by side like tiles. Fluid means those pieces are not glued down; they drift and jiggle sideways within the sheet, so the membrane behaves more like a film of oil than a rigid wall. The foundation of the whole structure is the phospholipid bilayer: two layers of phospholipid molecules arranged tail-to-tail. Each phospholipid is amphipathic — it has a hydrophilic ("water-loving") phosphate head and two hydrophobic ("water-fearing") fatty-acid tails. Drop these molecules in water and they self-assemble automatically: the heads face out toward the watery fluid on both sides, and the oily tails hide in the middle, away from water. That greasy core is the secret to the membrane's pickiness — small, fat-soluble molecules slip through it easily, while ions and water-loving molecules are turned away and need a protein doorway.

Studded through this lipid sheet are the membrane proteins, and they do most of the interesting work. Integral (transmembrane) proteins pass all the way through the bilayer and form the channels, carriers, and pumps that move substances across. Peripheral proteins cling to one surface and often act as enzymes or anchors. Other proteins serve as receptors that bind hormones and signals, or as identity markers that label the cell as "self." Threaded among the lipids are molecules of cholesterol, which act as a fluidity buffer — keeping the membrane from becoming too stiff when it is cold or too runny when it is warm. Finally, short sugar chains attached to proteins and lipids form the glycocalyx, a fuzzy sugar coat on the cell's outer face that handles recognition, adhesion, and self-vs-nonself identity.

ComponentWhat it isMain job
Phospholipid bilayerTwo layers of amphipathic phospholipids, tails inwardThe basic barrier; blocks water-soluble molecules, passes small fat-soluble ones
Integral proteinsProteins spanning the whole membraneChannels, carriers, and pumps for transport; receptors
Peripheral proteinsProteins attached to one surfaceEnzymes, structural anchors, cell-shape support
CholesterolLipid molecules wedged between phospholipidsStabilizes fluidity across temperature changes
GlycocalyxSugar chains on glycoproteins and glycolipidsCell recognition, adhesion, self-identity markers
Selectivity is the whole point. The membrane is not just a bag — it is a bouncer. Fat-soluble things (O₂, CO₂, steroid hormones) walk right through the oily core; charged ions (Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺) and even water need a protein channel. This single fact explains almost everything about how substances cross cells, and it is why the transplant team frets over the "self" markers in the glycocalyx.

Tap each piece of the membrane below to meet it up close.

Interactive A jiggling double layer of phospholipids studded with proteins, cholesterol, and sugar tags — tap each piece to meet it. Open full ↑
Key points
  • The plasma membrane separates, controls traffic (selectively permeable), and communicates.
  • The fluid mosaic model: a fluid phospholipid bilayer (hydrophilic heads out, hydrophobic tails in) tiled with proteins, cholesterol, and sugars.
  • Small fat-soluble molecules cross the oily core freely; ions and water-loving molecules need protein channels or carriers.
  • Cholesterol stabilizes fluidity; the glycocalyx handles cell recognition and self-identity.

Section 2Moving things across

The one question to always ask: does it cost energy?

A cell must constantly bring in nutrients, push out wastes, and hold the right balance of salts and water. Every method it uses to move a substance across the membrane falls into one of two camps, and the dividing line is energy. In passive transport, substances move down their concentration gradient — from where they are crowded to where they are sparse — and this costs the cell no ATP, because it is simply going with the natural flow. In active transport, the cell moves substances up a gradient, against the flow, from low concentration to high — and that always requires an input of energy, usually ATP. Before you memorize a list of transport names, just ask: is it drifting downhill for free (passive) or being pushed uphill at a cost (active)?

Passive transport — drifting downhill

The simplest form is simple diffusion: molecules are always in random motion, so over time they spread out from crowded to less-crowded areas until they are evenly mixed. Small, nonpolar molecules such as oxygen and carbon dioxide diffuse straight through the lipid core — which is exactly how O₂ enters and CO₂ leaves every cell. When a molecule is too big or too charged to cross the lipids on its own, it uses facilitated diffusion: it still moves downhill for free, but through a protein channel or carrier that gives it a passage. Glucose entering a cell is the classic example. A special, vital case of passive transport is osmosis — the diffusion of water across a selectively permeable membrane — which is important enough to earn its own section at the end of this chapter.

Active transport — pushing uphill

Sometimes a cell needs a substance to be more concentrated on one side than the other, which means moving it against its gradient — and that requires energy. In primary active transport, a pump burns ATP directly. The most famous is the sodium–potassium pump (Na⁺/K⁺ pump), which pushes 3 Na⁺ out of the cell and 2 K⁺ in for every ATP it spends. This pump runs in every cell you own and is the foundation of the electrical charge that lets nerves fire and muscles contract. In secondary active transport, the cell rides the gradient built by a primary pump, coupling the downhill rush of one ion to drag another substance uphill alongside it. Finally, for cargo far too large for any channel — whole droplets or particles — the cell uses bulk transport, wrapping material in a piece of membrane: endocytosis brings it in (including phagocytosis, "cell eating," and pinocytosis, "cell drinking"), and exocytosis ships it out.

MethodEnergy?Direction & example
Simple diffusionNo (passive)Down gradient, straight through lipids — O₂, CO₂
Facilitated diffusionNo (passive)Down gradient, through a protein — glucose, ions
OsmosisNo (passive)Water moves toward the saltier side
Primary active transportYes — ATP directlyUp gradient by a pump — Na⁺/K⁺ pump (3 out, 2 in)
Secondary active transportYes — ion gradientRides another ion's gradient — glucose–Na⁺ cotransport in the gut
Bulk transportYes (active)Membrane-wrapped cargo — endocytosis (in), exocytosis (out)
Follow the ATP. The Na⁺/K⁺ pump quietly consumes a large share of your resting energy budget just to keep sodium out and potassium in. When a cell loses its oxygen supply and can no longer make ATP — as in a stroke or a heart attack — the pump stalls, sodium and water pour in, and the cell swells and dies. Cell death very often begins as a failed pump.

Pick a transport method below and watch the molecules cross.

Interactive Some things drift across for free, some need a protein doorway, and some must be pumped uphill — pick a method and watch molecules cross. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Passive transport moves substances down their gradient for free; active transport moves them up a gradient at a cost in energy.
  • Passive types: simple diffusion, facilitated diffusion (through a protein), and osmosis (water).
  • The Na⁺/K⁺ pump (3 out, 2 in per ATP) is primary active transport and underlies nerve and muscle electricity.
  • Bulk transport handles the biggest cargo: endocytosis in, exocytosis out.

Section 3Inside the cell: the organelles

A factory with departments

Look inside the membrane and you find not an empty bag but a busy, organized workshop. Suspended in a gel-like fluid called the cytosol are the organelles — "little organs," each a specialized department with its own job. Dividing the labor this way lets the cell run many chemical processes at once without them interfering, much as walls separate the departments of a factory. Some organelles are wrapped in their own membranes (a design that keeps their contents and reactions sealed off), while a few, like ribosomes, are not. Learning the cell means learning who does what.

Control and manufacturing

The largest organelle is usually the nucleus, the cell's control center. It is bounded by a double membrane called the nuclear envelope, dotted with pores that let messages pass, and it holds the cell's DNA — the master instructions for building every protein. Between divisions this DNA is spread out as a loose tangle called chromatin; when the cell is about to divide it condenses into visible chromosomes. Inside the nucleus sits a dense spot, the nucleolus, where ribosomes are assembled. Ribosomes themselves are the protein-building machines; some float free in the cytosol, and some stud the rough endoplasmic reticulum (rough ER), a folded membrane network that manufactures and packages proteins destined for export or the membrane. Next door, the smooth ER (no ribosomes) makes lipids, handles detoxification, and stores calcium. Proteins and lipids then travel to the Golgi apparatus, the cell's shipping-and-receiving department, which modifies, sorts, and packages them into vesicles addressed to their destinations.

Power, cleanup, and shape

All of this work needs energy, and that comes from the mitochondria — the "powerhouses" of the cell, where nutrients and oxygen are converted into ATP, the cell's usable energy currency. Cells that work hard, like muscle and liver cells, are packed with them. Cleanup falls to two organelles: lysosomes, membrane sacs full of digestive enzymes that break down worn-out parts, bacteria, and debris, and peroxisomes, which neutralize toxic byproducts. Holding the whole cell together is the cytoskeleton, a scaffold of protein filaments that gives the cell its shape, anchors organelles, and drives movement; the centrosome (with its pair of centrioles) organizes this scaffold and, crucially, builds the fibers that pull chromosomes apart during division.

OrganelleFunctionMemory hook
NucleusStores DNA; directs the cellThe control room / library
NucleolusAssembles ribosomesRibosome factory inside the nucleus
RibosomeBuilds proteins from mRNAThe 3-D printer
Rough ERMakes and packages proteins (has ribosomes)Protein assembly line
Smooth ERMakes lipids, detoxifies, stores Ca²⁺Lipid + detox lab
Golgi apparatusModifies, sorts, ships proteins in vesiclesThe post office
MitochondrionMakes ATP from nutrients + O₂The powerhouse
LysosomeDigests debris and worn-out partsThe recycling / demolition crew
CytoskeletonShape, support, transport, movementThe internal scaffold
Mitochondria are almost tenants. Mitochondria carry their own small loop of DNA and are inherited almost entirely from your mother, a clue that they descend from ancient bacteria that took up residence inside our cells. Because heart, muscle, and nerve cells are so energy-hungry, inherited mitochondrial diseases tend to strike exactly those tissues first.

Tap an organelle to light it up, or hit Tour to walk the whole factory floor.

Interactive A cell is a tiny factory, and its organelles are the departments — tap one to light it up, or take the full tour. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Organelles are specialized departments suspended in the cytosol; membranes keep their reactions separate.
  • The nucleus stores DNA (as chromatin, condensing into chromosomes); the nucleolus builds ribosomes.
  • Protein pathway: ribosomes → rough ER → Golgi → vesicles; smooth ER makes lipids and stores calcium.
  • Mitochondria make ATP; lysosomes digest debris; the cytoskeleton gives shape and moves cargo.

Section 4From gene to protein

The instructions and the problem they must solve

Your DNA is a recipe book, and each gene is a single recipe for one protein. But there is a logistical problem: the DNA is locked inside the nucleus, while proteins are built by ribosomes out in the cytoplasm. The master book can never leave the library. The solution is to make a disposable working copy of just the one recipe you need and carry that copy out to the workshop. This is the heart of protein synthesis, and it happens in two moves — transcription (copy the gene) and translation (read the copy and build the protein). This flow of information, from DNA to RNA to protein, is so central it is called the central dogma of biology.

Transcription: copying the gene

Transcription takes place inside the nucleus. An enzyme called RNA polymerase unzips the DNA at the target gene and reads one strand, building a matching strand of messenger RNA (mRNA). RNA is similar to DNA but single-stranded and built from slightly different parts — notably it uses the base uracil (U) everywhere DNA would use thymine. The pairing rule is faithful: DNA's C pairs with G, and DNA's A specifies U in the RNA. The finished mRNA is a portable transcript of the gene — the working copy — and it slips out through a nuclear pore into the cytoplasm to find a ribosome.

Translation: reading the copy

Translation happens at a ribosome. Here the mRNA is read three bases at a time; each triplet is a codon, and each codon specifies one amino acid — the building block of proteins. Delivering those amino acids is the job of transfer RNA (tRNA): each tRNA carries a specific amino acid and displays a three-base anticodon that matches one codon. The ribosome moves along the mRNA codon by codon, tRNAs dock in order, and the ribosome links their amino acids into a growing chain. When a "stop" codon is reached, the finished chain is released, folds into its working shape, and becomes a protein. The whole path — DNA → mRNA → protein — converts a stored instruction into a physical molecule that does a job.

StepWhereWhat happens
TranscriptionNucleusRNA polymerase copies a gene from DNA into mRNA
mRNA leavesNuclear poreThe working copy travels to the cytoplasm
TranslationRibosomeCodons are read three bases at a time; tRNA anticodons deliver matching amino acids
ProductCytoplasm / ERAmino acids link into a chain that folds into a working protein
One letter can change everything. A codon is exactly three bases. In sickle cell disease, a single wrong DNA base swaps one amino acid in the hemoglobin protein — and that one substitution is enough to deform red blood cells into sickles. It is a vivid reminder that the exact sequence of the code, letter by letter, is what makes the protein work.

Step through the whole journey from gene to protein below.

Interactive Transcription copies the gene into mRNA, and translation reads that mRNA to build the protein — step through the whole journey. Open full ↑
Key points
  • A gene is the recipe for one protein; the central dogma is DNA → RNA → protein.
  • Transcription (in the nucleus) copies a gene into mRNA; RNA uses uracil in place of thymine.
  • Translation (at a ribosome) reads mRNA in three-base codons; tRNA anticodons deliver the matching amino acids.
  • A single base change can alter one amino acid and break the protein (sickle cell).

Section 5The cell cycle & mitosis

Living, then dividing

Your body makes billions of new cells every day — to grow, to heal a wound, to replace skin and blood cells that wear out. Every one of those new cells comes from an existing cell that grew and split in two. The orderly sequence a cell follows to do this is the cell cycle, and it has two very unequal parts. The long stretch, interphase, is the cell's ordinary working life: it grows, does its job, and quietly prepares to divide. The short, dramatic finale, mitosis, is the actual splitting of the nucleus. Most of a cell's life is spent in interphase; mitosis is over quickly.

Interphase: grow, copy, prepare

Interphase is divided into three stages. In G₁ (first gap) the cell grows, makes proteins, and carries out its normal functions. In S phase (synthesis) the cell copies its entire DNA, so that each future daughter cell will get a complete set — this is the single most important preparation for division. In G₂ (second gap) the cell finishes growing, builds the organelles it will need, and double-checks the copied DNA for errors. Only after passing these preparations does the cell commit to dividing. Guarding the transitions are checkpoints — quality-control stops where the cell verifies that everything is in order before proceeding.

Mitosis: splitting the nucleus, then the cell

Mitosis divides the duplicated DNA evenly into two nuclei, and it unfolds in four stages you can remember as PMAT. In prophase, the copied chromatin condenses into visible chromosomes and the spindle fibers begin to form. In metaphase, the chromosomes line up single file across the middle of the cell. In anaphase, the spindle fibers pull the two copies of each chromosome apart toward opposite ends. In telophase, two new nuclear envelopes form around the two separated sets. Mitosis divides the nucleus; a separate step, cytokinesis, pinches the cytoplasm in two — and the result is two genetically identical daughter cells, each ready to begin interphase of its own.

PhasePart of cycleWhat happens
G₁InterphaseCell grows and does its normal work
SInterphaseDNA is copied in full
G₂InterphaseFinal growth; DNA is checked for errors
ProphaseMitosisChromatin condenses into chromosomes; spindle forms
MetaphaseMitosisChromosomes line up across the middle
AnaphaseMitosisChromosome copies are pulled to opposite poles
TelophaseMitosisTwo new nuclei form; cytokinesis splits the cell
Cancer is the cell cycle gone rogue. The checkpoints exist to stop a damaged cell from dividing. When the genes that run those checkpoints — guardians like p53 — are broken, cells with errors keep dividing unchecked. That uncontrolled division is cancer, which is why so much of oncology comes back to the machinery of this one diagram.

Step through interphase and the four phases of mitosis below.

Interactive A cell grows, copies its DNA, then splits it evenly — most of the time it is just living (interphase), and the dramatic split is mitosis. Step through it. Open full ↑
Key points
  • The cell cycle = long interphase (G₁ → S → G₂) + short mitosis; DNA is copied in S phase.
  • Mitosis in order is PMAT: prophase, metaphase, anaphase, telophase; cytokinesis then splits the cytoplasm.
  • The result is two genetically identical daughter cells.
  • Checkpoints (guarded by genes like p53) block damaged cells from dividing; their failure leads to cancer.

Section 6Osmosis & tonicity

Water chases salt

We saved the most clinically important form of transport for last. Osmosis is the movement of water across a selectively permeable membrane, and it follows one simple rule: water moves toward the side with more dissolved particles (more solute, "saltier"). The membrane blocks the solute from moving to even things out, so water moves instead — flowing toward the saltier compartment until the concentrations balance. Because a cell's membrane lets water pass freely but holds many solutes in place, the amount of solute outside the cell decides which way water flows across it, and therefore whether the cell gains or loses water.

Tonicity: three solutions, three fates

Tonicity describes how a solution affects a cell's water balance, comparing the solute outside the cell to the solute inside. There are three possibilities. An isotonic solution has the same solute concentration as the cell's interior, so water moves in and out equally and the cell stays the same — its normal, healthy state. A hypotonic solution is more dilute than the cell (less solute outside), so water rushes in; the cell swells, and if enough water enters it can burst, an event called lysis (in red blood cells, hemolysis). A hypertonic solution is saltier than the cell (more solute outside), so water is pulled out; the cell shrivels, a shrinking called crenation. The trick to keeping these straight is that water always follows the solute — find the saltier side and water heads there.

SolutionOutside vs. cellWater movesCell result
IsotonicEqual soluteIn = out (no net change)Stays normal
HypotonicLess solute outside (dilute)Into the cellSwells, may burst (lysis)
HypertonicMore solute outside (salty)Out of the cellShrivels (crenation)
