This is why the IV fluid matters. Fluids given into a vein bathe your blood cells directly. Normal saline (0.9% NaCl) is isotonic, so it expands blood volume without harming cells. Pure water or a very dilute fluid is dangerously hypotonic — it would make red cells swell and burst — which is why sterile water is never infused on its own. Choosing an IV fluid is applied tonicity, and getting it wrong can be fatal.

Drop a cell into each solution below and watch its fate.

Interactive Water always drifts toward saltier water, so the solution you place a cell in decides its fate — stay normal, swell and burst, or shrivel up. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Osmosis is water moving across a membrane toward the side with more solute.
  • Isotonic → no net change; hypotonic → water in, cell swells/bursts (lysis); hypertonic → water out, cell shrivels (crenation).
  • Water always follows the solute — find the saltier side.
  • IV fluids apply this directly: 0.9% normal saline is isotonic; never infuse pure water.

The chapter in one breath

  • Every cell is wrapped in a selectively permeable plasma membrane, described by the fluid mosaic model — a fluid phospholipid bilayer tiled with proteins, cholesterol, and sugars.
  • Substances cross by passive transport (down a gradient, free: diffusion, facilitated diffusion, osmosis) or active transport (up a gradient, costs energy: the Na⁺/K⁺ pump, bulk transport).
  • Inside, organelles split the labor: the nucleus stores DNA, ribosomes → ER → Golgi build and ship proteins, mitochondria make ATP, and lysosomes clean up.
  • Protein synthesis flows DNA → RNA → protein: transcription copies a gene into mRNA in the nucleus; translation reads its codons at a ribosome to build the protein.
  • The cell cycle is long interphase (G₁/S/G₂, with DNA copied in S) plus short mitosis (PMAT) and cytokinesis, yielding two identical daughter cells; broken checkpoints lead to cancer.
  • Osmosis moves water toward the saltier side; tonicity decides a cell's fate — isotonic (normal), hypotonic (swells/bursts), hypertonic (shrivels) — which is exactly why IV fluids are chosen with care.

ReferenceWords worth knowing

A quick reference for the terms in this chapter — skim it now, or circle back whenever a word trips you up.

Active transport
Movement of a substance up its concentration gradient (low to high), which requires an input of energy such as ATP.
Codon
A sequence of three mRNA bases that specifies one amino acid during translation.
Crenation
The shriveling of a cell that loses water in a hypertonic solution.
Cytokinesis
The division of the cytoplasm that follows mitosis, physically splitting one cell into two.
Cytoskeleton
The internal network of protein filaments that gives a cell shape and support and moves its contents.
Diffusion
The passive spread of molecules from an area of high concentration to one of low concentration until evenly mixed.
Fluid mosaic model
The description of the plasma membrane as a fluid phospholipid bilayer studded with a mosaic of proteins, cholesterol, and carbohydrates.
Golgi apparatus
The organelle that modifies, sorts, and packages proteins and lipids into vesicles for delivery.
Hypertonic
A solution with a higher solute concentration than the cell, causing water to leave and the cell to shrivel.
Hypotonic
A solution with a lower solute concentration than the cell, causing water to enter and the cell to swell or burst.
Interphase
The long portion of the cell cycle (G₁, S, G₂) during which a cell grows, copies its DNA, and prepares to divide.
Isotonic
A solution with the same solute concentration as the cell, so there is no net movement of water and the cell keeps its normal shape.
Lysosome
A membrane-bound sac of digestive enzymes that breaks down debris, worn-out organelles, and ingested material.
Mitochondrion
The organelle that generates ATP from nutrients and oxygen; the cell's powerhouse.
Mitosis
Division of the nucleus into two genetically identical nuclei, proceeding through prophase, metaphase, anaphase, and telophase.
Nucleus
The membrane-bound control center that stores the cell's DNA and directs its activities.
Osmosis
The diffusion of water across a selectively permeable membrane toward the side with the higher solute concentration.
Passive transport
Movement of a substance down its concentration gradient, requiring no energy from the cell.
Phospholipid bilayer
The double layer of amphipathic phospholipids — hydrophilic heads out, hydrophobic tails in — that forms the core of the membrane.
Sodium–potassium pump
A primary active transport protein that pushes 3 Na⁺ out and 2 K⁺ in per ATP, maintaining the cell's ion gradients.
Transcription
The copying of a gene from DNA into messenger RNA inside the nucleus.
Translation
The reading of mRNA codons at a ribosome to assemble amino acids into a protein.

Check yourself

1. The fluid mosaic model has two key words. Explain what "fluid" and "mosaic" each describe about the membrane.
"Fluid" = the components drift and jiggle sideways; "mosaic" = it is a patchwork of different pieces (lipids, proteins, sugars). The membrane behaves like a film of oil, not a rigid wall, and its many different tiles sit side by side within the same fluid sheet.
2. Why can oxygen slip straight through the membrane while sodium ions (Na⁺) need a protein channel?
Oxygen is small and fat-soluble, so it dissolves through the oily lipid core; Na⁺ is charged and water-loving, so it is repelled by the core and must use a channel. The hydrophobic center of the bilayer is the gatekeeper — it welcomes small nonpolar molecules and blocks ions.
3. Sort each into passive or active transport: simple diffusion, the Na⁺/K⁺ pump, osmosis, exocytosis.
Passive: simple diffusion and osmosis. Active: the Na⁺/K⁺ pump and exocytosis. Passive methods move substances down a gradient for free; active methods move them up a gradient or in bulk and cost energy.
4. The Na⁺/K⁺ pump moves ions in a fixed ratio. What is that ratio and its direction, and why does it matter clinically?
3 Na⁺ out and 2 K⁺ in per ATP. It builds the ion gradients behind nerve and muscle electricity; when a cell loses O₂ and cannot make ATP (stroke, heart attack), the pump fails, sodium and water flood in, and the cell swells and dies.
5. Trace a protein's path through the organelles from where its recipe is read to where it is shipped out.
Ribosome (on the rough ER) → rough ER → Golgi apparatus → vesicle to its destination. The ribosome builds it, the rough ER packages it, and the Golgi — the cell's post office — modifies, sorts, and ships it.
6. Which organelle makes ATP, and why are muscle and heart cells especially rich in them?
The mitochondrion; it makes ATP from nutrients and oxygen. Hard-working tissues like muscle, heart, and nerve demand large amounts of energy, so they are packed with mitochondria — and are hit first by mitochondrial diseases.
7. Name the two steps of protein synthesis, say where each occurs, and give the product of each.
Transcription (in the nucleus) makes mRNA; translation (at a ribosome) makes the protein. Transcription copies the gene into a portable mRNA; translation reads that mRNA's codons to assemble amino acids into the finished protein.
8. A codon is read from mRNA and matched by a tRNA. How many bases is a codon, and what does each codon specify?
Three bases; each codon specifies one amino acid. A tRNA carrying a matching three-base anticodon delivers that amino acid to the ribosome, which links it into the growing protein chain.
9. In which phase of the cell cycle is DNA copied, and why must this happen before mitosis?
S phase (synthesis), part of interphase. The DNA must be fully duplicated first so that when mitosis divides it, each of the two daughter cells receives a complete, identical set of instructions.
10. Put the four stages of mitosis in order and give the one-line event of each.
Prophase (chromatin condenses, spindle forms) → metaphase (chromosomes line up in the middle) → anaphase (copies pulled to opposite poles) → telophase (two new nuclei form). Remember it as PMAT; cytokinesis then splits the cytoplasm into two identical cells.
11. A red blood cell is placed in pure water. Is that solution hypotonic, isotonic, or hypertonic, and what happens to the cell?
Hypotonic; water rushes in, the cell swells and can burst (hemolysis). Pure water has less solute than the cell's interior, so water follows the solute inward — which is exactly why sterile water is never given as an IV fluid on its own.
12. Which IV fluid is isotonic to blood, and why is that the safe default for expanding blood volume?
0.9% normal saline is isotonic. Because its solute concentration matches the cells it bathes, water moves in and out equally, expanding blood volume without swelling or shriveling red blood cells.
Anatomy & Physiology · Chapter 4

The Tissue Level of Organization

In the last chapter we zoomed in on the single cell; now we take one step back up the ladder. Cells rarely work alone — they gather into tissues, teams of similar cells that share a job and, together, do something no lone cell could. The remarkable part is how few kinds there are: your entire body, every organ in it, is stitched together from just four primary tissues. This chapter introduces all four — how each looks under the microscope, where it lives, and what it does — and ends with the story every tissue eventually has to tell: how it heals when it is hurt.

By the end of this chapter you can

  • Define tissue and name the four primary tissue types, giving the general job of each.
  • List the shared features of epithelial tissue and classify any epithelium by its layering and cell shape.
  • Describe the common blueprint of all connective tissue — cells scattered in an extracellular matrix of fibers and ground substance — and identify its major subtypes.
  • Distinguish the three muscle tissues by striations, voluntary vs. involuntary control, and cell shape.
  • Diagram a neuron, label its parts, and trace how nervous tissue carries a signal in one direction.
  • Outline the four stages of tissue repair and contrast regeneration with fibrosis (scarring).
  • Explain how the four tissues combine to build organs and body membranes.

Section 1The four tissue types

A society of cells

A tissue is a group of similar cells that work together to carry out a shared function. That is the whole definition, but the word "together" is doing a lot of work. The cells of a tissue are not just neighbors who happen to look alike — they are coordinated, held in place by junctions or by the material they secrete around themselves, and specialized for one kind of job. The branch of biology that studies tissues under the microscope is histology, and it is essentially anatomy at very high magnification: instead of asking what an organ looks like to the naked eye, we ask what it is made of.

Four families do all the work

What makes the whole subject learnable is that no matter how complicated an organ appears, it is assembled from only four primary tissue types. Learn these four and you have the vocabulary to describe any structure in the body. Each family answers a different fundamental need — a body must cover and line its surfaces, bind and support its parts, move, and communicate.

TissueWhat it doesWhere you find it
EpithelialCovers surfaces, lines cavities, forms glands; protects, absorbs, secretesSkin surface, gut lining, kidney tubules, gland cells
ConnectiveBinds, supports, protects, stores, and transports; the body's packing materialBone, fat, tendon, cartilage, blood
MuscleContracts to generate movement and force; also makes heatSkeletal muscles, heart wall, walls of hollow organs
NervousSenses, processes, and rapidly transmits electrical signalsBrain, spinal cord, peripheral nerves

Notice how different the four are in how much room the cells give one another. Epithelial cells are packed together so tightly there is almost no space between them — that is what makes a good barrier. Connective tissue does the opposite: its cells are few and far apart, floating in a sea of non-living material they secrete. That single contrast — crowded cells versus scattered cells in a matrix — is one of the fastest ways to tell the first two tissues apart on a slide.

Every tissue traces back to three germ layers. Very early in development the embryo forms three sheets — ectoderm, mesoderm, and endoderm. Epithelium arises from all three, most connective and muscle from mesoderm, and nervous tissue from ectoderm. Four tissues, three origins — a tidy reminder that all this variety grows from a common start.

Meet all four tissues side by side in the interactive below.

Interactive Meet each of the four tissues — how it looks, where it lives, and what it does. Open full ↑
Key points
  • A tissue is a group of similar cells performing a shared function; studying them is histology.
  • The body has four primary tissues: epithelial (cover/line), connective (bind/support), muscle (move), and nervous (communicate).
  • Epithelial cells are tightly packed; connective cells are scattered in a matrix — a quick way to tell them apart.

Section 2Naming an epithelium

What every epithelium has in common

Epithelial tissue covers every free surface of the body and lines every cavity, tube, and duct — the outer skin, the inside of your gut, the airways, the blood vessels. Because it stands at every boundary, it shares a set of features built for that role. Its cells are tightly packed with very little material between them, so almost nothing crosses without going through a cell. Every epithelium has polarity: an apical surface that faces the open space (often decorated with microvilli or cilia) and a basal surface anchored to a thin basement membrane that glues it to the connective tissue below. Epithelium is avascular — it has no blood vessels of its own and must be fed by diffusion from the tissue underneath — yet it is richly supplied with nerves, and it regenerates quickly, replacing cells lost to the constant wear of being a surface.

The two-word naming rule

Here is the payoff of this section: every epithelium is named by the same simple formula, so once you learn the rule you can name a tissue you have never seen. The name has two parts — first the number of layers, then the shape of the cells at the surface.

  • Layers. One layer of cells is simple; two or more stacked layers is stratified.
  • Shape. Flat, scale-like cells are squamous; cube-shaped cells are cuboidal; tall, column-shaped cells are columnar.

Snap the two halves together and you have the name: a single flat layer is simple squamous; many stacked flat layers make stratified squamous; one row of tall cells is simple columnar. The structure even predicts the job. Thin simple epithelia are ideal where things must cross quickly — simple squamous lines the air sacs of the lung and the walls of capillaries because oxygen and nutrients slip across it in an instant. Thick stratified epithelia are built for protection — stratified squamous forms the surface of your skin and the lining of the mouth and esophagus, layers you can afford to lose and rebuild.

EpitheliumStructureBest-known job and location
Simple squamousOne layer of flat cellsRapid diffusion — lung air sacs, capillary and vessel lining (endothelium)
Simple cuboidalOne layer of cube cellsSecretion and absorption — kidney tubules, gland ducts
Simple columnarOne layer of tall cellsAbsorption and secretion — lining of the stomach and intestines
Stratified squamousMany stacked flat layersProtection against abrasion — skin surface, mouth, esophagus, vagina
Pseudostratified columnarOne layer that looks layeredCiliated lining of the airways that sweeps mucus upward
TransitionalStretchy, shape-changing layersBladder and ureters — stretches as they fill

Two entries in that table are the famous exceptions. Pseudostratified columnar epithelium is really a single layer — every cell touches the basement membrane — but because the nuclei sit at different heights it looks stacked, hence "pseudo" (false) stratified. Transitional epithelium is the shape-shifter of the urinary tract: its cells slide over one another and flatten as the bladder fills, then bunch back up as it empties.

Simple = movement, stratified = protection. When you meet a new epithelium, read its name like a job description. If it is simple, something is meant to cross it (absorb, secrete, diffuse). If it is stratified, it is standing guard against wear — the extra layers are armor, refreshed from below as the top cells flake away.

Build an epithelium's name box by box and watch it appear in the interactive.

Interactive Pick layers and a cell shape to build an epithelium's name and see it drawn. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Epithelia are tightly packed, polar (apical vs. basal), sit on a basement membrane, are avascular, and regenerate fast.
  • Name any epithelium as layers (simple = one, stratified = many) + cell shape (squamous, cuboidal, columnar).
  • Simple epithelia favor absorption/secretion/diffusion; stratified epithelia favor protection; pseudostratified and transitional are the special cases.

Section 3Connective tissue

One recipe, endless variety

Connective tissue is the most abundant and the most diverse tissue in the body, and at first it can seem like a grab-bag: bone, fat, tendon, cartilage, and even blood all belong to it. What ties them together is a single shared blueprint. Unlike epithelium, where the cells are everything, connective tissue is mostly extracellular matrix — a non-living material the cells secrete and then sit inside. Its cells are relatively few and widely scattered; the character of the tissue comes almost entirely from the matrix around them. This is the key insight of the whole section: change the matrix and you change the tissue. Make the matrix soft and fatty and you get adipose (fat); make it a firm rubbery gel and you get cartilage; harden it with calcium salts and you get bone; make it liquid and you get blood.

The matrix: fibers and ground substance

The extracellular matrix always has two ingredients. First are the protein fibers, of which there are three kinds: strong, rope-like collagen fibers that resist pulling; stretchy elastic fibers that snap back into shape; and fine, mesh-like reticular fibers that form supporting scaffolds. Second is the ground substance, the material that fills the space between the fibers and cells — anywhere from watery to gel-like to rock-solid, depending on the tissue. The cells themselves usually come in two flavors named by a simple convention: a cell whose name ends in -blast is the young, active builder that secretes matrix (a fibroblast, osteoblast, or chondroblast), while one ending in -cyte is the mature cell that maintains it (a fibrocyte, osteocyte, or chondrocyte).

Connective tissueMatrixJob and location
Loose (areolar)Soft, gel-like, loosely woven fibersAll-purpose packing and cushioning; underlies epithelia everywhere
Adipose (fat)Cells so full of fat that matrix is minimalEnergy storage, insulation, padding around organs
Dense regularTightly packed parallel collagenStrong pull in one direction — tendons and ligaments
CartilageFirm, rubbery gelFlexible support — nose, ears, joint surfaces, discs
Bone (osseous)Collagen hardened with calcium saltsRigid support and protection; mineral storage
BloodLiquid matrix (plasma)Transport of cells, gases, nutrients, and wastes

Reading that table top to bottom, you can watch a single idea flex through every possible stiffness — from a soft cushion, to a padded fat pad, to a tough cable, to a rubbery shock absorber, to solid mineral, all the way to a flowing liquid. Same recipe, radically different results, tuned entirely by what the cells put into the matrix.

Cartilage heals slowly because it has no blood supply. Like epithelium, cartilage is avascular — nutrients must diffuse in through its gel matrix. That is why a torn knee cartilage can nag for years while a broken bone (which is richly vascular) knits in weeks. Blood supply, more than anything, decides how fast a tissue repairs.

Rebuild the matrix in the interactive and watch one tissue turn into the next.

Interactive Change the matrix and watch one connective tissue become another — fat, cartilage, bone, blood. Open full ↑
Key points
  • All connective tissue follows one recipe: scattered cells in an extracellular matrix of fibers + ground substance.
  • Three fiber types — collagen (strength), elastic (stretch), reticular (scaffold); -blast cells build the matrix and -cyte cells maintain it.
  • Changing the matrix yields the whole family: loose, adipose, dense, cartilage, bone, and blood.

Section 4Muscle tissue

Cells built to shorten

Muscle tissue has one talent that no other tissue has: it can contract, actively shortening to pull on something. Its cells are packed with two protein filaments — actin and myosin — that slide past one another to shorten the cell, and everything about a muscle cell's shape serves that pull. When these filaments line up in orderly, repeating rows, they catch the light in alternating bands and the tissue looks striated (striped); when they are arranged less regularly, the tissue looks smooth. Contraction not only moves the body but also generates much of your body heat — which is why shivering warms you up.

The three muscle tissues

There are exactly three muscle tissues, and you can tell them apart with three questions: Is it striped? Can you control it consciously? What shape are its cells? Skeletal muscle is the meat that moves your bones — it is striated, voluntary (under conscious control), and made of long, cylindrical, multinucleated fibers. Cardiac muscle is found only in the wall of the heart — it is striated but involuntary, and its short, branching cells are joined end-to-end by intercalated discs that let the whole heart contract as one coordinated unit. Smooth muscle lines the walls of hollow organs like the gut, blood vessels, and bladder — it is non-striated, involuntary, and built of small, tapered, spindle-shaped cells that squeeze those organs slowly and tirelessly.

FeatureSkeletalCardiacSmooth
StriationsStriatedStriatedNon-striated
ControlVoluntaryInvoluntaryInvoluntary
Cell shapeLong cylindersShort, branchedTapered spindles
Nuclei per cellManyOne (or two)One
LocationAttached to bonesHeart wall onlyWalls of hollow organs

A useful mental shortcut: the two striated muscles (skeletal and cardiac) are the fast, powerful ones, while the one smooth muscle is the slow, steady one. And only one of the three — skeletal — answers to your will. The heart and the gut run themselves.

Intercalated discs let the heart beat as one. These specialized junctions between cardiac cells carry the electrical signal instantly from cell to cell, so the heart contracts in a single coordinated wave rather than a jumble of twitches. It is a structural feature that directly enables the function — structure fitting function, once again.

Watch each of the three muscles contract in the interactive below.

Interactive Compare skeletal, cardiac, and smooth muscle — and watch each one contract. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Muscle tissue contracts using sliding actin and myosin filaments; orderly filaments make it look striated.
  • Skeletal = striated, voluntary, long multinucleated fibers; cardiac = striated, involuntary, branched with intercalated discs; smooth = non-striated, involuntary, spindle cells.
  • Tell them apart with three questions: striped? voluntary? cell shape?

Section 5Nervous tissue

The neuron: a cell shaped like a message

Nervous tissue senses changes, makes decisions, and sends electrical signals faster than any other tissue in the body. Its star cell is the neuron, and the neuron's strange shape is really a map of how a signal flows through it — in one end, out the other. Signals arrive at the bushy dendrites, short branching extensions that receive input from other cells. They funnel into the cell body (or soma), which holds the nucleus and does the cell's housekeeping. If the incoming signal is strong enough, the neuron fires an electrical pulse that races away down the axon, a single long fiber that carries the message toward the next cell. Many axons are wrapped in a fatty myelin sheath that acts like insulation on a wire, letting the signal jump along much faster. The traffic is strictly one-way: dendrite → cell body → axon, every time.

PartWhat it isIts job in the signal
DendritesShort, branching extensionsReceive incoming signals from other cells
Cell body (soma)Contains nucleus and organellesIntegrates the input; keeps the cell alive
AxonSingle long fiberSends the electrical pulse toward the next cell
Myelin sheathFatty insulating wrap on the axonSpeeds the signal along the axon
Axon terminalsBranched tips of the axonPass the signal to the next cell at a synapse

Neuroglia: the support crew

Neurons get the glory, but they are outnumbered several times over by neuroglia (or glial cells) — the support cells of nervous tissue. Glia do not carry the signal themselves; instead they nourish neurons, insulate their axons with myelin, hold the tissue together, and defend it against invaders. Two properties define what nervous tissue as a whole can do: excitability, the ability to respond to a stimulus by generating an electrical signal, and conductivity, the ability to carry that signal from one place to another. Together they let this tissue act as the body's high-speed communication network.

Most neurons cannot divide — so protect the ones you have. Unlike skin or gut lining, mature neurons in the brain and spinal cord largely lose the ability to replace themselves. That is why damage to the central nervous system — a stroke or a spinal cord injury — can be permanent, while a scraped knee heals in days. Nervous tissue repairs poorly precisely because its key cell will not divide.

Tap a part of the neuron and watch a signal fire in the interactive.

Interactive Tap each part of a neuron, then watch a signal travel in through the dendrites and out along the axon. Open full ↑
Key points
  • The neuron carries a one-way signal: dendrites receive → cell body integrates → axon transmits.
  • The fatty myelin sheath insulates the axon and speeds the signal; neuroglia support and protect neurons.
  • Nervous tissue is defined by excitability and conductivity; mature neurons rarely divide, so CNS damage often heals poorly.

Section 6Tissue repair

Two ways to fix a wound

When a tissue is injured, the body can mend it in one of two ways. The better outcome is regeneration — the damaged cells are replaced with new cells of the very same type, and the tissue is restored to normal, as if the injury never happened. The fallback is fibrosis, in which the gap is filled with dense fibrous scar tissue instead of the original cell type. Scar tissue plugs the hole and restores strength, but it does not do the original job — scar in the heart cannot beat, and scar in the skin has no hair or sweat glands. Which path a tissue takes depends largely on how well its cells can divide: fast-dividing tissues like skin and gut regenerate beautifully, while tissues whose cells barely divide — cardiac muscle, neurons — tend to scar.

The healing playbook

Whatever the tissue, the body runs the same four-step repair program in order. Think of it as a construction project on an emergency timeline: first stop the flooding, then clear the site, then rebuild the frame, then finish the surfaces.

StageWhat happensPurpose
1. HemostasisBlood clots seal the broken vessels; a scab formsStop the bleeding
2. InflammationVessels widen; immune cells arrive to clear debris and germsClean up the wound
3. ProliferationNew capillaries and collagen build pink granulation tissue; epithelium migrates acrossRebuild the tissue
4. RemodelingCollagen is reorganized and strengthened; the scar matures and shrinksRestore strength over weeks to months

The visible signs of the second stage — redness, heat, swelling, and pain — are the classic marks of inflammation, and although they feel like the problem, they are actually the body doing its job. The widened, leaky vessels that cause the redness and swelling are what deliver the clotting factors, infection-fighters, and clean-up crews the wound needs. Inflammation is not the injury; it is the repair beginning.

Not all cells are equally replaceable. Tissues are graded by dividing ability: labile cells (skin, gut lining) divide constantly and heal fast; stable cells (liver, kidney) divide only when called upon; permanent cells (neurons, cardiac muscle) barely divide at all and heal by scarring. This single trait predicts, better than almost anything, how well an organ will recover.

Step through a healing wound from clot to scar in the interactive below.

Interactive Step through a healing wound — stop the bleeding, clean up, rebuild, and remodel. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Healing happens by regeneration (same cells restored) or fibrosis (scar tissue fills the gap); which one depends on how well the cells divide.
  • The repair sequence is always hemostasis → inflammation → proliferation → remodeling.
  • Inflammation (redness, heat, swelling, pain) is the repair process at work, not the injury itself.

The chapter in one breath

  • A tissue is a group of similar cells sharing a job; the whole body is built from just four of them.
  • Epithelial tissue covers and lines; it is tightly packed, polar, avascular, and named by layers (simple/stratified) + cell shape (squamous/cuboidal/columnar).
  • Connective tissue binds and supports; its cells sit scattered in an extracellular matrix of fibers and ground substance — change the matrix and you get fat, cartilage, bone, or blood.
  • Muscle tissue contracts: skeletal (striated, voluntary), cardiac (striated, involuntary, intercalated discs), and smooth (non-striated, involuntary).
  • Nervous tissue communicates; the neuron carries a one-way signal from dendrites → cell body → axon, supported by neuroglia.
  • Injured tissue heals by regeneration or fibrosis, always through hemostasis → inflammation → proliferation → remodeling.
  • How well a tissue repairs comes down mostly to its blood supply and how readily its cells divide.

ReferenceWords worth knowing

A quick reference for the terms in this chapter — skim it now, or circle back whenever a word trips you up.

Apical surface
The free, exposed surface of an epithelial cell that faces the open space, cavity, or lumen; often bears microvilli or cilia.
Areolar tissue
The most common loose connective tissue — a soft, all-purpose packing material that underlies epithelia and cushions organs.
Axon
The single long fiber of a neuron that carries the electrical signal away from the cell body toward the next cell.
Basement membrane
A thin layer that anchors the basal surface of an epithelium to the connective tissue beneath it.
Cardiac muscle
Striated, involuntary muscle found only in the heart wall; its branched cells are joined by intercalated discs so the heart contracts as one.
Collagen fiber
The strong, rope-like protein fiber of connective tissue that resists pulling forces.
Connective tissue
The diverse tissue that binds, supports, protects, and transports; made of scattered cells in an extracellular matrix.
Dendrite
A short, branching extension of a neuron that receives incoming signals from other cells.
Epithelial tissue
Tightly packed tissue that covers body surfaces, lines cavities and tubes, and forms glands.
Extracellular matrix
The non-living material — fibers plus ground substance — that connective tissue cells secrete and sit within.
Fibrosis
Repair by the deposition of fibrous scar tissue in place of the original cell type; restores strength but not full function.
Ground substance
The material of the extracellular matrix that fills the space between cells and fibers; ranges from watery to gel-like to solid.
Histology
The microscopic study of tissues — anatomy at high magnification.
Muscle tissue
Tissue specialized to contract using actin and myosin filaments, generating movement, force, and heat.
Nervous tissue
Tissue specialized to sense, process, and rapidly transmit electrical signals; composed of neurons and neuroglia.
Neuroglia
The support cells of nervous tissue that nourish, insulate, hold together, and protect neurons.
Neuron
The signal-carrying cell of nervous tissue, with dendrites, a cell body, and an axon.
Regeneration
Repair in which damaged cells are replaced by new cells of the same type, restoring normal structure and function.
Simple epithelium
An epithelium one cell layer thick, suited to diffusion, absorption, and secretion.
Squamous
Describing flat, scale-like epithelial cells, ideal where substances must cross quickly.
Stratified epithelium
An epithelium of two or more stacked cell layers, built to protect against abrasion.
Tissue
A group of similar cells that work together to perform a shared function.

Check yourself

1. Name the four primary tissue types and give the single-word general job of each.
Epithelial (cover/line), connective (support), muscle (move), nervous (communicate). Every organ in the body is assembled from these four; knowing each one's headline job lets you predict what a tissue does before you learn the details.
2. On a slide, one tissue has cells packed tightly with almost no space between them; another has just a few cells scattered in a large amount of material. Which is epithelial and which is connective, and why?
The tightly packed tissue is epithelial; the scattered-cells-in-a-matrix tissue is connective. Epithelium must form a barrier, so its cells are crowded together; connective tissue's character comes from its extracellular matrix, so its cells are few and far apart.
3. Give the two-part rule for naming an epithelium, and name the tissue that is a single layer of flat cells.
Layers (simple = one, stratified = many) + cell shape (squamous, cuboidal, columnar); a single flat layer is simple squamous. Simple squamous lines the lung air sacs and capillaries, where its thinness lets gases and nutrients diffuse across almost instantly.
4. Why is the outer surface of the skin made of stratified squamous epithelium rather than simple squamous?
Because the skin needs protection against abrasion, and many stacked layers provide it. Stratified epithelia are armor — the top cells wear away and are constantly replaced from below — whereas a single simple layer would be far too fragile for a body surface.
5. State the shared blueprint of all connective tissue, then explain how the same recipe produces both bone and blood.
Scattered cells in an extracellular matrix of fibers plus ground substance; changing the matrix changes the tissue. Harden the matrix with calcium salts and you get rigid bone; make the matrix a liquid (plasma) and you get flowing blood — same plan, opposite consistency.
6. Name the three connective tissue fibers and what each one is best at.
Collagen (strength/resists pulling), elastic (stretch and recoil), reticular (fine supporting scaffold). A cell whose name ends in -blast builds this matrix, while a -cyte cell maintains it (fibroblast → fibrocyte, and so on).
7. A tissue is striated and under conscious control, with long cells that each contain many nuclei. Which muscle tissue is it, and where is it found?
Skeletal muscle, attached to bones. It is the only muscle that is voluntary; the three-question test — striped? voluntary? cell shape? — separates it from cardiac (striped, involuntary, branched) and smooth (non-striated, involuntary, spindle).
8. What are intercalated discs, and why does the heart depend on them?
Specialized junctions joining cardiac muscle cells end-to-end that pass the electrical signal instantly between cells. They let the heart contract as one coordinated wave rather than a jumble of independent twitches — structure directly enabling function.
9. Trace the path of a signal through a neuron, naming the three main parts in order.
Dendrites (receive) → cell body/soma (integrate) → axon (transmit). The flow is strictly one-way, and the neuron's shape is essentially a map of that path; a fatty myelin sheath around the axon speeds the signal along.
10. Why does a spinal cord injury often cause permanent loss, while a scraped knee heals in days?
Mature neurons largely cannot divide, so damaged nervous tissue cannot regenerate; skin cells divide rapidly and replace themselves quickly. A tissue's capacity to repair depends heavily on how readily its cells divide (and on its blood supply).
11. List the four stages of tissue repair in order, and state the goal of each.
Hemostasis (stop bleeding) → inflammation (clean up) → proliferation (rebuild) → remodeling (strengthen). The body runs the same construction sequence for any wound: seal the flooding, clear the site, rebuild the frame, then finish and strengthen over weeks to months.
12. A wound is red, warm, swollen, and painful. Name this process and explain why these signs are actually a good thing.
Inflammation — the second stage of repair. The widened, leaky blood vessels that cause the redness and swelling are exactly what deliver clotting factors, infection-fighting cells, and clean-up crews to the wound; the signs are the repair beginning, not the injury itself.
Anatomy & Physiology · Chapter 5

The Integumentary System

Spread flat, your skin would cover about two square meters and weigh more than any other organ you own — and yet we rarely think of it as an organ at all. This chapter changes that. You will build the skin from the top down, layer by layer, then follow a single cell on its month-long journey from a living factory floor to a dead flake on the surface. You will learn where skin color comes from, meet the hair, nails, and glands that grow out of the skin, and see the five quiet jobs your covering does every second. We finish where the clinic begins — with burns and skin cancer, the two ways this remarkable barrier fails.

By the end of this chapter you can

  • Name the two true layers of the skin — the epidermis and dermis — and the hypodermis beneath, and describe the tissue and job of each.
  • List the five epidermal strata in order and trace a keratinocyte's keratinization journey from birth to shedding.
  • Explain how melanocytes and melanin produce skin color and shield cell DNA from ultraviolet light.
  • Describe the structure of hair, nails, and the sebaceous and sweat glands, and state what each contributes.
  • Summarize the skin's five functions: protection, thermoregulation, sensation, vitamin D synthesis, and excretion.
  • Classify burns by depth, estimate burned area with the rule of nines, and distinguish the three skin cancers.
  • Apply the ABCDE warning signs to tell a harmless mole from a possible melanoma.

Section 1Layers of the skin

An organ you can see

The integumentary system is the skin plus everything that grows out of it — hair, nails, and glands. The word integument simply means "a covering," and that is the plainest way to think of the skin: it is the wrapper that separates the wet, carefully controlled world inside you from the dry, dangerous world outside. But calling it a wrapper undersells it. The skin is a true organ, built of every tissue type, and it is the largest organ in the body by both weight and surface area. Everything in this chapter hangs on one anatomical fact: the skin is made of two layers stacked on a third supporting layer that, strictly speaking, is not skin at all.

Two layers of skin, one layer beneath

Read the skin from the surface down and you pass through three named layers. The top two are the skin proper; the third anchors it to the body.

LayerTissueWhat lives there / what it does
EpidermisKeratinized stratified squamous epitheliumThe tough, waterproof outer sheet; no blood vessels — it is fed by diffusion from below
DermisDense irregular connective tissueThe living core — blood vessels, nerves, hair follicles, glands, and the collagen that gives skin its strength
Hypodermis (subcutaneous)Areolar and adipose tissueNot part of the skin; anchors skin to muscle and bone, insulates, and stores energy

The epidermis is pure epithelium and, importantly, is avascular — it contains no blood vessels of its own. Its living cells survive on oxygen and nutrients that seep up from the dermis below, which is why the outer epidermis, cut off from that supply, is entirely dead. The dermis is where the action is: a dense feltwork of collagen and elastic fibers packed with the blood vessels, sensory nerves, hair follicles, and glands that make skin do its jobs. It has two sublayers — a thin, bumpy papillary layer on top (its dermal papillae create your fingerprints) and a thick reticular layer below that carries most of the collagen. Beneath the dermis, the hypodermis (also called the subcutaneous layer or superficial fascia) is mostly fat; it pads and insulates the body and tethers the skin loosely to the structures underneath.

The epidermis eats by diffusion. Because it has no blood supply of its own, every nutrient the epidermis uses must travel up from dermal capillaries. That single fact explains why a superficial scrape doesn't bleed, why the surface cells are dead, and why the dermis — not the epidermis — bleeds and hurts when you're cut.

Thick skin and thin skin

Not all skin is the same thickness. Thick skin covers the palms of the hands and the soles of the feet — the surfaces that take the most abrasion — and it carries all five epidermal layers, including an extra clear one. Thin skin covers the rest of the body; it is more delicate, has only four epidermal layers, and is the only skin that grows hair. The names refer to the epidermis, not to total skin thickness, which is a common point of confusion.

Interactive Tap a layer to explore the tough epidermis, the living dermis, and the fatty hypodermis that anchors it all. Open full ↑
Key points
  • The integumentary system = skin + hair, nails, and glands; the skin is the body's largest organ.
  • The skin has two true layers: the epithelial epidermis (avascular) over the connective-tissue dermis (vascular, holds the structures).
  • The hypodermis beneath is fat and areolar tissue — not skin — and anchors and insulates.
  • Thick skin (palms, soles) has five epidermal strata; thin skin has four and bears hair.

Section 2The five epidermal strata

A conveyor belt of cells

The epidermis is a factory that never stops. Its main cell, the keratinocyte, is born at the very bottom, gets pushed steadily upward as new cells form beneath it, fills with a tough protein called keratin, dies along the way, and finally flakes off the top. The whole trip takes about four weeks — roughly 25 to 45 days — which means the outermost surface of you is completely replaced every month or so. This slow upward march is called keratinization, and it is the single most important process in the epidermis.

The five layers, bottom to top

In thick skin the keratinocyte passes through five named strata on its way up. Learn them in order from the deepest (where cells are born) to the surface (where they are shed):

Stratum (deep → superficial)NicknameWhat happens here
Stratum basaleThe nurseryA single row of dividing stem cells; new keratinocytes are born here by mitosis. Also home to melanocytes and touch-sensing Merkel cells.
Stratum spinosumThe spiny layerSeveral rows of keratinocytes held together by strong desmosomes (which give a spiny look). Immune dendritic cells patrol here.
Stratum granulosumThe dying layerCells fill with granules of keratin and a waterproofing lipid; nuclei break down and the cells begin to die.
Stratum lucidumThe clear layerA thin, translucent band of dead cells — found only in thick skin (palms and soles).
Stratum corneumThe horny layer15–30 layers of flat, dead, fully keratinized cells — the tough, waterproof barrier you actually touch.

A memory hook helps: reading from the base up, Basale, Spinosum, Granulosum, Lucidum, Corneum — "Bad Students Get Lousy Classes." The bottom two strata (basale and spinosum) are living and are sometimes grouped as the stratum germinativum, the growth zone. From the granulosum up, the cells are dying or dead. By the time a cell reaches the stratum corneum it is nothing but a flattened bag of keratin, glued to its neighbors and coated in lipid — and that dead, waterproof crust is exactly what makes the skin such an effective barrier. We shed roughly a million of these cells every day.

Dead cells do the hard work. It feels backward, but the layer that protects you best is the one made entirely of dead cells. The stratum corneum's keratin-packed corpses, sealed with lipid, are what keep water in and microbes out. Much of household dust is shed corneum.
Interactive New cells are born at the bottom, pushed up through five layers, fill with keratin and die, then flake off the top — watch a cell make the month-long journey. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Keratinocytes are born in the stratum basale, pushed upward, and shed from the surface in about a month — the process of keratinization.
  • Order deep to superficial: basale → spinosum → granulosum → lucidum → corneum ("Bad Students Get Lousy Classes").
  • The stratum lucidum appears only in thick skin; the stratum corneum is the dead, keratinized barrier you touch.

Section 3Skin color & melanin

Three pigments, one dominant player

The color of skin comes from three pigments blended together. Melanin is the main one — a brown-to-black (or red-to-yellow) pigment made in the epidermis. Carotene is a yellow-orange pigment that comes from the diet and collects in the stratum corneum and the fatty hypodermis. And hemoglobin, the red pigment of blood, shows through where the epidermis is thin, giving lighter skin its pink flush. Of the three, melanin is by far the most important, and it is the one that varies most from person to person.

Melanocytes hand off their pigment

Melanin is made by a specialized cell called the melanocyte, which sits down in the stratum basale. Here is the surprising part: melanocytes do not keep their pigment. They package melanin into little sacs called melanosomes and hand them off to the surrounding keratinocytes. Once inside a keratinocyte, the melanin migrates to the sunny side of the cell and forms a little cap over the top of the nucleus — like a tiny umbrella held over the cell's DNA. There it absorbs incoming ultraviolet (UV) radiation before it can reach and damage the genetic material. This is the whole point of skin pigment: melanin is sunscreen your cells make for themselves.

Everyone has about the same number of melanocytes. Differences in skin color come almost entirely from how much melanin those cells make and what kind — not from how many melanocytes you have. A tan is simply melanocytes ramping up production after UV exposure, a repair-and-defend response to DNA-damaging light.

When pigment goes wrong — and reading skin color in the clinic

Two inherited conditions show what happens at the extremes. In albinism, melanocytes are present but cannot make melanin, so the skin, hair, and eyes have little or no pigment and burn easily. In vitiligo, the immune system destroys melanocytes in patches, leaving pale blotches of skin. Beyond pigment disorders, skin color is one of the fastest bedside signs of illness, because it reflects what is happening in the blood underneath.

Color changeAppearanceWhat it usually means
CyanosisBluish skin, lips, and nail bedsPoorly oxygenated blood (low O₂)
PallorPale, blanched skinLow blood flow or anemia (shock, fear, cold)
ErythemaRednessIncreased blood flow — fever, inflammation, embarrassment, sunburn
JaundiceYellow skin and scleraeBilirubin buildup — usually a liver problem
BronzingGolden-bronze toneAdrenal insufficiency (Addison disease)
Interactive Melanocytes make melanin and hand it to nearby skin cells, where it caps each nucleus like a tiny umbrella — turn up the sun and watch it protect the DNA. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Skin color blends three pigments: melanin (main), carotene, and blood hemoglobin.
  • Melanocytes in the stratum basale make melanin and hand it to keratinocytes, where it caps the nucleus and absorbs UV to protect DNA.
  • Everyone has similar melanocyte numbers; color differences come from melanin amount and type. A tan is a UV defense response.
  • Skin color reads the blood: cyanosis, pallor, erythema, jaundice, bronzing each signal a specific problem.

Section 4Hair, nails & glands

Hair: keratin threads with a living root

Every accessory structure of the skin is born from the epidermis but tucked down into the dermis, where the blood supply is. Hair is a flexible strand of dead, hard keratin. The part you see above the skin is the shaft; the part below is the root, sheathed in a tube called the hair follicle. At the very bottom the follicle swells into a bulb, and inside it a knot of connective tissue called the papilla delivers the blood vessels that feed growth. Just above the papilla, the matrix is the only living, dividing part of a hair — which is why a haircut doesn't hurt but a plucked hair does. Attached to each follicle is a tiny arrector pili muscle; when it contracts (from cold or fear) it pulls the hair upright and dimples the skin into "goosebumps."

Nails: a window over the fingertip

A nail is a plate of hard keratin that protects the tip of each finger and toe and gives us a tool for scratching and grasping. The visible nail body sits on the nail bed; the whitish crescent at its base, the lunula, marks the underlying nail matrix, which is where the nail actually grows. The cuticle (eponychium) is the fold of skin that seals the base against dirt and microbes. Nails are a genuinely useful clinical window: they turn blue with poor oxygenation, become curved and clubbed in chronic heart or lung disease, and spoon inward in iron-deficiency anemia.

Glands: oil and sweat

The skin holds two great families of glands. Sebaceous (oil) glands usually empty into a hair follicle and secrete an oily mixture called sebum that lubricates the hair and skin and slows bacterial growth. When a follicle clogs with sebum and dead cells, the result is acne. Sweat (sudoriferous) glands come in two types worth separating carefully:

GlandWhere / what it secretesJob
Sebaceous (oil)Into hair follicles; oily sebumLubricates skin and hair; mildly antibacterial
Eccrine (merocrine) sweatAll over the body, onto the surface; watery sweatCools the body by evaporation — the main thermoregulation gland
Apocrine sweatArmpits and groin, into hair follicles; thicker, protein-rich sweatActivates at puberty; feeds skin bacteria that cause body odor
Ceruminous & mammaryEar canal / breastModified sweat glands: earwax and milk
Eccrine cools, apocrine smells. The sweat that cools you on a hot day is watery eccrine sweat, found nearly everywhere on the body. The sweat behind body odor is thicker apocrine sweat from the armpits and groin — and it doesn't smell until skin bacteria break it down.
Interactive The skin's built-in accessories — hair, nails, and glands, all born from the epidermis and tucked into the dermis. Tap each to see what it is and does. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Hair is dead keratin; it grows only from the living matrix at the base, fed by the papilla, and stands up when the arrector pili contracts.
  • Nails are keratin plates that grow from the nail matrix under the lunula; their color and shape reveal systemic disease.
  • Sebaceous glands make oily sebum; eccrine sweat glands cool the body, while apocrine glands (armpit, groin) cause body odor after bacteria act on their sweat.

Section 5What the skin does

One organ, five jobs

It is tempting to think the skin just covers you, but covering is only the first of five real jobs it performs every moment. Hold onto all five — they come up again and again in later chapters.

FunctionHow the skin does it
1. ProtectionA physical barrier (keratin, tight cells), a chemical one (the acidic "acid mantle" of sweat and sebum), and a biological one (immune dendritic cells that catch invaders)
2. ThermoregulationTo cool: sweat evaporates and dermal blood vessels dilate to dump heat. To warm: vessels constrict to keep heat in
3. SensationPacked with receptors for touch, pressure, vibration, temperature, and pain — the body's largest sense organ
4. Vitamin D synthesisUV light converts a cholesterol relative in the skin into vitamin D₃, needed to absorb dietary calcium
5. Excretion & storageSweat sheds small amounts of water, salts, and wastes; the dermis stores a blood reservoir (up to ~5% of blood volume)

The barrier you rebuild every month

Protection is the headline act. The dead, keratin-packed stratum corneum keeps water in (you would dehydrate in hours without it) and keeps microbes, chemicals, and UV out. Sweat and sebum coat the surface in a slightly acidic film — the acid mantle — that discourages bacteria and fungi, while dendritic immune cells woven through the epidermis grab any invader that slips past. Because the whole barrier is rebuilt roughly every month, minor damage heals almost invisibly.

Temperature, sensation, and a vitamin the skin makes

The skin is also your master thermostat. When you overheat, eccrine glands pour out sweat that carries heat away as it evaporates, and dermal blood vessels widen to radiate heat from the surface — which is why you flush pink when hot. When you are cold, those same vessels clamp down to trap warmth in the core. Meanwhile the dermis is crowded with sensory receptors that make the skin the largest sense organ you own, feeding the nervous system a constant stream of touch, pressure, temperature, and pain. And in a job no other organ shares, the skin manufactures vitamin D: ultraviolet light strikes a cholesterol-like molecule in the epidermis and starts its conversion into vitamin D₃, without which you cannot absorb the calcium your bones and nerves depend on.

Sunlight is a nutrient — in moderation. The same UV that damages DNA also kick-starts vitamin D synthesis. That is the tension at the heart of this whole chapter: enough sun to make vitamin D and lift mood, but not so much that you overwhelm melanin's protection and court skin cancer.
Interactive Your largest organ is a multitasker — beyond covering you, it does five big jobs. Tap each one. Open full ↑
Key points
  • The skin's five jobs: protection, thermoregulation, sensation, vitamin D synthesis, and excretion/storage.
  • Protection is physical (keratin), chemical (the acidic acid mantle), and biological (immune dendritic cells).
  • Cooling uses sweat + vessel dilation; warming uses vessel constriction. Vitamin D₃ is made in skin using UV and is needed to absorb calcium.

Section 6Burns & skin cancer

Burns: it's all about depth

A burn destroys skin with heat, electricity, chemicals, or radiation, and its severity is judged first by how deep it goes. Because the skin's barrier, water control, and temperature regulation all live in these layers, the deeper the burn, the more dangerous it becomes — not because it hurts more, but sometimes because it stops hurting at all.

Burn (depth)Layers destroyedLook & feel
First-degree (superficial)Epidermis onlyRed, dry, painful, no blisters — a typical sunburn; heals in days
Second-degree (partial thickness)Epidermis + part of dermisBlisters, red, very painful; heals but may scar
Third-degree (full thickness)Entire epidermis + dermisWhite, brown, or charred and leathery; often painless because nerves are destroyed; needs skin grafting

The two great dangers of a serious burn are fluid loss and infection: once the barrier is gone, water pours out and microbes pour in, and the body can no longer hold its temperature. To gauge how much skin is involved, clinicians use the rule of nines, which divides the adult body surface into regions worth about 9% each (or multiples of nine) — each arm 9%, each leg 18%, the front of the trunk 18%, and so on. A burn is generally considered critical when it covers more than 25% of the body, or involves the hands, face, or feet, or is third-degree over more than a few percent.

A painless burn is a worse burn. It sounds paradoxical, but a full-thickness (third-degree) burn is often numb — the sensory nerves in the dermis have been destroyed along with everything else. Never take absence of pain as a sign a burn is minor.

Skin cancer: three kinds, one that kills

Chronic UV exposure is the leading cause of skin cancer, which is the most common cancer of all. There are three main types, and telling them apart matters enormously because they range from a nuisance to a killer. Notice that each one arises from a cell you already met earlier in this chapter.

CancerCell of originBehavior
Basal cell carcinomaCells of the stratum basaleMost common, least dangerous; rarely spreads; usually cured by removal
Squamous cell carcinomaKeratinocytes of the stratum spinosumLess common; can spread if ignored, but usually curable if caught early
MelanomaMelanocytesLeast common but most deadly; spreads (metastasizes) early and fast — catching it early saves lives

The dangerous one is melanoma, a cancer of the pigment-making melanocytes. It often begins in or near a mole, and because it metastasizes early, spotting it early is everything. The ABCDE rule is the tool used to screen a suspicious mole: Asymmetry (one half unlike the other), Border (ragged or blurred edges), Color (uneven, several shades), Diameter (larger than about 6 mm, the size of a pencil eraser), and Evolving (any change in size, shape, or color over time). Any mole that checks these boxes deserves a professional look.

Interactive Explore how deep a burn goes and how much body it covers, plus the three skin cancers — including the deadly one. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Burns are graded by depth: first-degree (epidermis), second-degree (into dermis, blisters), third-degree (full thickness, often painless, needs grafts).
  • Serious burns kill through fluid loss and infection; the rule of nines estimates the percent of body surface burned.
  • Three skin cancers: basal cell (common, mild), squamous cell (can spread), and melanoma (rare but deadly). Screen moles with ABCDE.

The chapter in one breath

  • The integumentary system is the skin plus hair, nails, and glands — the body's largest organ.
  • Skin has two true layers: the avascular epithelial epidermis over the vascular connective-tissue dermis, resting on the fatty hypodermis.
  • Keratinocytes ride an upward conveyor through five strata — basale → spinosum → granulosum → lucidum → corneum — filling with keratin, dying, and shedding in about a month.
  • Melanocytes make melanin and pass it to keratinocytes, where it caps the nucleus and absorbs UV to protect DNA; color differences reflect melanin amount, not melanocyte number.
  • Hair and nails are keratin structures grown from living matrices; sebaceous glands make oil, eccrine glands cool, and apocrine glands cause body odor.
  • The skin performs five jobs: protection, thermoregulation, sensation, vitamin D synthesis, and excretion/storage.
  • Burns are graded by depth (first- to third-degree) and area (rule of nines); the three skin cancers are basal cell, squamous cell, and the deadly melanoma, screened with ABCDE.

ReferenceWords worth knowing

A quick reference for the terms in this chapter — skim it now, or circle back whenever a word trips you up.

Apocrine sweat gland
A sweat gland in the armpit and groin that empties into a hair follicle and secretes a thick, protein-rich sweat; active from puberty and responsible for body odor after bacteria break it down.
Arrector pili
A tiny smooth muscle attached to a hair follicle that pulls the hair upright and produces "goosebumps" when it contracts.
Basal cell carcinoma
The most common and least dangerous skin cancer, arising from cells of the stratum basale; rarely metastasizes.
Carotene
A yellow-orange pigment from the diet that accumulates in the stratum corneum and hypodermis and contributes to skin color.
Dermis
The deep, living layer of skin made of dense irregular connective tissue; holds blood vessels, nerves, hair follicles, and glands.
Eccrine (merocrine) sweat gland
The most numerous sweat gland, opening directly onto the skin surface all over the body; its watery secretion cools the body by evaporation.
Epidermis
The superficial layer of skin, a keratinized stratified squamous epithelium with no blood vessels of its own.
Hair follicle
The tube of epidermal and dermal tissue that surrounds the hair root and from which a hair grows.
Hypodermis (subcutaneous layer)
The fatty, areolar layer beneath the skin (not part of the skin proper) that anchors it, insulates, and stores energy.
Integument
A covering; the integumentary system is the skin together with hair, nails, and glands.
Keratin
A tough, water-resistant fibrous protein that fills epidermal cells, hair, and nails and gives them strength and waterproofing.
Keratinocyte
The main cell of the epidermis; it is born in the stratum basale, fills with keratin as it is pushed upward, dies, and is shed.
Melanin
The brown-to-black (or red-to-yellow) pigment that gives skin most of its color and absorbs ultraviolet light to protect DNA.
Melanocyte
The pigment-producing cell in the stratum basale that makes melanin and transfers it to nearby keratinocytes.
Melanoma
The least common but most dangerous skin cancer, arising from melanocytes; it metastasizes early and is screened with the ABCDE rule.
Rule of nines
A method for estimating the percent of total body surface area affected by a burn, dividing the adult body into regions of about 9% or multiples of nine.
Sebaceous gland
An oil gland, usually emptying into a hair follicle, that secretes sebum to lubricate skin and hair and inhibit bacteria.
Sebum
The oily secretion of sebaceous glands; when a follicle clogs with sebum and dead cells, acne results.
Squamous cell carcinoma
A skin cancer arising from keratinocytes of the stratum spinosum; can metastasize if not treated, but usually curable if caught early.
Stratum basale
The deepest epidermal layer, a single row of dividing stem cells that also contains melanocytes and Merkel cells.
Stratum corneum
The outermost epidermal layer, 15–30 rows of dead, keratin-filled cells forming the tough, waterproof barrier.
Vitamin D
A vitamin the skin begins to make when ultraviolet light strikes a cholesterol relative in the epidermis; needed to absorb dietary calcium.

Check yourself

1. A patient has a shallow scrape that does not bleed at all. Which skin layer was damaged, and why is the lack of bleeding expected?
Only the epidermis was scraped, and the epidermis is avascular (has no blood vessels). Blood vessels live in the dermis below, so a wound has to reach the dermis before it bleeds or hurts much. The epidermis survives on nutrients diffusing up from the dermis.
2. Which layer sits directly beneath the skin, what tissue is it made of, and why is it not counted as part of the skin?
The hypodermis (subcutaneous layer), made mostly of adipose and areolar tissue. It anchors the skin, insulates, and stores fat, but because it is not epithelium-plus-dermis it is considered support tissue beneath the skin rather than skin itself.
3. Name the five epidermal strata from deepest to most superficial, and state which one is found only in thick skin.
Stratum basale → spinosum → granulosum → lucidum → corneum; the stratum lucidum is found only in thick skin (palms and soles). Remember "Bad Students Get Lousy Classes." Cells are born in the basale and shed from the corneum.
4. In which stratum are keratinocytes born, roughly how long does the journey to the surface take, and what is the outermost layer made of?
They are born in the stratum basale; the trip takes about a month (~25–45 days); the stratum corneum is made of dead, keratin-filled cells. This upward, dying march is called keratinization, and the dead corneum is what makes the skin a waterproof barrier.
5. Two people have very different skin colors. Is this because one has far more melanocytes than the other? Explain.
No — everyone has roughly the same number of melanocytes. The difference is in how much melanin those cells produce and what type. More active pigment production means darker skin; a tan is the same cells ramping up melanin after UV exposure.
6. A melanocyte makes melanin but hands it off. Where does the pigment end up inside a keratinocyte, and what protective job does it do there?
The melanin collects as a cap over the top of the keratinocyte's nucleus, like a tiny umbrella. There it absorbs incoming ultraviolet radiation before it can damage the cell's DNA — melanin is essentially sunscreen the cell makes for itself.
7. Why does a haircut not hurt, but plucking a hair does? Point to the structure responsible for hair growth.
The visible shaft is dead keratin, so cutting it is painless; the living, growing part is the matrix at the base of the follicle, fed by the papilla. Plucking disturbs that living root and its nerve supply, which is why it hurts.
8. Distinguish eccrine from apocrine sweat glands: where is each found, and which one is responsible for cooling versus body odor?
Eccrine glands are all over the body and secrete watery sweat that cools by evaporation; apocrine glands are in the armpits and groin and secrete a thick sweat linked to body odor. Apocrine sweat itself is odorless until skin bacteria break it down.
9. List the five functions of the skin, and name the one that is a nutrient-related job no other organ shares.
Protection, thermoregulation, sensation, vitamin D synthesis, and excretion/storage; vitamin D synthesis is the unique nutritional job. UV light starts the conversion of a cholesterol relative into vitamin D₃, which the body needs to absorb calcium.
10. On a hot day the skin does two things to shed heat. Name both, and describe how the same blood vessels respond when you are cold.
To cool: eccrine glands produce sweat that evaporates, and dermal blood vessels dilate to radiate heat. When cold, those vessels constrict to keep heat in the core. This makes the skin the body's main temperature-regulating organ.
11. A burn is charred, leathery, and surprisingly painless. What degree of burn is this, which layers are destroyed, and why is it painless?
A third-degree (full-thickness) burn, destroying the entire epidermis and dermis; it is painless because the sensory nerves in the dermis have been destroyed. These burns usually require skin grafting, and absence of pain never means a burn is minor.
12. Name the three skin cancers from least to most dangerous and their cell of origin, and give the ABCDE warning signs for the deadliest one.
Basal cell carcinoma (stratum basale, mildest), squamous cell carcinoma (stratum spinosum keratinocytes), and melanoma (melanocytes, deadliest). Screen a mole for melanoma with ABCDE: Asymmetry, Border irregularity, Color variation, Diameter over ~6 mm, and Evolving/changing over time.
Anatomy & Physiology · Chapter 6

Bone Tissue & the Skeletal System

It is easy to picture the skeleton as a dry rack of scaffolding — the part of us that outlasts everything else. That picture is wrong in the most important way: bone is living tissue, laced with blood vessels and nerves, rebuilt every day of your life, and quietly running jobs that have nothing to do with holding you up. In this chapter you will meet the skeleton as an organ system — how it is built, what its cells do, how it keeps your blood calcium in a razor-thin range, and how it heals itself when it breaks. Learn bone as a tissue that is alive, and the rest of the skeletal course falls into place.

By the end of this chapter you can

  • List the major functions of the skeletal system and split the skeleton into its axial and appendicular divisions.
  • Classify bones by shape — long, short, flat, irregular, and sesamoid — and give an example of each.
  • Name the gross parts of a long bone and describe the difference between compact and spongy bone.
  • Identify the four bone cells and explain how remodeling balances bone deposition against resorption.
  • Describe the organic and inorganic components of the bone matrix and what each contributes.
  • Diagram how parathyroid hormone and calcitonin hold blood calcium near its set point.
  • Recognize common fracture patterns and place the four stages of bone repair in order.

Section 1What the skeleton is for

A frame that is also a factory

Every bone is an organ — it contains several tissue types (bone tissue, cartilage, dense connective tissue, blood, nervous tissue) working together, and it is very much alive. Snap a fresh bone and it bleeds; leave a broken one alone and it heals. The whole set of bones, cartilages, and joints makes up the skeletal system, and that system does far more than give you a shape. It works as a support beam, a suit of armor, a system of levers, a mineral bank, and a blood-cell factory — often all at the same moment.

The jobs bones do

It is worth memorizing the skeleton's core functions as a short list, because almost everything else in the chapter is one of these jobs seen up close. Bone stores fat and minerals, builds blood, moves you, protects you, and holds you upright.

FunctionHow the skeleton delivers it
SupportA rigid framework that holds the body up and gives soft organs and muscles a place to attach
ProtectionHard cases shield vital organs — the skull guards the brain, the vertebrae the spinal cord, the rib cage the heart and lungs
MovementBones act as levers that muscles pull on; where two bones meet at a joint, motion happens
Mineral & fat storageBone is a reservoir for calcium and phosphate, released or stored on demand; yellow marrow stores energy as fat
Blood-cell formationHematopoiesis — red marrow inside certain bones makes red cells, white cells, and platelets

Axial and appendicular: two halves of one set

An adult skeleton has 206 bones, and they split into two named divisions. The axial skeleton (about 80 bones) runs down the body's central axis — the skull, the vertebral column, and the thoracic cage — and its main job is protection and support. The appendicular skeleton (about 126 bones) is everything that hangs off that axis: the arms and legs plus the two girdles (pectoral and pelvic) that anchor the limbs to the trunk. Its main job is movement. In the reference figure, the axial bones are tinted one color and the appendicular bones another — a useful way to keep the two straight.

Bone is the body's calcium bank. Roughly 99% of the body's calcium is deposited in the skeleton. That single fact drives one of the chapter's biggest ideas: when blood calcium runs low, the body can withdraw it from bone — even at the skeleton's expense — because a steady blood level matters more moment to moment than any single bone.

Tap each of the skeleton's jobs in the interactive to see what carries it out.

Interactive Bones aren't a dead frame — they're living organs doing five jobs at once, from holding you up to making your blood. Open full ↑
Key points
  • A bone is a living organ; the skeletal system is bones, cartilages, and joints together.
  • Core jobs: support, protection, movement, mineral/fat storage, and blood-cell formation (hematopoiesis).
  • The axial skeleton (~80 bones) protects and supports; the appendicular skeleton (~126 bones) enables movement; 206 bones total in an adult.
  • About 99% of the body's calcium is stored in bone.

Section 2Classifying bones by shape

Shape follows the job

Bones come in a handful of recognizable shapes, and — just as with the rest of the body — the shape is a clue to the function. A bone built for leverage looks nothing like a bone built to shield a soft organ. Sorting the skeleton into five shape classes gives you a quick vocabulary for describing any bone you meet.

The five shapes

ClassShape & roleExamples
Long boneLonger than wide, with a shaft and two ends; act as levers for movementFemur, humerus, phalanges (fingers & toes)
Short boneRoughly cube-shaped; provide stability with limited motionCarpals (wrist), tarsals (ankle)
Flat boneThin, often curved plates; protect organs and give broad muscle attachmentSkull bones, sternum, ribs, scapula
Irregular boneComplex shapes that fit no other class; varied protective and support rolesVertebrae, hip bones, many facial bones
Sesamoid boneSmall bones embedded inside a tendon; protect the tendon and improve its pullPatella (kneecap)

Notice that the phalanges of your fingers count as long bones even though they are tiny — the label is about proportion (longer than wide, with a shaft) and about function (leverage), not about absolute size. The reference figure also shows a sutural (Wormian) bone, a small extra bone that can appear within the seams of the skull; think of it as a special case of a flat bone rather than a sixth class.

The patella is the body's largest sesamoid. Because a sesamoid bone sits inside a tendon, it lifts that tendon away from the joint and increases the muscle's leverage — which is exactly why the kneecap makes the quadriceps a more powerful knee extender. Small sesamoids also hide in the tendons of the hands and feet.

Tap each shape in the interactive to see the bone and where it lives in the body.

Interactive Bones come in five shapes, and the shape follows the job — tap each to see it and where you'll find it. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Five shape classes: long, short, flat, irregular, sesamoid.
  • Long bones are levers; short bones give stable support; flat bones protect; irregular bones are specialized; sesamoids sit inside tendons.
  • The patella is the largest sesamoid; the phalanges are small long bones.

Section 3Inside a long bone

The shaft and the ends

A long bone such as the femur has a clean architecture once you learn its parts. The long central tube is the diaphysis, or shaft. Its wall is a cylinder of dense compact bone, and its hollow center — the medullary cavity — is filled in adults with fatty yellow marrow. Each rounded end is an epiphysis, filled with lightweight spongy (trabecular) bone whose spaces hold red marrow, the site of blood-cell formation. Where the joint surface meets another bone, the epiphysis is capped with slick articular cartilage that cushions the joint and lets it glide. Between the shaft and each end lies the metaphysis, which in a growing child contains the epiphyseal (growth) plate — a disc of cartilage where the bone lengthens. When growth finishes, that plate ossifies into a thin epiphyseal line.

The living wrappers

Two thin membranes keep bone alive and connected. The periosteum is a tough sheath covering the outer surface everywhere except the joint caps; it carries the blood vessels and nerves, anchors tendons and ligaments, and holds bone-forming cells for growth and repair. Lining the inner surfaces — the medullary cavity and the spaces of spongy bone — is the delicate endosteum, also stocked with bone cells. A nutrient artery pierces the shaft through a small opening to supply the bone from within.

Zoom in: the osteon

Under the microscope, compact bone is not a solid block but a bundle of tiny weight-bearing pillars called osteons (Haversian systems). Each osteon is a set of concentric rings of bone matrix, the lamellae, wrapped around a central canal that carries a blood vessel and nerve. Trapped in small pockets called lacunae between the rings are the mature bone cells, the osteocytes, and they stay connected to one another and to the central vessel through hair-thin channels called canaliculi. This canal-and-channel plumbing is how a hard, mineralized tissue keeps its buried cells fed and alive.

PartWhat it is / holds
DiaphysisThe shaft; a tube of compact bone around the medullary cavity
EpiphysisAn expanded end filled with spongy bone and red marrow
MetaphysisThe region between shaft and end; site of the growth plate/line
Medullary cavityThe hollow center of the shaft; holds yellow (fatty) marrow in adults
Articular cartilageSmooth cartilage capping the joint surface of each epiphysis
PeriosteumOuter membrane; vessels, nerves, attachment, bone-forming cells
EndosteumInner lining of the marrow cavity and spongy spaces
The growth plate is a child's weak point. Because the epiphyseal plate is cartilage, it is softer than the bone around it — so in children a hard blow can crack straight through it. These growth-plate (Salter–Harris) fractures need careful attention, since damage there can disturb how the limb grows. Once the plate seals into the epiphyseal line in adulthood, that vulnerability disappears.

Tap each labeled part in the interactive to find it on the bone and learn its role.

Interactive A long bone is more organized than it looks — a hollow shaft, spongy ends, and living wrappers. Tap each part to find it and learn its role. Open full ↑
Key points
  • The diaphysis (shaft) is compact bone around a marrow-filled medullary cavity; the epiphyses (ends) are spongy bone with red marrow.
  • The metaphysis holds the epiphyseal plate (growth) that becomes the epiphyseal line when growth stops.
  • The periosteum wraps the outside; the endosteum lines the inside.
  • Compact bone is built of osteons — lamellae around a central canal, with osteocytes in lacunae linked by canaliculi.

Section 4Bone cells & remodeling

What bone is made of

Bone tissue is a masterpiece of engineering because it combines two opposite qualities. Its organic part — mostly collagen fibers — gives bone flexibility and tensile strength, so it bends a little under load instead of snapping. Its inorganic part — mineral salts of calcium and phosphate that crystallize as hydroxyapatite — gives bone its hardness and compressive strength. Take the mineral away and bone becomes rubbery; take the collagen away and it becomes brittle as chalk. Healthy bone needs both, in balance.

Four cells, one crew

Four cell types build and maintain that matrix, and the easiest way to remember them is by their jobs. Stem-like osteogenic (osteoprogenitor) cells divide to produce the builders. Osteoblasts lay down new bone matrix — they build. Once an osteoblast walls itself into the matrix it has made, it matures into an osteocyte, the caretaker cell that lives in a lacuna and keeps the tissue healthy. And the demolition crew are the osteoclasts, large multinucleated cells that dissolve old bone and release its minerals back into the blood.

CellJobMemory hook
Osteogenic cellStem cell that divides into osteoblastsThe source
OsteoblastBuilds new bone matrix“b” for build
OsteocyteMature cell; maintains bone, senses stressRetired builder, walled in
OsteoclastBreaks down bone; frees calcium“c” for consume/carve

The tug-of-war called remodeling

Bone is never finished. Throughout life, osteoclasts dissolve old bone while osteoblasts lay down fresh bone right behind them — a continuous cycle called remodeling that replaces roughly 10% of the skeleton every year. When the two crews stay balanced, bone mass holds steady. Two things tune the balance. First, mechanical stress: bone thickens where it is loaded and thins where it is not — the principle known as Wolff's law, which is why weight-bearing exercise strengthens the skeleton and why bones weaken during bed rest or spaceflight. Second, hormones, especially the calcium-regulating pair you will meet next. When resorption outpaces formation for years — as often happens after menopause — the result is osteoporosis, porous bone that fractures easily.
