Blasts build, clasts consume. The whole system is a balance between the two: osteoblasts build bone up, osteoclasts chew it down. Keep them even and the skeleton stays strong; tip the balance toward the clasts and bone thins toward osteoporosis.

Watch the crews trade places in the interactive, and meet the four cells one at a time.

Interactive One crew lays down new bone while another dissolves old bone — the constant tug-of-war is remodeling. Watch it happen, and meet the four cells. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Bone matrix = collagen (flexibility) + hydroxyapatite mineral (hardness); it needs both.
  • Four cells: osteogenic (source), osteoblast (build), osteocyte (maintain), osteoclast (break down).
  • Remodeling balances resorption and deposition, replacing ~10% of bone yearly; it responds to load (Wolff's law) and hormones.
  • Chronic imbalance toward resorption gives osteoporosis.

Section 5Calcium homeostasis

Why calcium is worth guarding

Blood calcium (Ca²⁺) must stay in a tight window, roughly 9–10 mg/dL, because so much depends on it: calcium triggers muscle contraction, lets nerves fire, is required for blood clotting, and acts as a messenger inside cells. Drift too low and nerves become jumpy, causing muscle spasms and cramps (tetany); drift too high and nerves and muscles go sluggish. This is a textbook example of the homeostasis idea from Chapter 1 — a value held near a set point by feedback — and the skeleton is the reservoir the body draws on to defend it.

PTH raises calcium

When blood calcium falls, the parathyroid glands (four small glands on the back of the thyroid) release parathyroid hormone (PTH). PTH pushes calcium up through three routes at once. In bone, it stimulates osteoclasts to dissolve matrix and release calcium into the blood. In the kidneys, it tells the tubules to reclaim calcium that would otherwise be lost in urine, and it activates vitamin D into calcitriol. And through calcitriol, it drives the intestines to absorb more calcium from food. Bone, kidney, and gut all pour calcium into the blood, and the level climbs back toward normal.

Calcitonin lowers calcium

When blood calcium runs high, the parafollicular (C) cells of the thyroid gland release calcitonin, which does the opposite of PTH. Calcitonin inhibits osteoclasts and favors osteoblasts, so calcium is pulled out of the blood and deposited back into bone, and the level settles down. The two hormones form a push–pull pair — PTH the accelerator, calcitonin the brake — that keeps blood calcium remarkably steady.

FeatureParathyroid hormone (PTH)Calcitonin
Released whenBlood calcium is lowBlood calcium is high
SourceParathyroid glandsThyroid C (parafollicular) cells
Effect on calciumRaises itLowers it
BoneStimulates osteoclasts (breakdown)Inhibits osteoclasts (favors buildup)
Kidney / gutReabsorbs Ca; activates vitamin D → more gut absorptionMinor role
The body defends blood calcium before the skeleton. If the diet is short on calcium, PTH will keep pulling it out of bone to protect the blood level — because a normal blood calcium is needed for the heart to beat and nerves to fire right now. Over years, that steady withdrawal is one road to weak, brittle bone, which is why dietary calcium and vitamin D matter so much.

Knock blood calcium off its set point in the interactive and watch the right hormone respond.

Interactive Blood calcium must stay near 9–10 mg/dL. Two hormones guard it: PTH raises it, calcitonin lowers it — knock it off and watch the right one respond. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Blood calcium is held near 9–10 mg/dL because it runs nerves, muscle, and clotting.
  • PTH (from the parathyroids) raises calcium via bone, kidney, and gut (through calcitriol/vitamin D).
  • Calcitonin (from thyroid C cells) lowers calcium by inhibiting osteoclasts.
  • The body will withdraw calcium from bone to protect the blood level.

Section 6Fractures & repair

How bones break

A fracture is simply a broken bone, but clinicians name the pattern of the break because the pattern hints at what caused it and how it should be treated. The first and most important distinction is whether the skin stayed intact. A closed (simple) fracture leaves the skin unbroken; an open (compound) fracture breaks through the skin, which is far more serious because it opens a door for infection. Beyond that, breaks are named for their line and severity.

TypeWhat it looks like
Closed vs openSkin intact (closed) vs bone piercing the skin (open) — open carries infection risk
TransverseBreak runs straight across the shaft
ObliqueBreak runs at an angle across the bone
SpiralTwisting break that winds around the shaft; suggests a rotational force
ComminutedBone shatters into three or more fragments
ImpactedOne broken end is driven into the other
GreenstickIncomplete break; bone bends and cracks on one side (mostly in children)

How a bone heals: four stages

A broken bone repairs itself in a predictable sequence that can take months to finish. First, torn vessels bleed and clot, forming a fracture hematoma that seals the gap and sets the stage. Next, cells build a soft fibrocartilaginous (soft) callus of collagen and cartilage that splints the ends together. Osteoblasts then replace that soft bridge with a bony (hard) callus of spongy bone, roughly six weeks after the injury. Finally, over months, the bone remodels — osteoclasts and osteoblasts reshape the callus back toward the bone's original form, guided by the same stresses that shape every bone.

StageWhat happens
1. Fracture hematomaBleeding forms a clot that fills and seals the break
2. Soft (fibrocartilaginous) callusCollagen and cartilage bridge the gap, splinting the ends
3. Hard (bony) callusOsteoblasts replace the soft callus with spongy bone (~6 weeks)
4. Bone remodelingThe callus is reshaped over months toward the original contour

To help this happen, a displaced fracture is first reduced — the ends are lined back up, either by manipulation from outside (closed reduction) or by surgery (open reduction) — and then immobilized in a cast or with hardware so the delicate callus is not disturbed while it matures.

Kids bend before they break. A child's bones contain more collagen and less mineral than an adult's, so they are springier — which is why children get greenstick fractures, where the bone bows and cracks partway through like a fresh twig instead of snapping clean. The same flexibility helps children heal faster than adults.

Explore each fracture pattern in the interactive, then step through the four stages of repair.

Interactive Bones break in characteristic patterns — and then heal themselves in four stages. Explore the types, then step through how a bone repairs. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Closed = skin intact; open (compound) = bone breaks skin and risks infection.
  • Patterns include transverse, oblique, spiral, comminuted, impacted, greenstick.
  • Healing runs in four stages: hematoma → soft callus → hard callus → remodeling.
  • Treatment is reduction (realigning) then immobilization to protect the callus.

The chapter in one breath

  • Bone is living tissue, and the skeletal system does five jobs: support, protection, movement, mineral/fat storage, and blood-cell formation.
  • The skeleton splits into the axial (~80 bones) and appendicular (~126 bones) divisions, 206 in all.
  • Bones are classed by shape as long, short, flat, irregular, or sesamoid, and the shape follows the function.
  • A long bone has a diaphysis of compact bone around a marrow cavity and spongy epiphyses; compact bone is built of osteons.
  • Four cells — osteogenic, osteoblast, osteocyte, osteoclast — drive remodeling, the lifelong balance of building and dissolving bone.
  • The matrix combines collagen (flexibility) with hydroxyapatite mineral (hardness).
  • PTH raises and calcitonin lowers blood calcium, keeping it near 9–10 mg/dL — the skeleton is the calcium bank.
  • Fractures come in named patterns and heal in four stages: hematoma → soft callus → hard callus → remodeling.

ReferenceWords worth knowing

A quick reference for the terms in this chapter — skim it now, or circle back whenever a word trips you up.

Appendicular skeleton
The bones of the limbs plus the pectoral and pelvic girdles that attach them to the axis (~126 bones); specialized for movement.
Axial skeleton
The bones along the body's central axis — skull, vertebral column, and thoracic cage (~80 bones); specialized for support and protection.
Calcitonin
A hormone from the thyroid's parafollicular (C) cells that lowers blood calcium by inhibiting osteoclasts.
Calcitriol
The active form of vitamin D; increases calcium absorption from the intestine and is activated with the help of PTH.
Callus
The repair bridge that forms across a fracture — first a soft (fibrocartilaginous) callus, later a hard (bony) callus.
Compact bone
Dense, strong bone tissue built of osteons; forms the outer wall of bones and the shaft of long bones.
Diaphysis
The tubular shaft of a long bone; compact bone surrounding the medullary cavity.
Endosteum
The thin membrane lining the internal surfaces of bone — the medullary cavity and spongy-bone spaces.
Epiphyseal plate
The cartilage growth plate in the metaphysis where a long bone lengthens; becomes the epiphyseal line when growth ends.
Epiphysis
The expanded end of a long bone, made of spongy bone with red marrow and capped by articular cartilage.
Hematopoiesis
The formation of blood cells (red cells, white cells, platelets) in the red marrow of bone.
Hydroxyapatite
The crystalline calcium-phosphate mineral of bone matrix that gives bone its hardness.
Medullary cavity
The hollow center of a long bone's shaft; stores fatty yellow marrow in adults.
Osteoblast
The bone-building cell that secretes new matrix; matures into an osteocyte once surrounded by bone.
Osteoclast
The large, multinucleated cell that dissolves bone matrix and releases its minerals into the blood.
Osteocyte
A mature bone cell housed in a lacuna that maintains the matrix and senses mechanical stress.
Osteon
The structural unit of compact bone — concentric lamellae of matrix around a central (Haversian) canal.
Parathyroid hormone (PTH)
The hormone from the parathyroid glands that raises blood calcium via bone, kidney, and intestine.
Periosteum
The tough outer membrane of a bone; carries vessels and nerves, anchors tendons, and holds bone-forming cells.
Remodeling
The lifelong cycle of bone resorption by osteoclasts balanced against deposition by osteoblasts.
Sesamoid bone
A small bone embedded within a tendon (such as the patella) that protects the tendon and improves its leverage.
Spongy bone
Lightweight, porous bone tissue of struts (trabeculae) found in the epiphyses; its spaces hold red marrow.

Check yourself

1. A friend says the skeleton is just “dead scaffolding.” Give two pieces of evidence that bone is living tissue.
Bone is richly supplied with blood vessels and nerves, it is continually remodeled by living cells, and it heals itself after a fracture. Any of these shows that bone is a living organ, not inert scaffolding — a broken bone even bleeds and repairs.
2. List the major functions of the skeletal system, and which one involves red bone marrow.
Support, protection, movement, mineral and fat storage, and blood-cell formation; the last one — hematopoiesis — occurs in red marrow. Red marrow in spongy bone makes red cells, white cells, and platelets.
3. Sort these into axial or appendicular skeleton: femur, sternum, a vertebra, the scapula, the skull.
Axial: sternum, vertebra, skull. Appendicular: femur, scapula. The axial skeleton is the central axis (skull, vertebral column, thoracic cage); the appendicular is the limbs and their girdles — the scapula is part of the pectoral girdle.
4. Classify each bone by shape: the femur, a carpal, a rib, a vertebra, the patella.
Femur = long; carpal = short; rib = flat; vertebra = irregular; patella = sesamoid. Shape follows function — long bones lever, short bones stabilize, flat bones protect, irregular bones are specialized, and sesamoids sit inside tendons.
5. Name the shaft of a long bone, the tissue that fills its hollow center in an adult, and the tissue found in the expanded ends.
The shaft is the diaphysis; the medullary cavity holds yellow (fatty) marrow; the epiphyses contain spongy bone with red marrow. Compact bone forms the shaft wall; spongy bone fills the ends.
6. What is the difference between the epiphyseal plate and the epiphyseal line, and why does it matter clinically in children?
The epiphyseal plate is the active cartilage growth plate in a growing bone; the epiphyseal line is the bony remnant left after growth stops. Because the plate is softer cartilage, a fracture through it in a child can disturb future limb growth.
7. Match the cell to its job: osteoblast, osteoclast, osteocyte. Which one raises blood calcium when it is active?
Osteoblast builds new bone; osteocyte maintains bone; osteoclast breaks down bone — and the osteoclast raises blood calcium by dissolving matrix. Remember: blasts build, clasts consume.
8. Bone matrix has an organic and an inorganic part. Name each and the property it provides.
Organic collagen gives flexibility and tensile strength; inorganic hydroxyapatite (calcium phosphate) gives hardness and compressive strength. Bone needs both — without mineral it is rubbery, without collagen it is brittle.
9. What is bone remodeling, and how does Wolff's law explain why weight-bearing exercise strengthens bone?
Remodeling is the ongoing balance of resorption by osteoclasts and deposition by osteoblasts; Wolff's law says bone remodels in response to the stress placed on it. Loading the skeleton signals osteoblasts to add bone where it is needed, so weight-bearing exercise builds density while disuse thins it.
10. Blood calcium drops below normal. Which hormone responds, where is it made, and what are its three targets?
Parathyroid hormone (PTH), made by the parathyroid glands, acts on bone (stimulates osteoclasts), the kidneys (reabsorb calcium and activate vitamin D), and the intestine (absorb more calcium via calcitriol). All three raise blood calcium back toward its 9–10 mg/dL set point.
11. Contrast an open fracture with a closed fracture, and explain why one is more dangerous.
In a closed (simple) fracture the skin stays intact; in an open (compound) fracture the broken bone pierces the skin. The open fracture is more dangerous because the wound exposes bone to the outside and carries a high risk of infection.
12. Put the four stages of bone repair in order, starting right after the break.
Fracture hematoma → soft (fibrocartilaginous) callus → hard (bony) callus → bone remodeling. A clot fills the gap, cartilage and collagen splint it, spongy bone replaces that bridge, and finally the callus is reshaped toward the original bone.
Anatomy & Physiology · Chapter 7

The Axial Skeleton

Down the center of your body runs a bony core that holds you upright, guards your most delicate organs, and gives every muscle something to pull against. This is the axial skeleton — the skull, the spine, and the rib cage — eighty bones arranged along the body's long axis. In this chapter we will take that core apart piece by piece: we will name the bones of the skull, stack the twenty-six vertebrae of the backbone, dissect a single vertebra to learn the plan they all share, build the cage that shields the heart and lungs, and trace the graceful S-curve that lets the spine carry your weight without buckling. By the end, an X-ray of the chest or a diagram of the skull will read like a map you already know.

By the end of this chapter you can

  • Divide the skeleton into its axial and appendicular parts and list what belongs to each.
  • Name the eight cranial and fourteen facial bones and identify the skull's major sutures.
  • List the five regions of the vertebral column with the number of bones in each.
  • Label the parts of a typical vertebra and explain what the body, arch, and processes each do.
  • Classify the ribs as true, false, or floating and name the three parts of the sternum.
  • Distinguish primary from secondary spinal curves and recognize kyphosis, lordosis, and scoliosis.
  • Connect each axial structure to the organ or function it protects or supports.

Section 1Axial vs appendicular

Two skeletons in one

The adult body has 206 bones, and anatomists sort every one of them into two families. The axial skeleton forms the central axis of the body — the vertical line running from the top of the skull, down the spine, out through the ribs. It has 80 bones, and its job is protection and support: it houses the brain, the spinal cord, the heart, and the lungs, and it gives the trunk its rigid core. The appendicular skeleton is everything hung off that axis — the appendages. It has 126 bones: the arms and legs plus the two girdles (shoulder and hip) that anchor them. Its job is movement. A quick way to keep them straight: if a bone sits on the midline or cradles an organ, it is axial; if it lets you reach, walk, or grip, it is appendicular.

What the axial 80 are made of

The eighty axial bones fall into just three groups, and the whole of this chapter is a tour of them. The skull and its associated bones account for twenty-nine, the vertebral column for twenty-six, and the thoracic cage for twenty-five. Notice how neatly these track the organs they defend: the skull around the brain, the vertebrae around the spinal cord, the rib cage around the heart and lungs. The axial skeleton is, in a real sense, the body's suit of armor.

RegionBones includedCount
Skull8 cranial + 14 facial bones22
Associated skull bones6 auditory ossicles + 1 hyoid bone7
Vertebral column24 vertebrae + sacrum + coccyx26
Thoracic cage24 ribs (12 pairs) + 1 sternum25
Axial total—80
80 + 126 = 206. If you ever blank on how the skeleton divides, anchor to the total. The axial 80 protect and support along the midline; the appendicular 126 move. Their sum is the classic adult bone count — a number worth committing to memory.

Toggle between the two skeletons in the interactive below and watch the axial and appendicular bones light up in turn.

Interactive Toggle to light up the axial skeleton along the midline versus the appendicular limbs and girdles. Open full ↑
Key points
  • The skeleton splits into the axial (80 bones, central axis, protection/support) and appendicular (126 bones, limbs and girdles, movement).
  • The axial skeleton has three parts: skull, vertebral column, and thoracic cage.
  • Axial bones sit on the midline and guard the brain, spinal cord, heart, and lungs; 80 + 126 = 206.

Section 2The skull

Eight bones cradle the brain

The skull is 22 bones, and the first split is between the part that surrounds the brain and the part that builds the face. The cranium (the braincase) is made of eight cranial bones. Four are single midline bones: the frontal (forehead), the occipital (back and base, pierced by the large foramen magnum where the spinal cord exits), the sphenoid (a bat-shaped keystone bone spanning the floor of the cranium), and the ethmoid (a delicate bone between the eyes forming part of the nasal cavity). The other two come as pairs: the two parietal bones roofing the top and sides, and the two temporal bones housing the ears low on each side. Together they form a rigid vault whose only purpose is to keep the brain safe.

Fourteen bones build the face

The 14 facial bones give the face its shape and frame the openings for sight, smell, and eating. Most are paired: the two maxillae (the upper jaw, fused at the midline), the two zygomatic bones (the cheekbones), the two nasal bones (the bridge of the nose), the two lacrimal bones (tiny bones at the inner eye that channel tears), the two palatine bones (the back of the hard palate), and the two inferior nasal conchae (curled shelves inside the nose). Two are single: the vomer (the lower part of the nasal septum) and the mandible (the lower jaw). The mandible is special — it is the only freely movable bone in the skull, meeting the temporal bone at the temporomandibular joint so you can chew and speak.

GroupSingle bonesPaired bones
Cranial (8)Frontal, occipital, sphenoid, ethmoidParietal (2), temporal (2)
Facial (14)Mandible, vomerMaxilla (2), zygomatic (2), nasal (2), lacrimal (2), palatine (2), inferior nasal concha (2)

Sutures, sinuses, and the infant skull

The cranial bones do not meet in smooth joints; they interlock along jagged, immovable seams called sutures. Four are worth naming: the coronal suture between the frontal and parietal bones, the sagittal suture running front-to-back between the two parietals, the lambdoid suture between the parietals and the occipital, and the squamous suture along the top of each temporal bone. In a newborn these seams have not yet closed; the gaps between them are the soft fontanelles ("soft spots") that let the skull flex during birth and expand as the brain grows. Several cranial and facial bones are also hollowed out by air-filled paranasal sinuses, which lighten the skull and add resonance to the voice.

The foramen magnum is the skull's front door. This large hole in the occipital bone is where the brainstem becomes the spinal cord. Its position at the very base of the skull — rather than at the rear — is a signature of upright, two-legged posture, balancing the head directly on top of the spine.

Tap any bone on the side view in the interactive to find and name it.

Interactive Tap a bone on this side view to locate it among the 8 cranial and 14 facial bones. Open full ↑
Key points
  • The skull is 22 bones: 8 cranial around the brain and 14 facial shaping the face.
  • The single cranial bones are the frontal, occipital, sphenoid, and ethmoid; the parietals and temporals are paired.
  • The mandible is the only freely movable skull bone; cranial bones meet at immovable sutures, which are open as fontanelles in infants.

Section 3The vertebral column

Twenty-six bones, five regions

The vertebral column — the backbone — is a stack of 26 bones that runs from the base of the skull to the pelvis. It does three big jobs at once: it supports the head and trunk, it protects the spinal cord threading down its center, and it stays flexible enough to let you bend and twist. The individual bones are vertebrae, and they are grouped into five regions from top to bottom. A classic mnemonic ties the counts to mealtimes — breakfast at 7, lunch at 12, dinner at 5:

RegionBonesLocation & note
Cervical7 (C1–C7)The neck; C1 is the atlas, C2 the axis
Thoracic12 (T1–T12)The chest; each bears a pair of ribs
Lumbar5 (L1–L5)The lower back; largest, most weight-bearing
Sacral1 (5 fused)The sacrum; joins the hip bones
Coccygeal1 (4 fused)The coccyx, or tailbone

Counting the individual pieces, a child actually starts with 33 vertebrae; five of them fuse into the single sacrum and four into the coccyx, so the adult total settles at 26. Notice the pattern: the vertebrae grow steadily larger as you move down the column, because each lower bone must carry the weight of everything above it. The lumbar vertebrae, holding up the entire upper body, are the biggest and blockiest of all.

Discs, the cushions between

Between adjacent vertebrae (from C2 downward) sits an intervertebral disc — a pad with a tough fibrous ring, the anulus fibrosus, wrapped around a soft gel core, the nucleus pulposus. There are 23 of these discs, and they do double duty: they act as shock absorbers that cushion the jolt of every step, and they form slightly movable joints that let the spine flex. When the soft core pushes through a weakened ring — a herniated (“slipped”) disc — it can press on a nearby spinal nerve and send pain shooting down a limb.

Seven necks for almost every mammal. Nearly every mammal has exactly seven cervical vertebrae — a mouse, a human, and a giraffe all share the same count. The giraffe's neck is long not because it has more bones, but because each of its seven is enormously elongated.

Tap each region in the interactive to isolate it and see its vertebrae.

Interactive Tap a region to isolate the 26 bones of the S-curved backbone by section. Open full ↑
Key points
  • The column is 26 bones in five regions: 7 cervical, 12 thoracic, 5 lumbar, plus the fused sacrum and coccyx (mnemonic 7–12–5).
  • Vertebrae grow larger downward to bear increasing weight; the lumbar vertebrae are the largest.
  • Intervertebral discs cushion the spine and allow movement; a herniated disc can compress a spinal nerve.

Section 4A typical vertebra

The body: the weight-bearer

Vertebrae look different from region to region, but they all share one blueprint. The front of every vertebra is the body (or centrum) — a thick, roughly cylindrical block of bone that faces anteriorly and does the heavy lifting. Stacked one on top of another with the discs between them, the bodies form the load-bearing pillar that carries the weight of the head and trunk. This is why the bodies get progressively larger toward the lumbar spine: the lower you go, the more weight there is to hold up.

The arch: the shield

Behind the body is the vertebral arch, a bony ring that projects backward. It is built from two pedicles (the short walls that rise from the body) and two laminae (the roof plates that meet in the midline). The body in front and the arch behind enclose the vertebral foramen — the hole through the center of the bone. Stack all the vertebrae and their foramina line up into the continuous vertebral canal, the bony tunnel that shelters the spinal cord along its whole length. The arch, in other words, is the spine's version of the skull's cranium: pure protection.

The processes: the handles

Projecting from the arch are seven bony processes that serve as levers and joints. The single spinous process points straight back — these are the bumps you feel down the middle of your back. The two transverse processes stick out to the sides. Spinous and transverse processes are attachment points where muscles and ligaments grab hold. The remaining four are the paired superior and inferior articular processes, whose smooth facets form movable joints with the vertebrae directly above and below, linking the whole column together.

PartNumberJob
Body (centrum)1Bears weight; faces anteriorly
Pedicles + laminae2 + 2Form the vertebral arch
Vertebral foramen1Opening that transmits the spinal cord
Spinous process1Posterior; muscle/ligament attachment
Transverse processes2Lateral; muscle/ligament attachment
Articular processes4Form joints with adjacent vertebrae
Two foramina, two very different jobs. Don't confuse the vertebral foramen (the big central hole that the spinal cord runs through) with the intervertebral foramina (the small side openings between stacked vertebrae where individual spinal nerves branch out to the body). One protects the cord; the others are the exit doors for its nerves.

Look straight down on a vertebra and tap each part to name it in the interactive.

Interactive Look straight down on a vertebra and tap the body, arch, and processes to label them. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Every vertebra has a weight-bearing body in front and a protective arch (pedicles + laminae) behind.
  • Body and arch enclose the vertebral foramen; stacked, these form the vertebral canal that houses the spinal cord.
  • The spinous and transverse processes anchor muscles; the articular processes form joints with neighboring vertebrae.

Section 5The thoracic cage

The sternum: the breastplate

The front of the chest is anchored by the sternum, or breastbone, a flat dagger-shaped bone with three parts. The broad manubrium sits on top and joins the collarbones. The long body forms the middle. The small xiphoid process is the pointed cartilage-tipped tail at the bottom. Where the manubrium meets the body there is a slight ridge, the sternal angle (angle of Louis), a landmark you can feel through the skin — it lines up with the second rib, giving clinicians a reliable starting point for counting ribs and placing a stethoscope.

Twelve pairs of ribs: true, false, and floating

Curving back from the sternum to the thoracic spine are 12 pairs of ribs. Every rib attaches to a thoracic vertebra behind, but they differ in how — or whether — they reach the sternum in front, and that is how we classify them. The true ribs (pairs 1–7) attach directly to the sternum, each by its own strip of costal cartilage. The false ribs (pairs 8–12) do not reach the sternum directly. Of these, pairs 8–10 join the costal cartilage of the rib just above them, while the last two — pairs 11 and 12 — have no anterior attachment at all and are called floating ribs, ending freely in the muscle of the back wall.

TypeRib pairsAnterior attachment
True ribs1–7Directly to the sternum by their own costal cartilage
False ribs8–10Indirectly — to the costal cartilage of the rib above
Floating ribs11–12None — no sternal attachment

A cage that breathes

The thoracic cage is not a solid box — it is a flexible basket that moves with every breath. When you inhale, muscles lift and swing the ribs upward and outward, enlarging the chest so the lungs can expand; when you exhale, the ribs drop back down. All the while the cage shields the heart, lungs, and great vessels beneath it, and it gives the muscles of breathing and of the upper body a framework to pull on. It is protection and machinery in a single structure.

Find the xiphoid before you compress. During CPR, the heel of the hand goes on the sternal body, not the xiphoid process. The small xiphoid can snap under pressure and injure the liver beneath it, so locating it first — and staying above it — is a safety step every rescuer learns.

Tap the sternum, the spine, and each rib group in the interactive to see how the cage fits together.

Interactive Tap the sternum, thoracic spine, and each rib group to see how the cage is built. Open full ↑
Key points
  • The sternum has three parts — manubrium, body, xiphoid process; the sternal angle marks the second rib.
  • Ribs are true (1–7, direct to sternum), false (8–10, indirect via the rib above), or floating (11–12, no anterior attachment).
  • The cage protects the heart and lungs and expands with each breath.

Section 6Spinal curvatures

Four curves, two born and two learned

Seen from the side, a healthy spine is not straight — it makes a gentle S-shape of four curves that balance your weight over your hips and spring like a shock absorber with every step. Two of them are primary curves, present at birth and shaped like the curled-up posture of a fetus: the thoracic curve (chest) and the sacral curve (pelvis), both concave toward the front. The other two are secondary curves that develop after birth as a baby learns to hold up its head and then to walk: the cervical curve appears when an infant begins lifting its head, and the lumbar curve appears when the toddler stands and walks. Together the four curves let the column carry a heavy head and trunk without toppling.

CurveTypeAppears
CervicalSecondaryWhen an infant lifts its head
ThoracicPrimaryPresent at birth
LumbarSecondaryWhen a toddler stands and walks
SacralPrimaryPresent at birth

When curves go wrong

The curves are healthy in moderation, but when one becomes exaggerated or turns the wrong way it gets a clinical name. Kyphosis is an excessive thoracic curve — a rounded, hunched upper back, sometimes called “hunchback,” common with osteoporosis in older adults. Lordosis is an excessive lumbar curve — an exaggerated inward sway of the lower back, or “swayback.” Scoliosis is different from both: an abnormal sideways (lateral) curve of the spine, so that from behind the column bends into a C or S. Scoliosis is the one screened for in schoolchildren, because catching it early — while the skeleton is still growing — gives the best chance to correct it.

Kyphosis and lordosis run front-to-back; scoliosis runs side-to-side. A simple way to keep them apart: kyphosis and lordosis are exaggerations of the normal curves you already have (seen from the side), while scoliosis is an abnormal curve in a plane where the spine should look straight (seen from behind).

Compare the normal S-curve with kyphosis, lordosis, and scoliosis side by side in the interactive.

Interactive Compare the healthy S-curve with kyphosis, lordosis, and scoliosis side by side. Open full ↑
Key points
  • The spine has four curves: primary (thoracic, sacral, present at birth) and secondary (cervical, lumbar, learned with head-lifting and walking).
  • Kyphosis = excessive thoracic curve; lordosis = excessive lumbar curve — both front-to-back exaggerations.
  • Scoliosis is an abnormal lateral curve, screened for during childhood growth.

The chapter in one breath

  • The skeleton divides into the axial (80 bones — skull, spine, thoracic cage; protection and support) and the appendicular (126 bones — limbs and girdles; movement).
  • The skull is 22 bones: 8 cranial around the brain and 14 facial, with the mandible the only movable one and bones joined by immovable sutures.
  • The vertebral column is 26 bones in five regions — 7 cervical, 12 thoracic, 5 lumbar, plus fused sacrum and coccyx — cushioned by intervertebral discs.
  • Every vertebra has a weight-bearing body, a protective arch enclosing the vertebral foramen, and processes for muscles and joints.
  • The thoracic cage is the three-part sternum plus 12 rib pairs — true, false, and floating — guarding the heart and lungs and moving with each breath.
  • The spine's four curves (primary thoracic/sacral, secondary cervical/lumbar) form a shock-absorbing S; exaggerations are kyphosis, lordosis, and the lateral scoliosis.
  • Throughout, the axial skeleton's shape follows one theme: armor for the body's most vital organs.

ReferenceWords worth knowing

A quick reference for the terms in this chapter — skim it now, or circle back whenever a word trips you up.

Appendicular skeleton
The 126 bones of the limbs and the two girdles (shoulder and hip) that attach them; specialized for movement.
Atlas (C1)
The first cervical vertebra, which supports the skull and allows the head to nod “yes.”
Axial skeleton
The 80 bones along the body's central axis — skull, vertebral column, and thoracic cage — that protect and support.
Axis (C2)
The second cervical vertebra, whose upward peg lets the head rotate to say “no.”
Coccyx
The small tailbone at the base of the spine, formed by four fused vertebrae.
Costal cartilage
The strip of cartilage connecting a rib to the sternum, directly or by way of the rib above.
Cranial bones
The eight skull bones (frontal, occipital, sphenoid, ethmoid, and paired parietals and temporals) that enclose the brain.
Facial bones
The fourteen skull bones that shape the face and frame the eyes, nose, and mouth.
Floating ribs
Rib pairs 11 and 12, which attach only to the vertebrae behind and have no anterior connection.
Fontanelle
A soft membranous gap between the cranial bones of an infant, allowing the skull to flex and the brain to grow.
Foramen magnum
The large opening in the occipital bone through which the spinal cord joins the brain.
Intervertebral disc
A fibrocartilage pad between adjacent vertebral bodies that absorbs shock and permits movement.
Kyphosis
An excessive posterior (front-to-back) curvature of the thoracic spine, producing a hunched upper back.
Lordosis
An excessive anterior curvature of the lumbar spine, producing an exaggerated swayback.
Mandible
The lower jaw — the only freely movable bone of the skull.
Manubrium
The broad upper part of the sternum that articulates with the clavicles and the first ribs.
Sacrum
The triangular bone of five fused vertebrae that joins the vertebral column to the hip bones.
Scoliosis
An abnormal lateral (side-to-side) curvature of the spine.
Sternal angle
The palpable ridge where the manubrium meets the sternal body, aligned with the second rib.
Suture
An immovable interlocking joint between two cranial bones.
Vertebral foramen
The central opening of a vertebra, formed by the body and arch, through which the spinal cord passes.
Xiphoid process
The small pointed inferior tip of the sternum, an important landmark for CPR.

Check yourself

1. Sort these into axial or appendicular: the sternum, the femur (thigh bone), a rib, the shoulder blade, and a vertebra.
Axial: sternum, rib, vertebra. Appendicular: femur, shoulder blade. Axial bones lie on the midline and protect organs; the limbs and their girdles (like the scapula) are appendicular and built for movement.
2. The axial skeleton has 80 bones and the appendicular 126. What do these add up to, and what does each group specialize in?
206 bones total; axial = protection and support, appendicular = movement. The axial 80 form the central axis around the brain, cord, heart, and lungs; the appendicular 126 are the limbs and girdles that let you move.
3. How many cranial bones and how many facial bones make up the skull, and which four cranial bones are unpaired?
8 cranial + 14 facial = 22 bones; the single cranial bones are the frontal, occipital, sphenoid, and ethmoid. The parietals and temporals are the two paired cranial bones.
4. Which is the only freely movable bone of the skull, and what kind of joint holds the rest of the cranial bones together?
The mandible (lower jaw); the other cranial bones meet at immovable sutures. In infants the sutures are still open as soft spots called fontanelles.
5. Give the number of bones in each region of the vertebral column, top to bottom.
Cervical 7, thoracic 12, lumbar 5, sacrum 1 (5 fused), coccyx 1 (4 fused) — 26 total. The mealtime mnemonic 7–12–5 covers the three movable regions.
6. Why are the lumbar vertebrae the largest bones in the column?
Because they bear the most weight. Each vertebra carries everything above it, so the bodies grow progressively larger toward the bottom; the lumbar region supports the entire head and trunk.
7. Name the part of a vertebra that bears weight and the part that protects the spinal cord.
The body (centrum) bears weight; the vertebral arch protects the cord. Together the body and arch enclose the vertebral foramen, and the stacked foramina form the vertebral canal around the spinal cord.
8. What is the difference between the vertebral foramen and the intervertebral foramina?
The vertebral foramen is the central hole the spinal cord passes through; the intervertebral foramina are the side openings between vertebrae where spinal nerves exit. One holds the cord; the others are the exit doors for its nerves.
9. Name the three parts of the sternum from top to bottom.
Manubrium, body, xiphoid process. The sternal angle, where the manubrium meets the body, is a palpable landmark lined up with the second rib.
10. Classify rib pairs 1–7, 8–10, and 11–12 by how they attach in front.
1–7 are true ribs (direct to the sternum); 8–10 are false ribs (to the cartilage of the rib above); 11–12 are floating ribs (no anterior attachment). All 12 pairs attach to the thoracic vertebrae behind.
11. Which spinal curves are primary (present at birth) and which are secondary (developing later), and what triggers each secondary curve?
Primary: thoracic and sacral. Secondary: cervical (lifting the head) and lumbar (standing and walking). The four curves together form the spine's shock-absorbing S-shape.
12. Define kyphosis, lordosis, and scoliosis, and say which one is a sideways curve.
Kyphosis = excessive thoracic (hunchback) curve; lordosis = excessive lumbar (swayback) curve; scoliosis = an abnormal lateral, side-to-side curve. Kyphosis and lordosis exaggerate front-to-back curves; scoliosis is the lateral one screened for in growing children.
Anatomy & Physiology · Chapter 8

The Appendicular Skeleton

If the axial skeleton is the body's central mast, the appendicular skeleton is everything that hangs off it and lets you act on the world — the arms that reach and grip, the legs that carry and spring, and the two bony girdles that bolt those limbs to the trunk. It is a set of 126 bones, and almost every one of them is a lever built for movement. In this chapter we work outward from each girdle to the fingertips and toes, and you will meet a recurring theme: the shoulder is engineered for freedom, the hip for strength, and the whole design is a series of trade-offs between the two. Learn the bones in this order and the hand and foot — 27 and 26 bones each — stop being a jumble and become a logical map.

By the end of this chapter you can

  • Define the appendicular skeleton and list its four divisions, accounting for all 126 bones.
  • Name the two bones of the pectoral girdle and explain why the upper limb's only bony link to the axial skeleton is the sternoclavicular joint.
  • Identify the bones of the upper limb — humerus, radius, ulna — and place the radius on the thumb side in anatomical position.
  • Sort the 27 bones of the hand and wrist into carpals, metacarpals, and phalanges, and name the eight carpals.
  • Describe how three bones fuse to form each hip bone and contrast the pelvic girdle's stability with the pectoral girdle's mobility.
  • Compare the male and female pelvis and explain the adaptations for childbirth.
  • Identify the bones of the lower limb and distinguish the weight-bearing tibia from the non-weight-bearing fibula.
  • Sort the 26 bones of the foot and ankle into tarsals, metatarsals, and phalanges, and explain how the foot's arches absorb shock.

Section 1The pectoral girdle

Two bones, one clever compromise

The pectoral girdle — the shoulder girdle — is the frame that connects each upper limb to the trunk, and it is built from only two bones on each side: the clavicle (collarbone) in front and the scapula (shoulder blade) behind. What is striking is how little the whole arm is bolted to the rest of the skeleton. The entire upper limb hangs from the axial skeleton at a single small joint — the sternoclavicular joint, where the medial end of the clavicle meets the manubrium of the sternum. The scapula, by contrast, is not directly joined to the ribs or spine at all; it floats on the back of the rib cage, held in place only by muscles. That loose arrangement is a deliberate compromise: the shoulder trades away stability to buy an enormous range of motion. You can circle your arm through nearly a full sphere — and the price is that the shoulder is one of the most easily dislocated joints in the body.

The clavicle: a slender strut

The clavicle is an S-shaped bone that acts as a brace, holding the shoulder out and away from the chest so the arm can swing freely. Its rounded sternal (medial) end articulates with the sternum; its flattened acromial (lateral) end meets the acromion of the scapula at the acromioclavicular (AC) joint. Because it is thin and sits right under the skin with nothing to cushion it, the clavicle is the most frequently fractured bone in the body — usually snapping in its middle third when someone falls onto an outstretched arm or the point of the shoulder.

The scapula: a triangle of landmarks

The scapula is a flat, triangular bone loaded with muscle-attachment features. Running across its back is the spine, which ends laterally in the acromion — the bony tip of your shoulder that you can feel. Curving forward like a finger is the coracoid process, an anchor for muscles and ligaments. Most important is the glenoid cavity (glenoid fossa), the shallow socket on the scapula's lateral angle that receives the head of the humerus to form the shoulder joint. That shallowness is the key to the whole design: a shallow socket lets the humerus swing widely, but it also lets the head slip out too easily.

Bone / featureWhat it isWhy it matters
ClavicleS-shaped collarbone, front of girdleBraces the shoulder outward; most commonly fractured bone
ScapulaTriangular shoulder blade, behindHeld only by muscles; carries the socket for the arm
Glenoid cavityShallow socket on the scapulaReceives the humeral head — shallow = mobile but unstable
AcromionLateral tip of the scapular spineForms the point of the shoulder; joins the clavicle
Sternoclavicular jointClavicle meets sternumThe only bony joint linking the upper limb to the axial skeleton
The shoulder's freedom is its weakness. A "separated shoulder" is an injury to the acromioclavicular joint, while a "dislocated shoulder" is the humeral head popping out of the shallow glenoid cavity. Both are direct consequences of the same design choice — mobility purchased at the cost of stability.

Explore both bones and the landmarks that ride on them in the interactive below.

Interactive The shoulder girdle attaches each arm to the axial skeleton by just two bones — the clavicle and scapula. Open full ↑
Key points
  • The pectoral girdle is just two bones — the clavicle and scapula — on each side.
  • The upper limb's only bony attachment to the axial skeleton is the sternoclavicular joint; the scapula floats on muscle.
  • The glenoid cavity is shallow, trading stability for a huge range of motion.
  • The clavicle is the body's most commonly fractured bone.

Section 2Bones of the upper limb

Three long bones between shoulder and wrist

Each upper limb contains 30 bones, but the three big long bones do the heavy structural work. The upper arm (the brachium) holds a single bone, the humerus. The forearm (the antebrachium) holds two bones side by side, the radius and the ulna. Learning which is which is easy if you anchor it to the anatomical position — palms facing forward: the radius is on the thumb (lateral) side, and the ulna is on the little-finger (medial) side. A phrase that sticks: the radius rotates around the ulna, and the radius is on the same side as the thumb.

The humerus and the elbow

The humerus is the long bone of the arm. Its rounded head fits into the glenoid cavity up at the shoulder. Just below the head is the surgical neck, a frequent fracture site that sits dangerously close to the axillary nerve. Down the shaft is the deltoid tuberosity, where the deltoid muscle attaches. At the elbow end, two rounded surfaces do the articulating: the capitulum meets the radius, and the pulley-shaped trochlea grips the ulna. Flanking them are the medial and lateral epicondyles — the medial one overlies the ulnar nerve, which is why a knock there sends a jolt down your arm (your "funny bone").

Radius and ulna: a rotating pair

The two forearm bones are joined along their length by a fibrous interosseous membrane and connect to each other at small joints top and bottom. This lets the forearm perform a beautiful trick: supination (palm up) and pronation (palm down). In pronation the radius pivots and crosses over the ulna. At the elbow, the ulna is the dominant partner — its olecranon forms the bony point of the elbow, and its trochlear notch wraps around the humeral trochlea like a wrench on a bolt. At the wrist, the radius takes over — it is the forearm bone that does most of the articulating with the carpal bones. Both bones end in a pointed styloid process.

BoneLocationKey role
HumerusUpper arm (brachium)Head fits the glenoid; distal end forms the elbow
RadiusForearm, thumb (lateral) sideRotates in pronation/supination; forms the wrist
UlnaForearm, pinky (medial) sideOlecranon forms the elbow point; stabilizes the forearm
Thumb side = radius. Whenever you lose track, look at your palm-forward hand: the bone lined up under your thumb is the radius, and it is the one that "radiates" out to spin the wrist. The ulna stays put at the elbow — think "elbow-u-lna."

Tap each of the three bones and rotate the forearm from supination to pronation in the interactive.

Interactive Three long bones — one in the upper arm, two in the forearm; the radius sits on the thumb side, the ulna on the pinky side. Open full ↑
Key points
  • The upper limb has three long bones: the humerus (arm) and the radius and ulna (forearm).
  • In anatomical position the radius is lateral (thumb side), the ulna is medial (pinky side).
  • The ulna dominates the elbow (olecranon); the radius dominates the wrist and does the rotating.

Section 3The hand & wrist

Twenty-seven bones in three groups

The hand is the most dexterous structure in the body, and its dexterity comes from packing 27 bones — and all the joints between them — into a small space. They sort neatly into three groups working from the wrist outward: 8 carpals in the wrist, 5 metacarpals in the palm, and 14 phalanges in the fingers. Get those three numbers (8, 5, 14) and their order and you have the whole hand.

The carpals: eight little stones

The carpals are eight small bones arranged in two rows of four, bound tightly by ligaments so the wrist bends as a unit. From the thumb side across the proximal row, then the distal row, they are: scaphoid, lunate, triquetrum, pisiform, then trapezium, trapezoid, capitate, hamate. A classic mnemonic keeps the order: "Sally Left The Party To Take Cathy Home." The scaphoid deserves special attention — it is the most commonly fractured carpal, usually from a fall on an outstretched hand, and because its blood supply enters from one end, a fracture can starve part of the bone and cause it to die.

Metacarpals and phalanges

The metacarpals are the five bones of the palm, numbered 1 to 5 starting at the thumb. Their rounded distal heads are your knuckles. The thumb's metacarpal meets the trapezium at a saddle joint — the special joint that lets the thumb swing across the palm to touch the other fingers, the opposable grip that makes the human hand so capable. The phalanges are the finger bones: each finger has three (proximal, middle, distal), but the thumb (pollex) has only two. That is 14 per hand. (A single one is a phalanx.)

GroupCountWhere / notes
Carpals8Wrist; two rows of four (scaphoid is the one most often broken)
Metacarpals5Palm; numbered 1–5 from the thumb; heads = knuckles
Phalanges14Fingers have 3 each; the thumb has only 2
Wrist pain after a fall on the hand? Think scaphoid. Tenderness in the "anatomical snuffbox" at the base of the thumb suggests a scaphoid fracture — and because these fractures can be invisible on an early X-ray yet risk avascular necrosis, they are splinted and re-imaged rather than dismissed.

Tap each of the three groups — wrist, palm, and fingers — in the interactive below.

Interactive Each hand packs 27 bones into three groups — the wrist, the palm, and the fingers. Open full ↑
Key points
  • The hand has 27 bones: 8 carpals + 5 metacarpals + 14 phalanges.
  • The eight carpals run scaphoid, lunate, triquetrum, pisiform, trapezium, trapezoid, capitate, hamate.
  • Each finger has 3 phalanges; the thumb has 2, and its saddle joint gives opposition.

Section 4The pelvic girdle

Built for strength, not freedom

Where the shoulder was engineered for mobility, the pelvic girdle is engineered for the opposite — strength and weight-bearing. It has to transmit the entire weight of the upper body down into the legs and absorb the pounding of every step. So instead of a bone floating on muscle, the pelvic girdle attaches firmly to the axial skeleton at the strong, nearly immovable sacroiliac joints. The girdle itself is the pair of hip bones (also called coxal bones or os coxae); together with the sacrum and coccyx they form the bony pelvis.

Three bones fused into one

Each hip bone begins life as three separate bones that fuse during adolescence into a single unit, meeting at a deep, cup-shaped socket called the acetabulum. The ilium is the large, fan-shaped upper part — its rim, the iliac crest, is the "hip" you rest your hands on. The ischium is the posterior-inferior part, and its rough ischial tuberosity is what you sit on. The pubis is the anterior part; the two pubic bones meet in front at the pubic symphysis, a joint of fibrocartilage. Notice the contrast with the shoulder: the acetabulum is a deep socket that grips the head of the femur firmly, so the hip almost never dislocates — the mirror image of the shallow, mobile glenoid.

Male and female pelvis: shaped by childbirth

The pelvis is the one part of the skeleton that differs clearly between the sexes, because the female pelvis is adapted to let a baby pass through. Compared with the male, the female pelvis is wider and shallower, with a larger, more rounded pelvic inlet, and a broader subpubic angle (the angle of the pubic arch) that is greater than 90°. The male pelvis is narrower and heavier, with a heart-shaped inlet and a subpubic angle less than 90°. These differences are reliable enough that anatomists and forensic scientists use the pelvis to determine sex from a skeleton.

FeatureFemale pelvisMale pelvis
Overall shapeWider, shallower, lighterNarrower, deeper, heavier
Pelvic inletRounded, oval, largerHeart-shaped, smaller
Subpubic angleWide — greater than 90°Narrow — less than 90°
Purpose of the differenceRoomy birth canal for childbirth—
Deep socket, stable joint. The acetabulum grips the femoral head far more deeply than the glenoid grips the humerus. That is the whole story of girdle design in one line: the shoulder gives up stability for motion, the hip gives up motion for stability — each shaped by its job.

Tap the ilium, ischium, and pubis, then toggle between the male and female pelvis in the interactive.

Interactive The pelvic girdle is built for strength: each hip bone fuses three bones into one — and its shape differs in males and females. Open full ↑
Key points
  • The pelvic girdle is two hip bones, each a fusion of ilium + ischium + pubis meeting at the acetabulum.
  • It attaches firmly to the axial skeleton at the sacroiliac joints — built for weight-bearing, not mobility.
  • The female pelvis is wider with a subpubic angle greater than 90°, adapted for childbirth.

Section 5Bones of the lower limb

The strongest bones in the body

The lower limb carries your entire body weight and drives every step, so its bones are the largest and strongest you own. Like the arm, each limb has 30 bones, and four of them are the big structural players. The thigh holds a single bone, the femur. The knee is guarded by the patella (kneecap). The lower leg holds two bones, the tibia and fibula — the counterparts to the forearm's radius and ulna, but with a crucial difference we will get to.

Femur and patella

The femur is the longest, heaviest, and strongest bone in the body. Its ball-shaped head sits deep in the acetabulum; below the head, the angled neck is the classic site of a "hip fracture" in older adults with thin bone. Bumps called the greater and lesser trochanters anchor powerful hip muscles, and a ridge on the back, the linea aspera, gives more muscle attachment. At the knee, the femur's rounded condyles roll on the top of the tibia. The patella is the body's largest sesamoid bone — a bone that forms inside a tendon. Sitting in the quadriceps tendon, it acts like a pulley that improves the leverage of the thigh muscles when you straighten the knee, and it shields the joint from a direct blow.

Tibia and fibula: one carries, one steadies

Here is the difference from the forearm. In the leg the two bones do not share the load. The tibia (shinbone) is the thick, medial bone, and it bears essentially all the weight — its broad top (the tibial plateau) supports the femur, and its lower end forms the medial malleolus, the inner ankle bump. The fibula is the slender, lateral bone. It carries almost no weight; its jobs are to stabilize the ankle and give muscles a place to attach. Its lower end forms the lateral malleolus, the outer ankle bump. Notably, the fibula does not even reach the knee — only the tibia meets the femur there.

BoneLocationRole
FemurThighLongest, strongest bone; head fits the acetabulum
PatellaFront of kneeLargest sesamoid bone; improves quadriceps leverage
TibiaLeg, medialWeight-bearing shinbone; forms the medial malleolus
FibulaLeg, lateralNon-weight-bearing; stabilizes ankle, forms lateral malleolus
Tibia takes the load, fibula takes the side. The tibia is medial and weight-bearing; the fibula is lateral and mostly for stability and muscle attachment. Because the fibula does so little load-bearing, surgeons can harvest a piece of it to graft elsewhere in the body with little effect on walking.

Tap each of the four bones and find the two malleoli at the ankle in the interactive.

Interactive The leg carries your whole weight, so its bones are the largest and strongest — the thigh, the kneecap, and two in the lower leg. Open full ↑
Key points
  • The lower limb's four big bones are the femur, patella, tibia, and fibula.
  • The femur is the body's longest and strongest bone; the patella is its largest sesamoid bone.
  • The tibia (medial) bears the weight and forms the knee; the fibula (lateral) only stabilizes the ankle.

Section 6The foot & ankle

Twenty-six bones, built to bear weight

The foot echoes the hand — three groups of bones — but it is tuned for a completely different job: instead of grasping, it must bear weight and spring you forward. It holds 26 bones: 7 tarsals in the ankle and heel, 5 metatarsals in the sole, and 14 phalanges in the toes. It is the same three-group plan as the hand, with slightly different counts to remember (7, 5, 14).

The tarsals and the weight it takes

The tarsals are seven bones forming the ankle and back of the foot. Two of them do the serious work. The talus sits at the top and is the bone the tibia and fibula grip — it receives the body's weight from the leg and passes it down. The calcaneus (heel bone) is the largest tarsal; it forms the heel and takes the weight when your foot strikes the ground. The other five — the navicular, the cuboid, and three cuneiforms — bridge the ankle to the sole. The metatarsals (numbered 1–5 from the big toe) form the sole and the ball of the foot, and the phalanges are the toe bones: three in each toe, but only two in the big toe (the hallux) — exactly the pattern of the hand.

The arches: the foot's suspension

What truly separates the foot from the hand is its arches. The tarsals and metatarsals are held by ligaments and tendons into three curves — a medial longitudinal arch (the high one on the inside), a lateral longitudinal arch, and a transverse arch across the foot. These arches turn the foot into a natural spring and shock absorber: they flatten slightly to soak up the impact of each step, then recoil to push you off. When the arches drop, the result is flat feet (pes planus), which can lead to foot and leg fatigue because the shock absorber is gone.

GroupCountWhere / notes
Tarsals7Ankle & heel; talus takes weight from the leg, calcaneus is the heel
Metatarsals5Sole; numbered 1–5 from the big toe; heads = ball of the foot
Phalanges14Toes have 3 each; the big toe (hallux) has 2
Weight lands on a tripod. Standing weight is shared among the heel (calcaneus) and the heads of the first and fifth metatarsals — a stable three-point base. The arches spanning between them are why a healthy foot can absorb tons of cumulative force over a day without pain.

Tap each of the three groups — ankle, sole, and toes — and see the arches in the interactive below.

Interactive Each foot has 26 bones in three groups — built to bear weight, with bony arches that act as shock absorbers. Open full ↑
Key points
  • The foot has 26 bones: 7 tarsals + 5 metatarsals + 14 phalanges.
  • The talus receives weight from the leg; the calcaneus is the heel and largest tarsal.
  • Three arches make the foot a shock absorber and spring; their collapse is flat feet.

The chapter in one breath

  • The appendicular skeleton is 126 bones — the two limb girdles plus the arms and legs that hang from them.
  • The pectoral girdle (clavicle + scapula) links the arm to the trunk by a single bony joint (sternoclavicular), buying mobility at the cost of stability.
  • The upper limb has three long bones — humerus, plus radius (thumb side) and ulna (pinky side); the ulna rules the elbow, the radius rules the wrist.
  • The hand is 27 bones: 8 carpals, 5 metacarpals, 14 phalanges, with an opposable thumb for dexterity.
  • The pelvic girdle is two hip bones (ilium + ischium + pubis fused at the acetabulum), built for strength and weight-bearing; the female pelvis is adapted for childbirth.
  • The lower limb has the body's strongest bones — femur, patella, and the weight-bearing tibia with the stabilizing fibula.
  • The foot is 26 bones (7 tarsals, 5 metatarsals, 14 phalanges) whose arches make it a spring and shock absorber.
  • Throughout, the design is a trade-off: the shoulder chooses freedom, the hip chooses strength.

ReferenceWords worth knowing

A quick reference for the terms in this chapter — skim it now, or circle back whenever a word trips you up.

Acetabulum
The deep, cup-shaped socket on the hip bone that receives the head of the femur to form the hip joint.
Appendicular skeleton
The 126 bones of the limbs and the two girdles (pectoral and pelvic) that attach the limbs to the axial skeleton.
Calcaneus
The heel bone; the largest tarsal, which bears weight when the foot strikes the ground.
Carpals
The eight small bones of the wrist, arranged in two rows of four.
Clavicle
The S-shaped collarbone that braces the shoulder; the most commonly fractured bone in the body.
Femur
The thigh bone; the longest, heaviest, and strongest bone in the body.
Fibula
The slender lateral bone of the leg; non-weight-bearing, it stabilizes the ankle and forms the lateral malleolus.
Glenoid cavity
The shallow socket of the scapula that receives the head of the humerus at the shoulder joint.
Hip bone (os coxae)
The coxal bone; each one is a fusion of the ilium, ischium, and pubis, and the pair forms the pelvic girdle.
Humerus
The single long bone of the upper arm, running from the shoulder to the elbow.
Metacarpals
The five bones of the palm, numbered 1 to 5 from the thumb; their heads form the knuckles.
Metatarsals
The five bones of the sole of the foot, numbered 1 to 5 from the big toe.
Patella
The kneecap; the largest sesamoid bone, formed within the quadriceps tendon to improve leverage.
Pectoral girdle
The shoulder girdle — clavicle and scapula — that attaches each upper limb to the axial skeleton.
Pelvic girdle
The two hip bones that attach the lower limbs to the axial skeleton and bear the body's weight.
Phalanges
The bones of the fingers and toes; three per digit except the thumb and big toe, which have two (singular: phalanx).
Radius
The lateral (thumb-side) bone of the forearm; it rotates in pronation and forms most of the wrist joint.
Scapula
The triangular shoulder blade, held to the trunk only by muscles and carrying the glenoid cavity.
Talus
The tarsal bone that the tibia and fibula grip; it receives the body's weight from the leg.
Tarsals
The seven bones of the ankle and posterior foot, including the talus and calcaneus.
Tibia
The medial shinbone; the weight-bearing bone of the leg that forms the knee and the medial malleolus.
Ulna
The medial (pinky-side) bone of the forearm; its olecranon forms the point of the elbow.

Check yourself

1. The appendicular skeleton totals 126 bones. Name its four divisions.
The pectoral girdles, the upper limbs, the pelvic girdle, and the lower limbs. These are the "appendages" and the two girdles that bolt them to the axial skeleton; together they make up 126 of the skeleton's 206 bones.
2. Which two bones make up the pectoral girdle, and what is the only bony joint connecting the entire upper limb to the axial skeleton?
The clavicle and scapula; the sternoclavicular joint is the only bony link. The scapula is held to the trunk by muscles alone, which is what gives the shoulder its enormous range of motion.
3. Why is the shoulder easy to dislocate while the hip almost never dislocates?
The glenoid cavity is a shallow socket; the acetabulum is a deep one. The shoulder trades stability for mobility, so the humeral head slips out easily; the hip trades mobility for stability, so the femoral head stays put.
4. In anatomical position, which forearm bone is on the thumb side, and which dominates the elbow?
The radius is on the thumb (lateral) side; the ulna dominates the elbow. The ulna's olecranon forms the point of the elbow, while the radius does most of the articulating at the wrist and rotates during pronation and supination.
5. How many bones are in the hand, and how do they divide into three groups?
27 bones: 8 carpals (wrist) + 5 metacarpals (palm) + 14 phalanges (fingers). Each finger has three phalanges; the thumb has only two, which still totals 14 per hand.
6. A patient falls on an outstretched hand and has tenderness at the base of the thumb. Which carpal bone is most likely fractured, and why is it worrying?
The scaphoid. It is the most commonly fractured carpal, and because its blood supply enters from one end, a fracture can cut off blood to part of the bone and cause it to die (avascular necrosis).
7. Each hip bone is formed by the fusion of three bones meeting at one landmark. Name the three bones and the landmark.
The ilium, ischium, and pubis, fused at the acetabulum. The ilium is the broad upper part (its crest is the "hip"), the ischium is what you sit on, and the pubis meets its partner at the pubic symphysis in front.
8. Give two ways the female pelvis differs from the male pelvis, and explain why.
The female pelvis is wider and shallower with a larger, rounded inlet, and its subpubic angle is greater than 90°. These changes widen the birth canal for childbirth; the male pelvis is narrower with a heart-shaped inlet and a subpubic angle under 90°.
9. Which bone of the leg bears the body's weight, and which mostly provides stability? Which one forms the knee?
The tibia bears the weight and forms the knee; the fibula mainly stabilizes the ankle. The fibula does not even reach the knee joint — only the tibia articulates with the femur there. The two form the medial and lateral malleoli at the ankle.
10. What kind of bone is the patella, and what does it do for the knee?
It is the body's largest sesamoid bone — a bone that forms inside a tendon. Sitting in the quadriceps tendon, it acts as a pulley that improves the leverage of the thigh muscles when straightening the knee, and it shields the joint from blows.
11. Name the two tarsal bones that do the main weight-bearing work of the foot and the job of each.
The talus receives weight from the leg; the calcaneus (heel bone) takes weight at foot strike. The talus is the bone the tibia and fibula grip, and the calcaneus is the largest tarsal, forming the heel.
12. What structural feature makes the foot a shock absorber, and what is the condition called when it fails?
Its arches — the medial and lateral longitudinal arches and the transverse arch — and the failure is flat feet (pes planus). The arches flatten to absorb each step's impact and recoil to push off; when they collapse, the foot loses its spring and can cause fatigue and pain.
Anatomy & Physiology · Chapter 9

Joints

Bones give the body its frame, but a frame that could not bend would be a statue. Wherever two bones meet, there is a joint — and joints are where movement is born. In this chapter you will learn the two ways anatomists sort every joint in the body, meet the six shapes of the freely-moving synovial joints, take apart the fluid-filled capsule that lets them glide, and put a name to every motion your body can make. Along the way you will see the single theme that governs all of them: a constant, unavoidable trade-off between how much a joint moves and how stable it stays.

By the end of this chapter you can

  • Classify any joint two ways — structurally (fibrous, cartilaginous, synovial) and functionally (synarthrosis, amphiarthrosis, diarthrosis) — and explain how the two schemes line up.
  • State the mobility–stability trade-off and predict it from a joint's structure.
  • Name the six types of synovial joint, describe the movement each allows, and give a body example of each.
  • Label the parts of a synovial joint and explain the three jobs of synovial fluid.
  • Perform and name the body movements in their opposite pairs, including the special movements of the forearm, foot, and thumb.
  • Distinguish the subtypes of fibrous (suture, syndesmosis, gomphosis) and cartilaginous (synchondrosis, symphysis) joints.
  • Describe the structure and signature weakness of the shoulder, hip, knee, and elbow.
  • Connect common injuries — sprains, dislocations, torn menisci, arthritis — to the joint structures that fail.

Section 1Two ways to classify a joint

What is a joint, and why do we sort them twice?

A joint (or articulation) is simply any place where two bones — or a bone and cartilage, or a tooth and bone — come together. That is a huge and varied group: the immovable seams of the skull are joints, and so is the wildly mobile shoulder. To make sense of that range, anatomists describe every joint in two independent ways at once, and you should get in the habit of giving both.

The first scheme is structural — it asks what material binds the bones together, and is there a fluid-filled space between them? By that test there are exactly three kinds: fibrous joints, held by dense connective tissue with no cavity; cartilaginous joints, held by cartilage with no cavity; and synovial joints, which have a fluid-filled cavity between the bones. The second scheme is functional — it asks the simpler question how much does it move? A synarthrosis is immovable, an amphiarthrosis is slightly movable, and a diarthrosis is freely movable.

Movement versus stability: the great trade-off

These two schemes are not random — they track each other, because the material that holds a joint together decides how much it can move. Bind two bones with tough fibers and you get a rock-solid but nearly motionless union (fibrous, usually a synarthrosis). Cushion them with cartilage and you get a little give (cartilaginous, usually an amphiarthrosis). Put a slippery fluid-filled gap between them and they can swing freely — but now nothing but ligaments and muscles holds them in place (synovial, always a diarthrosis).

That is the single most important idea in the whole chapter: mobility and stability pull against each other. Every joint in your body sits somewhere on that spectrum, and its design is a compromise chosen for the job it does. The skull needs to protect the brain, so it trades away all movement for maximum stability. The shoulder needs to throw and reach, so it trades away stability for a huge range of motion — which is exactly why it is the joint people dislocate most.

Functional classMovementUsual structureExample
SynarthrosisImmovableFibrous (some cartilaginous)Skull sutures; a tooth in its socket
AmphiarthrosisSlightly movableCartilaginous (some fibrous)Pubic symphysis; intervertebral discs
DiarthrosisFreely movableSynovialShoulder, hip, knee, elbow, fingers
Read the prefixes and you know the movement. In Greek, arthron means joint. Syn- (together, fused) → no movement; amphi- (on both sides, a little) → slight movement; di- (through, apart) → free movement. Learn the three prefixes once and the functional classes name themselves.

Tap through the classification tree in the interactive and watch how much each joint actually moves.

Interactive Sort joints two ways — by how much they move and by what holds them — and watch each one move. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Every joint is described twice: structurally (fibrous / cartilaginous / synovial) and functionally (synarthrosis / amphiarthrosis / diarthrosis).
  • Structure = the binding material and whether there is a cavity; function = how much it moves.
  • The schemes track each other because material dictates motion: fibrous → immovable, cartilaginous → slightly movable, synovial → freely movable.
  • The master theme is the mobility–stability trade-off: more movement always costs stability.

Section 2The six synovial joints

Same blueprint, six shapes

All synovial joints share the same fluid-filled design (that is the next section), but the shape of the two bone ends that meet determines how the joint can move. There are six shapes, and each allows a characteristic set of motions. The tidy way to keep them straight is by their axes of movement — the number of directions in which the bones can swing.

A nonaxial joint slides but does not swing. A uniaxial joint moves in one plane, like a door on a hinge. A biaxial joint moves in two planes, and a multiaxial joint moves in all directions and can spin as well. As you go down that list, mobility climbs — and, true to the chapter's theme, stability falls.

TypeAxes / movementWhere you have one
Plane (gliding)Nonaxial — flat surfaces slide back and forthIntercarpal joints of the wrist; between vertebral facets
HingeUniaxial — flexion and extension onlyElbow, knee, joints of the fingers
PivotUniaxial — rotation around a single long axisAtlas–axis (shaking “no”); radius at the elbow
Condyloid (ellipsoid)Biaxial — flexion/extension + abduction/adductionKnuckles (metacarpophalangeal); wrist
SaddleBiaxial, with a wider range than condyloidBase of the thumb (first carpometacarpal)
Ball-and-socketMultiaxial — movement in every plane plus rotationShoulder and hip

Reading the shape tells you the move

Once you can picture the shape, the motion follows automatically. A hinge is a convex end sitting in a concave trough, so it can only rock one way — the elbow cannot swing side to side any more than a door can. A ball-and-socket is a rounded head buried in a cup, free to swing in any direction and to spin, which is why the shoulder and hip are the two most mobile joints you own. The saddle joint at the base of your thumb, where each bone is shaped like a rider on a saddle, is what gives humans a truly opposable thumb — the anatomy behind every grip.

The thumb is a saddle, not a ball. People assume the thumb's amazing range means a ball-and-socket joint, but its base is a saddle joint. That shape gives it two axes plus the special motion of opposition — touching the thumb to each fingertip. Lose that one joint to arthritis and simple tasks like turning a key become painful.

Tap each of the six shapes in the interactive to see the motion it allows and where you have one.

Interactive The six synovial joint shapes — tap each to see it move and where you've got one. Open full ↑
Key points
  • All six synovial types share one design; the shape of the bone ends sets the movement.
  • By axes: plane (nonaxial) → hinge and pivot (uniaxial) → condyloid and saddle (biaxial) → ball-and-socket (multiaxial).
  • Ball-and-socket (shoulder, hip) is the most mobile; plane joints only glide.
  • The saddle joint at the thumb base makes the human grip possible.

Section 3Inside a synovial joint

The essential parts

What lets a synovial joint move so freely is a small fluid-filled space, the joint (synovial) cavity, sitting between the two bone ends so they never actually touch. Everything else in the joint exists to protect, feed, and lubricate that gap. Learn these parts and you can label any synovial joint in the body.

Each bone end is capped with a smooth layer of articular cartilage — glassy hyaline cartilage that presents a nearly frictionless surface and cushions the impact of every step. The whole joint is enclosed by a two-layered articular capsule: a tough outer fibrous layer that is continuous with the bones' covering and holds everything together, and a delicate inner synovial membrane that lines the cavity and makes its fluid. That fluid, synovial fluid, fills the cavity like oil in a hinge.

StructureWhat it does
Articular cartilageHyaline cartilage capping each bone end; a smooth, shock-absorbing surface
Joint (synovial) cavityFluid-filled space between the bones that allows free movement
Articular capsuleOuter fibrous layer for strength; inner synovial membrane that secretes fluid
Synovial fluidSlippery fluid rich in hyaluronic acid — lubricates, feeds cartilage, absorbs shock
LigamentsBands of dense connective tissue that tie bone to bone and reinforce the capsule
Bursae & tendon sheathsFluid-filled sacs that cushion where tendons or skin rub over the joint

Synovial fluid: oil, food, and cushion

Synovial fluid quietly does three jobs at once. First, it lubricates — its hyaluronic acid makes it slick, so the cartilage surfaces glide with less friction than ice on ice. Second, it feeds the cartilage: articular cartilage has no blood supply of its own, so it soaks up oxygen and nutrients from the fluid and dumps waste back into it. Third, it absorbs shock. When you press on a joint, fluid is squeezed out of the compressed cartilage; when you release, it seeps back in — a self-oiling mechanism called weeping lubrication, which is one reason gentle movement is good for aching joints.

Bursae: the joint's shock pads

Around many joints sit small flattened sacs of synovial fluid called bursae, positioned wherever a tendon, ligament, or patch of skin would otherwise chafe against bone. They act like greased cushions. Overuse them — kneeling all day, an overhead throwing motion — and a bursa can become inflamed and painful, the condition called bursitis.

Cartilage has no blood supply — so it barely heals. Because articular cartilage is avascular, a worn or torn surface cannot mount a normal repair. Decades of wear thin that cartilage until bone grinds on bone: this is osteoarthritis, the most common joint disease, and the reason synovial joints are the ones that wear out with age.

Tap each labeled part in the interactive to see how the capsule, cavity, cartilage, and fluid fit together.

Interactive The fluid-filled cavity, slick cartilage, and wrapping capsule — tap each part. Open full ↑
Key points
  • The defining feature is a fluid-filled joint cavity that keeps the bone ends apart.
  • Articular cartilage caps the bones; the articular capsule has a tough fibrous outer layer and a fluid-making synovial membrane inside.
  • Synovial fluid does three jobs: lubricate, feed the avascular cartilage, and absorb shock.
  • Bursae cushion tendons and skin over the joint; their inflammation is bursitis, and worn cartilage causes osteoarthritis.

Section 4The movements of the body

Motions come in opposite pairs

Every movement a synovial joint produces has a precise name, and the beauty of the system is that the names come in opposing pairs — learn one and you have practically learned its partner. All of them are described from the anatomical position, the same upright, palms-forward pose you met in Chapter 1.

The most common pair is flexion and extension. Flexion decreases the angle at a joint (bending the elbow, curling forward at the waist); extension increases it, straightening the joint back out. Push a joint past its straight position and you get hyperextension — tilting the head back to look at the ceiling. The next pair moves limbs toward or away from the body's midline: abduction carries a limb away from the midline, adduction brings it back toward the midline. Trace a full cone with an outstretched arm and you have circumduction, which strings flexion, abduction, extension, and adduction into one circle. Finally, rotation turns a bone around its own long axis — shaking your head “no” rotates the skull on the spine.

MovementWhat happensEveryday example
Flexion / ExtensionDecrease / increase the joint angleBending vs. straightening the elbow
Abduction / AdductionAway from / toward the midlineRaising the arm out to the side and back down
CircumductionCone-shaped circling of a limbWindmilling the whole arm
RotationTurning a bone on its long axisShaking the head “no”
Supination / PronationPalm turned up / palm turned downTurning a doorknob back and forth
Dorsiflexion / Plantar flexionToes toward the shin / toes pointing downStanding on the heels vs. the toes
