Inversion / EversionSole turned inward / outwardRolling the ankle in or out
Elevation / DepressionRaising / lowering a partShrugging and dropping the shoulders

The special movements

A handful of joints make motions that need their own names. At the forearm, supination turns the palm up (as if carrying soup) and pronation turns it down. At the ankle, lifting the toes toward the shin is dorsiflexion and pointing them down is plantar flexion, while turning the sole inward is inversion and outward is eversion. The jaw and shoulders can protract (move forward) and retract (move back), and elevate and depress. And the thumb performs opposition, crossing the palm to meet the fingertips — the movement that makes the human hand a tool.

To abduct is to take away. Kidnappers abduct a person — carry them away. In the body, abduction carries a limb away from the midline, and adduction adds it back. For the forearm, remember you supinate to hold a bowl of soup.

Tap each movement in the interactive to watch a body perform it and see its opposite.

Interactive The named movements of synovial joints, in opposite pairs — tap each to watch it happen. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Movements are named in opposite pairs and defined from the anatomical position.
  • Flexion/extension change a joint angle; abduction/adduction move a limb away from/toward the midline; rotation spins a bone on its axis; circumduction is the combined cone.
  • Special movements include supination/pronation (forearm), dorsiflexion/plantar flexion and inversion/eversion (foot), elevation/depression and protraction/retraction, and opposition of the thumb.

Section 5Fibrous & cartilaginous joints

Joints without a cavity

Not every joint is built for motion. The two structural classes that lack a synovial cavity — fibrous and cartilaginous joints — trade movement for the stability the body needs at certain spots. They hold bones together with tissue rather than a fluid gap, and most of them fall on the immovable or barely-movable end of the functional scale.

Fibrous joints: bound by tough fibers

In a fibrous joint, dense fibrous connective tissue stitches the bones directly together. There are three kinds. A suture is the jagged, interlocking seam between the flat bones of the skull, held by a thin layer of fibrous tissue — essentially immovable (a synarthrosis), which is exactly what a case protecting the brain should be. A syndesmosis connects two parallel bones across a broader sheet or cord of fibrous tissue, such as the interosseous membrane between the radius and ulna; it permits a little give (an amphiarthrosis). A gomphosis is the peg-in-socket joint that anchors each tooth in its bony socket, held by the periodontal ligament — also immovable.

Cartilaginous joints: bound by cartilage

In a cartilaginous joint, cartilage does the binding. A synchondrosis uses hyaline cartilage: the classic example is the epiphyseal (growth) plate in a growing long bone, and the joint between the first rib and the sternum — both immovable. A symphysis uses tough, resilient fibrocartilage and allows slight movement (an amphiarthrosis); the pubic symphysis at the front of the pelvis and the intervertebral discs between the vertebrae are the two you must know — the discs let the spine bend a little at each level, which adds up to a flexible back.

Structural typeSubtypeBinding materialMovementExample
FibrousSutureThin fibrous tissueImmovableSkull seams
SyndesmosisSheet/cord of fibrous tissueSlightly movableRadius–ulna membrane
GomphosisPeriodontal ligamentImmovableTooth in its socket
CartilaginousSynchondrosisHyaline cartilageImmovableEpiphyseal plate; 1st rib to sternum
SymphysisFibrocartilageSlightly movablePubic symphysis; intervertebral discs
Some joints are meant to disappear. The epiphyseal plate is a temporary synchondrosis: once a bone finishes growing, the cartilage ossifies and the two parts fuse into solid bone — a bony union called a synostosis. Skull sutures do the same thing slowly with age. A joint built to vanish is a normal and useful design.

Tap each fibrous and cartilaginous type in the interactive to see how it trades movement for stability.

Interactive Joints without a cavity — held by tough fibers or by cartilage. Tap each type. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Fibrous and cartilaginous joints have no cavity and favor stability over movement.
  • Fibrous subtypes: suture (skull, immovable), syndesmosis (radius–ulna membrane, slightly movable), gomphosis (tooth, immovable).
  • Cartilaginous subtypes: synchondrosis (hyaline — growth plate, immovable) and symphysis (fibrocartilage — pubic symphysis and discs, slightly movable).
  • A growth plate is a temporary joint that later fuses into a synostosis.

Section 6Four joints that do the work

Shoulder and hip: two ball-and-sockets, two philosophies

The shoulder and the hip are both ball-and-socket joints, yet they answer the mobility–stability question in opposite ways. The shoulder (glenohumeral) joint is built for reach: the ball of the humerus sits against a shallow, saucer-like socket, giving it the widest range of any joint in the body. The price is fragility — the socket barely holds the ball, so muscles of the rotator cuff are its main stabilizers, and it is the joint people dislocate most often. The hip joint makes the opposite bargain: the head of the femur is sunk deep into a true cup, the acetabulum, and ringed by thick ligaments. It gives up some range to become one of the body's most stable joints — it has to be, because it carries your whole body weight with every step.

The knee: a hinge that does more than hinge

The knee is the largest and most complex joint in the body. It works mainly as a hinge — flexing and extending the leg — but it also allows a little rotation when bent, which is where trouble starts. Two crescents of fibrocartilage, the menisci, sit on top of the tibia to spread load and cushion the joint, and two crossing ligaments deep inside, the anterior and posterior cruciate ligaments (ACL and PCL), keep the femur and tibia from sliding past each other. A hard twist on a planted foot can tear the ACL and meniscus together — the reason knee injuries end so many athletic seasons.

The elbow: stability by design

The elbow shows what a joint looks like when the body chooses stability. It is a hinge where the humerus and ulna interlock so snugly that the joint moves cleanly in one plane and rarely dislocates. Strong collateral ligaments on each side reinforce it, and a separate pivot at the radius lets the forearm rotate into supination and pronation without loosening the hinge. It is a quiet lesson in the chapter's theme: a joint that gives up range gains a durability the shoulder can only envy.

JointTypeMain stabilizersSignature injury
Shoulder (glenohumeral)Ball-and-socketRotator-cuff muscles (shallow socket)Dislocation; rotator-cuff tear
HipBall-and-socketDeep socket + thick ligamentsFracture (often with age)
KneeHinge (+ slight rotation)Cruciate ligaments, menisci, collateralsACL / meniscus tear
ElbowHinge (+ pivot)Bony interlock + collateral ligamentsDislocation (uncommon)
The “unhappy triad” of the knee. A blow to the outside of a planted knee classically tears three things at once — the ACL, the medial meniscus, and the tibial (medial) collateral ligament. Knowing which structures share a joint tells you which ones fail together, and why the shoulder dislocates while the elbow almost never does.

Tap each of the four joints in the interactive to compare how each balances mobility against stability.

Interactive Four workhorse joints — each trades mobility against stability differently. Tap each. Open full ↑
Key points
  • The shoulder maximizes mobility with a shallow socket — and pays for it as the most-dislocated joint, stabilized by the rotator cuff.
  • The hip is a deep, ligament-bound ball-and-socket built for stable weight-bearing.
  • The knee is a complex hinge stabilized by menisci and cruciate ligaments; twisting injuries tear them.
  • The elbow trades range for a snug, durable hinge, with a pivot for forearm rotation.

The chapter in one breath

  • A joint is any place two bones meet; every joint is classified structurally (fibrous, cartilaginous, synovial) and functionally (synarthrosis, amphiarthrosis, diarthrosis).
  • The two schemes align because material dictates motion, and the master theme is the mobility–stability trade-off: more movement always costs stability.
  • Synovial joints are the freely-movable ones and come in six shapes — plane, hinge, pivot, condyloid, saddle, and ball-and-socket — from nonaxial to multiaxial.
  • A synovial joint centers on a fluid-filled cavity between cartilage-capped bones inside an articular capsule; synovial fluid lubricates, feeds the avascular cartilage, and absorbs shock.
  • Movements are named in opposite pairs — flexion/extension, abduction/adduction, rotation, circumduction — plus special motions like supination/pronation and opposition.
  • Fibrous (suture, syndesmosis, gomphosis) and cartilaginous (synchondrosis, symphysis) joints have no cavity and favor stability; some, like the growth plate, later fuse into bone.
  • The shoulder, hip, knee, and elbow each strike the mobility–stability balance differently, which predicts how each one gets injured.

ReferenceWords worth knowing

A quick reference for the terms in this chapter — skim it now, or circle back whenever a word trips you up.

Abduction
Movement of a limb away from the body's midline (raising the arm out to the side).
Adduction
Movement of a limb toward the midline, back to the body.
Amphiarthrosis
A functionally slightly-movable joint (for example, the pubic symphysis or an intervertebral disc).
Articular capsule
The two-layered sleeve enclosing a synovial joint — a tough fibrous outer layer and a fluid-secreting synovial membrane inside.
Articular cartilage
The smooth hyaline cartilage that caps each bone end in a synovial joint, reducing friction and absorbing shock.
Bursa
A small fluid-filled sac that cushions where a tendon, ligament, or skin rubs over bone; its inflammation is bursitis.
Circumduction
Cone-shaped movement of a limb that combines flexion, abduction, extension, and adduction in sequence.
Diarthrosis
A functionally freely-movable joint; all synovial joints are diarthroses.
Fibrous joint
A joint in which dense fibrous connective tissue directly binds the bones, with no cavity (suture, syndesmosis, gomphosis).
Flexion
A movement that decreases the angle at a joint, such as bending the elbow.
Gomphosis
The immovable peg-in-socket fibrous joint anchoring a tooth in its bony socket via the periodontal ligament.
Ligament
A band of dense connective tissue that connects bone to bone and reinforces a joint.
Meniscus
A crescent of fibrocartilage (as in the knee) that sits between bone ends to spread load and improve fit.
Suture
An immovable fibrous joint forming the interlocking seams between the flat bones of the skull.
Symphysis
A slightly-movable cartilaginous joint united by fibrocartilage (pubic symphysis, intervertebral discs).
Synarthrosis
A functionally immovable joint (for example, a skull suture).
Synchondrosis
A cartilaginous joint united by hyaline cartilage, such as the epiphyseal growth plate.
Syndesmosis
A fibrous joint in which a sheet or cord of connective tissue links two bones, allowing slight movement (radius–ulna).
Synovial fluid
The slippery, hyaluronic-acid–rich fluid filling a joint cavity; it lubricates, nourishes cartilage, and cushions.
Synovial joint
A freely-movable joint with a fluid-filled cavity between cartilage-capped bones enclosed in a capsule.

Check yourself

1. A joint is described as “a synovial diarthrosis.” What does each word tell you, and why do we always give both?
“Synovial” is the structural class (a fluid-filled cavity between the bones); “diarthrosis” is the functional class (freely movable). We give both because structure and function are separate questions — one names what holds the joint together, the other names how much it moves — and together they fully describe it.
2. State the mobility–stability trade-off and use it to explain why the skull is fused while the shoulder is loose.
The more a joint moves, the less stable it is — movement and stability pull against each other. The skull must protect the brain, so it trades away all movement (immovable sutures) for maximum stability; the shoulder must reach, so it trades away stability for a huge range, which is why it dislocates most easily.
3. Match each functional class — synarthrosis, amphiarthrosis, diarthrosis — to its movement and give one example of each.
Synarthrosis = immovable (skull suture); amphiarthrosis = slightly movable (pubic symphysis or intervertebral disc); diarthrosis = freely movable (shoulder, knee). The prefixes give it away: syn- = fused, amphi- = a little, di- = free.
4. Name the six types of synovial joint in order of increasing mobility, and give the axis category of each.
Plane (nonaxial), hinge and pivot (uniaxial), condyloid and saddle (biaxial), ball-and-socket (multiaxial). Plane joints only glide; hinge and pivot move in one axis; condyloid and saddle in two; ball-and-socket in all directions plus rotation.
5. The base of the thumb allows an unusually wide range for a small joint. What type of synovial joint is it, and what special movement does it make possible?
A saddle joint (the first carpometacarpal joint), which allows opposition. Its saddle shape gives two axes of motion and lets the thumb cross the palm to touch each fingertip — the anatomy behind the human grip.
6. List the three jobs of synovial fluid, and explain why the “feeding” job is so important.
Lubrication, nutrition of the cartilage, and shock absorption. Articular cartilage is avascular — it has no blood supply — so it depends entirely on synovial fluid for oxygen and nutrients and to carry away waste; without that route the cartilage could not survive.
7. Why does worn articular cartilage heal so poorly, and what common disease results from decades of that wear?
Articular cartilage is avascular, so it cannot mount a normal blood-borne repair. Over years the cartilage thins until bone grinds on bone, producing osteoarthritis, the most common joint disease.
8. Give the opposite of each movement: flexion, abduction, supination, dorsiflexion.
Extension, adduction, pronation, plantar flexion. Body movements are named in opposing pairs, so learning one member of a pair effectively teaches its partner.
9. Distinguish the three fibrous joints — suture, syndesmosis, gomphosis — by example and by how much each moves.
Suture = skull seams, immovable; syndesmosis = the radius–ulna interosseous membrane, slightly movable; gomphosis = a tooth in its socket, immovable. All three bind bone with dense fibrous tissue and have no joint cavity.
10. A synchondrosis and a symphysis are both cartilaginous joints. What cartilage does each use, how much does each move, and give an example of each.
A synchondrosis uses hyaline cartilage and is immovable (the epiphyseal growth plate); a symphysis uses fibrocartilage and is slightly movable (the pubic symphysis or intervertebral discs). The tougher fibrocartilage of a symphysis is what allows its small, cushioned movement.
11. Both the shoulder and hip are ball-and-socket joints, yet one is far more stable. Explain the structural difference and its consequence.
The shoulder has a shallow socket (stabilized mainly by the rotator-cuff muscles), giving huge range but frequent dislocation; the hip's femoral head sits deep in a true cup ringed by thick ligaments, giving up range for stability to bear body weight. Same joint type, opposite trade-offs.
12. An athlete plants a foot, twists, and injures the knee. Which structures are most likely damaged, and why does a twist — rather than a straight bend — do the harm?
The cruciate ligaments (often the ACL) and a meniscus, sometimes with the medial collateral ligament — the classic “unhappy triad.” The knee is built as a hinge and tolerates only a little rotation; a forceful twist on a planted foot exceeds that limit and tears the very structures that normally restrain rotation.
Anatomy & Physiology · Chapter 10

Muscle Tissue

Muscle is the tissue that turns a decision into a movement. Every heartbeat, every blink, every stride, every push of food through your gut is a muscle cell doing the one thing muscle does supremely well — shortening on command. In this chapter we take a single skeletal muscle apart, layer by layer, until we reach the microscopic engine that actually pulls: the sarcomere. Then we watch that engine run — how a nerve signal crosses into the muscle, how calcium throws the switch, how filaments slide past one another, and how the body dials the force up from a delicate flicker to an all-out heave. By the end you will understand contraction not as magic but as a beautifully ordered chain of events you can follow from thought to motion.

By the end of this chapter you can

  • Compare the three types of muscle tissue — skeletal, cardiac, and smooth — by structure, control, and location.
  • Trace the connective-tissue wrappings of a skeletal muscle from epimysium down to a single fiber, and name the parts inside that fiber.
  • Diagram a sarcomere and identify its bands, zones, and lines, and explain why skeletal muscle looks striped.
  • Explain the sliding filament theory and the steps of the cross-bridge cycle, including the roles of ATP and calcium.
  • Describe the neuromuscular junction and follow a signal from motor neuron to muscle action potential.
  • Define a motor unit and explain how recruitment and summation grade muscle tension from a twitch to tetanus.
  • Distinguish the three skeletal muscle fiber types and match each to the kind of work it does best.

Section 1How a muscle is built

Three kinds of muscle tissue

Before we open up one muscle, it helps to know that the body builds muscle in three different flavors, each tuned to a different job. Skeletal muscle attaches to bones and moves the body; it is under voluntary control, and under the microscope its cells are long, striped (striated), and packed with many nuclei. Cardiac muscle is found only in the heart wall; it is striated too, but it is involuntary, its cells are branched with usually a single nucleus, and they are joined end-to-end by intercalated discs that let the whole heart contract as one. Smooth muscle lines the walls of hollow organs — blood vessels, gut, bladder, airways — where it squeezes slowly and tirelessly; it is involuntary, has no stripes, and its cells are tapered with one central nucleus. This chapter concentrates on skeletal muscle, because it shows every principle most clearly, but keep the other two in mind — the contraction machinery is largely shared.

FeatureSkeletalCardiacSmooth
LocationAttached to bonesHeart wall onlyWalls of hollow organs & vessels
StriationsStriatedStriatedNot striated
ControlVoluntaryInvoluntaryInvoluntary
Cell shapeLong cylinder, many nucleiBranched, usually 1 nucleusTapered spindle, 1 nucleus
Special featureMotor-neuron controlledIntercalated discs; self-rhythmicSlow, sustained, self-triggering

Bundles inside bundles

A whole skeletal muscle — your biceps, say — is not one solid block but a nested set of bundles, each wrapped in its own sheet of connective tissue. Working from the outside in: the entire muscle is sheathed in the epimysium; inside that, fibers are gathered into bundles called fascicles, each wrapped by perimysium; and inside each fascicle, every individual muscle cell (a muscle fiber) is wrapped in a delicate endomysium. These three sheaths are continuous with one another and, at the ends of the muscle, they merge and extend beyond it as a tendon (a cord) or an aponeurosis (a flat sheet) that anchors the muscle to bone. This arrangement is not just tidy packaging: the connective tissue carries the blood vessels and nerves in to each fiber, and it transmits the pull of every cell to the tendon so the whole muscle moves as one.

“Epi, peri, endo” = outside, around, within. The same Greek prefixes label the wrappings of a nerve (epineurium, perineurium, endoneurium). Learn the pattern once and it works for both — epi covers the whole organ, peri surrounds each bundle, endo hugs each individual cell.

Inside a single fiber

Zoom into one muscle fiber and you find a cell built for pulling. Its plasma membrane is called the sarcolemma, and its cytoplasm the sarcoplasm (the prefix sarco- means “flesh”). The sarcolemma dives deep into the cell as tunnels called transverse (T) tubules, which carry the electrical signal into the fiber’s core. Wrapped around the working machinery is a specialized smooth endoplasmic reticulum, the sarcoplasmic reticulum (SR), which stores and releases the calcium that triggers contraction. Filling most of the cell are hundreds of long threads called myofibrils, and each myofibril is a chain of the contractile units — the sarcomeres — made of myofilaments of the proteins actin and myosin. Because a muscle fiber forms during development by many cells fusing together, it holds many nuclei pushed to the edge just under the sarcolemma, and a reserve of satellite cells sits nearby to repair damage.

Follow all of this in the interactive: start at the whole muscle and zoom, wrapping by wrapping, down to the sarcomere.

Interactive Zoom in from the whole muscle down to the tiny unit that actually shortens — the sarcomere. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Three muscle types: skeletal (voluntary, striated, bones), cardiac (involuntary, striated, heart), smooth (involuntary, unstriated, hollow organs).
  • A skeletal muscle nests bundles inside bundles: epimysium → fascicle (perimysium) → fiber (endomysium), all continuous with the tendon.
  • Inside a fiber: sarcolemma, T-tubules, sarcoplasmic reticulum, myofibrils of actin and myosin, and many nuclei at the edge.

Section 2The sarcomere

Stripes with a purpose

The stripes that give skeletal and cardiac muscle their name are not decoration — they are the visible signature of astonishingly ordered machinery. Inside every myofibril, the contractile proteins are lined up in identical repeating segments, and each segment is a sarcomere, the functional unit of contraction. One sarcomere runs from one Z-disc to the next Z-disc. When millions of these little units all shorten at once, in register, the whole muscle shortens — and it is their alternating dense and light regions, stacked side by side across the fiber, that our eyes read as striations.

The bands, zones, and lines

A sarcomere is built from two kinds of filament. The thick filaments are made of myosin, each with a hinged head that sticks out; the thin filaments are made of actin. Where the two overlap, the sarcomere looks dark; where only one is present, it looks lighter. Learning the map of bands is worth the effort, because in the next section you will watch these regions change size as the muscle contracts.

RegionWhat it containsAppearance
A bandThe full length of the thick filaments (with overlap at its edges)Dark; does not change length in contraction
I bandThin filaments only, spanning two adjacent sarcomeresLight; narrows during contraction
H zoneThick filaments only, center of the A band (no overlap)Lighter center; narrows during contraction
Z-disc (Z-line)Anchors the thin filaments; the border of the sarcomereDark line; Z-discs are pulled together
M-lineAnchors the thick filaments at the sarcomere’s centerFine central line

Thick, thin, and the switch proteins

The thin filament is more than plain actin. Wrapped along it lies a ropelike protein, tropomyosin, that in a resting muscle physically covers the spots where myosin would attach — the myosin-binding sites. Holding tropomyosin in place is a three-part protein complex, troponin, one piece of which (troponin C) is a calcium receptor. This is the molecular switch: when calcium binds troponin, the whole complex shifts, tropomyosin slides aside, and the binding sites open. Two more proteins keep the sarcomere honest: giant, springy titin runs from Z-disc to M-line, keeping the thick filament centered and giving muscle its passive recoil, and nebulin runs alongside the thin filament, setting its length. Structure, once again, is built exactly for the job.

A is for dark, I is for light. The bands are named for how they bend polarized light — the A band is Anisotropic (dark) and the I band is Isotropic (light). A quick mnemonic: the letters with a straight vertical stroke, A and dark, go together; the thin, light I band is the pale one that shrinks when you flex.

Tap each part in the interactive to see how the filaments build the stripes.

Interactive Zoom into a myofibril to see the repeating sarcomeres and the two filaments that create the light and dark bands. Open full ↑
Key points
  • The sarcomere (Z-disc to Z-disc) is the functional unit of contraction; ordered sarcomeres create the visible striations.
  • Thick filaments are myosin (with heads); thin filaments are actin, guarded by tropomyosin and the calcium-sensing troponin.
  • The A band stays constant while the I band and H zone narrow during contraction — the tell-tale of sliding filaments.

Section 3The sliding filament theory

Sliding, not shrinking

Here is the single most important idea in this chapter, and it surprises almost everyone the first time: the filaments do not get shorter. When a muscle contracts, the thick and thin filaments slide past one another, increasing their overlap and dragging the Z-discs closer together. Each filament keeps its exact length; the sarcomere is what shortens. That is why, in the reference figure, the A band (the length of the thick filament) never changes, while the I band and H zone shrink and the Z-discs march inward. Multiply that tiny slide across millions of sarcomeres in series and the whole muscle noticeably shortens and pulls on its tendon.

The cross-bridge cycle

The sliding is powered by the myosin heads, which act as tiny oars. Each head reaches out, grabs actin to form a cross-bridge, and swivels — a power stroke — that ratchets the thin filament a few nanometers toward the center. Then it lets go, re-cocks, and grabs again, farther along. The cycle repeats many times per second, and it runs on ATP:

StepWhat happensRole of ATP / Ca²⁺
1. Cross-bridge formsThe energized myosin head binds an exposed site on actinRequires Ca²⁺ to have opened the site
2. Power strokeThe head pivots, sliding the thin filament inward; ADP & P₃ are releasedUses energy stored from a previous ATP
3. DetachmentA fresh ATP binds the myosin head, which releases from actinATP binding breaks the cross-bridge
4. Re-cockingATP is split to ADP + P₃, re-energizing and re-setting the headATP hydrolysis re-cocks the head

Notice the twin jobs of ATP: it is needed to release the head (step 3) and to re-cock it (step 4). This has a memorable consequence — in death, when ATP production stops, myosin heads bind actin and can no longer let go, and the body stiffens into rigor mortis.

Calcium throws the switch: excitation–contraction coupling

Cross-bridges can only form if the binding sites are uncovered, and that is calcium’s job. The link between an electrical signal and a mechanical pull is called excitation–contraction coupling. A muscle action potential races along the sarcolemma and dives down the T-tubules into the fiber. That voltage change tells the sarcoplasmic reticulum to dump its stored Ca²⁺ into the sarcoplasm. Calcium binds troponin, troponin tugs tropomyosin off the actin, the binding sites open, and cross-bridging — and sliding — begins. Relaxation is just the reverse: when the signal stops, a pump called SERCA hauls Ca²⁺ back into the SR, tropomyosin re-covers the sites, cross-bridges can no longer form, and the muscle goes slack. No calcium, no contraction.

Rigor mortis is a chemistry lesson. Stiffening begins a few hours after death because cross-bridges are locked without the ATP needed to release them, and Ca²⁺ leaks freely once the SR’s pumps fall silent. It fades a day or two later only as the muscle proteins themselves break down — a vivid reminder that both binding and release cost energy.

Watch the Z-lines pull together while the filaments hold their length in the interactive.

Interactive Myosin heads grab actin and ratchet it inward — the filaments stay the same length while the sarcomere gets shorter. Open full ↑
Key points
  • In the sliding filament theory, filaments keep their length; the sarcomere shortens as thick and thin filaments overlap more.
  • The cross-bridge cycle — bind, power stroke, detach, re-cock — runs on ATP, which is needed to release and to re-energize the myosin head.
  • Excitation–contraction coupling: an action potential → T-tubules → SR releases Ca²⁺ → troponin/tropomyosin uncover binding sites; SERCA reclaims calcium to relax.

Section 4The neuromuscular junction

From nerve to muscle

A skeletal muscle fiber will not contract on its own — it waits for the command of a nerve. That command is delivered at a specialized synapse called the neuromuscular junction (NMJ). A motor neuron reaches out from the spinal cord, and its axon terminal comes to rest just above a folded patch of sarcolemma called the motor end plate. The two never touch; a tiny gap, the synaptic cleft, separates them. To bridge that gap, the neuron uses a chemical messenger — the neurotransmitter acetylcholine (ACh).

The handoff, step by step

The transfer of the signal from nerve to muscle is one of the most reliable events in physiology — a single nerve impulse essentially always triggers a muscle impulse. Follow the chain:

StepEvent
1A nerve action potential arrives at the axon terminal.
2Voltage-gated Ca²⁺ channels open; calcium rushes into the terminal.
3Calcium triggers vesicles to release acetylcholine into the synaptic cleft.
4ACh crosses the cleft and binds nicotinic receptors on the motor end plate.
5Receptor channels open; Na⁺ floods in, creating an end-plate potential.
6The depolarization sets off a muscle action potential that spreads along the sarcolemma and down the T-tubules — and contraction follows.

Notice how this dovetails with the last section: step 6 is exactly the muscle action potential that starts excitation–contraction coupling. The NMJ is the doorway; the sliding filament is the room it opens onto.

Switching the signal off — and where it breaks

A signal that never ended would leave the muscle locked in contraction, so the cleft contains an enzyme, acetylcholinesterase (AChE), that chops ACh apart within milliseconds. This tidy on/off system is also where many drugs and diseases act, which makes the NMJ one of the highest-yield spots in clinical medicine. In myasthenia gravis, the immune system destroys ACh receptors, so muscles fatigue and weaken. Curare (an arrow poison) blocks the receptors, causing paralysis. Organophosphate pesticides and nerve agents inhibit AChE, so ACh piles up and muscles seize. Botulinum toxin (Botox) blocks ACh release, relaxing muscle — useful for spasms and wrinkles.

Follow the transmitter to predict the disease. Anything that removes ACh signaling (myasthenia gravis, curare, botulinum) causes weakness or paralysis; anything that lets ACh accumulate (AChE inhibitors, nerve agents) causes uncontrolled contraction and spasm. Knowing which side of the switch a toxin hits tells you the symptoms.

Step through the chemical handoff yourself in the interactive.

Interactive A nerve signal hands off a chemical message — acetylcholine — that sparks the muscle fiber to contract. Open full ↑
Key points
  • The neuromuscular junction is the synapse where a motor neuron signals a muscle fiber using acetylcholine across the synaptic cleft.
  • ACh binds nicotinic receptors on the motor end plate, letting Na⁺ in to trigger a muscle action potential; acetylcholinesterase then ends the signal.
  • NMJ disorders map to the transmitter: blocking ACh → weakness (myasthenia gravis, curare, botulinum); prolonging ACh → spasm (AChE inhibitors, nerve agents).

Section 5Motor units & muscle tension

The motor unit

A single motor neuron rarely commands just one muscle fiber. Instead it branches and connects to a group of fibers, and the neuron together with all the fibers it controls is a motor unit. When that neuron fires, every fiber in its unit contracts at once — they are an all-or-nothing team. The size of the unit sets how finely a muscle can be controlled. Muscles that need delicacy, like those moving the eye or the fingers, have small motor units (a neuron to only a handful of fibers). Muscles built for power, like the quadriceps or the calf, have large motor units (a neuron to hundreds of fibers). Small units mean fine control; large units mean strong, coarse force.

Twitch, summation, tetanus

One brief stimulus produces a single, quick contraction-and-relaxation called a twitch, which has three phases: a latent period (the hidden delay while calcium is released), a contraction phase, and a relaxation phase. A twitch is a flick, not a useful pull. To get smooth, strong movement, the nervous system fires the motor neuron again before the fiber fully relaxes, so the second contraction stacks on top of the first. This stacking is wave (temporal) summation. Fire faster still and the individual twitches blur into a partly smoothed unfused (incomplete) tetanus, and faster yet into a steady, maximal, perfectly smooth fused (complete) tetanus. Nearly all normal movements use tetanus, not single twitches.

ResponseStimulus patternResult
TwitchOne stimulusA single brief contraction and full relaxation
Wave summationA second stimulus before relaxation endsThe second contraction adds to the first — more tension
Unfused tetanusRapid, repeated stimuliStrong, sustained pull with slight wobbles
Fused tetanusVery high-frequency stimuliMaximal, perfectly smooth, sustained contraction

Two dials for force: recruitment and rate

The body grades how hard a muscle pulls using two controls at once. The first is recruitment — switching on more motor units. Under the size principle, small, weak, fatigue-resistant units are recruited first, and larger, more powerful ones are added only as more force is needed, so movements start smooth and controllable. The second dial is rate coding — firing each active unit faster to climb the summation-to-tetanus ladder above. Together, recruitment and rate let you lift a feather or a barbell with the same muscle. Even at rest, a few units are always active in rotation, keeping muscles firm and ready — this steady low-level tension is muscle tone.

Small first, for a reason. The size principle isn’t arbitrary — recruiting the smallest, most fatigue-resistant motor units first means everyday, low-force tasks are handled by the units that can keep going all day, while the big, quickly-tiring power units are held in reserve for when you truly need them.

Pick a stimulation pattern in the interactive and watch tension climb from twitch to tetanus.

Interactive A single zap gives a quick twitch; fire them faster and the twitches pile up into a smooth, powerful pull. Open full ↑
Key points
  • A motor unit is one motor neuron plus all the fibers it controls; small units give fine control, large units give power.
  • Tension builds from a twitch through wave summation to tetanus as stimulation frequency rises; real movement uses tetanus.
  • Force is graded by recruitment (adding units, smallest first — the size principle) and rate coding (firing faster); resting activity is muscle tone.

Section 6The three fiber types

Speed versus stamina

Not every skeletal muscle fiber is the same. They come in three types that trade speed against endurance, because no single design can be both maximally fast and maximally tireless. Slow oxidative (Type I) fibers contract slowly and generate modest force, but they resist fatigue for hours — the endurance specialists. Fast glycolytic (Type IIx) fibers contract quickly and powerfully but tire within minutes — the sprint-and-lift specialists. In between sit fast oxidative (Type IIa) fibers, which are fast and reasonably fatigue-resistant — a versatile middle ground.

Aerobic versus anaerobic engines

The difference comes down to how each fiber makes its ATP. Oxidative fibers run on aerobic respiration: they are packed with mitochondria, richly supplied with capillaries, and loaded with myoglobin — an oxygen-binding pigment that makes them look red. That red, oxygen-fed machinery is slow but sustainable. Glycolytic fibers rely on anaerobic glycolysis: they have few mitochondria, little myoglobin (so they look pale), and large glycogen stores for quick bursts. They deliver explosive power but flood with lactate and fatigue fast.

PropertySlow oxidative (I)Fast oxidative (IIa)Fast glycolytic (IIx)
Contraction speedSlowFastFast
Fatigue resistanceHighModerateLow
Main ATP sourceAerobic (oxidative)BothAnaerobic (glycolytic)
Mitochondria / capillariesManyManyFew
Myoglobin / colorHigh / redHigh / red-pinkLow / white
Best forPosture, enduranceWalking, sustained effortSprinting, lifting

Everybody is a mixture — and a nod to the other two muscles

Almost every real muscle contains all three fiber types, blended in a ratio set partly by your genes and partly by how you train. Endurance training coaxes fibers toward the oxidative, fatigue-resistant end; strength and sprint training push toward larger, more powerful fibers. It is why a marathoner’s calves and a sprinter’s thighs are built for different feats. And remember the two muscle types we set aside at the start: cardiac muscle behaves like a permanently endurance-tuned, self-rhythmic tissue joined by intercalated discs so the heart never truly rests, while smooth muscle has no sarcomeres at all and can hold tension for long stretches with very little energy — a “latch” state perfect for keeping a blood vessel or a sphincter squeezed shut all day.

White meat, dark meat. A chicken’s breast is pale because flight muscles fire in fast, glycolytic bursts; its legs are dark because standing and walking all day demand red, myoglobin-rich oxidative fibers. The color on your dinner plate is a fiber-type lesson.

Tap each fiber type in the interactive to compare speed, color, and stamina.

Interactive Some fibers are built for endurance, some for explosive power — and most muscles mix all three. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Three fiber types trade speed for stamina: slow oxidative (I) = endurance; fast glycolytic (IIx) = power; fast oxidative (IIa) = in between.
  • Oxidative fibers are red (many mitochondria, myoglobin, capillaries) and aerobic; glycolytic fibers are pale, anaerobic, powerful, and quick to fatigue.
  • Most muscles are a mix shaped by genetics and training; cardiac and smooth muscle are separate designs tuned for tireless work.

The chapter in one breath

  • Muscle comes in three types — skeletal (voluntary, striated), cardiac (involuntary, striated, self-rhythmic), and smooth (involuntary, unstriated) — sharing the same basic contraction machinery.
  • A skeletal muscle is bundles within bundles wrapped in epimysium, perimysium, and endomysium, all continuous with the tendon; inside each fiber are the sarcolemma, T-tubules, sarcoplasmic reticulum, and myofibrils.
  • The sarcomere (Z-disc to Z-disc) is the functional unit; overlapping thick (myosin) and thin (actin) filaments create the striations, guarded by tropomyosin and troponin.
  • In the sliding filament theory, filaments keep their length while the sarcomere shortens; the cross-bridge cycle runs on ATP, and Ca²⁺ released by the SR uncovers the binding sites.
  • The neuromuscular junction hands the signal from nerve to muscle using acetylcholine, then clears it with acetylcholinesterase; toxins here cause weakness or spasm.
  • A motor unit is one neuron and its fibers; tension is graded from a twitch to tetanus by recruitment and rate coding.
  • Three fiber types trade speed for endurance, and most muscles blend all three in a ratio shaped by genes and training.

ReferenceWords worth knowing

A quick reference for the terms in this chapter — skim it now, or circle back whenever a word trips you up.

A band
The dark region of a sarcomere spanning the full length of the thick filaments; it does not change length during contraction.
Acetylcholine (ACh)
The neurotransmitter released by a motor neuron at the neuromuscular junction to trigger a muscle action potential.
Actin
The protein of the thin filament, carrying the myosin-binding sites that cross-bridges attach to.
Cross-bridge
The connection formed when a myosin head binds an exposed site on actin; its cycle of binding, power stroke, and release drives filament sliding.
Endomysium
The delicate connective-tissue sheath wrapping each individual muscle fiber.
Epimysium
The connective-tissue sheath surrounding an entire skeletal muscle.
Excitation–contraction coupling
The sequence linking a muscle action potential to contraction: the signal travels down T-tubules and triggers Ca²⁺ release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum.
Fascicle
A bundle of muscle fibers wrapped by perimysium.
Motor unit
A single motor neuron together with all the muscle fibers it innervates; they contract as one.
Myofibril
A long, threadlike organelle inside a muscle fiber made of a chain of sarcomeres.
Myosin
The protein of the thick filament, whose hinged heads form cross-bridges and perform the power stroke.
Neuromuscular junction
The synapse between a motor neuron and a skeletal muscle fiber where acetylcholine passes the signal.
Recruitment
Increasing muscle force by activating more motor units, smallest and most fatigue-resistant first (the size principle).
Sarcolemma
The plasma membrane of a muscle fiber; its inward tunnels are the T-tubules.
Sarcomere
The functional contractile unit of striated muscle, running from one Z-disc to the next.
Sarcoplasmic reticulum (SR)
The specialized smooth ER of a muscle fiber that stores and releases the Ca²⁺ that triggers contraction.
Sliding filament theory
The model that muscle shortens because thick and thin filaments slide past one another, not because the filaments themselves shorten.
Tetanus
A smooth, sustained, maximal contraction produced when stimuli arrive too fast for the fiber to relax between them.
Tropomyosin
The thin-filament protein that covers the myosin-binding sites at rest and slides aside when calcium binds troponin.
Troponin
The thin-filament protein complex that binds Ca²⁺ and moves tropomyosin to expose the binding sites.
Twitch
A single brief contraction and relaxation of a muscle fiber in response to one stimulus, with latent, contraction, and relaxation phases.

Check yourself

1. Name the three types of muscle tissue and, for each, state whether it is voluntary or involuntary and whether it is striated.
Skeletal — voluntary, striated; cardiac — involuntary, striated; smooth — involuntary, not striated. Skeletal moves the skeleton, cardiac is found only in the heart wall (with intercalated discs), and smooth lines hollow organs and vessels.
2. Working from the outside in, name the three connective-tissue wrappings of a skeletal muscle and what each surrounds.
Epimysium (the whole muscle) → perimysium (each fascicle) → endomysium (each fiber). The three sheaths are continuous and merge at the muscle’s ends to form the tendon that anchors it to bone.
3. What are the sarcolemma, the sarcoplasmic reticulum, and the T-tubules, and what does each contribute to contraction?
The sarcolemma is the fiber’s membrane, the T-tubules are its inward tunnels, and the SR stores calcium. The action potential runs along the sarcolemma and down the T-tubules, prompting the SR to release Ca²⁺ that triggers the filaments to slide.
4. A sarcomere is defined as the region between two of which structures, and why is it called the “functional unit” of muscle?
Between two Z-discs; it is the smallest unit that actually performs contraction. When millions of sarcomeres shorten in series and in register, the whole muscle shortens.
5. During contraction, which sarcomere regions get narrower and which stays the same length? What does that tell you?
The I band and H zone narrow; the A band stays the same. Because the A band equals the length of the thick filaments, its constancy proves the filaments slide rather than shorten.
6. What do tropomyosin and troponin do at rest, and what changes when calcium arrives?
At rest, tropomyosin covers the myosin-binding sites on actin, held by troponin. When Ca²⁺ binds troponin, the complex shifts, tropomyosin slides aside, the binding sites open, and cross-bridges can form.
7. State the two distinct jobs ATP performs in the cross-bridge cycle.
ATP binding detaches the myosin head from actin; ATP hydrolysis then re-cocks (re-energizes) the head. Both release and re-setting cost energy — which is why a body with no ATP after death stiffens into rigor mortis.
8. Put the events of excitation–contraction coupling in order: Ca²⁺ binds troponin; SR releases calcium; action potential travels down T-tubules; binding sites open.
Action potential down T-tubules → SR releases Ca²⁺ → Ca²⁺ binds troponin → binding sites open. To relax, the SERCA pump returns calcium to the SR, tropomyosin re-covers the sites, and cross-bridges stop forming.
9. At the neuromuscular junction, which neurotransmitter is released, which ion enters the muscle to depolarize the end plate, and which enzyme ends the signal?
Acetylcholine is released; Na⁺ enters the muscle; acetylcholinesterase breaks down the ACh. ACh binds nicotinic receptors on the motor end plate, letting Na⁺ in to create an end-plate potential and then a muscle action potential.
10. Myasthenia gravis destroys ACh receptors, while a nerve agent blocks acetylcholinesterase. Predict the effect of each on the muscle, and explain the difference.
Myasthenia gravis → weakness/fatigue; a nerve agent → uncontrolled contraction and spasm. Removing ACh signaling weakens the muscle; preventing ACh breakdown lets it pile up and over-stimulate the fiber.
11. Define a motor unit, and explain why the muscles that move the eye have much smaller motor units than the quadriceps.
A motor unit is one motor neuron plus all the fibers it innervates. Small units (few fibers per neuron) allow the precise, fine control the eye needs, while large units in the quadriceps deliver powerful but coarse force.
12. How does a muscle progress from a single twitch to a smooth, forceful tetanus, and which two mechanisms grade the overall force a whole muscle produces?
Faster, repeated stimuli cause wave summation that builds into tetanus; force is graded by recruitment and rate coding. Recruitment adds more motor units (smallest first, the size principle) and rate coding fires each unit faster.
Anatomy & Physiology · Chapter 11

The Muscular System

Every voluntary thing you do — a smile, a step, a breath, a signature — is a muscle pulling on a bone or on your skin. In the last chapters you met the microscopic machinery inside a muscle fiber; now we zoom out to the whole body and ask a different set of questions. How do more than 600 skeletal muscles cooperate to produce smooth, controlled movement when each one can only pull? Why do their names read like Latin riddles — and how can you crack those riddles to know a muscle you have never memorized? This chapter is a guided tour of the body's muscles, region by region, from the tiny muscles that shape a facial expression to the powerhouses that launch you off the ground.

By the end of this chapter you can

  • Explain why skeletal muscles can only pull, and how that forces them to work in opposing pairs across a joint.
  • Define a muscle's origin and insertion, and assign the roles of agonist, antagonist, synergist, and fixator in a given movement.
  • Describe how bones and joints act as levers, and explain the speed-versus-force trade-off of a third-class lever.
  • Use the six naming clues to predict a muscle's location, shape, size, fiber direction, number of heads, or action from its name alone.
  • Distinguish the muscles of facial expression from the muscles of mastication, and name the cranial nerve that drives each group.
  • Identify the major muscles of the trunk — the breathing muscles, the abdominal wall, and the deep back — and state what each does.
  • Name the principal muscles that move the shoulder, arm, and forearm, including the rotator cuff and the classic flexor/extensor pairs.
  • Name the principal muscles that move the hip, knee, and ankle, including the quadriceps, hamstrings, and calf.

Section 1How muscles work together

A muscle can only pull

Here is the single fact that everything in this section is built on: a muscle can only pull, never push. When a muscle contracts, its fibers shorten and draw its two ends toward each other — that is the only trick it has. It cannot lengthen itself to shove a bone away. So how does your forearm both bend up and straighten back down? The answer is that muscles are almost never soloists. They are arranged in opposing teams on either side of a joint, and to reverse a movement the body simply relaxes one team and contracts the other.

To describe that pull precisely, we name a muscle's two ends. The origin is the attachment on the bone that stays relatively still — usually the more proximal, heavier bone. The insertion is the attachment on the bone that actually moves — usually the more distal one. Between them is the thick, contractile middle called the belly. When the muscle shortens, the insertion is dragged toward the origin. Muscles attach to bone through tough, cord-like tendons, or through broad, flat sheets called aponeuroses.

Four roles in every movement

Because muscles cooperate, any single motion is really a small committee at work, and each member has a job. Learn these four roles and you can analyze any movement in the body:

RoleWhat it doesExample (bending the elbow)
Agonist (prime mover)The main muscle producing the movementBiceps brachii flexes the elbow
AntagonistOpposes the agonist; relaxes and stretches as it actsTriceps brachii relaxes to allow flexion
SynergistAssists the agonist or steadies the jointBrachialis helps the biceps flex
FixatorStabilizes the origin so the pull is efficientShoulder muscles hold the scapula still

Notice that the same muscle switches roles depending on the movement. When you bend your elbow, the biceps is the agonist and the triceps is the antagonist; when you straighten it, they swap jobs entirely. There is no such thing as a “flexor muscle” in the abstract — a muscle is a flexor or an extensor only for a particular joint in a particular motion.

Bones are levers, joints are fulcrums

Muscles do not move bones by brute force alone; they work through levers. A lever is a rigid bar that pivots on a fixed point, and the body is full of them: the bone is the bar, the joint is the pivot (the fulcrum), the muscle's pull is the effort, and the weight being moved is the load. The way these three points are arranged decides whether a joint favors power or speed and range.

Most joints in the body are third-class levers, where the effort is applied between the fulcrum and the load — exactly the setup at your elbow, where the biceps inserts just past the joint while the load sits far out at the hand. This arrangement is mechanically “inefficient” for lifting heavy weight, but it buys something more useful: a small, quick muscle shortening produces a large, fast sweep of the hand. Your body traded raw force for speed and reach, which is why you can hurl a ball but strain to curl a heavy dumbbell.

Reciprocal inhibition keeps you smooth. Your nervous system does not just contract the agonist — it actively signals the antagonist to relax at the same time. Without that automatic release, the biceps and triceps would fight each other and your arm would move in jerky spasms. Smooth motion is a duet, not a solo.

Bend and straighten the elbow in the interactive and watch the biceps and triceps trade the agonist and antagonist roles.

Interactive Watch the biceps and triceps take turns as you bend and straighten the elbow, and tap each role. Open full ↑
Key points
  • A muscle can only pull; opposing muscles across a joint reverse each other's actions.
  • The origin is the fixed (usually proximal) attachment; the insertion is the moving (usually distal) one; the insertion is drawn toward the origin.
  • Every movement uses an agonist, antagonist, synergist, and fixator, and muscles swap these roles depending on the motion.
  • Bones act as levers; most body joints are third-class levers that favor speed and range over force.

Section 2How muscles are named

Names are descriptions in disguise

The first time you see words like flexor carpi radialis longus, muscle names feel like a wall of Latin memorization. They are not. Anatomists named muscles the way sailors named knots — by describing them. Almost every muscle name is a compact description of where the muscle is, what it looks like, how big it is, which way its fibers run, how many heads it has, or what it does. Once you know the vocabulary, a name you have never seen becomes a set of instructions for finding and understanding the muscle.

The six clues

There are six kinds of information a name can carry, and many muscles pack several clues into one name:

ClueTells youExample words & muscles
LocationThe body region or nearby bonetemporalis (temporal bone), tibialis anterior (front of tibia)
ShapeThe muscle's outlinedeltoid (triangle), trapezius (trapezoid), rhomboid
SizeRelative size or lengthmaximus/minimus, longus/brevis, major/minor
Fiber directionHow the fibers run relative to the midlinerectus (straight), transversus (across), oblique (angled)
Number of headsHow many origins the muscle hasbiceps (two), triceps (three), quadriceps (four)
ActionThe movement it producesflexor, extensor, adductor, levator, masseter (chewer)

Watch the clues stack. Extensor digitorum longus unpacks to “the long muscle that extends the digits” — action, then body part, then size. Rectus femoris is “the straight muscle of the femur.” Even a monster like flexor carpi radialis is just “the muscle that flexes the wrist (carpus) along the radial side.” You do not have to memorize it as one blob; you read it like a sentence.

Decode the sternocleidomastoid. The longest name in the neck is actually the friendliest: sterno- (sternum) + cleido- (clavicle) + mastoid (the mastoid process behind the ear). The name literally lists the three bones it connects — so you already know where it runs and, roughly, that pulling those points together turns and flexes the head.

Tap each of the six clues in the interactive to see how a name reveals a muscle.

Interactive Learn the six clues and figure out where a muscle is and what it does from its name alone. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Muscle names are descriptions, not arbitrary labels — decode them and you can reason about muscles you never memorized.
  • The six clues: location, shape, size, fiber direction, number of heads, and action.
  • Many names combine several clues; read them left to right like a sentence.

Section 3Muscles of the head and neck

The muscles that make a face

The muscles of facial expression are unlike almost any others in the body, because most of them do not move a bone at all — they insert into the skin. When they contract, they tug the skin of the face into the countless expressions that carry human emotion: a smile, a frown, a raised eyebrow, a wink. This whole group is driven by a single nerve, the facial nerve (cranial nerve VII). Key players include the orbicularis oris, the ring around the mouth that purses your lips to whistle or kiss; the orbicularis oculi, the ring around the eye that closes it to blink and squint; the buccinator in the cheek, which flattens the cheek to blow out a candle and holds food against the teeth while you chew; the zygomaticus, which pulls the corner of the mouth up to smile; and the occipitofrontalis, which raises the eyebrows and wrinkles the forehead.

The muscles that chew

The second job up here is mastication — chewing. Unlike the facial muscles, the muscles of mastication do move a bone: they close and grind the mandible (lower jaw) at the temporomandibular joint. This group answers to a different nerve, the trigeminal nerve (cranial nerve V). The masseter runs from the cheekbone to the angle of the jaw and is, for its size, one of the strongest muscles in the body — you can feel it bulge when you clench your teeth. The fan-shaped temporalis spreads over the side of the skull and helps close and retract the jaw, while the deep pterygoid muscles produce the side-to-side grinding motion.

GroupJobInserts onNerveMembers
Facial expressionMove the skin of the faceSkinFacial (CN VII)Orbicularis oris & oculi, buccinator, zygomaticus, occipitofrontalis
MasticationMove the mandible to chewMandible (bone)Trigeminal (CN V)Masseter, temporalis, pterygoids
NeckMove and support the headSkull & girdleAccessory / cervicalSternocleidomastoid, trapezius

Down in the neck, two large muscles move the head itself. The paired sternocleidomastoid muscles flex the neck when they work together and rotate the head to the opposite side when one works alone. The broad trapezius extends the head and stabilizes the shoulder girdle behind it.

Bell's palsy reads the face like a chart. When the facial nerve (CN VII) is inflamed on one side, every muscle of expression it feeds goes slack: that half of the face droops, the eyelid will not close, and the smile pulls only to the good side. Because CN VII controls expression and CN V controls chewing, a person with Bell's palsy can still bite hard — the trigeminal nerve is untouched.

Tap each muscle in the interactive to light it up and see whether it makes an expression or chews.

Interactive Muscles of facial expression move skin; muscles of mastication chew — tap each to light it up. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Muscles of facial expression insert into skin and are driven by the facial nerve (CN VII).
  • Muscles of mastication move the mandible to chew and are driven by the trigeminal nerve (CN V); the masseter is the strongest for its size.
  • The sternocleidomastoid flexes the neck and rotates the head to the opposite side.

Section 4Muscles of the trunk

The muscles that breathe

The trunk muscles do the body's quiet, essential work — and none is more essential than breathing. The diaphragm is the primary muscle of respiration: a broad dome of muscle that seals off the bottom of the chest. When it contracts it flattens, enlarging the thoracic cavity, dropping the pressure inside, and pulling air into the lungs. Relax it and the dome springs back up, pushing air out. Helping it are the external intercostals, the muscles between the ribs that lift the rib cage during inhalation; the internal intercostals pull the ribs down for forceful exhalation, such as when you cough or blow out a candle.

The abdominal wall and the back

The front and sides of the belly are wrapped by four flat muscles layered like plywood, with their fibers running in different directions for strength. The rectus abdominis — the “six-pack” — runs straight up the midline and flexes the vertebral column (the motion of a sit-up). Over the sides run the external oblique, the internal oblique, and, deepest of all, the transversus abdominis, whose fibers wrap horizontally like a corset. Together this wall compresses the abdomen, bends and twists the trunk, protects the soft organs behind it, and boosts the pressure needed for coughing, childbirth, and moving the bowels.

Along the back, the erector spinae — a set of three muscle columns running the length of the spine — do the opposite of the abdominals: they extend the vertebral column to straighten you up from a bend and hold you upright against gravity all day long.

RegionMusclesMain action
BreathingDiaphragm, external & internal intercostalsChange chest volume to move air in and out
Abdominal wallRectus abdominis, external & internal obliques, transversus abdominisFlex/rotate trunk, compress and protect abdomen
Deep backErector spinae groupExtend the spine and maintain upright posture
Pelvic floorLevator ani and companionsSupport pelvic organs; control continence
“C3, 4, 5 keep the diaphragm alive.” The diaphragm is powered by the phrenic nerve, which arises from spinal cord segments C3–C5 in the neck. That is why a spinal cord injury above this level can stop a person from breathing on their own, while an injury below it usually spares breathing — the nerve to the diaphragm leaves the cord surprisingly high up.

Tap each trunk muscle in the interactive to see whether it breathes, bends, or protects.

Interactive The trunk muscles breathe, protect the organs, and bend and twist the torso — tap each. Open full ↑
Key points
  • The diaphragm is the primary breathing muscle; contracting it flattens the dome and pulls air in.
  • Four layered abdominal muscles — rectus abdominis plus the two obliques and transversus abdominis — flex and rotate the trunk and protect the viscera.
  • The erector spinae extends the spine and holds posture; the diaphragm runs on the phrenic nerve (C3–C5).

Section 5Muscles of the upper limb

Anchoring and moving the shoulder

The upper limb is built for reach and dexterity, and it starts at the shoulder — the most mobile, and therefore least stable, joint in the body. Several large muscles anchor the shoulder blade and swing the arm. The trapezius and serratus anterior hold and rotate the scapula so it can serve as a moving base. The fan-shaped pectoralis major on the chest pulls the arm forward and across the body, while the broad latissimus dorsi on the back pulls it down and behind — the muscle of a swimmer's stroke. Capping the shoulder is the deltoid, the triangular muscle that abducts the arm, lifting it out to the side.

Wrapped deep around the joint itself is the rotator cuff, a set of four small muscles — supraspinatus, infraspinatus, teres minor, and subscapularis (the “SITS” group). They do not generate big movements; their job is to hold the ball of the humerus snugly in its shallow socket while the larger muscles move the arm.

The arm and forearm

The arm proper (shoulder to elbow) hosts the classic agonist/antagonist pair. On the front, the biceps brachii flexes the elbow and turns the palm up (supination), aided beneath it by the brachialis. On the back, the triceps brachii — the only major muscle there — extends the elbow to straighten the arm. Cross the elbow into the forearm and you find two opposing compartments: a group of flexors on the palm side that curl the wrist and fingers, and a group of extensors on the back side that straighten them. Their long tendons run down into the hand, which is why so much of the power for gripping actually comes from muscle bellies up in the forearm.

MuscleLocationMain action
DeltoidCap of the shoulderAbducts the arm
Pectoralis majorAnterior chestFlexes and adducts the arm
Latissimus dorsiBroad of the backExtends and adducts the arm
Rotator cuff (SITS)Deep around the shoulderStabilizes the humeral head
Biceps brachiiAnterior armFlexes elbow, supinates forearm
Triceps brachiiPosterior armExtends the elbow
The deltoid is the shot muscle — and “SITS” is worth memorizing. The thick, well-vascularized deltoid is the standard site for many injections in adults. And the rotator cuff spells itself: Supraspinatus, Infraspinatus, Teres minor, Subscapularis. The supraspinatus is the one most often torn, because it is pinched under the bony arch of the shoulder every time you raise your arm.

Tap each muscle in the interactive to find the shoulder, arm, and forearm movers and their pairs.

Interactive The muscles that move the shoulder, arm, and hand — and the classic agonist/antagonist pairs. Open full ↑
Key points
  • The deltoid abducts the arm; pectoralis major and latissimus dorsi pull it forward and back.
  • The rotator cuff (SITS) stabilizes the loose shoulder joint; the supraspinatus tears most often.
  • At the elbow, biceps brachii flexes and supinates while triceps brachii extends; forearm flexors and extensors move the wrist and fingers by long tendons.

Section 6Muscles of the lower limb

The hip and the buttock

The lower limb carries the whole weight of the body and drives every step, so it holds the largest, strongest muscles you own. At the hip, the gluteus maximus — the bulk of the buttock and the single largest muscle in the body — powerfully extends the hip to drive you up from a squat, up stairs, and into a run. Beneath and to the side sit the gluteus medius and gluteus minimus, which abduct the thigh and, crucially, steady the pelvis every time you stand on one leg to take a step. On the front, the deep iliopsoas is the chief hip flexor, lifting the thigh toward the chest.

The thigh and the leg

The thigh is organized into opposing groups. On the front, the quadriceps femoris — four muscles (rectus femoris and the three vastus muscles) sharing one tendon over the kneecap — extend the knee to straighten the leg for a kick or a step. On the back, the three hamstrings (biceps femoris, semitendinosus, semimembranosus) do the reverse: they flex the knee and help extend the hip. On the inner thigh, the adductors squeeze the legs together.

Below the knee, the calf provides the push-off. The two-headed gastrocnemius and the deeper soleus join into the Achilles (calcaneal) tendon and plantarflex the ankle — they point the toes and lift the heel, launching you into every step and jump. Opposing them on the shin, the tibialis anterior dorsiflexes the ankle, pulling the toes up to clear the ground as your foot swings forward.

Muscle / groupLocationMain action
Gluteus maximusButtockExtends the hip (running, stairs)
Gluteus medius & minimusLateral hipAbduct thigh; stabilize pelvis when walking
IliopsoasDeep anterior hipFlexes the hip
Quadriceps femorisAnterior thighExtends the knee
HamstringsPosterior thighFlex the knee, extend the hip
Gastrocnemius & soleusCalfPlantarflex the ankle (push off)
Tibialis anteriorAnterior leg (shin)Dorsiflexes the ankle
The gluteus medius keeps you level. When you lift one foot to step, the gluteus medius on the standing leg contracts to keep the pelvis from dropping on the swinging side. If that muscle is weak, the hip sags with each step — the tell-tale “Trendelenburg” waddle. The muscle you never think about is the one that keeps your walk from tipping over.

Tap each muscle in the interactive to trace the movers of the hip, knee, and ankle.

Interactive The biggest, strongest muscles in the body move your hips, knees, and ankles — tap each. Open full ↑
Key points
  • The gluteus maximus extends the hip; the gluteus medius/minimus abduct the thigh and stabilize the pelvis in walking.
  • The quadriceps extend the knee and the hamstrings flex it — a front/back antagonist pair; the iliopsoas flexes the hip.
  • The gastrocnemius and soleus plantarflex the ankle through the Achilles tendon; the tibialis anterior dorsiflexes it.

The chapter in one breath

  • A skeletal muscle can only pull, so muscles work in opposing pairs across joints, drawing the insertion toward the fixed origin.
  • Every movement has an agonist, antagonist, synergist, and fixator, and bones act as levers — most of them third-class levers built for speed and range.
  • Muscle names are descriptions coded from six clues: location, shape, size, fiber direction, number of heads, and action.
  • The head carries muscles of facial expression (insert into skin, CN VII) and muscles of mastication (move the mandible, CN V).
  • The trunk holds the diaphragm and intercostals for breathing, the four-layer abdominal wall, and the erector spinae for posture.
  • The upper limb is moved by the deltoid, pectoralis major, latissimus dorsi, the stabilizing rotator cuff (SITS), and the biceps/triceps pair.
  • The lower limb's powerhouses are the glutes, the quadriceps and hamstrings, and the calf muscles that plantarflex through the Achilles tendon.

ReferenceWords worth knowing

A quick reference for the terms in this chapter — skim it now, or circle back whenever a word trips you up.

Abduction
Movement of a limb away from the midline of the body (the deltoid abducts the arm).
Adduction
Movement of a limb toward the midline of the body (the thigh adductors squeeze the legs together).
Agonist (prime mover)
The muscle chiefly responsible for producing a particular movement.
Antagonist
The muscle that opposes an agonist, relaxing and lengthening while the agonist contracts.
Aponeurosis
A broad, flat sheet of connective tissue that attaches a muscle to bone or to another muscle.
Belly
The thick, contractile middle portion of a muscle between its origin and insertion.
Diaphragm
The dome-shaped primary muscle of breathing that separates the thoracic and abdominal cavities; powered by the phrenic nerve.
Erector spinae
The group of deep back muscles that extend the vertebral column and maintain upright posture.
Fixator
A muscle that stabilizes the origin (or a nearby joint) so a prime mover can act efficiently.
Insertion
The attachment of a muscle on the bone that moves — usually the more distal bone.
Intercostal muscles
The muscles between the ribs; externals lift the rib cage to inhale, internals pull it down for forced exhalation.
Lever
A rigid bar (a bone) that pivots on a fulcrum (a joint) to move a load; most body levers are third-class.
Masseter
A powerful muscle of mastication running from the cheekbone to the angle of the jaw; closes the mandible.
Mastication
Chewing — the grinding of food produced by muscles that move the mandible, supplied by the trigeminal nerve (CN V).
Muscles of facial expression
Muscles that insert into the skin of the face to create expressions; supplied by the facial nerve (CN VII).
Origin
The attachment of a muscle on the bone that stays relatively fixed — usually the more proximal bone.
Quadriceps femoris
The four-headed anterior thigh muscle group that extends the knee.
Rotator cuff
Four muscles (supraspinatus, infraspinatus, teres minor, subscapularis — “SITS”) that stabilize the shoulder joint.
Sternocleidomastoid
A neck muscle connecting the sternum, clavicle, and mastoid process; flexes the neck and rotates the head.
Synergist
A muscle that assists an agonist or stabilizes a joint during a movement.
Tendon
A tough, cord-like band of dense connective tissue that attaches a muscle to a bone.

Check yourself

1. A student claims the biceps “pushes” the forearm back down when you lower a weight. Correct the statement, and explain what actually straightens the elbow.
Muscles can only pull, never push. The biceps cannot push anything; it relaxes and lengthens while the triceps on the back of the arm contracts and pulls the elbow into extension. Reversing a movement always means relaxing one team and contracting the opposing one.
2. Define origin and insertion, and state which one moves toward the other when a muscle contracts.
The origin is the fixed (usually proximal) attachment; the insertion is the moving (usually distal) attachment; the insertion moves toward the origin. When the muscle shortens, it drags the movable bone (insertion) toward the anchored bone (origin).
3. When you flex your elbow, name the agonist, the antagonist, and one synergist.
Agonist = biceps brachii; antagonist = triceps brachii; synergist = brachialis. The biceps produces the flexion, the triceps relaxes to allow it, and the brachialis assists. Note the same muscles swap roles when you extend the elbow.
4. Most body joints are third-class levers. What does the body gain, and what does it give up, with this arrangement?
It gains speed and range of motion but gives up mechanical force. In a third-class lever the effort is applied between the fulcrum and the load, so a small, quick muscle shortening sweeps the far end of the bone a long way fast — great for reaching and throwing, poor for lifting heavy loads.
5. Decode the muscle name “extensor digitorum longus” into plain English, and name which naming clues it uses.
“The long muscle that extends the digits (fingers or toes).” It combines action (extensor), location/body part (digitorum = digits), and size (longus = long). Reading a name as a sentence tells you what the muscle does and roughly where it is.
6. What three bony landmarks are hidden in the name “sternocleidomastoid,” and what does this tell you about the muscle?
The sternum, the clavicle (cleido-), and the mastoid process. The name lists the muscle's attachments, so you know it runs from the front of the chest up to behind the ear — a path that lets it flex the neck and rotate the head to the opposite side.
7. Muscles of facial expression and muscles of mastication differ in what they attach to and which nerve controls them. Contrast the two.
Facial-expression muscles insert into skin and run on the facial nerve (CN VII); mastication muscles move the mandible and run on the trigeminal nerve (CN V). That is why a facial-nerve palsy droops the face but leaves chewing strength intact.
8. Describe exactly what the diaphragm does to draw air into the lungs, and name the nerve and spinal levels that power it.
When the diaphragm contracts it flattens, enlarging the thoracic cavity and lowering the pressure so air flows in; it is driven by the phrenic nerve from C3–C5. Relaxation lets the dome rise and pushes air out. Because the phrenic nerve arises high in the neck, a cord injury above C3 can stop breathing.
9. Name the four muscles of the abdominal wall and two functions they share.
Rectus abdominis, external oblique, internal oblique, and transversus abdominis. Together they flex and rotate the trunk, compress and protect the abdominal organs, and raise abdominal pressure for coughing, childbirth, and defecation.
10. What is the rotator cuff, which muscles form it, and what is its main job?
Four deep shoulder muscles — supraspinatus, infraspinatus, teres minor, subscapularis (“SITS”) — that stabilize the humeral head in its shallow socket. They hold the joint together rather than producing large movements; the supraspinatus is the one most often torn.
11. The quadriceps and hamstrings are an antagonist pair at the knee. State the action of each group.
The quadriceps (anterior thigh) extend the knee; the hamstrings (posterior thigh) flex the knee and also extend the hip. Straightening the leg contracts the quads while the hamstrings relax, and bending the knee does the reverse.
12. Which calf muscles plantarflex the ankle, what tendon do they share, and which muscle opposes them by dorsiflexing?
The gastrocnemius and soleus plantarflex the ankle through the Achilles (calcaneal) tendon; the tibialis anterior dorsiflexes it. Plantarflexion points the toes and lifts the heel for push-off; dorsiflexion pulls the toes up to clear the ground during the swing of a step.
Anatomy & Physiology · Chapter 12

The Nervous System & Nervous Tissue

Every thought you have ever had, every muscle you have ever moved, and every sensation you have ever felt was carried by a wave of electricity racing along a living wire. This chapter is the story of that wire — the neuron — and the tissue it lives in. We will map how the whole nervous system is organized, meet the loyal support cells that keep neurons alive, and then get to the heart of the matter: how a neuron builds an electrical charge, fires an action potential, sends it flying down a myelinated axon, and hands the message to the next cell across a microscopic gap. Master these ideas and the rest of neuroscience — the brain, the spinal cord, reflexes, the senses — becomes a set of variations on one theme.

By the end of this chapter you can

  • Divide the nervous system into its central and peripheral parts and into its sensory, motor, somatic, and autonomic pathways.
  • State the nervous system's three core jobs — sensory input, integration, and motor output — and give an example of each.
  • Name the parts of a neuron and the six neuroglia, and say what each glial cell does and where it lives.
  • Explain how the resting membrane potential of about −70 mV is created and maintained.
  • Walk through the phases of an action potential and explain why it is all-or-nothing and one-way.
  • Contrast continuous and saltatory conduction and list the two factors that set conduction speed.
  • Describe the steps of chemical synaptic transmission and tell an excitatory signal from an inhibitory one.
  • Connect each mechanism to a real clinical picture — multiple sclerosis, local anesthetics, nerve gas, and more.

Section 1How the nervous system is organized

One control center, wires everywhere

The nervous system is the body's fast communication network, and anatomists split it two different ways — by where the parts are and by which direction the traffic flows. By location there are just two divisions. The central nervous system (CNS) is the brain and spinal cord — the headquarters where information is processed and decisions are made. The peripheral nervous system (PNS) is everything else: the nerves and ganglia that fan out from the CNS to reach every corner of the body. Think of the CNS as the computer and the PNS as the cables running to every sensor and every motor.

Three jobs: sense, decide, act

No matter how complicated a nervous pathway looks, it is always doing one of three things. First comes sensory input — receptors detect a change, inside the body or out, and send that news toward the CNS. Second is integration — the CNS processes and interprets the incoming signals and decides what, if anything, to do. Third is motor output — a command travels away from the CNS to an effector (a muscle or a gland) that carries out the response. Touch a hot stove and all three fire in a heartbeat: skin receptors sense heat (input), the spinal cord decides "pull back" (integration), and arm muscles yank the hand away (output).

The words for these directions are worth memorizing because they appear on every diagram. Neurons carrying signals toward the CNS are afferent (sensory); neurons carrying commands away from the CNS are efferent (motor). The efferent side splits again by target. The somatic nervous system carries voluntary commands to skeletal muscle — the movements you choose. The autonomic nervous system (ANS) runs the automatic housekeeping, sending signals to cardiac muscle, smooth muscle, and glands without your conscious say-so. And the ANS itself has two arms that usually oppose each other: the sympathetic division (the "fight-or-flight" accelerator) and the parasympathetic division (the "rest-and-digest" brake).

DivisionWhat it includesDirection / job
Central (CNS)Brain and spinal cordIntegration — processes input, issues commands
Peripheral (PNS)Nerves and gangliaCarries signals to and from the CNS
Sensory (afferent)Sensory neuronsToward the CNS — input
Motor (efferent)Motor neuronsAway from the CNS — output
SomaticMotor neurons to skeletal muscleVoluntary movement
Autonomic (ANS)Motor neurons to cardiac/smooth muscle & glandsInvoluntary control
  SympatheticThoracolumbar outflowFight-or-flight (accelerate)
  ParasympatheticCraniosacral outflowRest-and-digest (calm)
Afferent Arrives, Efferent Exits. The two words differ by a single letter and students mix them up for a lifetime. Anchor them with the vowel: Afferent signals Arrive at the CNS; Efferent signals Exit toward muscles and glands.

Tap through each division — central and peripheral, sensory and motor, somatic and autonomic — in the interactive below.
