Interactive Split the nervous system by anatomy (central vs peripheral) and by traffic direction (in vs out). Open full ↑
Key points
  • By location: the CNS is the brain and spinal cord; the PNS is the nerves and ganglia.
  • By function: afferent (sensory) signals arrive at the CNS; efferent (motor) signals exit toward effectors.
  • The motor side divides into somatic (voluntary, skeletal muscle) and autonomic (involuntary), and the autonomic into sympathetic and parasympathetic.
  • Every nervous act reduces to three steps: sensory input → integration → motor output.

Section 2Neurons and their support crew

The neuron: a cell built to signal

Before we meet the support cells, meet the star. A neuron is an excitable cell shaped for one purpose — to receive a signal at one end and pass it along from the other. Three parts do the work. The dendrites are branching antennae that receive incoming signals. The cell body (or soma) holds the nucleus and does the cell's living and housekeeping; it is also where incoming signals are added up. The axon is a single long fiber that carries the outgoing signal away, often wrapped in a fatty myelin sheath and ending in axon terminals that hand the message to the next cell. Neurons are also amitotic — most cannot divide to replace themselves — which is exactly why damage to the brain and spinal cord is so hard to repair.

The support crew: six kinds of glia

For every neuron there are roughly as many, or more, neuroglia (or glial cells) — the crew that feeds, insulates, cleans, and protects. Glia do not fire action potentials, but without them neurons would starve, short-circuit, and drown in debris. There are six types: four live in the CNS and two in the PNS.

Glial cellWhereJob
AstrocyteCNSMost abundant; anchors neurons to blood supply, controls the chemical environment, helps form the blood–brain barrier
OligodendrocyteCNSWraps CNS axons in myelin (one cell myelinates many axons)
MicrogliaCNSThe brain's immune cells — engulf pathogens and dead cells
Ependymal cellCNSLine the brain's cavities; circulate cerebrospinal fluid
Satellite cellPNSSurround and support neuron cell bodies in ganglia
Schwann cellPNSWraps PNS axons in myelin (one cell myelinates one segment)

Two cells make myelin — keep them straight

The two myelinating cells cause endless confusion, so pin them down now. In the CNS, an oligodendrocyte reaches out several arms and myelinates many different axons at once. In the PNS, a Schwann cell wraps itself around a single segment of one axon like a jelly roll. Both make the same fatty insulation, but the difference matters clinically: diseases that attack CNS myelin (multiple sclerosis) and those that attack PNS myelin (Guillain–Barré syndrome) are entirely separate conditions with different courses.

Microglia are the brain's cleanup crew. Because the blood–brain barrier keeps most immune cells out, the CNS grows its own defenders. Microglia patrol constantly, and when they detect injury or infection they swarm, engulf debris, and sound the alarm — which is why they are central to the story of nearly every brain disease.

Tap each of the six glial cells to see where it sits and what it does.

Interactive Six neuroglia — four in the CNS, two in the PNS — that feed, insulate, clean, and protect neurons. Open full ↑
Key points
  • A neuron has three parts: dendrites (receive), cell body/soma (integrate and live), and axon (transmit).
  • The six glia: CNS — astrocyte, oligodendrocyte, microglia, ependymal; PNS — satellite, Schwann.
  • Oligodendrocytes myelinate many CNS axons; Schwann cells each myelinate one PNS segment.
  • Microglia are the CNS's immune cells; astrocytes are the most abundant glia and help form the blood–brain barrier.

Section 3The resting membrane potential

A charged battery, waiting

A neuron at rest is not doing nothing — it is armed. Across its membrane sits a tiny voltage called the resting membrane potential, about −70 mV, meaning the inside of the cell is 70 millivolts negative compared with the outside. A membrane that holds this separation of charge is said to be polarized, like a charged battery ready to be tapped. Everything a neuron does — every signal — is a brief, controlled discharge of that stored charge. So the first question of all neurophysiology is simply: how does the cell build the charge in the first place?

Why the inside is negative

The answer is unequal ion distribution. The cell carefully keeps different ions at different concentrations inside versus outside. Sodium (Na⁺) is piled up outside the cell; potassium (K⁺) is concentrated inside. In addition, the inside is stuffed with large negatively charged proteins and phosphates that are simply too big to leave. Now add the membrane's channels. At rest the membrane is far leakier to potassium than to sodium — there are many more open leak channels for K⁺. So potassium trickles out down its gradient, carrying positive charge with it and leaving the inside more negative. That potassium leak, working against the trapped internal anions, is the main reason the resting voltage lands near −70 mV.

IonConcentrated whereRole at rest
Sodium (Na⁺)Outside the cellPoised to rush in; membrane mostly closed to it at rest
Potassium (K⁺)Inside the cellLeaks out through many open channels — sets the resting voltage
Chloride (Cl⁻)Outside the cellContributes to the negative interior
Large anions (proteins)Trapped insideCannot cross — hold negative charge in

The pump that never sleeps

Leaks would eventually run the gradients down — sodium would trickle in, potassium would drift out, and the battery would go flat. To prevent that, the membrane runs the sodium–potassium pump (Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase) around the clock. Burning ATP for fuel, it pumps 3 Na⁺ out for every 2 K⁺ in, restoring both gradients continuously. Because it moves three positives out for every two brought in, the pump also removes a little net positive charge, nudging the interior slightly more negative. This pump is so essential that it consumes a large share of the body's resting energy budget — a big reason the brain is such a hungry organ.

Potassium is the voltage-setter. It is tempting to credit the sodium–potassium pump for the resting potential, but the pump mainly maintains the gradients. The −70 mV itself comes chiefly from potassium leaking out through open K⁺ channels. That is why disorders of blood potassium (hyper- and hypokalemia) so quickly disturb the heart and nerves.

Explore the ion gradients, the leak channels, and the tireless pump in the interactive below.

Interactive A resting neuron is a charged battery at about −70 mV — see the gradients, channels, and pump that build it. Open full ↑
Key points
  • The resting membrane potential is about −70 mV, inside negative — the membrane is polarized.
  • It arises from unequal ion distribution: Na⁺ high outside, K⁺ high inside, large anions trapped inside.
  • Potassium leaking out through open channels is the main setter of the resting voltage.
  • The Na⁺/K⁺ pump (3 out, 2 in, using ATP) maintains the gradients and adds a small negative bias.

Section 4The action potential

All or nothing

An action potential is the neuron's signal — a brief, dramatic reversal of the membrane voltage that shoots down the axon. It does not happen unless the membrane is pushed from its resting −70 mV up to a critical threshold of about −55 mV. Below threshold, nothing fires. Reach threshold and a full-sized spike goes off every time — the same height whether the stimulus was barely enough or enormous. This is the famous all-or-nothing law: a neuron cannot fire a partial action potential any more than a gun can fire a partial bullet. Strength is coded not by bigger spikes but by how often the neuron fires.

The upstroke: sodium floods in

The whole event is run by voltage-gated channels — ion gates that open in response to voltage. When threshold is reached, voltage-gated Na⁺ channels snap open and sodium, which was piled up outside and pulled inward by both its concentration gradient and the negative interior, floods in. Each entering positive charge makes the inside less negative, which opens still more sodium gates — a fast, self-amplifying rush called depolarization that drives the voltage from −55 all the way up to about +30 mV. For an instant, the inside of the cell is actually positive.

The downstroke and the reset

The spike ends as fast as it began. At the peak the Na⁺ channels inactivate (they auto-close), and now the slower voltage-gated K⁺ channels finish opening. Potassium rushes out, carrying positive charge away and dropping the voltage back down — this is repolarization. The potassium gates are sluggish to close, so a little too much K⁺ leaves and the membrane briefly dips below rest to about −80 mV, a hyperpolarization or undershoot. Finally the leak channels and the Na⁺/K⁺ pump restore the tidy −70 mV. During the recovery there is a refractory period when the axon either cannot fire at all (absolute) or needs an unusually strong stimulus (relative).

PhaseWhat the channels doVoltage
RestingOnly leak channels open−70 mV
ThresholdEnough Na⁺ gates begin to open−55 mV
DepolarizationVoltage-gated Na⁺ channels open; Na⁺ rushes inrising to +30 mV
RepolarizationNa⁺ channels inactivate; K⁺ channels open, K⁺ exitsfalling back down
HyperpolarizationK⁺ channels close slowly — slight overshoot~−80 mV
Return to restLeak channels + Na⁺/K⁺ pump restore balance−70 mV
The refractory period makes signals one-way. Right after a stretch of membrane fires, its sodium channels are inactivated and briefly cannot reopen. That forces the action potential to keep marching forward into fresh membrane rather than backward, and it caps how fast a neuron can fire — a built-in speed limit that protects the code.

Hit "Stimulate" and follow the voltage through every phase in the interactive below.

Interactive Reach threshold at −55 mV and fire an all-or-nothing spike: sodium in, then potassium out. Open full ↑
Key points
  • An action potential fires only if the membrane reaches threshold (~−55 mV), and then it is all-or-nothing.
  • Depolarization = voltage-gated Na⁺ channels open, Na⁺ floods in, voltage climbs to ~+30 mV.
  • Repolarization = Na⁺ channels inactivate and K⁺ channels open, K⁺ exits, voltage falls; a brief hyperpolarization follows.
  • The refractory period keeps the signal moving one way and limits firing rate; stimulus strength is coded by frequency.

Section 5Saltatory conduction & speed

Crawling versus leaping

Once an action potential is born, it has to travel the length of the axon, and there are two ways to do that. On a bare, unmyelinated axon the spike regenerates itself at every adjacent patch of membrane, marching along step by step — continuous conduction. It works, but it is slow. On a myelinated axon the fatty sheath insulates long stretches so the current cannot leak out there; the action potential can only regenerate at the bare gaps between myelin segments. So the signal appears to jump from gap to gap, skipping the insulated lengths in between. This leaping is saltatory conduction, from the Latin saltare, "to leap," and it is dramatically faster.

Nodes of Ranvier

The bare gaps have a name worth knowing: the nodes of Ranvier. These little exposed rings are packed with voltage-gated sodium channels, so the action potential is regenerated fresh and full-strength at each node. Between the nodes, the insulated segment (the internode) carries the current quickly and passively, with no channels to slow it down. The result is that the impulse effectively hops from node to node — regenerate, glide, regenerate, glide — covering far more ground per unit time than a continuous crawl.

What sets conduction speed

Two features determine how fast a fiber conducts. The first is myelination: a myelinated axon massively outpaces an unmyelinated one of the same size. The second is axon diameter: fatter axons conduct faster because they offer less resistance to current, the same way a wide pipe moves water faster than a narrow one. The body uses both dials deliberately — the large, heavily myelinated fibers that carry reflexes and limb-position sense can conduct at speeds over 100 meters per second, while the thin, unmyelinated fibers that carry dull, aching pain crawl along at barely one. (Sharp, localized pain travels on thinner myelinated fibers — faster than the dull, unmyelinated kind, but far short of that top speed.)

FactorFaster conductionSlower conduction
MyelinMyelinated (saltatory)Unmyelinated (continuous)
Axon diameterLarge diameterSmall diameter
Typical roleReflexes, sharp/localized pain, positionDull aching pain, temperature, autonomic
Strip the myelin and the signal fails. In multiple sclerosis, the immune system destroys CNS myelin; without insulation the current leaks away and conduction slows or stops, producing weakness, numbness, and vision loss. In Guillain–Barré syndrome the same thing happens to PNS myelin, causing ascending paralysis. Myelin is not optional — it is the difference between a nerve that works and one that doesn't.

Race a myelinated signal against an unmyelinated one and watch it leap the nodes in the interactive below.

Interactive A myelinated signal leaps node to node, skipping the insulated stretches — watch two signals race. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Continuous conduction regenerates the spike at every patch (slow, unmyelinated); saltatory conduction leaps between gaps (fast, myelinated).
  • The bare gaps are the nodes of Ranvier, packed with Na⁺ channels; the insulated internodes carry current passively.
  • Speed rises with myelination and with axon diameter.
  • Demyelinating disease (MS in the CNS, Guillain–Barré in the PNS) slows or blocks conduction.

Section 6The synapse: passing the message on

Neurons don't quite touch

When an action potential reaches the end of an axon, it faces a problem: the next cell is not connected to it. There is a microscopic gap in between called the synaptic cleft, and the junction as a whole is the synapse. The neuron sending the message is presynaptic; the cell receiving it is postsynaptic. Because the electrical signal cannot jump the gap on its own, most synapses convert it into a chemical messenger — a neurotransmitter — that drifts across and re-creates the signal on the far side. Electrical, then chemical, then electrical again.

Six steps across the gap

Chemical transmission always follows the same choreography. (1) The action potential arrives at the presynaptic axon terminal. (2) Its voltage opens voltage-gated calcium (Ca²⁺) channels, and calcium flows into the terminal. (3) Calcium triggers tiny sacs called synaptic vesicles to fuse with the membrane and dump their contents. (4) Neurotransmitter is released by exocytosis and diffuses across the cleft. (5) It binds receptors on the postsynaptic membrane, opening ion channels that create a small graded potential. (6) The neurotransmitter is quickly cleared — broken down by enzymes, taken back up (reuptake), or diffused away — so the signal is crisp and brief.

Excitatory or inhibitory?

What the postsynaptic cell does next depends entirely on which neurotransmitter and receptor are involved. An excitatory signal depolarizes the postsynaptic membrane, nudging it toward threshold — an excitatory postsynaptic potential (EPSP). An inhibitory signal hyperpolarizes it, pushing it away from threshold — an inhibitory postsynaptic potential (IPSP). A single neuron receives thousands of these at once and sums them; only if the excitation outweighs the inhibition enough to reach threshold does it fire. The synapse, in other words, is where the nervous system actually makes decisions.

NeurotransmitterTypical effectWhere / note
Acetylcholine (ACh)Excites skeletal muscleNeuromuscular junction; broken down by acetylcholinesterase
GlutamateExcitatoryThe main excitatory transmitter of the CNS
GABAInhibitoryThe main inhibitory transmitter of the CNS
GlycineInhibitoryCommon in the spinal cord
NorepinephrineExcites or inhibitsSympathetic (fight-or-flight), mood, arousal
DopamineModulatoryReward, movement; disrupted in Parkinson disease
The synapse is where the drugs act. Because transmission has so many steps, it is easy to sabotage — and most medicines and poisons do exactly that. Nerve gases block acetylcholinesterase so ACh never clears; many antidepressants block serotonin reuptake; caffeine, opioids, and anesthetics all work at synapses. Learn this handoff and you have the key to pharmacology.

Step through the electrical-to-chemical-to-electrical handoff in the interactive below.

Interactive An electrical signal becomes chemical across the cleft, then electrical again — step through the handoff. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Neurons meet at a synapse across the synaptic cleft; the sender is presynaptic, the receiver postsynaptic.
  • Transmission: AP arrives → Ca²⁺ enters → vesicles fuse → neurotransmitter released → binds receptors → cleared.
  • An EPSP pushes the postsynaptic cell toward threshold; an IPSP pushes it away; the cell sums them to decide whether to fire.
  • Key transmitters: ACh (muscle), glutamate (main excitatory), GABA (main inhibitory); the synapse is where most drugs act.

The chapter in one breath

  • The nervous system splits into the CNS (brain and spinal cord) and PNS (nerves and ganglia), and its work is always sensory input → integration → motor output.
  • Motor pathways divide into somatic (voluntary) and autonomic (involuntary, split into sympathetic and parasympathetic); afferent arrives, efferent exits.
  • A neuron (dendrites, soma, axon) is supported by six neuroglia — astrocyte, oligodendrocyte, microglia, ependymal (CNS) and satellite, Schwann (PNS).
  • At rest the membrane is polarized to ~−70 mV, set by K⁺ leaking out and trapped internal anions, and maintained by the Na⁺/K⁺ pump.
  • Reaching threshold (~−55 mV) fires an all-or-nothing action potential: Na⁺ in (depolarize) then K⁺ out (repolarize), with a refractory period.
  • Myelin lets the impulse leap node to node — saltatory conduction — and speed rises with myelination and diameter.
  • At the synapse, the signal crosses as a neurotransmitter; the postsynaptic cell sums EPSPs and IPSPs to decide whether to fire.

ReferenceWords worth knowing

A quick reference for the terms in this chapter — skim it now, or circle back whenever a word trips you up.

Action potential
A brief, all-or-nothing reversal of membrane voltage that travels down an axon; the neuron's electrical signal.
Afferent (sensory) neuron
A neuron that carries signals toward the central nervous system (afferent arrives).
All-or-nothing
The principle that a neuron either fires a full-sized action potential or none at all; stimulus strength is coded by firing frequency.
Astrocyte
The most abundant CNS glial cell; anchors neurons to blood vessels, regulates the chemical environment, and helps form the blood–brain barrier.
Autonomic nervous system
The involuntary motor division controlling cardiac muscle, smooth muscle, and glands; split into sympathetic and parasympathetic arms.
Axon
The single long fiber of a neuron that carries the action potential away from the cell body.
Depolarization
A shift of the membrane voltage toward positive (less negative), driven mainly by Na⁺ entering the cell.
Efferent (motor) neuron
A neuron that carries commands away from the central nervous system to an effector (efferent exits).
Myelin sheath
Fatty insulation wrapped around an axon (by oligodendrocytes in the CNS, Schwann cells in the PNS) that speeds conduction.
Neuroglia
The support cells of nervous tissue that feed, insulate, clean, and protect neurons; six types across the CNS and PNS.
Neuron
The excitable signaling cell of the nervous system, with dendrites (receive), a cell body (integrate), and an axon (transmit).
Neurotransmitter
A chemical messenger released across a synapse to relay a signal to the next cell.
Node of Ranvier
A bare gap between myelin segments, rich in Na⁺ channels, where an action potential is regenerated during saltatory conduction.
Refractory period
The brief interval after a spike when a region of membrane cannot fire again (absolute) or needs a stronger stimulus (relative), forcing one-way conduction.
Repolarization
The return of membrane voltage toward its negative resting value, driven mainly by K⁺ leaving the cell.
Resting membrane potential
The voltage (~−70 mV, inside negative) across a resting neuron's membrane, set by ion gradients and K⁺ leak.
Saltatory conduction
Fast impulse transmission along a myelinated axon in which the signal leaps from node to node.
Schwann cell
The PNS glial cell that wraps a single axon segment in myelin.
Sodium–potassium pump
An ATP-powered membrane transporter that moves 3 Na⁺ out and 2 K⁺ in, maintaining the ion gradients.
Synapse
The junction where one neuron communicates with another cell across the synaptic cleft.
Threshold
The membrane voltage (~−55 mV) that must be reached to trigger an action potential.

Check yourself

1. Name the two anatomical divisions of the nervous system and state which structures belong to each.
The central nervous system (CNS) and the peripheral nervous system (PNS). The CNS is the brain and spinal cord, where integration happens; the PNS is the nerves and ganglia that carry signals to and from the CNS.
2. A signal traveling toward the spinal cord from a pain receptor is carried by which kind of neuron, and what does the memory trick tell you?
An afferent (sensory) neuron. Afferent Arrives at the CNS; efferent Exits toward muscles and glands. Sensory input always travels afferently.
3. Which glial cell myelinates axons in the CNS, and which does the same job in the PNS? How do their coverage patterns differ?
Oligodendrocytes (CNS) and Schwann cells (PNS). One oligodendrocyte myelinates many axons at once; each Schwann cell myelinates only a single segment of one axon.
4. The brain is largely walled off from the bloodstream, yet it still needs immune defense. Which glial cell provides it, and what does it do?
Microglia — the CNS's resident immune cells. They patrol the tissue and engulf pathogens and dead cells, taking on the defensive role that circulating immune cells play elsewhere.
5. What is the approximate resting membrane potential, and which single ion's movement is chiefly responsible for setting it?
About −70 mV, and potassium (K⁺) leaking out. The membrane is far more permeable to K⁺ at rest; potassium exits down its gradient, leaving the inside negative. The Na⁺/K⁺ pump maintains the gradients but is not the main voltage-setter.
6. Describe what the sodium–potassium pump moves and why it slightly increases the negativity inside the cell.
It pumps 3 Na⁺ out for every 2 K⁺ in, using ATP. Because it removes three positive charges for every two it brings in, there is a small net loss of positive charge from the interior, nudging it a bit more negative — on top of restoring both gradients.
7. Explain what "all-or-nothing" means for an action potential, and how a neuron therefore signals a stronger stimulus.
If threshold (~−55 mV) is reached, a full-sized spike fires; below threshold, nothing. Spikes are always the same amplitude, so a stronger stimulus is coded by a higher frequency of firing, not by a bigger action potential.
8. Put the action-potential phases in order and name the main ion movement in each: repolarization, depolarization, resting, hyperpolarization.
Resting → depolarization (Na⁺ in) → repolarization (K⁺ out) → hyperpolarization (slight K⁺ overshoot) → back to resting. Voltage-gated Na⁺ channels drive the upstroke; K⁺ channels drive the downstroke and brief undershoot.
9. Why does the refractory period force an action potential to travel in only one direction?
Because the membrane that just fired has inactivated Na⁺ channels and cannot immediately fire again. The signal can only spread into fresh membrane ahead of it, not back into the region it just left, so it marches forward.
10. Compare continuous and saltatory conduction, and name the bare gaps where a myelinated signal regenerates.
Continuous conduction crawls patch-by-patch along unmyelinated axons; saltatory conduction leaps between the nodes of Ranvier on myelinated axons and is much faster. The nodes are packed with Na⁺ channels; the insulated internodes carry current passively.
11. Multiple sclerosis destroys CNS myelin. Using what you know about conduction, explain why this causes weakness and numbness.
Without myelin, current leaks out along the axon and saltatory conduction fails. Signals slow down or stop entirely, so motor commands and sensory information cannot get through — producing weakness, numbness, and visual problems.
12. List the steps of chemical synaptic transmission from the arriving action potential to the postsynaptic response, and name the ion that triggers neurotransmitter release.
AP arrives → voltage-gated Ca²⁺ channels open and calcium enters → vesicles fuse and release neurotransmitter → it diffuses across the cleft → binds postsynaptic receptors → opens channels, creating an EPSP or IPSP; then it is cleared. Calcium (Ca²⁺) entry is the trigger that makes the vesicles release their neurotransmitter.
Anatomy & Physiology · Chapter 13

Anatomy of the Nervous System

Tucked inside the vault of your skull sits the most complicated object we know of in the universe — about three pounds of tissue that lets you read this sentence, remember your childhood, and pull your hand off a hot stove before you even feel the burn. This chapter is a guided tour of that hardware. We will walk the wrinkled surface of the cerebrum, drop down through its inner regions to the life-support brainstem, float in the cerebrospinal fluid that cushions it all, peel back the three protective meninges, follow the spinal cord down its bony canal, and finish by counting off the twelve cranial nerves that leave the brain directly. By the end you will be able to hold a whole brain in your mind's eye and name what you are looking at.

By the end of this chapter you can

  • Identify the four lobes of the cerebrum, name the skull bone each lies under, and match each lobe to its main jobs.
  • Locate the primary motor and primary somatosensory cortices and the central sulcus that separates them.
  • Divide the brain into its four major regions — cerebrum, diencephalon, brainstem, and cerebellum — and state what each does.
  • Trace the flow of cerebrospinal fluid through the four ventricles and out into the subarachnoid space, and explain its three functions.
  • Name the three meninges in order, describe the spaces between them, and connect each to a clinical emergency.
  • Read a cross-section of the spinal cord — gray horns, white columns, dorsal and ventral roots — and explain the one-way traffic rule.
  • List the twelve cranial nerves in order, classify each as sensory, motor, or mixed, and give its main function.

Section 1The cerebral lobes

A wrinkled sheet of gray matter

The part of the brain you picture when you hear the word — that pale, deeply folded dome — is the cerebrum, the largest region and the seat of everything we call thought. Its outer shell, only a few millimeters thick, is the cerebral cortex: a sheet of gray matter packed with billions of neuron cell bodies. Underneath the cortex lies white matter, the myelinated axons that wire one region to another. The famous wrinkles are not decoration. Each ridge is a gyrus and each groove a sulcus (a deep one is a fissure), and folding the sheet this way lets an enormous area of cortex fit inside a skull — roughly two-thirds of it is hidden down in the sulci. More cortex means more processing power, which is why the brains of intelligent animals are so heavily folded.

Four lobes, four skull bones

The cortex of each cerebral hemisphere is divided into four lobes, and nature made them easy to name: each lobe is called after the skull bone lying directly over it. Two landmark grooves mark the borders. The central sulcus runs down across the top of the brain and separates the frontal lobe in front from the parietal lobe behind it. The deep lateral sulcus (Sylvian fissure) runs along the side and marks off the temporal lobe below. At the very back sits the occipital lobe. A fifth region, the insula, is buried deep inside the lateral sulcus and is often counted as a hidden fifth lobe.

The two hemispheres are not identical twins working in lockstep — they show lateralization, a division of labor in which one side tends to lead on certain tasks (language usually on the left, spatial and musical processing often on the right). They stay in constant conversation through a thick bridge of white matter called the corpus callosum, which carries hundreds of millions of axons from one hemisphere to the other.

Who does what

Each lobe has its own specialties, and a handful of cortical areas are worth memorizing because damage to them produces classic, testable deficits. The strip of cortex just in front of the central sulcus — the precentral gyrus — is the primary motor cortex, which issues the commands for voluntary movement. The strip just behind the central sulcus — the postcentral gyrus — is the primary somatosensory cortex, which receives touch, pressure, and temperature from the whole body. In each of these strips the body is mapped out upside-down and out of proportion, with huge territory given to the hands and face.

LobeSits underMain jobs
FrontalFrontal boneVoluntary movement (primary motor cortex), planning, judgment, personality, and speech production (Broca’s area)
ParietalParietal boneTouch, pressure, pain, and temperature (primary somatosensory cortex); spatial awareness
TemporalTemporal boneHearing, smell, memory, and language comprehension (Wernicke’s area)
OccipitalOccipital boneVision — the primary visual cortex
Central sulcus splits movement from feeling. Just in FRONT of it is the motor strip (precentral gyrus); just BEHIND it is the sensory strip (postcentral gyrus). One easy memory hook: the letter M for M-otor comes before the letter S for S-ensory, and the motor strip comes in front. Damage in front of the sulcus weakens movement; damage behind it dulls sensation.

Tap through the lobes and watch each one light up in the interactive below.

Interactive The wrinkled cortex divided into lobes, each named for the skull bone over it — tap a lobe to light it up. Open full ↑
Key points
  • The cerebral cortex is a folded sheet of gray matter; gyri (ridges) and sulci (grooves) pack more surface into the skull.
  • Four lobes — frontal, parietal, temporal, occipital — each named for the overlying skull bone.
  • The central sulcus separates the motor cortex (precentral, in front) from the somatosensory cortex (postcentral, behind).
  • The corpus callosum connects the two hemispheres, which show lateralization of function.

Section 2The major brain regions

Slice it down the middle

The lobes are only the outer story. To see how the whole brain is built, imagine cutting it exactly down the midline — a midsagittal section — and looking at the cut face. Four major regions appear, stacked from the thinking machinery on top to the life-support wiring below: the cerebrum, the diencephalon, the brainstem, and the cerebellum. As a rough rule, the higher a structure sits, the more “human” and deliberate its job; the lower it sits, the more ancient and automatic. You can lose a good deal of cerebrum and still live, but a tiny stroke in the brainstem can be fatal.

The diencephalon: the brain’s relay and thermostat

Buried beneath the cerebrum, wrapped around the third ventricle, is the diencephalon, a small but critical hub with two stars. The thalamus is the grand central station of the brain: nearly every sensory signal on its way to the cortex — sight, sound, touch, taste — stops here first to be sorted and forwarded. (Smell is the one sense that skips it.) Below and in front sits the hypothalamus, a structure the size of an almond that runs an astonishing amount of homeostasis: body temperature, hunger and thirst, the sleep–wake cycle, and the whole hormonal output of the pituitary gland dangling beneath it. The hypothalamus is where the nervous system and the endocrine system shake hands.

The brainstem and the cerebellum

The brainstem connects the brain to the spinal cord and, region for region, is the most vital tissue you own. From top to bottom it is the midbrain, the pons, and the medulla oblongata. The medulla contains the reflex centers that set your heart rate, blood pressure, and breathing rhythm — jobs you never think about but cannot live without. Every one of the twelve cranial nerves except the first two attaches to the brainstem. Behind the brainstem, tucked under the occipital lobe, is the cerebellum (“little brain”), a densely folded structure that fine-tunes movement, coordinates balance, and keeps your motions smooth. It does not start a movement; it edits one, which is why cerebellar damage makes a person clumsy and unsteady rather than paralyzed.

RegionKey partsMain job
CerebrumTwo hemispheres, cortex, corpus callosumThought, sensation, voluntary movement, memory, language
DiencephalonThalamus, hypothalamus, epithalamusSensory relay (thalamus); homeostasis and hormones (hypothalamus)
BrainstemMidbrain, pons, medulla oblongataVital reflexes (heartbeat, breathing), cranial-nerve hub, brain–cord highway
CerebellumTwo hemispheres, folia, vermisCoordination, balance, smooth and precise movement
The brainstem is life support. The medulla oblongata holds the cardiac, vasomotor, and respiratory centers — which is exactly why brain death is defined by the loss of brainstem function, and why a small brainstem stroke can stop breathing when a much larger cortical stroke does not.

Cut the brain down the middle and tap each region in the interactive below.

Interactive Slice the brain down the middle to reveal its four regions, from the thinking cerebrum to the life-sustaining brainstem — tap each. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Four major regions: cerebrum, diencephalon, brainstem, cerebellum — higher means more deliberate, lower means more automatic.
  • The thalamus relays almost all sensory input to the cortex; the hypothalamus runs homeostasis and the pituitary.
  • The brainstem (midbrain, pons, medulla) controls vital reflexes and anchors most cranial nerves.
  • The cerebellum coordinates and smooths movement and balance — it edits motion, it does not command it.

Section 3Ventricles & cerebrospinal fluid

A brain that floats

The brain is not packed solid. Running through its core is a set of connected hollow chambers, the ventricles, and the whole organ is bathed inside and out in a clear liquid called cerebrospinal fluid, or CSF. There are four ventricles: a pair of large, C-shaped lateral ventricles (one in each cerebral hemisphere), a slit-like third ventricle in the middle of the diencephalon, and a diamond-shaped fourth ventricle between the brainstem and the cerebellum. CSF is produced by the choroid plexus, a lacework of capillaries lining the ventricles that filters plasma from the blood at a rate of about 500 mL a day, keeping the total volume around 150 mL by constantly draining and replacing it several times over.

Following the flow

CSF is not a stagnant pool; it circulates on a one-way path from the top of the ventricles down and out around the brain and cord. Trace it once and it sticks: fluid made in the lateral ventricles passes through the interventricular foramina into the third ventricle, then down the narrow cerebral aqueduct into the fourth ventricle. From there it escapes through three small openings into the subarachnoid space that surrounds the entire brain and spinal cord, giving the whole central nervous system a liquid cushion. Finally, it is reabsorbed into the blood through cauliflower-like arachnoid granulations that poke up into the large veins draining the brain, completing the loop back into the circulation.

StepStructureWhat happens
1Choroid plexus (lateral ventricles)CSF is made from filtered blood plasma
2Interventricular foraminaFluid passes into the third ventricle
3Cerebral aqueductFluid descends to the fourth ventricle
4Apertures → subarachnoid spaceFluid bathes the brain and spinal cord
5Arachnoid granulationsFluid is reabsorbed into venous blood

Why the fluid matters

CSF does three jobs. First, buoyancy: the brain effectively floats, so that an organ weighing about 1,400 g presses down with an effective weight of only around 50 g — without this, the brain would crush its own base under its weight. Second, protection: the liquid layer absorbs shocks, letting the brain bob rather than slam against the skull during a jolt. Third, chemical stability: CSF carries nutrients in and washes wastes out, and it helps maintain the precise ionic environment neurons need to fire.

When the drain clogs, pressure climbs. If CSF cannot circulate or reabsorb — a blocked aqueduct, for instance — fluid backs up and the ventricles swell, a condition called hydrocephalus. In infants, whose skull sutures have not yet fused, the head enlarges; in adults, the rising pressure squeezes the brain. It is treated by placing a shunt to divert the excess fluid.

Watch the fluid circulate and tap each chamber in the interactive below.

Interactive The brain floats in cerebrospinal fluid made and circulated through four hollow ventricles — watch the fluid flow and tap each part. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Four ventricles — two lateral, the third, and the fourth — are hollow chambers filled with CSF.
  • CSF is made by the choroid plexus and flows lateral → third → fourth ventricle → subarachnoid space → venous blood.
  • CSF provides buoyancy, shock protection, and chemical stability for the brain and cord.
  • Blocked circulation causes hydrocephalus — swollen ventricles and rising pressure.

Section 4The meninges

Three coats around the cord

The brain and spinal cord are far too precious to leave bare against bone, so they are wrapped in three connective-tissue membranes called the meninges. From the outside in, they are the dura mater, the arachnoid mater, and the pia mater — a sequence worth memorizing in order, because the spaces between them are where the important clinical drama happens. The Latin names describe them: dura mater means “tough mother,” and pia mater means “tender mother.”

From tough to tender

The dura mater is the outermost layer: thick, leathery, and strong. Around the brain it has two sublayers, and where they separate they form the dural venous sinuses that drain blood from the brain, and inward folds like the falx cerebri that dip between the hemispheres to hold the brain steady. Beneath the dura lies the arachnoid mater, a delicate, web-like layer — its name comes from the Greek for spider, because fine threads called arachnoid trabeculae span the gap beneath it. That gap, the subarachnoid space, is filled with the CSF from the last section. Finally, the pia mater is a microscopically thin, richly vascular film that clings to every gyrus and sulcus of the brain like shrink-wrap, following the surface into its every fold.

LayerCharacterSpace / feature
Dura materTough, leathery outer coatForms dural venous sinuses; epidural space lies outside it
Arachnoid materThin, web-like middle layerSubdural space above, subarachnoid space (CSF) below
Pia materDelicate, vascular inner filmAdheres directly to the brain and cord surface
The spaces tell the story of a bleed. An epidural hematoma is usually arterial (a torn middle meningeal artery), collects fast, and looks lens-shaped on a CT. A subdural hematoma is usually venous (torn bridging veins), collects slowly, and looks crescent-shaped. And when the meninges themselves become infected — meningitis — the inflamed subarachnoid space produces the classic stiff neck, fever, and headache.

Peel back the layers one at a time in the interactive below.

Interactive Three protective membranes wrap the brain and cord, from a tough outer layer to a delicate inner one with fluid-filled spaces between — tap each layer. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Three meninges, outside in: dura mater (tough), arachnoid mater (web-like), pia mater (delicate).
  • The subarachnoid space, beneath the arachnoid, holds circulating CSF.
  • The pia mater follows every fold of the brain; the dura forms venous sinuses and stabilizing folds like the falx cerebri.
  • The spaces define the bleeds: epidural (arterial, lens-shaped) vs subdural (venous, crescent-shaped); infection of the meninges is meningitis.

Section 5The spinal cord

A cable and a switchboard

The spinal cord is the great communication cable of the body, running from the base of the brainstem down through the protective canal of the vertebral column to about the level of the first or second lumbar vertebra (L1–L2). Below that point it frays into a spray of nerve roots called the cauda equina (“horse’s tail”). The cord does two things at once: it carries traffic up and down between the body and the brain, and it acts as its own local switchboard, handling reflexes without waiting for the brain to weigh in.

Reading a slice

Cut the cord across and the anatomy is beautifully organized. In the center is a butterfly- or H-shaped region of gray matter — neuron cell bodies — surrounded by white matter, the myelinated tracts. This is the reverse of the brain, where gray is on the outside. The gray matter has wings called horns: the dorsal (posterior) horns handle incoming sensory information, the ventral (anterior) horns send out motor commands, and small lateral horns in the trunk region house autonomic neurons. A tiny central canal runs down the middle, filled with CSF.

One-way traffic at the roots

Where each spinal nerve joins the cord it splits into two roots, and they obey a strict traffic rule. The dorsal root carries sensory signals into the cord, and it bears a visible bulge, the dorsal root ganglion, where the sensory neuron cell bodies sit. The ventral root carries motor signals out to muscles and glands. Just beyond the cord the two roots merge into a single mixed spinal nerve — and there are 31 pairs of them. The white matter between is organized into columns of ascending (sensory) and descending (motor) tracts, the express lanes carrying information the length of the cord.

StructureTypeFunction
Dorsal (posterior) hornGray matterReceives sensory input
Ventral (anterior) hornGray matterSends motor output to muscles
Dorsal root (with ganglion)SensoryCarries sensation into the cord
Ventral rootMotorCarries commands out of the cord
White columns / tractsWhite matterAscending (sensory) and descending (motor) highways
Dorsal in, ventral out. Sensation enters through the back (dorsal root), motor commands leave through the front (ventral root) — a one-way street called the Bell–Magendie rule. And because the cord itself ends near L1–L2, a lumbar puncture is safely done lower down (around L3–L4), where the needle enters the cauda equina’s loose roots without risking the cord.

Slice the cord and tap each part in the interactive below.

Interactive A cross-section reveals a gray butterfly of cell bodies inside white tracts, with sensory input entering the back and motor commands leaving the front — tap each part. Open full ↑
Key points
  • The cord runs to about L1–L2, then continues as the nerve roots of the cauda equina; 31 pairs of spinal nerves branch off.
  • In cross-section, central gray matter (horns) is surrounded by white matter (tracts) — the opposite arrangement to the brain.
  • Dorsal horns/roots = sensory in; ventral horns/roots = motor out (the Bell–Magendie rule).
  • The dorsal root ganglion holds sensory cell bodies; a lumbar puncture is done below the cord for safety.

Section 6The cranial nerves

Twelve nerves straight from the brain

Most nerves branch off the spinal cord, but a special set of twelve pairs emerges directly from the brain itself: the cranial nerves. They are numbered with Roman numerals I through XII, from front to back, in the order their roots appear along the underside of the brain. The first two — olfactory and optic — grow out of the cerebrum and diencephalon; the remaining ten sprout from the brainstem. Together they handle the special senses of the head, move the eyes and face and tongue, and — in the case of one wandering nerve — reach all the way down into the chest and abdomen.

Sensory, motor, or both

Each cranial nerve is classified by the kind of traffic it carries. Some are purely sensory (they bring information in), some are purely motor (they send commands out), and some are mixed, doing both. A time-honored mnemonic captures the pattern — “Some Say Marry Money, But My Brother Says Big Brains Matter Most” — where the first letter of each word (S, S, M, M, B, M, B, S, B, B, M, M) tells you whether nerve I through XII is Sensory, Motor, or Both. For the names themselves, generations of students have used “On Old Olympus’ Towering Tops, A Finn And German Viewed Some Hops.”

#NameTypeMain function
IOlfactorySensorySmell
IIOpticSensoryVision
IIIOculomotorMotorMost eye movements; pupil constriction; raises the eyelid
IVTrochlearMotorMoves the eye (superior oblique muscle)
VTrigeminalBothFacial sensation; chewing muscles
VIAbducensMotorMoves the eye laterally (lateral rectus)
VIIFacialBothFacial expression; taste (front tongue); tears and saliva
VIIIVestibulocochlearSensoryHearing and balance
IXGlossopharyngealBothTaste (back tongue); swallowing; saliva
XVagusBothParasympathetic control of heart, lungs, and gut
XIAccessoryMotorMoves the neck and shoulders (sternocleidomastoid, trapezius)
XIIHypoglossalMotorMoves the tongue

The wandering nerve

One cranial nerve deserves a special note. The vagus nerve (X) is the odd one out: while every other cranial nerve stays in the head and neck, the vagus — its name literally means “wanderer” — travels down into the thorax and abdomen. It is the great parasympathetic highway, slowing the heart, driving digestion, and monitoring the internal organs. When you hear about the “rest-and-digest” response, you are hearing about the vagus at work.

Only the vagus leaves the head. Cranial nerves are nerves of the head and neck — with one exception. CN X, the vagus, wanders all the way to the gut, carrying the bulk of the body’s parasympathetic output. Remember it as the one cranial nerve that refuses to stay home.

Number off each nerve and tap it in the interactive below.

Interactive Twelve pairs of nerves emerge straight from the brain, numbered I to XII front to back — some sensory, some motor, some both. Tap each one. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Twelve pairs of cranial nerves, numbered I–XII front to back; all but the first two arise from the brainstem.
  • Each is sensory, motor, or mixed — the mnemonic “Some Say Marry Money…” gives the pattern.
  • Several move the eye (III, IV, VI); others carry special senses (I smell, II vision, VIII hearing/balance).
  • The vagus (X) is the only cranial nerve to leave the head and neck, carrying most parasympathetic output to the organs.

The chapter in one breath

  • The cerebrum is a folded sheet of cortical gray matter split into four lobes — frontal, parietal, temporal, occipital — each under its namesake skull bone.
  • The central sulcus divides the motor cortex (front) from the somatosensory cortex (back); the corpus callosum links the hemispheres.
  • Four brain regions stack from thinking to survival: cerebrum → diencephalon → brainstem → cerebellum, with the medulla running vital reflexes.
  • Cerebrospinal fluid made by the choroid plexus flows through four ventricles into the subarachnoid space, giving the brain buoyancy, protection, and chemical stability.
  • Three meninges — dura, arachnoid, pia — wrap the CNS; the spaces between them define epidural, subdural, and subarachnoid bleeds.
  • The spinal cord holds central gray horns inside white tracts; sensation enters the dorsal root, motor leaves the ventral root.
  • Twelve cranial nerves (I–XII) serve the head and neck — each sensory, motor, or mixed — and the vagus alone reaches the chest and abdomen.

ReferenceWords worth knowing

A quick reference for the terms in this chapter — skim it now, or circle back whenever a word trips you up.

Arachnoid mater
The web-like middle meningeal layer; beneath it, the subarachnoid space holds circulating cerebrospinal fluid.
Brainstem
The stalk connecting brain to spinal cord — midbrain, pons, and medulla oblongata — housing vital reflex centers and most cranial nerves.
Central sulcus
The groove separating the frontal lobe (and its motor cortex) from the parietal lobe (and its somatosensory cortex).
Cerebellum
The “little brain” behind the brainstem that coordinates balance and smooths voluntary movement.
Cerebral cortex
The thin outer sheet of gray matter covering the cerebrum, where higher thought, sensation, and voluntary movement are processed.
Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF)
The clear liquid made by the choroid plexus that fills the ventricles and subarachnoid space, cushioning and nourishing the CNS.
Choroid plexus
The capillary network lining the ventricles that filters blood plasma to produce cerebrospinal fluid.
Corpus callosum
The thick band of white matter carrying axons between the two cerebral hemispheres.
Cranial nerves
The twelve pairs of nerves (I–XII) emerging directly from the brain to serve the head, neck, and (via the vagus) the internal organs.
Diencephalon
The central brain region containing the thalamus and hypothalamus, wrapped around the third ventricle.
Dorsal root ganglion
The swelling on a spinal nerve’s dorsal root that houses the cell bodies of sensory neurons.
Dura mater
The tough, leathery outermost meningeal layer; it forms the dural venous sinuses and folds like the falx cerebri.
Gyrus
A raised ridge on the surface of the cerebral cortex (plural, gyri).
Hypothalamus
The diencephalon structure that regulates temperature, hunger, thirst, sleep, and hormone release, linking the nervous and endocrine systems.
Medulla oblongata
The lowest part of the brainstem, containing the reflex centers for heart rate, blood pressure, and breathing.
Meninges
The three connective-tissue membranes — dura, arachnoid, and pia mater — that wrap the brain and spinal cord.
Pia mater
The delicate, vascular innermost meningeal layer that clings directly to the surface of the brain and cord.
Sulcus
A shallow groove between the ridges of the cerebral cortex (plural, sulci); a deep one is a fissure.
Thalamus
The paired diencephalon relay station that sorts and forwards nearly all sensory input on its way to the cortex.
Vagus nerve
Cranial nerve X, the only cranial nerve to extend beyond the head and neck, carrying most parasympathetic output to the heart, lungs, and gut.
Ventricles
The four interconnected fluid-filled chambers within the brain — two lateral, third, and fourth — that make and circulate CSF.

Check yourself

1. Why is the cerebral cortex so deeply folded into gyri and sulci, and what does this accomplish?
Folding packs far more cortical surface area into the fixed volume of the skull. About two-thirds of the cortex is hidden down in the sulci; more cortical area means more neurons and greater processing power.
2. A patient has weakness of voluntary movement on one side of the body. Which gyrus and which lobe would you suspect, and how does the central sulcus help you locate it?
The precentral gyrus (primary motor cortex) of the frontal lobe. The motor strip lies just in front of the central sulcus; the somatosensory strip lies just behind it, so movement problems point in front of the sulcus and sensory problems behind it.
3. Name the four lobes of the cerebrum and give one main function of each.
Frontal (movement, judgment, speech production), parietal (touch and other body sensation), temporal (hearing, memory, language comprehension), occipital (vision). Each lobe is named for the skull bone directly over it.
4. Which brain structure relays almost all sensory information to the cortex, and which nearby structure runs homeostasis and controls the pituitary gland?
The thalamus is the sensory relay; the hypothalamus runs homeostasis and controls the pituitary. Both sit in the diencephalon. Smell is the one sense that bypasses the thalamus.
5. Why can a small stroke in the medulla oblongata be fatal when a much larger stroke in the cerebrum may not be?
The medulla houses the vital reflex centers for heart rate, blood pressure, and breathing. Losing these stops the automatic functions that keep the body alive, whereas large areas of cerebrum can be damaged without stopping life support.
6. Trace a drop of CSF from where it is made to where it is reabsorbed, naming the four ventricles and the key passages in order.
Choroid plexus of the lateral ventricles → interventricular foramina → third ventricle → cerebral aqueduct → fourth ventricle → subarachnoid space → arachnoid granulations into venous blood. It is a one-way circuit that is drained and replaced several times a day.
7. List the three functions of cerebrospinal fluid, and explain the buoyancy function with numbers.
Buoyancy, protection (shock absorption), and chemical stability. By floating in CSF, a brain that weighs about 1,400 g presses down with an effective weight of only around 50 g, sparing it from crushing under its own mass.
8. Name the three meninges from outside in, and state which space between them holds circulating CSF.
Dura mater, arachnoid mater, pia mater; the subarachnoid space (below the arachnoid) holds the CSF. The dura is tough and outermost; the pia is a delicate film hugging the brain surface.
9. On a CT scan, one bleed looks lens-shaped and one looks crescent-shaped. Which meningeal space and vessel type produces each?
Lens-shaped = epidural hematoma (arterial, often the middle meningeal artery); crescent-shaped = subdural hematoma (venous, torn bridging veins). The epidural bleed collects fast under arterial pressure; the subdural bleed accumulates more slowly.
10. In a cross-section of the spinal cord, which root carries sensory information into the cord and which carries motor commands out? Where do the sensory cell bodies sit?
The dorsal (posterior) root carries sensation in; the ventral (anterior) root carries motor out; sensory cell bodies sit in the dorsal root ganglion. This one-way arrangement is the Bell–Magendie rule — dorsal in, ventral out.
11. The spinal cord ends around the L1–L2 vertebra. Why is this fact important when performing a lumbar puncture?
Because the cord ends high, the needle can be inserted lower (around L3–L4) to sample CSF without piercing the cord. Below L1–L2 the canal contains only the loose, floating nerve roots of the cauda equina, which the needle can safely push aside.
12. Which cranial nerve is the only one to extend beyond the head and neck, what is its main role, and what does its name mean?
The vagus nerve (CN X); its name means “wanderer,” and it carries most of the body’s parasympathetic output to the heart, lungs, and digestive organs. Every other cranial nerve stays within the head and neck.
Anatomy & Physiology · Chapter 14

The Somatic Nervous System

The somatic nervous system is the part of you that deals with the outside world — it carries sensation in and sends voluntary commands to your skeletal muscles out. This chapter follows that two-way traffic from beginning to end. We start where every sensation is born: at the receptors in your skin, then move up through the great special senses — the eye and its visual pathway, the ear that hears and balances you, and the chemical senses of taste and smell. Finally we close the loop with the fastest circuit in the body, the reflex arc, where a signal comes in and a muscle answers before your brain has even been told. By the end you will be able to trace a stimulus from the world all the way to a response.

By the end of this chapter you can

  • Distinguish general from special senses and classify sensory receptors by the stimulus they detect (mechano-, thermo-, noci-, chemo-, photoreceptors).
  • Name the cutaneous receptors for light touch, deep pressure, temperature, and pain, and explain sensory adaptation.
  • Identify the three tunics of the eye and trace the path of light to the retina, explaining refraction and accommodation.
  • Follow the visual pathway from photoreceptor through the optic chiasm to the visual cortex, and predict the field defect from a given lesion.
  • Describe the outer, middle, and inner ear and explain how the organ of Corti turns a sound wave into a nerve signal.
  • Explain how taste and smell detect chemicals, and why smell is uniquely tied to memory and emotion.
  • List the five parts of a reflex arc and contrast a monosynaptic stretch reflex with a polysynaptic withdrawal reflex.

Section 1How sensation begins: the receptors

Two families of senses

Every experience of the world begins with a sensory receptor — a specialized nerve ending that converts some form of energy into an electrical signal the nervous system can read. That conversion is called transduction, and it is the first step in all sensation. We sort the senses into two families. The general senses — touch, pressure, vibration, temperature, pain, and the body’s own position sense (proprioception) — are spread all over the body in the skin, muscles, joints, and organs. The special senses — vision, hearing, balance, taste, and smell — are concentrated in complex organs in the head, and they fill the rest of this chapter.

Receptors are named for what they detect

The simplest way to organize the dozens of receptor types is by the kind of stimulus each one responds to. Learn these five categories and you can place almost any receptor in the body:

Receptor typeResponds toExample
MechanoreceptorPhysical deformation — touch, pressure, stretch, vibration, soundTactile corpuscles; hair cells of the ear
ThermoreceptorTemperature and changes in itWarm and cold free nerve endings in skin
NociceptorTissue damage — the pain receptorsFree nerve endings that signal cuts, burns, inflammation
ChemoreceptorChemicals dissolved or airborneTaste and smell receptors; blood O₂/CO₂ sensors
PhotoreceptorLightRods and cones of the retina

The touch receptors of the skin

The skin alone holds a remarkable variety of endings, each tuned to a slightly different feeling. Free nerve endings are the simplest — bare fibers that report pain, temperature, and crude touch. Tactile (Merkel) discs sit just under the surface and read fine texture and edges. Tactile (Meissner’s) corpuscles — the egg-shaped structure in the reference figure — nestle in the dermal papillae of hairless skin such as the fingertips and lips, where they sense light touch and delicate, low-frequency vibration. Deeper down, the large, onion-layered lamellated (Pacinian) corpuscles answer to deep pressure and buzzing, high-frequency vibration, while bulbous (Ruffini) corpuscles register skin stretch and steady pressure.

Notice how density decides sensitivity. Your fingertips are crowded with receptors, each with a tiny receptive field, so you can feel two close points as separate — that is why you read Braille with a fingertip, not an elbow. Another key property is adaptation: many receptors are phasic (fast-adapting), firing hard when a stimulus starts or stops but going quiet if it holds steady. That is why you stop noticing the shirt on your back seconds after you put it on.

Pain refuses to adapt. Most touch and pressure receptors quiet down when a stimulus lingers, but nociceptors are tonic — they keep firing as long as the danger is there. That stubbornness is protective: your body will not let you ignore a burn or a broken bone, because the whole point of pain is to make you act.

Find and identify each of the skin’s receptors in the interactive below.

Interactive Skin packed with specialized nerve endings, each tuned to a different sensation — tap each receptor to find it. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Transduction converts a stimulus into a nerve signal; general senses are body-wide, special senses live in head organs.
  • Receptors are classed by stimulus: mechano-, thermo-, noci-, chemo-, and photoreceptors.
  • Skin touch runs from free nerve endings and Merkel discs to Meissner’s (light touch), Pacinian (deep pressure/vibration), and Ruffini (stretch) corpuscles.
  • Phasic receptors adapt to a steady stimulus; nociceptors (pain) barely adapt at all.

Section 2The eye: catching the light

Three layers wrapped around a ball of fluid

The eyeball is built from three concentric coats, or tunics. The tough outer fibrous tunic is the white sclera that gives the eye its shape, continuous in front with the clear, curved cornea — the eye’s main window and its strongest light-bender. Beneath it, the vascular tunic carries the blood supply: the dark choroid nourishes the retina and soaks up stray light, the ciliary body makes fluid and adjusts the lens, and the colored iris opens and closes the pupil to control how much light gets in. Innermost is the neural tunic, the retina, where light finally becomes a nerve signal.

TunicStructuresJob
Fibrous (outer)Sclera; corneaProtects and shapes the eye; the cornea does most of the focusing
Vascular (middle)Choroid; ciliary body; irisSupplies blood, makes aqueous humor, controls the lens and pupil
Neural (inner)Retina (rods, cones, and relay neurons)Transduces light into action potentials

The path of a light ray

Follow one ray from the world to the retina: it passes through the cornea → aqueous humor → pupil → lens → vitreous humor → retina. Two fluids fill the eye and keep it firm. The watery aqueous humor fills the anterior cavity in front of the lens, feeds the avascular cornea and lens, and constantly drains out through the scleral venous sinus (canal of Schlemm). Behind the lens, the jelly-like vitreous humor fills the large posterior cavity. Refraction — the bending of light — is done mostly by the cornea, with the lens making the fine adjustments.

Accommodation and the sharpest spot

To focus on something near, the eye performs accommodation: the ciliary muscle contracts, which slackens the suspensory ligaments and lets the elastic lens bulge into a rounder, more powerful shape. For distant objects the ciliary muscle relaxes, the ligaments pull tight, and the lens flattens. The image lands on the retina, where a small pit called the fovea centralis is packed almost entirely with cones and gives the sharpest, most detailed color vision. Where the optic nerve leaves the eye there are no photoreceptors at all — the optic disc, your natural blind spot. The retina holds two receptor types: rods, which are exquisitely sensitive in dim light but see no color, and cones, which need bright light and give us color and detail.

Look slightly away to see a faint star. The fovea is all cones, which are useless in the dark, so a dim star vanishes when you stare straight at it. Glance a little to the side and its light falls on the rod-rich periphery — and the star reappears. Astronomers call this “averted vision,” and it is pure retinal anatomy.

Explore every structure of the eyeball in the cross-section below.

Interactive A cross-section of the eyeball reveals the parts that bend light and turn it into a signal — tap each structure. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Three tunics: fibrous (sclera + cornea), vascular (choroid, ciliary body, iris), and neural (retina).
  • Light travels cornea → aqueous humor → pupil → lens → vitreous → retina; the cornea refracts most, the lens fine-tunes.
  • Accommodation: ciliary muscle contracts → lens rounds up for near vision.
  • The fovea (all cones) gives sharpest vision; the optic disc is the blind spot; rods serve dim light, cones serve color.

Section 3The visual pathway: from retina to cortex

The route to the back of the brain

Once the retina has made a signal, it must reach the part of the brain that can interpret it — the visual cortex in the occipital lobe, at the very back of the head. The journey runs: retinal ganglion cell axons → optic nerve (CN II) → optic chiasm → optic tract → lateral geniculate nucleus of the thalamus → optic radiations → primary visual cortex. The single most important stop is the optic chiasm, the X-shaped crossing just under the brain where the fibers from the nasal (inner) half of each retina cross to the opposite side while the temporal (outer) fibers stay put.

Why each brain half sees the opposite world

That partial crossover has a clean consequence: the left half of your visual field is processed by the right hemisphere, and the right half by the left hemisphere. It sounds backwards until you remember the lens flips every image, so the left half of the world lands on the nasal retina of the left eye and the temporal retina of the right eye — and both of those fiber groups converge on the right brain. This crossing is not a quirk — it is what lets the brain fuse the two eyes’ slightly different views into one three-dimensional picture. From the cortex the signal splits again into a dorsal “where/how” stream toward the parietal lobe and a ventral “what” stream toward the temporal lobe, exactly as the reference figure shows.

Lesions map to the pathway

Because the anatomy is so orderly, damage at each point produces a predictable loss of vision — a favorite of every neurology exam:

Lesion siteVisual field result
Optic nerve (one side)Total blindness in that one eye
Optic chiasm (center)Loss of both outer (temporal) fields — bitemporal hemianopia
Optic tract or beyond (one side)Loss of the opposite half-field in both eyes — homonymous hemianopia
A pituitary tumor steals your peripheral vision. The gland sits directly below the optic chiasm, so a growing pituitary tumor presses on the crossing nasal fibers first. Those fibers carry the temporal (outer) fields — so the classic early sign is bitemporal hemianopia, the sensation of wearing blinders. Knowing the pathway tells you exactly where the lesion is.

Step through the whole journey, from photoreceptor to cortex, in the interactive.

Interactive Light becomes a signal in the retina, then travels to the back of the brain — step through the journey from photoreceptor to visual cortex. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Pathway: retina → optic nerve → optic chiasm → optic tract → thalamus (LGN) → visual cortex in the occipital lobe.
  • At the chiasm, nasal fibers cross so each hemisphere handles the opposite half of the visual field.
  • Cortex feeds a dorsal “where” stream (parietal) and a ventral “what” stream (temporal).
  • Lesion location predicts the defect — chiasm damage gives bitemporal hemianopia.

Section 4The ear: hearing and balance

Three parts passing a wave inward

The ear does two jobs at once — it hears, and it keeps you balanced — and it is built in three parts. The outer ear (the auricle and the external acoustic meatus) funnels sound to the tympanic membrane, or eardrum, and makes it vibrate. The air-filled middle ear holds the three smallest bones in the body, the ossicles — malleus, incus, and stapes — which lever the vibration across and press it onto the oval window; the auditory (Eustachian) tube connects here to the throat to equalize pressure. The fluid-filled inner ear, buried in the temporal bone, contains the spiral cochlea for hearing plus the vestibule and semicircular canals for balance.

RegionKey structuresJob
Outer earAuricle, ear canal, tympanic membraneCollects sound and vibrates the eardrum
Middle earMalleus, incus, stapes; auditory tubeAmplifies and transmits vibration to the oval window
Inner earCochlea; vestibule & semicircular canalsTurns vibration into hearing; senses head position and motion

How the cochlea turns motion into sound

Inside the coiled cochlea sit three fluid channels. When the stapes rocks the oval window, it sends a pressure wave through the perilymph, and that wave sets the basilar membrane shivering. Riding on the basilar membrane is the organ of Corti (the spiral organ), studded with hair cells whose tiny stereocilia brush against the overhanging tectorial membrane. When the cilia bend, ion channels pop open, the hair cell depolarizes, and it fires the cochlear nerve (part of cranial nerve VIII). The round window bulges to relieve the pressure so the wave can flow.

Pitch is coded by place. The basilar membrane is narrow and stiff at the base of the cochlea, where it responds to high-frequency sounds, and wide and floppy at the apex, where it responds to low-frequency sounds. So which hair cells fire tells the brain what note it is hearing — a beautiful example of anatomy doing computation. Meanwhile the neighboring vestibule senses gravity and straight-line acceleration and the semicircular canals sense rotation, giving you your sense of balance through the same eighth nerve.

Two kinds of deafness, two different problems. Conductive hearing loss is a mechanical failure of the outer or middle ear — earwax, a perforated drum, stiff ossicles — so the wave never reaches the cochlea. Sensorineural loss is damage to the hair cells or the nerve itself, and it is usually permanent because human hair cells do not regenerate. That is why one loud concert or years of noise can quietly, irreversibly cost you your hearing.

Send a sound wave inward through all three parts of the ear in the interactive.

Interactive The ear both hears and keeps you balanced, in three parts — pass a sound wave inward and tap each structure. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Three parts: outer (collects sound), middle (ossicles amplify), inner (cochlea + balance organs).
  • The organ of Corti on the basilar membrane holds hair cells that transduce vibration into the cochlear nerve (CN VIII) signal.
  • Pitch is mapped by place on the basilar membrane — base for high tones, apex for low.
  • The vestibule and semicircular canals provide balance; hair-cell (sensorineural) loss is permanent.

Section 5Taste and smell: the chemical senses

Tasting the dissolved, smelling the airborne

Taste and smell are both chemoreceptors — they detect molecules rather than energy. The difference is only the medium: taste (gustation) reads chemicals dissolved in your saliva, while smell (olfaction) reads chemicals floating in the air. Taste happens in taste buds, which sit mostly on the bumps of the tongue called papillae — the mushroom-shaped fungiform, the ridged foliate, and the large circumvallate papillae at the back. (The pointed filiform papillae have no taste buds; they only grip food and sense texture.) Each bud is a cluster of gustatory cells whose taste hairs poke up through a taste pore to sample the fluid, backed by basal cells that are constantly replacing worn-out taste cells every week or two.

Basic tasteSignalsTrigger
SweetSugars, energyGlucose and other carbohydrates
SourAcids, unripe or spoiled foodHydrogen ions (H⁺)
SaltyMinerals, electrolytesSodium ions (Na⁺)
BitterPotential toxinsAlkaloids and many poisons
UmamiProtein, savorinessGlutamate (as in broth or aged cheese)

Smell: a neuron with its feet in the air

High in the roof of the nasal cavity sits the olfactory epithelium, whose receptor cells are true neurons with cilia bathed in mucus. With only about 400 receptor types the nose still distinguishes thousands of odors, because each smell activates its own combination of receptors — a chemical fingerprint the brain learns to read. The axons of these neurons thread up through the tiny holes of the cribriform plate of the ethmoid bone to reach the olfactory bulb. Here is what makes smell strange and special: unlike every other sense, smell signals go straight to the olfactory and limbic areas of the brain without first stopping in the thalamus. And unusually for the nervous system, olfactory neurons regenerate throughout life.

Why a scent hurls you back in time. Smell is the only sense that bypasses the thalamic relay and plugs directly into the limbic system — the amygdala and hippocampus, the brain’s seats of emotion and memory. That direct wiring is why a whiff of a certain perfume or baking bread can summon a decades-old memory more vividly than any photograph. It is also why a stuffy nose makes food taste flat: most of “flavor” is actually smell.

Switch between the two chemical senses and explore their receptors in the interactive.

Interactive Taste and smell are the chemical senses — switch between them and explore the receptors that detect molecules. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Both are chemoreceptors: taste reads dissolved molecules, smell reads airborne ones.
  • Taste buds on the papillae hold gustatory cells that detect five basic tastes — sweet, sour, salty, bitter, umami.
  • Olfactory neurons reach the brain through the cribriform plate and are among the few neurons that regenerate.
  • Smell alone skips the thalamus and wires straight to the limbic system, tying it to memory and emotion.

Section 6The reflex arc: sensation answered

The fastest circuit in the body

We began this chapter with sensation coming in; we end it with a response going out. A reflex is a rapid, automatic, and predictable reaction to a stimulus — and its defining feature is that it does not wait for conscious thought. The signal runs a short hardwired loop called the reflex arc, often turning around in the spinal cord and firing a muscle before your brain has even registered what happened. That speed is protective: you jerk your hand off a hot stove long before you feel the burn.

The five parts of every reflex arc

No matter how simple or complex, every reflex arc is built from the same five components in the same order. Learn them once and you can diagram any reflex in the body:

StepComponentWhat it does
1ReceptorDetects the stimulus (e.g., a muscle spindle senses stretch)
2Sensory (afferent) neuronCarries the signal into the CNS
3Integration centerOne or more synapses in the spinal cord or brainstem
4Motor (efferent) neuronCarries the command back out
5EffectorThe muscle or gland that responds

Knee-jerk simple, withdrawal complex

The classic stretch (patellar) reflex — the knee-jerk the doctor taps — is the simplest kind, a monosynaptic arc with just one synapse between the sensory and motor neurons. Tapping the tendon stretches the muscle; the muscle spindle fires; the sensory neuron synapses directly onto a motor neuron in the cord; and the quadriceps contracts, kicking the leg out. At the same instant, an interneuron inhibits the opposing hamstring muscle so it relaxes out of the way — a trick called reciprocal inhibition, visible in the reference figure. Other reflexes are polysynaptic, threading through interneurons: the withdrawal reflex that yanks your foot off a tack, paired with a crossed-extensor reflex that stiffens the other leg so you do not fall.

The reflex hammer is a diagnostic tool. Tapping a tendon tests the entire arc at once — receptor, sensory nerve, a specific cord segment, motor nerve, and muscle. Reflexes that are too brisk (hyperreflexia) point to a problem in the brain or spinal cord above the reflex; reflexes that are weak or absent point to damage in the nerve, root, or muscle itself. A single tap localizes the lesion.

Trigger the knee-jerk and follow the signal through all five parts of the arc.

Interactive A reflex is an automatic response that doesn’t wait for the brain — tap the tendon and follow the signal through the five parts of the knee-jerk arc. Open full ↑
Key points
  • A reflex is a fast, involuntary response that turns around in the cord or brainstem, not the conscious brain.
  • Every arc has five parts: receptor → sensory neuron → integration center → motor neuron → effector.
  • The stretch reflex is monosynaptic; withdrawal reflexes are polysynaptic and add interneurons.
  • Reciprocal inhibition relaxes the antagonist; testing reflexes checks the whole arc and helps localize a lesion.

The chapter in one breath

  • The somatic nervous system carries sensation in and voluntary commands to skeletal muscle out; all sensation begins when a receptor transduces a stimulus into a nerve signal.
  • Receptors are classed by stimulus (mechano-, thermo-, noci-, chemo-, photoreceptor); the skin’s touch endings run from free nerve endings to Meissner’s, Pacinian, and Ruffini corpuscles, and most adapt while pain does not.
  • The eye’s three tunics focus light through the cornea and lens onto the retina; accommodation rounds the lens for near vision, and rods serve dim light while cones and the fovea serve color and detail.
  • The visual pathway crosses at the optic chiasm so each hemisphere sees the opposite field; lesion location predicts the field defect (chiasm → bitemporal hemianopia).
  • The ear’s outer, middle, and inner regions pass a wave to the organ of Corti, where hair cells fire the cochlear nerve; the vestibule and semicircular canals handle balance.
  • Taste and smell are chemical senses; taste buds detect five basic tastes, and smell wires directly to the limbic system, bypassing the thalamus.
  • A reflex arc answers a stimulus in five steps (receptor → sensory neuron → integration center → motor neuron → effector); the stretch reflex is the monosynaptic model, and reflex testing localizes lesions.

ReferenceWords worth knowing

A quick reference for the terms in this chapter — skim it now, or circle back whenever a word trips you up.

Accommodation
The eye’s adjustment for near vision, in which the ciliary muscle contracts and the lens rounds up to bend light more strongly.
Adaptation (sensory)
The decline in a receptor’s firing when a stimulus is held steady; phasic (fast-adapting) receptors adapt quickly, tonic ones barely at all.
Basilar membrane
The membrane in the cochlea that supports the organ of Corti and vibrates in different places for different sound frequencies.
Chemoreceptor
A receptor that detects chemicals, such as the taste and smell receptors or the body’s blood-gas sensors.
Cochlea
The coiled, fluid-filled organ of the inner ear that transduces sound waves into nerve signals.
Cone
A retinal photoreceptor that operates in bright light and provides color and sharp detail; concentrated at the fovea.
Fovea centralis
A tiny pit in the retina packed with cones that produces the sharpest, most detailed vision.
General senses
The body-wide senses — touch, pressure, vibration, temperature, pain, and proprioception — served by receptors in skin, muscle, and organs.
Gustation
The sense of taste, produced by chemoreceptors in the taste buds detecting dissolved molecules.
Mechanoreceptor
A receptor that responds to physical deformation such as touch, pressure, stretch, vibration, or sound.
Nociceptor
A pain receptor (a free nerve ending) that signals actual or potential tissue damage and adapts very little.
Olfaction
The sense of smell, produced by regenerating receptor neurons in the nasal roof that detect airborne molecules.
Optic chiasm
The X-shaped crossing beneath the brain where the nasal retinal fibers cross to the opposite side, sending each visual field to the opposite hemisphere.
Organ of Corti
The spiral organ resting on the basilar membrane whose hair cells transduce vibration into the cochlear nerve signal.
Photoreceptor
A light-detecting receptor of the retina; the rods and cones.
Reflex arc
The five-part neural pathway (receptor, sensory neuron, integration center, motor neuron, effector) that carries out an automatic reflex.
Refraction
The bending of light as it passes through the cornea and lens so that it focuses on the retina.
Retina
The eye’s inner neural tunic, containing the photoreceptors and the relay neurons that begin the visual pathway.
Rod
