A retinal photoreceptor that is highly sensitive in dim light but detects no color; most numerous in the periphery.
Special senses
The senses housed in complex organs of the head: vision, hearing, balance, taste, and smell.
Stretch (patellar) reflex
The monosynaptic knee-jerk reflex in which stretching a muscle triggers its own contraction with a single synapse.
Taste bud
A cluster of gustatory receptor cells, mostly on the tongue’s papillae, that detects the five basic tastes through a taste pore.

Check yourself

1. A cell biologist and a clinician argue about whether the fingertip or the back has better touch. Using receptor density and receptive fields, who is right and why?
The fingertip, because of dense receptors and small receptive fields. Tightly packed receptors each cover a tiny patch of skin, so two nearby points stimulate separate receptors and feel distinct — giving the fine two-point discrimination the sparse, large-field back cannot match.
2. You stop feeling your socks minutes after dressing, yet a pebble in your shoe keeps hurting all day. Which receptor property explains each experience?
Adaptation. The pressure receptors sensing your socks are phasic (fast-adapting) and go quiet under a steady stimulus, so the socks vanish from awareness. Nociceptors are tonic (slow-adapting) and keep firing, so the painful pebble never fades — a protective design.
3. Trace a ray of light through the eye from the outside world to the photoreceptors, naming the structures it passes.
Cornea → aqueous humor → pupil → lens → vitreous humor → retina. The cornea does most of the bending (refraction) and the lens fine-tunes the focus so the image lands sharply on the retina.
4. To read this text up close, what must the ciliary muscle and lens do, and what is this process called?
Accommodation: the ciliary muscle contracts, the suspensory ligaments slacken, and the lens bulges into a rounder, more powerful shape. A rounder lens refracts light more strongly, which is needed to focus nearby objects onto the retina.
5. Why can you see a faint star better by looking slightly beside it than by staring straight at it?
Because staring places its image on the cone-only fovea, which is nearly blind in dim light. Glancing to the side shifts the faint light onto the rod-rich periphery, and rods are far more sensitive in the dark — the trick of averted vision.
6. At the optic chiasm, which retinal fibers cross, and what is the functional result for how the brain divides the visual world?
The nasal (inner) fibers cross; the temporal (outer) fibers stay on the same side. As a result each hemisphere processes the opposite half of the visual field — the left field goes to the right brain and vice versa.
7. A patient develops tunnel-like loss of both outer (temporal) visual fields. Where is the lesion, and what nearby structure is the usual culprit?
The optic chiasm — a bitemporal hemianopia — classically from a pituitary tumor. The pituitary sits just below the chiasm and compresses the crossing nasal fibers, which carry the temporal fields, producing the “blinders” pattern.
8. Follow a sound from the eardrum to the nerve: name the structures that carry and transduce the vibration in the middle and inner ear.
Tympanic membrane → malleus, incus, stapes → oval window → cochlear fluid → basilar membrane → hair cells of the organ of Corti → cochlear nerve (CN VIII). Bending of the hair-cell stereocilia against the tectorial membrane opens ion channels and fires the nerve.
9. Distinguish conductive from sensorineural hearing loss, and explain why one is often permanent.
Conductive loss is a mechanical block in the outer or middle ear; sensorineural loss is damage to the hair cells or cochlear nerve. Sensorineural loss is usually permanent because human cochlear hair cells do not regenerate, so loud-noise damage is irreversible.
10. Name the five basic tastes and give the specific stimulus for salty and for sour.
Sweet, sour, salty, bitter, and umami; salty is triggered by sodium ions (Na⁺) and sour by hydrogen ions (H⁺). Sweet, bitter, and umami act through receptor proteins, while salty and sour are sensed more directly through their ions.
11. Why does the smell of a specific food or perfume trigger vivid memories more powerfully than sight or sound?
Because smell bypasses the thalamus and projects directly to the limbic system. Olfactory signals reach the amygdala and hippocampus — the centers of emotion and memory — without a thalamic relay, wiring scent tightly to recollection and feeling.
12. List the five parts of a reflex arc in order and map the knee-jerk (patellar) reflex onto them.
Receptor → sensory neuron → integration center → motor neuron → effector. The muscle spindle (receptor) senses the tendon tap; a sensory neuron carries it to the spinal cord, where a single synapse (integration center) drives a motor neuron to the quadriceps (effector), which contracts and kicks the leg.
Anatomy & Physiology · Chapter 15

The Autonomic Nervous System

Right now, without a single deliberate thought, your heart is beating, your gut is churning breakfast, your pupils are adjusting to the light, and your blood pressure is being nudged up and down a dozen times a minute. All of that quiet housekeeping belongs to the autonomic nervous system — the part of you that runs the organs while your conscious mind is busy elsewhere. In this chapter you will meet its two divisions, the sympathetic "fight or flight" system and the parasympathetic "rest and digest" system, and see how they pull most organs in opposite directions to keep the body tuned. You will learn the two-neuron wiring that makes autonomic pathways different from every other motor route, the two chemicals that carry every autonomic message, and the elegant reflex that stops you from fainting each time you stand up.

By the end of this chapter you can

  • Define the autonomic nervous system and explain how it differs from the somatic (voluntary) motor system.
  • Contrast the sympathetic and parasympathetic divisions by their overall job, their outflow from the CNS, and their effects on target organs.
  • Describe the two-neuron autonomic pathway and locate the ganglia of each division.
  • Explain dual innervation and autonomic tone, and predict the net effect when one division dominates.
  • Trace the whole-body fight-or-flight response organ by organ and describe the parasympathetic return to calm.
  • Match the autonomic neurotransmitters (acetylcholine, norepinephrine) to their receptors and predict the effect at each target.
  • Diagram the baroreceptor reflex as a negative feedback loop that defends blood pressure.

Section 1Two divisions, opposite jobs

A motor system for the organs you never think about

The nervous system has two motor outputs. The somatic system carries your voluntary commands to skeletal muscle — the decision to lift a cup. The autonomic nervous system (ANS) does everything else: it is the involuntary, visceral motor system that controls smooth muscle, cardiac muscle, and glands. It sets your heart rate, the width of your pupils and airways, the pace of your digestion, the tone of your blood vessels, and the activity of your sweat and salivary glands — all automatically, below the level of awareness. You feel its work only indirectly, as a pounding heart before a test or a dry mouth when you are nervous.

Two divisions that pull opposite ways

The ANS is built from two divisions that usually act as opponents. The sympathetic division is the body's accelerator — the "fight or flight" system that ramps you up to meet a threat. The parasympathetic division is the brake — the "rest and digest" system that runs the body during calm, restful conditions and handles the unglamorous business of digestion, waste elimination, and energy storage. Most organs receive a nerve supply from both divisions, an arrangement called dual innervation, and the two supplies push the organ in opposite directions. The heart is the clearest example: sympathetic input speeds it up, parasympathetic input slows it down, and your actual heart rate at any moment is the running balance between the two.

Where each division leaves the central nervous system

The two divisions even exit the CNS from different places, which is the easiest way to tell them apart. Sympathetic fibers all emerge from the thoracolumbar region — the lateral horns of spinal segments T1 through L2 — so the sympathetic division is often called the thoracolumbar outflow. Parasympathetic fibers emerge from the two ends of the CNS: from the brainstem in four cranial nerves (III, VII, IX, and the far-reaching vagus, X) and from the sacral spinal cord (segments S2–S4). That top-and-bottom pattern gives the parasympathetic division its nickname, the craniosacral outflow.

Target organSympathetic ("fight or flight")Parasympathetic ("rest and digest")
HeartFaster rate, stronger beatSlower rate
PupilDilates (widens)Constricts (narrows)
Airways (bronchi)Dilate — more airConstrict
Digestive tractSlows; blood diverted awaySpeeds up; secretions flow
Salivary glandsScant, thick saliva (dry mouth)Copious, watery saliva
Urinary bladderRelaxes (holds urine)Contracts (empties)
LiverReleases glucose to the bloodStores glucose
Tone means both are always on. Neither division is ever fully switched off. Both fire at a low background rate called autonomic tone, so the body regulates an organ simply by dialing one division up and the other down — like steering with a gas pedal and a brake pressed at the same time. Resting heart rate, for instance, is dominated by ongoing vagal tone; cut the vagus and the heart races.

Tap any organ in the interactive to line up its two opposing nerve supplies side by side.

Interactive Tap an organ to compare its sympathetic and parasympathetic supply. Open full ↑
Key points
  • The autonomic nervous system is the involuntary motor system for smooth muscle, cardiac muscle, and glands.
  • Its two divisions are opponents: sympathetic = fight or flight (accelerator); parasympathetic = rest and digest (brake).
  • Sympathetic outflow is thoracolumbar (T1–L2); parasympathetic outflow is craniosacral (CN III, VII, IX, X and S2–S4).
  • Most organs have dual innervation and are steered by shifting the balance of ongoing autonomic tone.

Section 2The two-neuron pathway

Two neurons in a relay, not one

Here is the single structural fact that defines the whole autonomic system: it takes two neurons to carry a signal from the CNS to a visceral target, and they meet at a relay station called a ganglion. The first neuron, the preganglionic neuron, has its cell body inside the CNS; its axon runs out to a ganglion and synapses there. The second neuron, the postganglionic neuron, has its cell body in the ganglion and sends its axon the rest of the way to the organ. Compare this to the somatic system, where a single motor neuron reaches all the way from the spinal cord to the muscle with no relay in between. That extra synapse in the ganglion is where the two divisions do their most distinctive wiring.

Sympathetic wiring: short first, long second, and it spreads

In the sympathetic division, the ganglia sit close to the spinal cord. Most form the sympathetic chain ganglia (the paravertebral ganglia) — a beaded double column running alongside the vertebral column that you can see flanking the cord in the figure. A few others, the prevertebral (collateral) ganglia such as the celiac and mesenteric ganglia, sit out in front of the aorta and are reached by the splanchnic nerves. Because the relay is near the cord, sympathetic preganglionic fibers are short and postganglionic fibers are long. Crucially, each preganglionic fiber synapses on many postganglionic neurons — a fan-out called divergence — which is exactly why a sympathetic response is not local but sweeps the whole body at once.

Parasympathetic wiring: long first, short second, and it stays local

The parasympathetic division reverses the geometry. Its ganglia sit in or right beside the target organ (terminal or intramural ganglia), so preganglionic fibers are long and postganglionic fibers are very short. There is little divergence, so a parasympathetic signal produces a discrete, localized effect on one organ rather than a body-wide surge — you can slow the heart without also cramping the gut. This precision fits the calm, one-thing-at-a-time character of rest and digest.

FeatureSympatheticParasympathetic
CNS outflowThoracolumbar (T1–L2)Craniosacral (brainstem + S2–S4)
Ganglion locationNear the spinal cord (chain & prevertebral)Near or in the target organ
Preganglionic fiberShortLong
Postganglionic fiberLongShort
DivergenceHigh → widespread responseLow → localized response
The adrenal medulla is a modified ganglion. One sympathetic pathway breaks the two-neuron rule: preganglionic fibers run straight to the adrenal medulla and synapse on hormone-making chromaffin cells, which dump epinephrine (and some norepinephrine) directly into the bloodstream. Those cells are essentially postganglionic neurons that lost their axons. This is why fight or flight has a hormonal tail — the racing, jittery feeling that lingers minutes after the scare has passed.

Switch between the two divisions in the interactive to watch the ganglion move from beside the cord to beside the organ.

Interactive Every autonomic signal relays through a ganglion — switch divisions to see where. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Every autonomic route uses two neurons — preganglionic and postganglionic — relaying at a ganglion.
  • Sympathetic: ganglia near the cord (chain & prevertebral), short pre / long post fibers, high divergence → body-wide response.
  • Parasympathetic: ganglia at the organ, long pre / short post fibers, low divergence → localized response.
  • The adrenal medulla is a modified sympathetic ganglion that releases epinephrine as a hormone.

Section 3Fight or flight, rest and digest

One switch, and the whole body answers

Because sympathetic fibers diverge so widely and the adrenal medulla adds a hormonal boost, the sympathetic division works as a coordinated, all-at-once package. Imagine the moment you step off a curb and a car horn blares. Before you can think, your heart pounds, your breathing deepens, your eyes go wide, your palms sweat, and your stomach seems to drop. That is the fight-or-flight response — the sympathetic division reprioritizing the entire body toward one goal: sudden, intense physical exertion to escape or confront a threat. Every change is purposeful, and each one shifts resources away from long-term maintenance and toward immediate muscle power.

The sympathetic surge, organ by organ

Follow the surge through the body and every effect earns its place. The heart beats faster and harder to move oxygen quickly. The airways widen so more air reaches the lungs. The pupils dilate to take in more of the scene. The liver pours stored glucose into the blood to fuel muscle. Blood vessels constrict in the gut and skin but dilate in skeletal muscle, shunting the blood where it is needed. Digestion and urination pause — no time for either. Sweat glands switch on to pre-cool the body for exertion. It is a beautifully logical package: everything that helps you run or fight is turned up, and everything that can wait is turned down.

Fight-or-flight changeWhy it helps survival
Heart rate and force riseDelivers oxygen and fuel to muscle faster
Bronchioles dilateMore airflow, more oxygen uptake
Pupils dilateSharper, wider vision of the threat
Liver releases glucoseReady fuel for a burst of effort
Blood shifts to skeletal musclePower where it is needed, not the gut
Digestion and urination haltSaves energy for the emergency
Sweating increasesCools the body before heavy exertion

Rest and digest: paying the debt back

When the threat is gone, the parasympathetic division takes the body back to baseline and then to the quiet work the emergency interrupted. The heart slows, the airways and pupils return to normal, blood flows back to the gut, and digestion, absorption, and elimination resume. This is the state you spend most of your life in — the body building and storing rather than spending. The two divisions are not enemies so much as a well-matched pair: the sympathetic system is the sprint, the parasympathetic system is the recovery, and health depends on being able to swing smoothly between them.

Why you stay shaky after a fright. Nerve signals stop the instant the danger passes, but the epinephrine the adrenal medulla poured into your blood keeps circulating for minutes until the liver and other tissues clear it. That lingering hormone is the trembling hands and thudding heart you feel after a near-miss — the nervous response is already over, but the hormonal one is still fading.

Flip the switch in the interactive to trip the sympathetic surge, then let the parasympathetic division bring the body back down.

Interactive Flip the switch to fire the sympathetic surge, then return the body to calm. Open full ↑
Key points
  • The fight-or-flight response is a coordinated, body-wide sympathetic package aimed at intense physical effort.
  • It raises heart rate, airflow, pupil size, and blood glucose and shifts blood to muscle, while pausing digestion and urination.
  • The parasympathetic division restores baseline and resumes rest-and-digest housekeeping.
  • Circulating epinephrine outlasts the nerve signals, which is why arousal fades slowly after a scare.

Section 4The chemistry: acetylcholine and norepinephrine

Only two chemicals do all the talking

For a system that controls so much, the ANS speaks a remarkably small vocabulary: nearly every autonomic message is carried by just two neurotransmitters. A neuron that releases acetylcholine (ACh) is called cholinergic; one that releases norepinephrine (NE) is called adrenergic. Where in the pathway each chemical is used follows a simple pattern. All preganglionic neurons of both divisions are cholinergic — they release ACh in the ganglion. The postganglionic neurons then split: parasympathetic postganglionic fibers release ACh onto the organ, while sympathetic postganglionic fibers release NE. (Two tidy exceptions: sympathetic fibers to sweat glands use ACh, and the adrenal medulla releases epinephrine into the blood.)

The receptor decides the effect, not the chemical

Here is the idea students most need to grasp: the same neurotransmitter can excite one organ and inhibit another, because the outcome depends on the receptor the chemical lands on. Cholinergic receptors come in two flavors — nicotinic receptors (found in all autonomic ganglia and at the skeletal neuromuscular junction) and muscarinic receptors (on the organs that parasympathetic fibers control). Adrenergic receptors also come in families — alpha and beta subtypes. Norepinephrine acting on alpha-1 receptors constricts blood vessels; the very same NE (and epinephrine) acting on beta-2 receptors relaxes the airways. One molecule, opposite effects — the target cell's receptor writes the instruction.

FiberNeurotransmitterReceptor on target
Preganglionic (both divisions)AcetylcholineNicotinic
Parasympathetic postganglionicAcetylcholineMuscarinic
Sympathetic postganglionic (most)NorepinephrineAdrenergic (α or β)
Sympathetic to sweat glandsAcetylcholineMuscarinic
Adrenal medulla → bloodEpinephrine (hormone)Adrenergic (α or β)

A loose spray, not a tight synapse

Notice in the figure that the postganglionic axon does not end in a single neat terminal. Instead it is studded with beads called varicosities that release neurotransmitter in a diffuse spray over a wide patch of smooth muscle. This is why one autonomic fiber can influence a broad sheet of tissue at once — ideal for setting the tone of an entire vessel wall or gut segment rather than commanding a single cell.

Receptor subtypes are drug targets. This chemistry is the whole reason autonomic drugs can be so selective. A beta-blocker shields the heart's β1 receptors to slow it; albuterol for asthma stimulates β2 receptors to open the airways without racing the heart; atropine blocks muscarinic receptors to speed a dangerously slow heart or dry secretions before surgery. Learn which receptor sits on which organ and the pharmacology almost writes itself.

Tap each receptor in the interactive to see how the same two chemicals produce different effects.

Interactive Two chemicals, many effects — tap a receptor to see what it does. Open full ↑
Key points
  • All preganglionic fibers and all parasympathetic postganglionic fibers release acetylcholine (cholinergic); most sympathetic postganglionic fibers release norepinephrine (adrenergic).
  • The receptor — nicotinic vs muscarinic, alpha vs beta — determines whether a target is excited or inhibited.
  • Postganglionic axons release transmitter from varicosities, spraying a whole patch of tissue.
  • Receptor subtypes are the reason autonomic drugs (beta-blockers, albuterol, atropine) can be so targeted.

Section 5The baroreceptor reflex

The body's fastest blood-pressure guard

The autonomic system does more than react to emergencies — it runs continuous, silent reflexes that keep vital numbers steady. The most important of these is the baroreceptor reflex, the second-by-second guardian of blood pressure. It is a textbook negative feedback loop, and once you see its three parts you will recognize the same machinery you met in Chapter 1: a sensor, a control center, and effectors.

Sense, compare, correct

The sensors are baroreceptors — stretch receptors packed into the walls of the carotid sinus (where blood heads to the brain) and the aortic arch. When blood pressure rises, these vessel walls stretch more and the receptors fire faster; when pressure falls, they fire less. Their reports travel along cranial nerves IX (glossopharyngeal) and X (vagus) to the cardiovascular center in the medulla of the brainstem — the control center that compares the signal to the desired pressure. The medulla then adjusts its two autonomic effectors: it dials parasympathetic (vagal) and sympathetic output up or down to change heart rate, contractility, and vessel diameter. Because the response always opposes the disturbance, pressure is pushed back toward its set point.

Blood pressureBaroreceptor firingAutonomic responseResult
Rises too highIncreasesParasympathetic up, sympathetic downHeart slows, vessels dilate → BP falls
Falls too lowDecreasesSympathetic up, parasympathetic downHeart speeds, vessels constrict → BP rises

Standing up without fainting

You depend on this reflex every time you stand. When you rise, gravity pools blood in your legs and pressure at the head momentarily drops. Baroreceptors instantly sense the fall, the medulla ramps up sympathetic drive, your heart quickens and your vessels tighten, and pressure is restored before the brain is ever short-changed — all in a single heartbeat or two. When this reflex is sluggish (from aging, dehydration, or certain drugs), the correction lags and you feel lightheaded on standing, a condition called orthostatic hypotension.

The dizzy-on-standing test. That brief wooziness when you jump up too fast is the baroreceptor reflex being caught a half-second behind. It is also why a clinician checks orthostatic vitals — blood pressure lying, sitting, and standing — to unmask a reflex or a blood volume that cannot keep up with a change in posture.

Knock the pressure off-target in the interactive and watch the reflex pull it home.

Interactive Choose a disturbance and watch the negative feedback loop restore blood pressure. Open full ↑
Key points
  • The baroreceptor reflex is a negative feedback loop that stabilizes blood pressure moment to moment.
  • Baroreceptors in the carotid sinus and aortic arch sense stretch and report via CN IX and X to the medulla.
  • The medulla corrects pressure by shifting the sympathetic/parasympathetic balance to heart rate, force, and vessel diameter.
  • A slow reflex causes orthostatic hypotension — lightheadedness on standing.

Section 6Somatic versus autonomic

Two ways the brain moves the body

It is worth putting the autonomic system side by side with its sibling, the somatic motor system, because the contrast makes both clearer. The somatic system carries voluntary commands to skeletal muscle. It uses a single motor neuron from the CNS straight to the muscle, that neuron releases acetylcholine onto nicotinic receptors, and its effect is always the same: excitation — a somatic motor neuron can only tell a muscle to contract, never to relax. To stop a movement, the CNS simply stops firing.

Where the autonomic system differs

The autonomic system differs on nearly every count. It is involuntary; it targets smooth muscle, cardiac muscle, and glands; it uses a two-neuron chain relaying at a ganglion; it uses ACh or NE depending on the fiber; and it can be either excitatory or inhibitory at the target — the parasympathetic vagus can actively slow the heart, something no somatic neuron can do to a muscle. That ability to both stimulate and inhibit, division by division, is what lets the ANS fine-tune organs in both directions.

FeatureSomaticAutonomic
ControlVoluntaryInvoluntary
Effector (target)Skeletal muscleSmooth & cardiac muscle, glands
Neurons to targetOneTwo (relay at a ganglion)
NeurotransmitterAcetylcholine onlyAcetylcholine or norepinephrine
Effect on targetAlways excitatoryExcitatory or inhibitory

A third player: the gut's own brain

The figure also names a quieter third division, the enteric nervous system (ENS) — a vast web of neurons embedded in the wall of the digestive tract. So numerous that it is nicknamed the "second brain," the ENS can run local digestive reflexes on its own, coordinating the muscle contractions and secretions of the gut even when its links to the CNS are cut. The autonomic divisions still modulate it — parasympathetic input revs digestion, sympathetic input suppresses it — but the day-to-day churning of your intestines is largely self-governed.

Inhibition is the autonomic superpower. The single most useful line between the two systems: a somatic neuron can only excite its muscle, so relaxation means silence from the nerve. An autonomic target, by contrast, has two nerves that can each actively push it up or down. That is why your heart has a built-in brake (the vagus) and your gut has a built-in throttle.

Switch between the somatic and autonomic pathways in the interactive to compare their wiring and behavior.

Interactive Switch between the somatic and autonomic pathways to see how they differ. Open full ↑
Key points
  • The somatic system is voluntary, uses one neuron to skeletal muscle, releases only ACh, and is always excitatory.
  • The autonomic system is involuntary, uses a two-neuron chain to smooth/cardiac muscle and glands, uses ACh or NE, and can excite or inhibit.
  • The enteric nervous system is a self-governing "second brain" in the gut wall, modulated but not commanded by the ANS.

The chapter in one breath

  • The autonomic nervous system is the involuntary motor system that runs smooth muscle, cardiac muscle, and glands.
  • Its two divisions oppose each other: sympathetic (fight or flight, thoracolumbar) and parasympathetic (rest and digest, craniosacral), with most organs under dual innervation and steered by shifting tone.
  • Every autonomic route is a two-neuron chain relaying at a ganglion — sympathetic ganglia sit near the cord and diverge widely; parasympathetic ganglia sit at the organ and stay local.
  • The fight-or-flight response is a coordinated, body-wide sympathetic surge; the parasympathetic division restores calm, aided by the slow clearance of adrenal epinephrine.
  • Just two neurotransmitters — acetylcholine and norepinephrine — run the system, and the receptor decides whether a target is excited or inhibited.
  • The baroreceptor reflex is a negative feedback loop that defends blood pressure by balancing sympathetic and parasympathetic output through the medulla.
  • Compared with the somatic system (voluntary, one neuron, ACh, always excitatory), the autonomic system is involuntary, two-neuron, and can both excite and inhibit; the enteric system runs the gut semi-independently.

ReferenceWords worth knowing

A quick reference for the terms in this chapter — skim it now, or circle back whenever a word trips you up.

Acetylcholine (ACh)
The neurotransmitter released by all autonomic preganglionic fibers and by parasympathetic postganglionic fibers; neurons that use it are called cholinergic.
Adrenal medulla
The inner core of the adrenal gland; a modified sympathetic ganglion that releases epinephrine into the blood, giving fight or flight a hormonal, longer-lasting phase.
Adrenergic
Describing a neuron or receptor that uses norepinephrine (or epinephrine); adrenergic receptors are grouped into alpha and beta subtypes.
Autonomic nervous system
The involuntary motor division of the nervous system that controls smooth muscle, cardiac muscle, and glands.
Autonomic tone
The steady background firing of both divisions, so an organ is regulated by shifting the balance between them rather than switching one on from off.
Baroreceptor
A stretch receptor in the carotid sinus and aortic arch that senses blood pressure by how much the vessel wall is stretched.
Baroreceptor reflex
The negative feedback loop that stabilizes blood pressure by adjusting sympathetic and parasympathetic output to the heart and vessels.
Cholinergic
Describing a neuron or receptor that uses acetylcholine; cholinergic receptors are either nicotinic or muscarinic.
Craniosacral outflow
The origin of parasympathetic fibers from the brainstem (cranial nerves III, VII, IX, X) and sacral spinal cord (S2–S4).
Dual innervation
The supply of a single organ by both the sympathetic and parasympathetic divisions, which act on it in opposite directions.
Enteric nervous system
The extensive network of neurons in the wall of the digestive tract that can run local gut reflexes independently of the brain and spinal cord.
Fight-or-flight response
The coordinated, body-wide sympathetic reaction that prepares the body for intense physical exertion in the face of a threat.
Ganglion (autonomic)
The relay station outside the CNS where a preganglionic neuron synapses on a postganglionic neuron.
Muscarinic receptor
A cholinergic receptor on the target organs of parasympathetic (and sweat-gland) fibers; its effect can be excitatory or inhibitory depending on the organ.
Nicotinic receptor
A cholinergic receptor found in all autonomic ganglia and at the skeletal neuromuscular junction; always excitatory.
Norepinephrine (NE)
The neurotransmitter released by most sympathetic postganglionic fibers; it acts on adrenergic receptors.
Parasympathetic division
The "rest and digest" division of the ANS; craniosacral in origin, it conserves energy and runs digestion and elimination.
Postganglionic neuron
The second neuron of an autonomic pathway, with its cell body in the ganglion and its axon running to the effector organ.
Preganglionic neuron
The first neuron of an autonomic pathway, with its cell body in the CNS and its axon running to the ganglion.
Sympathetic chain ganglia
The paired columns of paravertebral ganglia alongside the vertebral column that house most sympathetic postganglionic cell bodies.
Sympathetic division
The "fight or flight" division of the ANS; thoracolumbar in origin, it mobilizes the body for exertion.
Thoracolumbar outflow
The origin of sympathetic fibers from the lateral horns of spinal segments T1 through L2.

Check yourself

1. What kind of tissue does the autonomic nervous system control, and how does that differ from what the somatic system controls?
The ANS controls smooth muscle, cardiac muscle, and glands (involuntary); the somatic system controls skeletal muscle (voluntary). The autonomic system is the visceral motor system that runs the organs automatically, below conscious awareness.
2. Name the two divisions of the ANS, give each its nickname, and state which one you are mostly in while resting on the couch.
Sympathetic ("fight or flight") and parasympathetic ("rest and digest"); resting on the couch you are mostly parasympathetic. The parasympathetic division dominates during calm conditions, running digestion and energy storage.
3. From which parts of the CNS does each division emerge, and what are the two "outflow" nicknames those origins produce?
Sympathetic = thoracolumbar outflow (T1–L2); parasympathetic = craniosacral outflow (brainstem CN III, VII, IX, X and S2–S4). The exit point from the CNS is the quickest way to tell the two divisions apart.
4. A single organ receives nerves from both divisions that push it in opposite directions. What is this arrangement called, and how does the body use it to set, say, heart rate?
Dual innervation. Because both divisions fire at a background level (autonomic tone), the body sets heart rate by shifting the balance — dialing sympathetic up and vagal (parasympathetic) down speeds it, and the reverse slows it.
5. How many neurons carry a signal from the CNS to an autonomic target, and what is the relay point between them called?
Two — a preganglionic and a postganglionic neuron — relaying at a ganglion. This is a defining feature of the ANS; the somatic system uses only one neuron from the cord all the way to the muscle.
6. Compare where the ganglia sit in each division, and explain why a sympathetic response is body-wide while a parasympathetic one is localized.
Sympathetic ganglia sit near the spinal cord; parasympathetic ganglia sit at the target organ. Sympathetic preganglionic fibers diverge onto many postganglionic neurons, spreading the signal widely; parasympathetic pathways show little divergence, so effects stay discrete.
7. The adrenal medulla is described as a "modified sympathetic ganglion." What does it do that makes fight or flight outlast the nerve signals?
It releases epinephrine (and some NE) directly into the blood as a hormone. Circulating epinephrine keeps acting for minutes after the nerve firing stops, which is why you stay shaky and racing after a scare passes.
8. List four organ changes of the fight-or-flight response and, for each, say why it aids survival.
For example: heart rate rises (delivers oxygen faster), airways dilate (more airflow), pupils dilate (better vision), liver releases glucose (fuel), blood shifts to muscle, digestion halts (saves energy). Every change turns up resources for exertion and turns down anything that can wait.
9. Which two neurotransmitters run the ANS, and which fibers use each? Note the location of the two exceptions.
Acetylcholine and norepinephrine. All preganglionic fibers and all parasympathetic postganglionic fibers use ACh; most sympathetic postganglionic fibers use NE. The exceptions: sympathetic fibers to sweat glands use ACh, and the adrenal medulla releases epinephrine into the blood.
10. Norepinephrine constricts blood vessels but relaxes the airways. How can one chemical do opposite things?
The receptor decides the effect, not the chemical. NE on alpha-1 receptors constricts vessels; the same NE (and epinephrine) on beta-2 receptors relaxes bronchioles. This receptor logic is why drugs like beta-blockers and albuterol can be so selective.
11. Trace the baroreceptor reflex when blood pressure suddenly rises: sensor, pathway, control center, and the autonomic correction.
Baroreceptors in the carotid sinus and aortic arch fire faster → signal travels via CN IX and X to the cardiovascular center in the medulla → the medulla raises parasympathetic (vagal) output and lowers sympathetic output → heart slows and vessels dilate, so pressure falls back to normal. It is a classic negative feedback loop — the response opposes the change.
12. Give three ways the autonomic system differs from the somatic system, and name the self-governing "second brain" in the gut wall.
Autonomic: involuntary, two neurons, targets smooth/cardiac muscle and glands, uses ACh or NE, and can excite or inhibit. Somatic: voluntary, one neuron, targets skeletal muscle, uses ACh only, always excitatory. The gut's own network is the enteric nervous system. The autonomic ability to actively inhibit (for example, the vagus slowing the heart) is its key advantage over the always-excitatory somatic system.
Anatomy & Physiology · Chapter 16

The Neurological Exam

A skilled clinician can find the exact spot where the nervous system has broken — a single nerve, a strip of spinal cord, one side of the brain — using nothing but a pin, a tuning fork, a flashlight, and a set of well-chosen questions. That is the quiet magic of the neurological exam: it turns the outside of a patient into a window on the inside. In this chapter you will walk through its five parts in the same order a clinician does, from the thinking cortex down to the walking feet. Along the way you will learn the single habit that makes it all work — localization, reasoning from a finding back to the piece of the nervous system that produced it.

By the end of this chapter you can

  • Explain the purpose of the neurological exam — to localize a lesion at the bedside — and name its five main parts in order.
  • Perform the mental status exam and match the major language areas (Broca’s and Wernicke’s) to the specific aphasia that a lesion in each produces.
  • List the twelve cranial nerves and describe the quick bedside maneuver that tests each one.
  • Use dermatome landmarks to localize a sensory level, and separate the two ascending sensory pathways — the spinothalamic tract and the dorsal columns.
  • Grade muscle strength on the 0–5 scale and distinguish upper from lower motor neuron weakness.
  • Carry out the cerebellar coordination tests and interpret dysmetria, ataxia, and a positive Romberg sign.
  • Reason from any single finding back to the region of the nervous system responsible for it.

Section 1The exam, part by part

An exam that localizes

Every other exam in medicine asks what is wrong. The neurological exam adds a second, sharper question: where. Because the nervous system is wired in an orderly, mapped-out way — this region for language, that nerve for the tongue, this strip of skin for one spinal segment — a careful examiner can take a scattering of findings and point to a single place. That skill is called localization, and it is the whole game. A weak right arm plus slurred speech plus a drooping right face localizes to the left side of the brain; a numb band around the belly localizes to a particular level of the spinal cord. The exam is, in a sense, a scanner made of questions — and long before any MRI existed, this is how neurologists found the lesion.

The five parts, from top to bottom

The exam is easiest to remember as a journey down the nervous system, from the highest thinking to the simplest walking. Each part probes a different level, so a deficit in one part tells you roughly where to look.

PartWhat it testsLocalizes to
1. Mental statusConsciousness, orientation, memory, languageThe cerebral cortex
2. Cranial nervesVision, eye movement, face, hearing, tongue — the twelve nervesThe brainstem and the nerves themselves
3. SensoryTouch, pain, temperature, vibration, positionA spinal nerve, the cord, or a sensory pathway
4. MotorStrength, tone, muscle bulk (and reflexes)The motor pathway — upper vs lower motor neuron
5. Coordination & gaitSmoothness, balance, walkingThe cerebellum

Notice how neatly the parts stack. Mental status and language live in the cortex at the top; the cranial nerves emerge from the brainstem in the middle; sensory and motor testing read the long tracts of the spinal cord and the peripheral nerves; and coordination checks the cerebellum tucked at the back. Reflexes are usually folded into the motor exam. Run the parts in order and you have sampled the nervous system from crown to sole.

Findings first, scanner second. A good neuro exam predicts what the scan will show before it is taken. Imaging confirms a location; the exam chooses the location to image. That is why clinicians still learn to do it by hand.

Tap through the five parts in the interactive and watch each one light up the region it localizes.

Interactive Tap each of the five parts of the exam and see the region it localizes. Open full ↑
Key points
  • The neuro exam exists to localize a lesion at the bedside, reasoning from a finding back to a region.
  • Its five parts — mental status, cranial nerves, sensory, motor, coordination & gait — sample the nervous system top to bottom.
  • Each part maps to a level: cortex, brainstem, cord/nerves, motor pathway, and cerebellum.

Section 2Mental status & language

Reading the cortex

The mental status exam checks the highest-order work of the brain — the cerebral cortex. It samples consciousness (is the patient alert?), orientation (do they know person, place, and time?), attention, memory, and, most revealing of all, language. You do not need a formal test to start: a two-minute conversation already tells you whether someone is confused, whether they can find their words, and whether they follow what you say. When something is off, the pattern of the deficit points to a specific patch of cortex, because different jobs live in different places.

The two language areas — and the aphasias

Language is famously lateralized — in nearly everyone it lives in the left hemisphere — and it is split between two areas with two different jobs. Broca’s area, in the frontal lobe just ahead of the motor strip, produces speech: it builds words and grammar. Wernicke’s area, in the temporal lobe near the hearing cortex, comprehends language: it attaches meaning to words. Damage each, and you get a strikingly different aphasia — a loss of language — that tells you exactly which area failed.

AreaIts jobIf damaged
Broca’s areaProduces speech (words, grammar)Broca’s (expressive) aphasia — slow, effortful, telegraphic speech; comprehension is intact, and the patient knows it is wrong and is frustrated
Wernicke’s areaComprehends language (meaning)Wernicke’s (receptive) aphasia — fluent but meaningless “word salad”; comprehension is lost, and the patient often does not realize anything is wrong
Prefrontal cortexExecutive function — judgment, planning, personality, impulse controlDisinhibition, poor judgment, personality change (the classic Phineas Gage story)
HippocampusForms new long-term memoriesAnterograde amnesia — cannot form new memories, though old ones survive

A quick way to hold the contrast: Broca’s is Broken speech — the words come out laboriously, but the mind behind them is clear and aware. Wernicke’s is Wordy nonsense — the speech flows easily and fluently, but the meaning is gone, both coming in and going out. Testing is simple: to check production, ask the patient to name objects and repeat a phrase; to check comprehension, give a command they must understand (“point to the ceiling, then the door”). To probe short-term memory, name three words and ask for them back a few minutes later — a bedside test of the hippocampus.

Fluent but empty is the danger sign. Because Wernicke’s aphasics speak smoothly and don’t know they are impaired, their deficit is easy to miss — they can be mistaken for confused or even psychotic. Always test comprehension, not just whether the words flow.

Tap each region in the interactive to see the language job it does and the aphasia a lesion there causes.

Interactive Tap a cortical region to see its language job — and the aphasia a lesion there causes. Open full ↑
Key points
  • The mental status exam reads the cortex — consciousness, orientation, memory, and especially language.
  • Language is usually left-lateralized: Broca’s produces speech, Wernicke’s comprehends it.
  • Broca’s aphasia = effortful speech, intact understanding, frustrated patient; Wernicke’s aphasia = fluent nonsense, lost understanding, unaware patient.

Section 3The cranial nerve exam

Twelve nerves, twelve tests

Twelve pairs of cranial nerves leave the underside of the brain and serve the head and neck — smelling, seeing, moving the eyes, feeling the face, hearing, tasting, swallowing, speaking, and turning the head. Because most of them arise from the brainstem, testing them one by one is a way of surveying the brainstem from top to bottom. The exam has a rhythm to it: each nerve has a single, quick bedside maneuver, and a failure points straight to that nerve or to the part of the brainstem it comes from.

NerveHow it is tested
I — OlfactoryIdentify a familiar smell (coffee, mint), eyes closed, one nostril at a time
II — OpticVisual acuity (eye chart), visual fields, and the pupil’s reaction to light
III — OculomotorPupil constriction to light and most eye movements; a blown pupil or drooping lid (ptosis) implicates CN III
IV — TrochlearLook down and in toward the nose; weakness gives vertical double vision on stairs
V — TrigeminalFacial sensation in three zones; clench the jaw to feel the masseter
VI — AbducensLook laterally (outward); failure means the eye cannot turn out
VII — FacialSmile, raise the eyebrows, puff the cheeks; one-sided droop signals a facial-nerve problem (e.g. Bell’s palsy)
VIII — VestibulocochlearHearing (whisper or finger-rub) and balance; tuning-fork tests refine it
IX — GlossopharyngealThe gag reflex and taste at the back of the tongue (tested with CN X)
X — VagusSay “ahh” — the palate and uvula should rise symmetrically; also voice and swallowing
XI — AccessoryShrug the shoulders and turn the head against resistance (trapezius & SCM)
XII — HypoglossalStick out the tongue — it deviates toward the weak (damaged) side

Small signs, precise meaning

The beauty of the cranial nerve exam is how much a tiny asymmetry can tell you. A tongue that pokes out crooked, drifting toward the bad side, localizes to CN XII on that side. A soft palate that lifts unevenly points to CN X. A pupil that will not shrink to light, paired with a droopy lid, whispers CN III — and in the right setting that is an emergency. Generations of students have kept the twelve nerves in order with the old mnemonic “On Old Olympus’ Towering Tops, A Finn And German Viewed Some Hops” — each first letter marking a nerve from I to XII.

Deviates toward the lesion. Two classic asymmetries point at the damaged side: the tongue (CN XII) deviates toward the weak side, and in a facial nerve (CN VII) palsy the mouth droops on the weak side while the good side pulls the face away from it. Watch which way things sag.

Tap any of the twelve nerves in the interactive to see how it is tested and what a failure would mean.

Interactive Tap any of the twelve cranial nerves to see how it’s tested and what a failure means. Open full ↑
Key points
  • The twelve cranial nerves serve the head and neck; most arise from the brainstem, so testing them surveys the brainstem.
  • Each nerve has a single quick maneuver — smell, eye chart, jaw clench, “ahh,” shoulder shrug, tongue out, and the rest.
  • The tongue (XII) and facial droop (VII) deviate toward the damaged side.

Section 4The sensory exam

Skin comes in strips

Each spinal nerve carries sensation from a specific band of skin called a dermatome. Stacked together, the dermatomes tile the whole body like the stripes of a map, and that map is astonishingly useful: if a patient has numbness in a particular strip, you can read the strip backward to the exact spinal level that supplies it. You do not have to memorize all thirty-odd dermatomes — a handful of reliable landmarks anchors the whole scheme, and the rest fall into place between them.

LevelLandmark
C6The thumb and lateral forearm
C7The middle finger (C7 also drives the triceps)
C8The little finger and medial forearm
T4The nipple line
T10The umbilicus (belly button)
L1The groin / inguinal region
L4The medial knee and shin (L4 drives the knee-jerk)
L5The top of the foot and big toe (L5 dorsiflexes the foot)
S1The little toe and sole (S1 drives the ankle-jerk)

Two roads to the brain

Sensation is not one channel but two, and the neuro exam tests them separately because they travel by different routes and cross the midline in different places. Pain and temperature ride the spinothalamic tract, which crosses to the opposite side almost immediately, right within the spinal cord. Fine touch, vibration, and position sense (proprioception) ride the dorsal columns, which stay on the same side all the way up and cross only high in the medulla. Because they cross in different spots, a single cord injury can knock out one sense on one side of the body and the other sense on the other side — a striking pattern that instantly tells you the two pathways are involved.

PathwayCarriesCrossesTested with
Spinothalamic tractPain & temperatureAt the cord (right away)Pinprick, warm/cold
Dorsal columnsTouch, vibration, positionIn the medulla (high up)Tuning fork, toe-position
Anchor the trunk with two numbers. T4 at the nipple, T10 at the umbilicus — remember just those two and you can estimate any thoracic level by counting ribs up or down. A band of numbness at the belly button says “think T10.”

Tap a landmark in the interactive to pinpoint its spinal level, and compare the two ascending pathways.

Interactive Tap a dermatome landmark to pinpoint its spinal level. Open full ↑
Key points
  • A dermatome is the skin strip served by one spinal nerve; numbness in a strip localizes to that spinal level.
  • Landmarks to memorize: C6 thumb, C7 middle finger, C8 little finger, T4 nipple, T10 umbilicus, L1 groin, S1 sole.
  • Two pathways: spinothalamic (pain/temp, crosses at the cord) and dorsal columns (touch/vibration/position, crosses in the medulla).

Section 5The motor exam

Grading strength, zero to five

The motor exam begins by measuring strength, muscle group by muscle group, against a standard scale that runs from 0 to 5. The scale is anchored at both ends by the pull of gravity: a grade of 3 is the pivot point, because 3 means the limb can just lift against gravity but not against any added resistance. Above that, muscles hold against your push; below it, they cannot even beat gravity. The scale is worth learning precisely, because writing “4/5” in a chart says something exact that another clinician can act on.

GradeWhat it means
5Normal power — full strength against full resistance
4Moves against gravity and some resistance, but weaker than normal
3Moves against gravity only — can lift the limb, but not against resistance
2Moves only with gravity removed (side to side)
1A flicker or trace of contraction, no joint movement
0No contraction at all — complete paralysis

Where does the weakness come from?

The motor pathway runs in two relays. The upper motor neuron (UMN) travels from the motor cortex down the corticospinal tract to the spinal cord; the lower motor neuron (LMN) runs from the cord out along a peripheral nerve to the muscle. Weakness can come from either relay — and the exam tells them apart by the company the weakness keeps. UMN damage releases the cord below it, so the muscle becomes spastic and over-reflexive. LMN damage cuts the muscle’s only lifeline, so it goes flaccid, wastes away, and twitches. Reading tone, reflexes, and muscle bulk turns “this arm is weak” into “the lesion is here.”

SignUpper motor neuronLower motor neuron
LocationBrain → spinal cordSpinal cord → muscle
ToneIncreased (spastic)Decreased (flaccid)
ReflexesIncreased (hyper)Decreased or absent
Babinski signPresent (big toe goes up)Absent
AtrophyLittle, and lateMarked, and early
FasciculationsAbsentPresent (visible twitches)
Up means upper. The Babinski sign — a big toe that turns up when you stroke the sole — is a hallmark of an upper motor neuron lesion. (It is also normal in babies, before the corticospinal tract has finished myelinating.) In a healthy adult, the toes should curl down.

Slide through the strength scale in the interactive, then switch to compare the two motor-neuron patterns.

Interactive Slide the 0–5 strength scale, then compare upper- and lower-motor-neuron signs. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Strength is graded 0–5; grade 3 is the pivot — movement against gravity but no added resistance.
  • The pathway has two relays: upper (cortex → cord) and lower (cord → muscle) motor neurons.
  • UMN = spastic, hyper-reflexic, Babinski present, little atrophy; LMN = flaccid, hypo-reflexic, early atrophy, fasciculations.

Section 6Coordination & gait

The smoothness check

Strength moves a limb; the cerebellum makes the movement smooth, accurate, and well-timed. The final part of the exam tests that polish. A patient can be perfectly strong yet unable to touch their own nose cleanly, because the cerebellum is what fine-tunes aim and steadies balance. When it fails, movements overshoot, wobble, and stagger — a cluster of signs grouped under the word ataxia. Each cerebellar test isolates one facet of coordination.

TestWhat it checksAbnormal finding
Finger-to-noseAccuracy of aimDysmetria (over/undershoot) and an intention tremor that worsens near the target
Rapid alternating movementsReversing a movement smoothlyDysdiadochokinesia — clumsy, irregular alternation
Heel-to-shinLower-limb coordinationA wobbling, off-track heel — limb ataxia
Romberg testProprioception (position sense)Steady with eyes open but falls with eyes closed — a sensory (dorsal-column) problem
GaitOverall balance and walkingA wide-based, staggering (ataxic) gait points to the cerebellum
Pronator driftSubtle upper-motor-neuron weaknessOne arm slowly drifts down and pronates — an early UMN sign

The Romberg trap

One test in this group is a clever detective, not a cerebellar test at all. In the Romberg test, the patient stands with feet together and closes their eyes. Balance depends on three inputs — vision, the inner ear, and proprioception (the dorsal-column sense of where your limbs are). Take vision away by closing the eyes, and the body must lean on the other two. A patient who is steady with eyes open but sways or falls with eyes closed has a positive Romberg — and that points to a sensory (dorsal-column) problem, not the cerebellum. A true cerebellar patient is unsteady either way, because the cerebellum never depended on the eyes to begin with. It is a small test that neatly separates two very different causes of imbalance.

Romberg is a sensory test in disguise. Falling only with the eyes shut means the problem is proprioception, not coordination. If someone is unsteady even with eyes open, look to the cerebellum instead.

Tap each test in the interactive to see what an abnormal result reveals.

Interactive Tap each cerebellar test to see what an abnormal result reveals. Open full ↑
Key points
  • The cerebellum makes movement smooth and accurate; its failure produces ataxia — dysmetria, intention tremor, and a wide-based gait.
  • Key tests: finger-to-nose, rapid alternating movements, heel-to-shin, gait, and pronator drift.
  • A positive Romberg (falls only with eyes closed) is a proprioceptive/dorsal-column sign, not a cerebellar one.

The chapter in one breath

  • The neuro exam exists to localize a lesion at the bedside, in five parts that sample the nervous system top to bottom.
  • Mental status reads the cortex; language is left-lateralized, with Broca’s (effortful speech, aware patient) and Wernicke’s (fluent nonsense, unaware patient) aphasias.
  • The twelve cranial nerves survey the brainstem, each with one quick maneuver; the tongue and facial droop deviate toward the lesion.
  • A dermatome localizes a sensory level (T4 nipple, T10 umbilicus, S1 sole); pain/temperature travel the spinothalamic tract, touch/vibration/position the dorsal columns.
  • Strength is graded 0–5; upper motor neuron lesions are spastic and hyper-reflexic with a Babinski, while lower are flaccid, wasted, and twitchy.
  • The cerebellum gives movement its smoothness; its failure is ataxia, and a positive Romberg is a sensory sign, not a cerebellar one.
  • Every finding is a clue to a place — the whole exam is the art of reasoning from a sign back to a region.

ReferenceWords worth knowing

A quick reference for the terms in this chapter — skim it now, or circle back whenever a word trips you up.

Aphasia
A loss or impairment of language caused by damage to a language area of the cortex; the type depends on which area failed.
Ataxia
Uncoordinated, clumsy movement — overshooting, wobbling, and staggering — typically from cerebellar dysfunction.
Babinski sign
Upward movement of the big toe when the sole is stroked; a sign of an upper motor neuron lesion in adults (and normal in infants).
Broca’s area
The frontal-lobe region that produces speech; damage causes an effortful, telegraphic expressive aphasia with intact comprehension.
Cranial nerves
The twelve paired nerves that leave the underside of the brain to serve the head and neck.
Decussation
The crossing of a nerve pathway from one side of the body to the other (for example, the dorsal columns crossing in the medulla).
Dermatome
The strip of skin whose sensation is carried by a single spinal nerve; the dermatomes tile the body as a localizing map.
Dorsal columns
The ascending pathway for fine touch, vibration, and position sense; it crosses high in the medulla.
Dysdiadochokinesia
Clumsy, irregular rapid alternating movements — a cerebellar sign.
Dysmetria
Inaccurate reach — overshooting or undershooting a target — seen on finger-to-nose testing in cerebellar disease.
Flaccid
Limp, with decreased muscle tone; characteristic of a lower motor neuron lesion.
Localization
Reasoning from the pattern of exam findings back to the specific region of the nervous system responsible.
Lower motor neuron (LMN)
The neuron running from the spinal cord out along a peripheral nerve to the muscle; damage gives flaccid weakness, atrophy, and fasciculations.
Mental status exam
The part of the neuro exam that tests cortical function — consciousness, orientation, attention, memory, and language.
Pronator drift
Slow downward drift and inward turning of an outstretched arm with eyes closed; an early sign of upper motor neuron weakness.
Proprioception
The sense of where one’s limbs and body are in space, carried by the dorsal columns and tested by the Romberg maneuver.
Romberg test
Standing with feet together and eyes closed; a fall indicates a proprioceptive (dorsal-column) deficit, not a cerebellar one.
Spasticity
Increased muscle tone and resistance to movement; characteristic of an upper motor neuron lesion.
Spinothalamic tract
The ascending pathway for pain and temperature; it crosses to the opposite side almost immediately, within the spinal cord.
Upper motor neuron (UMN)
The neuron running from the motor cortex down the corticospinal tract to the cord; damage gives spastic weakness, brisk reflexes, and a Babinski sign.
Wernicke’s area
The temporal-lobe region that comprehends language; damage causes fluent but meaningless speech with lost understanding.

Check yourself

1. What is the single purpose that unifies the whole neurological exam, and what is the name for the skill it depends on?
To find where in the nervous system the problem is — the skill of localization. Because the nervous system is mapped in an orderly way, a pattern of findings can be traced back to a specific region, which is what every part of the exam is really testing.
2. Name the five parts of the neuro exam in order, and give the region each mainly localizes to.
Mental status (cortex) → cranial nerves (brainstem) → sensory (spinal nerve/cord/pathways) → motor (upper vs lower motor neuron) → coordination & gait (cerebellum). The order walks the nervous system from highest thinking down to walking.
3. A patient speaks slowly and with great effort, in short telegraphic phrases, but clearly understands everything you say and is visibly frustrated. Which language area is damaged, and what is this aphasia called?
Broca’s area — Broca’s (expressive) aphasia. Broca’s area produces speech, so its loss makes output effortful, but comprehension (Wernicke’s) is intact — which is exactly why the patient knows something is wrong.
4. A different patient speaks fluently and effortlessly, but the sentences are meaningless “word salad,” and the patient seems unaware anything is wrong. Which area is damaged, and why the lack of awareness?
Wernicke’s area — Wernicke’s (receptive) aphasia. Wernicke’s comprehends language, so its loss removes the ability to understand speech — including the patient’s own — leaving them fluent but oblivious to the errors.
5. How would you quickly test cranial nerve XII (hypoglossal), and which way does the tongue point if one side is weak?
Ask the patient to stick out the tongue; it deviates toward the weak (damaged) side. The strong side pushes the tongue across toward the weak side, a small asymmetry that localizes the lesion.
6. You want to test cranial nerve X (vagus) at the bedside in one move. What do you ask the patient to do, and what should you see?
Ask the patient to say “ahh” and watch the soft palate and uvula rise symmetrically. An uneven lift signals a vagus (CN X) problem; the vagus also governs voice and swallowing.
7. A patient has a band of numbness right around the umbilicus (belly button). Which spinal level does this localize to, and what landmark sits at T4?
T10 — the umbilicus; T4 sits at the nipple line. These two trunk landmarks let you estimate any thoracic level by counting ribs up or down from them.
8. Pain-and-temperature and touch-vibration-position travel by two different pathways that cross the midline in different places. Name each pathway and where it crosses.
Pain & temperature → spinothalamic tract, crosses at the cord (right away); touch, vibration & position → dorsal columns, cross in the medulla (high up). Their different crossing points explain why one cord injury can affect the two senses on opposite sides of the body.
9. On the 0–5 strength scale, what does a grade of 3 mean, and why is 3 the crucial dividing line?
Grade 3 = movement against gravity only, but not against added resistance. Three is the pivot of the scale: at or above 3 the muscle can lift the limb against gravity; below 3 it cannot even beat gravity.
10. Two weak patients: one has spastic, hyper-reflexic muscles with an upgoing toe; the other has flaccid, wasting muscles that twitch. Classify each as upper or lower motor neuron.
Spastic + hyper-reflexic + Babinski = upper motor neuron; flaccid + atrophy + fasciculations = lower motor neuron. UMN damage releases the cord below it (over-active), while LMN damage cuts the muscle’s lifeline (under-active and wasting).
11. What exactly is the Babinski sign, and what does its presence in an adult indicate?
The big toe moves up when the sole is stroked; in an adult it indicates an upper motor neuron lesion. A healthy adult’s toes curl down; the upgoing response is normal only in infants before the corticospinal tract has myelinated.
12. A patient stands steadily with eyes open but falls when they close their eyes (positive Romberg). Is this a cerebellar problem? Explain.
No — a positive Romberg is a proprioceptive (dorsal-column) sensory problem, not cerebellar. Closing the eyes removes vision, exposing a loss of position sense; a true cerebellar patient is unsteady even with eyes open, because balance there never relied on vision.
Anatomy & Physiology · Chapter 17

The Endocrine System

The nervous system is the body's fast messenger — electrical, precise, over in milliseconds. The endocrine system is its patient counterpart: a network of glands that pours chemical messengers called hormones into the bloodstream to be carried everywhere at once. Its signals are slower to start and slower to fade, but they reach every corner of the body and can last for hours, days, or a lifetime. This is the system that decides how fast you burn fuel, how tall you grow, how you handle a stressful morning, and how much sugar sits in your blood after breakfast. In this chapter you will meet the major glands, learn the two very different ways a hormone can deliver its message to a cell, and trace the elegant feedback loops that keep the whole system in balance.

By the end of this chapter you can

  • Distinguish the endocrine system from the nervous system and from exocrine glands, and locate the major endocrine glands.
  • Define a hormone and explain why it affects only certain target cells despite reaching the whole body.
  • Contrast lipid-soluble and water-soluble hormones by where their receptors sit and how quickly they act.
  • Describe the hypothalamus–pituitary relationship and name the hormones of the anterior and posterior pituitary.
  • Explain how insulin and glucagon regulate blood glucose as an antagonistic pair.
  • Diagram the thyroid axis as a negative feedback loop and predict the signs of an under- or over-active thyroid.
  • Identify the layers of the adrenal gland and match each to the hormones it secretes.
  • Recognize how negative feedback underlies the control of nearly every hormone in the body.

Section 1The endocrine glands

Two systems that share the job of control

Keeping a body coordinated takes two communication systems working side by side. The nervous system sends fast electrical signals down wires (neurons) to precise targets — a message arrives in milliseconds and stops the moment the signal stops. The endocrine system works the opposite way. It has no wires; instead its glands release chemical messengers called hormones directly into the blood, which carries them to the entire body. The trade-off is speed for reach and duration: a hormone may take seconds to minutes to act, but its effect can be broad and long-lasting. Think of the nervous system as a phone call to one person and the endocrine system as a message broadcast to everyone — even though only the people it concerns will act on it.

Endocrine versus exocrine

The word endocrine literally means "to secrete within." Endocrine glands are ductless — they release their hormones straight into the surrounding fluid and blood. This is the key contrast with exocrine glands, which secrete their products through a duct onto a surface: sweat glands, salivary glands, and the tear glands are all exocrine. A few organs do both jobs. The pancreas is the classic example — its exocrine tissue drips digestive enzymes into a duct that empties into the intestine, while scattered islands of endocrine tissue release insulin and glucagon into the blood.

The cast of glands

The pure endocrine glands — organs whose main job is making hormones — include the pituitary, pineal, thyroid, parathyroid, and adrenal glands. The hypothalamus of the brain sits at the top of the chain of command, and several other organs contain endocrine tissue as a side job: the pancreas, the gonads (ovaries and testes), the thymus, and even the heart, kidneys, stomach, and fat tissue all release hormones. Learn the headline glands first; the part-time players will come up in their own chapters.

GlandSignature hormone(s)Main job
HypothalamusReleasing & inhibiting hormonesCommands the pituitary; links brain to endocrine system
PituitaryGH, TSH, ACTH, FSH, LH, ADH, oxytocinRelays orders to many other glands
ThyroidThyroid hormone (T₃/T₄), calcitoninSets metabolic rate; lowers blood calcium
ParathyroidParathyroid hormone (PTH)Raises blood calcium
AdrenalCortisol, aldosterone, epinephrineStress response, salt/water balance
Pancreas (islets)Insulin, glucagonRegulates blood glucose
PinealMelatoninSets the sleep–wake rhythm
GonadsEstrogen/progesterone; testosteroneReproduction and sex characteristics
Everywhere, but not everyone. A hormone travels to every cell in the body, yet it changes only the cells that carry the matching receptor for it — its target cells. It is a lock-and-key arrangement: the hormone is the key, and only cells with the right lock can open the door. That single idea explains how one bloodstream can carry dozens of hormones without them all acting on everything at once.

Tap each gland in the interactive to see where it sits and what it makes.

Interactive Tap a gland to meet it and learn the hormones it sends into the blood. Open full ↑
Key points
  • The endocrine system controls the body with hormones carried in the blood — slower but broader and longer-lasting than nervous signals.
  • Endocrine glands are ductless and secrete into blood; exocrine glands secrete through ducts onto a surface.
  • Headline glands: hypothalamus, pituitary, thyroid, parathyroid, adrenal, pancreas, pineal, gonads.
  • A hormone acts only on its target cells — those bearing the matching receptor.

Section 2How hormones act

Chemistry decides the delivery

Hormones fall into two broad chemical families, and the difference between them is not a technicality — it decides exactly how the hormone talks to a cell. Most hormones are amino-acid based: peptides and proteins (such as insulin and growth hormone) and amines (such as the adrenal catecholamines). These are water-soluble but lipid-insoluble, so they cannot cross the fatty cell membrane. The second family, the steroid hormones (cortisol, aldosterone, and the sex hormones), is built from cholesterol; these are lipid-soluble and slip through the membrane with ease. That one property — can it cross the membrane or not — splits all of hormone action into two clean stories.

Lipid-soluble hormones: walk right in

Because a lipid-soluble hormone can pass through the membrane, it does not need to knock from outside. It diffuses into the cell and binds an intracellular receptor, usually inside the nucleus. The hormone–receptor pair then acts directly on DNA, switching specific genes on or off. The cell responds by making new proteins — often new enzymes that change how it behaves. Because building proteins takes time, these hormones are slow to start, but because those proteins linger, their effects are long-lasting. Steroid hormones such as cortisol work this way.

Water-soluble hormones: knock from outside

A water-soluble hormone cannot enter the cell, so it binds a receptor on the cell surface instead. That binding does not reach the DNA directly; it triggers a relay inside the cell using a second messenger — most famously cyclic AMP (cAMP). The hormone is the "first messenger" that stays outside; the second messenger carries the signal onward, activating a cascade of enzymes already sitting in the cytoplasm. Because those enzymes are pre-built and simply switched on, the response is fast — but it also fades quickly once the hormone is gone. Peptide hormones like insulin and glucagon, and the amine epinephrine, act through this surface-receptor route.

FeatureLipid-soluble (steroid)Water-soluble (peptide/amine)
Crosses the membrane?Yes — enters the cellNo — stays outside
Receptor locationInside the cell (often the nucleus)On the cell surface
MechanismTurns genes on/off → new proteinsSecond messenger (cAMP) → enzyme cascade
Speed of onsetSlow (hours)Fast (seconds to minutes)
DurationLong-lastingBrief
ExamplesCortisol, aldosterone, estrogen, testosteroneInsulin, glucagon, growth hormone, epinephrine
Thyroid hormone breaks its own rule. Thyroid hormone is built from an amino acid (it is technically an amine), which would put it in the water-soluble camp — yet it is lipid-soluble and acts like a steroid, entering the cell and working on genes. It is the famous exception: an amine that behaves like a steroid, which is exactly why its effects are slow and enduring.

Switch between a lipid-soluble and a water-soluble hormone in the interactive and step through each pathway.

Interactive Switch between hormone types and step through how each one delivers its message. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Hormones are either amino-acid based (water-soluble) or steroid (lipid-soluble); solubility decides the mechanism.
  • Lipid-soluble hormones enter the cell, bind intracellular receptors, and change gene expression — slow but lasting.
  • Water-soluble hormones bind surface receptors and use a second messenger (cAMP) — fast but brief.
  • Thyroid hormone is the exception: an amine that is lipid-soluble and acts like a steroid.

Section 3The hypothalamus & pituitary axis

The real chain of command

The pituitary gland is a pea-sized structure hanging beneath the brain, and it is often called the "master gland" because it directs so many other endocrine organs. But the pituitary has a boss of its own: the hypothalamus, the small region of brain just above it. The hypothalamus is where the nervous and endocrine systems physically meet — it takes in signals from the brain and translates them into hormonal commands to the pituitary. So the true master is the hypothalamus; the pituitary is its powerful deputy. The gland comes in two lobes that could hardly be more different in how they are wired.

The posterior pituitary: a storage terminal

The posterior pituitary makes no hormones of its own. It is really nervous tissue — an extension of the hypothalamus. Two hormones, antidiuretic hormone (ADH) and oxytocin, are actually manufactured by hypothalamic neurons, then travel down their axons to be stored in the posterior pituitary and released on demand. ADH tells the kidneys to conserve water, concentrating the urine; oxytocin drives uterine contractions in childbirth and milk let-down during nursing. The posterior lobe, then, is a release terminal, not a factory.

The anterior pituitary: a true factory under remote control

The anterior pituitary is glandular tissue that makes and secretes its own hormones, but it does so only when the hypothalamus tells it to. The hypothalamus sends tiny releasing and inhibiting hormones through a private set of blood vessels — the hypophyseal portal system — that carries them the short distance to the anterior lobe. Several anterior pituitary hormones are tropic hormones, meaning their job is to command other glands: TSH drives the thyroid, ACTH drives the adrenal cortex, and FSH and LH drive the gonads. Others act directly on body tissues: growth hormone (GH) on nearly everything, and prolactin (PRL) on the breast.

HormoneLobeTarget & effect
Growth hormone (GH)AnteriorBody tissues — growth and metabolism
Thyroid-stimulating (TSH)AnteriorThyroid — make thyroid hormone
Adrenocorticotropic (ACTH)AnteriorAdrenal cortex — make cortisol
Follicle-stimulating (FSH)AnteriorGonads — egg/sperm maturation
Luteinizing (LH)AnteriorGonads — ovulation, sex hormones
Prolactin (PRL)AnteriorBreast — milk production
Antidiuretic (ADH)PosteriorKidney — conserve water
OxytocinPosteriorUterus & breast — contraction, let-down
FLAT PIG. A time-honored memory hook for the six anterior pituitary hormones: FSH, LH, ACTH, TSH are the tropic four (FLAT), and PRL, GH act directly (the PIG, with an i for insertion). The two posterior hormones, ADH and oxytocin, are only stored there — keep them in a separate mental bucket.

Tap a pituitary hormone in the interactive to trace the path from the brain to its target organ.

Interactive Tap a hormone to trace the command from hypothalamus to pituitary to its target. Open full ↑
Key points
  • The hypothalamus is the true master; it controls the pituitary, which controls many other glands.
  • The posterior pituitary only stores and releases ADH and oxytocin, both made in the hypothalamus.
  • The anterior pituitary makes six hormones under hypothalamic control; four are tropic (TSH, ACTH, FSH, LH).
  • Remember them with FLAT PIG.

Section 4Blood glucose regulation

Two hormones from one gland, pulling opposite ways

Blood glucose is one of the body's most tightly guarded values, and the pancreas guards it with a pair of hormones that oppose each other — an antagonistic pair. Scattered through the pancreas are clusters of endocrine cells called the pancreatic islets (islets of Langerhans). Their beta cells make insulin, and their alpha cells make glucagon. One hormone lowers blood sugar and the other raises it, so the pancreas can nudge glucose in either direction as needed — a beautiful example of negative feedback with a gas pedal and a brake.

Insulin: the "fed-state" hormone that lowers glucose

After a meal, blood glucose rises. The beta cells sense the rise and release insulin, whose whole purpose is to lower blood glucose. Insulin tells body cells — especially muscle and fat — to open up and take glucose in, and it tells the liver to store the surplus as glycogen. As cells absorb the sugar, blood glucose falls back to normal, and insulin release quiets down. A useful shorthand: insulin is the hormone of the fed state, moving fuel out of the blood and into storage.

Glucagon: the "fasting" hormone that raises glucose

Between meals, blood glucose drifts down. Now the alpha cells release glucagon, which does the reverse of insulin: it raises blood glucose. Glucagon tells the liver to break glycogen back down into glucose (glycogenolysis) and to manufacture fresh glucose from other molecules (gluconeogenesis), releasing that sugar into the blood. Glucose climbs back to the set point, and glucagon quiets down. Glucagon is the hormone of the fasting state, pulling fuel out of storage and back into the blood.

FeatureInsulinGlucagon
Made byBeta cellsAlpha cells
Released whenBlood glucose is high (after eating)Blood glucose is low (fasting)
Effect on glucoseLowers itRaises it
Action on the liverStore glucose as glycogenBreak glycogen down; make new glucose
Overall roleFed state — storageFasting state — mobilization
Insulin stands alone. Many hormones can raise blood glucose — glucagon, cortisol, epinephrine, and growth hormone all do — but insulin is the only hormone that lowers it. That is why losing insulin is so catastrophic in type 1 diabetes (the beta cells are destroyed) and why insulin resistance drives type 2 diabetes (the cells stop listening to insulin). With no working way to bring glucose down, it climbs to dangerous levels.

Pick a situation in the interactive — a meal or a fast — and watch insulin or glucagon pull glucose back to normal.

Interactive Choose a situation and watch insulin or glucagon return blood glucose to its set point. Open full ↑
Key points
  • The pancreatic islets regulate blood glucose with an antagonistic pair of hormones.
  • Insulin (beta cells) lowers glucose after a meal by driving uptake and glycogen storage.
  • Glucagon (alpha cells) raises glucose during fasting by breaking down glycogen and making new glucose.
  • Insulin is the only hormone that lowers blood glucose; its loss or resistance causes diabetes.

Section 5The thyroid axis

The gland that sets your idle speed

The thyroid gland, a butterfly-shaped organ wrapped around the front of the windpipe, makes thyroid hormone — the two forms are T₃ and T₄, named for the number of iodine atoms each carries (which is why the diet needs iodine to make them). Thyroid hormone sets the body's basal metabolic rate: the speed at which nearly every cell burns fuel and produces heat. Turn it up and the whole body runs faster and hotter; turn it down and everything slows. It is, in effect, the idle setting of the engine.

The feedback loop that keeps it steady

Thyroid hormone is regulated by a three-step chain called the hypothalamus–pituitary–thyroid (HPT) axis, and it is a textbook negative feedback loop. The hypothalamus releases TRH (thyrotropin-releasing hormone), which tells the anterior pituitary to release TSH (thyroid-stimulating hormone), which tells the thyroid to release T₃ and T₄. Here is the elegant part: the thyroid hormones then loop back and inhibit both the hypothalamus and the pituitary, dialing down TRH and TSH. When thyroid hormone is plentiful, that brake keeps it from climbing further; when thyroid hormone falls, the brake releases and TSH rises to push the thyroid harder.
