When the thyroid does too little or too much

Because thyroid hormone sets the metabolic pace, its disorders read like opposite ends of a dial. In hypothyroidism (too little), everything slows: weight gain, fatigue, feeling cold, dry skin, and a sluggish heart rate. In hyperthyroidism (too much), everything speeds up: weight loss despite a big appetite, feeling hot, anxiety, and a racing heart. Both can enlarge the gland into a visible goiter. The feedback loop is also a diagnostic gift: in primary hypothyroidism, the failing thyroid produces little hormone, so the brake lifts and TSH rises — which is why a high TSH points to an underactive thyroid.

SignHypothyroidism (too little)Hyperthyroidism (too much)
Metabolic rateSlowedRaised
Body weightGainLoss
TemperatureCold intoleranceHeat intolerance
Heart rateSlowFast
Energy & moodFatigue, sluggishnessAnxiety, restlessness
TSH level (primary)HighLow
Read the TSH backwards. Because of negative feedback, TSH usually moves opposite to the thyroid's own output. A high TSH means the pituitary is shouting at a lazy, underactive thyroid; a low TSH means the pituitary has gone quiet because an overactive thyroid is flooding the blood. That single blood test is the first-line screen for thyroid disease.

Run the interactive to see the normal loop, then push the thyroid to do too little or too much.

Interactive See the normal thyroid feedback loop, then an under- and over-active gland. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Thyroid hormone (T₃/T₄, iodine-based) sets the body's basal metabolic rate.
  • The HPT axis — TRH → TSH → T₃/T₄ — is a negative feedback loop; thyroid hormone inhibits TRH and TSH.
  • Hypothyroidism slows the body (weight gain, cold, fatigue); hyperthyroidism speeds it up (weight loss, heat, racing heart).
  • In primary disease, TSH runs opposite to thyroid output — high TSH signals an underactive gland.

Section 6The adrenal gland

Two glands stacked into one

Perched like a cap on top of each kidney, the adrenal gland is really two different glands fused together. The outer adrenal cortex makes steroid hormones and is essential for life; the inner adrenal medulla makes the catecholamines epinephrine and norepinephrine and is a direct extension of the sympathetic nervous system. The two respond on very different timescales — the medulla in seconds, the cortex over minutes to hours — but both are central to how the body handles stress.

The cortex: three layers, three kinds of hormone

The cortex is organized into three zones, and each makes a different class of steroid — a rare case where you can read the function straight off the anatomy, from outside in. The outermost zona glomerulosa makes mineralocorticoids, chiefly aldosterone, which tells the kidney to hold onto sodium (Na⁺) and water and dump potassium (K⁺), raising blood volume and pressure. The broad middle zona fasciculata makes glucocorticoids, chiefly cortisol, the body's main stress hormone — it raises blood glucose, mobilizes fuel, and damps inflammation. The innermost zona reticularis makes small amounts of androgens (sex hormones such as DHEA).

The medulla: instant adrenaline

At the very center, the medulla is wired directly into the sympathetic nervous system, so it fires the moment a threat appears. It floods the blood with epinephrine (adrenaline) and norepinephrine, producing the familiar fight-or-flight surge: pounding heart, dilated airways, glucose released for fuel, blood shunted to muscle. This is the fast, short-lived arm of the stress response — the jolt that fades within minutes. Cortisol, from the cortex, is the slower, sustained arm that carries the body through a long or repeated stress.

Layer (outside → in)Hormone classChief hormone & effect
Zona glomerulosaMineralocorticoidsAldosterone — retain Na⁺ & water, raise blood pressure
Zona fasciculataGlucocorticoidsCortisol — raise glucose, handle stress, reduce inflammation
Zona reticularisAndrogensDHEA — sex-hormone precursors
MedullaCatecholaminesEpinephrine — fast fight-or-flight surge
Salt, sugar, sex — then adrenaline. Read the adrenal from the outside in and the hormones line up neatly: the glomerulosa handles salt (aldosterone), the fasciculata handles sugar (cortisol), the reticularis handles sex (androgens), and the medulla in the middle supplies the adrenaline. "The deeper you go, the sweeter it gets" is one way students keep the order straight.

Tap each layer of the gland in the interactive, from the outer cortex to the inner medulla.

Interactive Peel the adrenal gland from its outer cortex layers inward to the medulla. Open full ↑
Key points
  • The adrenal gland is two glands in one: an outer cortex (steroids) and an inner medulla (catecholamines).
  • Cortex zones, outside in: glomerulosa → aldosterone (salt), fasciculata → cortisol (sugar/stress), reticularis → androgens (sex).
  • The medulla is an arm of the sympathetic system; it releases epinephrine for the fast fight-or-flight surge.
  • Cortisol is the slow, sustained stress hormone; epinephrine is the fast one.

The chapter in one breath

  • The endocrine system controls the body with hormones in the blood — slower but broader and longer-lasting than nervous signals; its glands are ductless.
  • A hormone reaches every cell but acts only on its target cells, which carry the matching receptor.
  • Lipid-soluble (steroid) hormones enter the cell and change genes — slow and lasting; water-soluble (peptide/amine) hormones use surface receptors and a second messenger — fast and brief.
  • The hypothalamus commands the pituitary: the posterior lobe stores ADH and oxytocin, and the anterior lobe makes six hormones, four of them tropic (FLAT PIG).
  • The pancreatic islets balance blood glucose with an antagonistic pair — insulin lowers it, glucagon raises it — and insulin is the only hormone that lowers it.
  • The thyroid sets metabolic rate through the HPT feedback axis; too little slows the body, too much speeds it, and TSH runs opposite to output.
  • The adrenal gland is a cortex (aldosterone, cortisol, androgens — salt, sugar, sex) wrapped around a medulla (epinephrine — fast fight-or-flight).

ReferenceWords worth knowing

A quick reference for the terms in this chapter — skim it now, or circle back whenever a word trips you up.

ACTH (adrenocorticotropic hormone)
An anterior pituitary tropic hormone that stimulates the adrenal cortex to make cortisol.
Adrenal cortex
The outer part of the adrenal gland; makes steroid hormones (aldosterone, cortisol, androgens) in three layered zones.
Adrenal medulla
The inner core of the adrenal gland; an extension of the sympathetic nervous system that releases epinephrine and norepinephrine.
Aldosterone
A mineralocorticoid from the zona glomerulosa that makes the kidney retain sodium and water, raising blood pressure.
Anterior pituitary
The glandular front lobe of the pituitary; makes six hormones (GH, TSH, ACTH, FSH, LH, PRL) under hypothalamic control.
Cortisol
The main glucocorticoid, from the zona fasciculata; the chief long-acting stress hormone that raises glucose and reduces inflammation.
Endocrine gland
A ductless gland that secretes hormones directly into the blood (contrast: an exocrine gland uses a duct).
Glucagon
A hormone from pancreatic alpha cells that raises blood glucose during fasting by breaking down glycogen and making new glucose.
Glucocorticoid
A class of adrenal steroid, chiefly cortisol, that regulates glucose and the stress response.
Hormone
A chemical messenger secreted into the blood by an endocrine gland that acts on distant target cells.
Hypothalamus
The brain region that links the nervous and endocrine systems and controls the pituitary gland.
Insulin
A hormone from pancreatic beta cells that lowers blood glucose after a meal by promoting uptake and glycogen storage.
Lipid-soluble hormone
A hormone (steroid, plus thyroid hormone) that crosses the cell membrane to bind intracellular receptors and change gene expression.
Negative feedback
A control loop in which a hormone's output inhibits its own production, holding the value near a set point.
Pancreatic islets
Clusters of endocrine cells in the pancreas; beta cells make insulin, alpha cells make glucagon.
Posterior pituitary
The back lobe of the pituitary; nervous tissue that stores and releases hypothalamus-made ADH and oxytocin.
Second messenger
An intracellular relay molecule (such as cyclic AMP) that carries a water-soluble hormone's signal after it binds a surface receptor.
Steroid hormone
A lipid-soluble hormone built from cholesterol (cortisol, aldosterone, sex hormones) that acts on genes inside the cell.
Target cell
A cell that responds to a particular hormone because it carries the matching receptor.
Thyroid hormone
The iodine-containing hormones T₃ and T₄ that set the body's basal metabolic rate.
Tropic hormone
A hormone whose target is another endocrine gland (for example TSH, ACTH, FSH, LH).
Water-soluble hormone
A hormone (peptide/protein or catecholamine) that cannot enter the cell and acts through a surface receptor and second messenger.

Check yourself

1. Both the nervous and endocrine systems control the body. Give two ways the endocrine system's signaling differs from the nervous system's.
It uses chemical hormones carried in the blood (not electrical signals in neurons), and its effects are slower to begin but broader and longer-lasting. The nervous system delivers fast, brief, precisely-targeted messages; the endocrine system broadcasts slower, wider, more durable ones.
2. What is the anatomical difference between an endocrine gland and an exocrine gland, and which type is the pancreas?
Endocrine glands are ductless and secrete into the blood; exocrine glands secrete through a duct onto a surface. The pancreas is both. Its islets are endocrine (insulin, glucagon into blood), while its acinar tissue is exocrine (enzymes down a duct to the intestine).
3. A hormone travels in the blood to every cell in the body. Why does it change only some cells?
Only cells with the matching receptor — the target cells — can respond to it. It is a lock-and-key system: the hormone is the key, and a cell without the right lock (receptor) simply ignores it.
4. A steroid hormone and a peptide hormone reach the same cell. Which one enters the cell, where is each one's receptor, and which acts faster?
The steroid (lipid-soluble) enters and binds an intracellular receptor; the peptide (water-soluble) binds a surface receptor. The peptide acts faster. Steroids change gene expression (slow, lasting); peptides trigger a second-messenger cascade of ready-made enzymes (fast, brief).
5. Thyroid hormone is made from an amino acid, yet it acts like a steroid. Explain the apparent contradiction.
Although it is an amine, thyroid hormone is lipid-soluble, so it can cross the membrane and act on genes inside the cell — the steroid mechanism. This is why its effects are slow to appear and long to last, despite its amino-acid origin.
6. Which gland truly controls the pituitary, and how does it reach the anterior lobe versus the posterior lobe?
The hypothalamus. It reaches the anterior lobe by releasing/inhibiting hormones through the hypophyseal portal blood vessels, and it reaches the posterior lobe through the axons of its own neurons. The anterior pituitary is remote-controlled chemically; the posterior pituitary is a direct neural extension of the hypothalamus.
7. The posterior pituitary releases ADH and oxytocin, yet it is said to make no hormones. Reconcile this.
Both hormones are actually made by hypothalamic neurons; they travel down axons to be stored in the posterior pituitary, which only releases them. The posterior lobe is a storage-and-release terminal, not a manufacturing site.
8. After a large meal, which pancreatic cell and hormone respond, and what two things does that hormone do to lower blood glucose?
Beta cells release insulin, which lowers blood glucose. It drives glucose uptake into body cells (especially muscle and fat) and tells the liver to store the surplus as glycogen, returning glucose to normal.
9. Why is losing insulin so much more dangerous than losing any single glucose-raising hormone?
Insulin is the only hormone that lowers blood glucose, whereas several hormones (glucagon, cortisol, epinephrine, GH) can raise it. Without insulin, as in type 1 diabetes, the body has no way to bring glucose down and it climbs to dangerous levels.
10. Trace the thyroid axis from the brain to the gland, and explain how negative feedback keeps thyroid hormone steady.
Hypothalamus releases TRH → pituitary releases TSH → thyroid releases T₃/T₄. Those hormones then inhibit the hypothalamus and pituitary. When thyroid hormone is high the brake is applied (less TRH/TSH); when it falls the brake lifts and TSH rises to push the thyroid harder.
11. A patient's blood test shows a very high TSH. Is the thyroid likely over- or under-active, and why does the TSH point that way?
Under-active (hypothyroidism). A failing thyroid makes little hormone, so it stops inhibiting the pituitary; the pituitary responds by pumping out more TSH to try to stimulate the lazy gland — a high TSH signals an underactive thyroid.
12. Name the three cortical zones of the adrenal gland from outside in with their main hormone, and say what the medulla adds.
Zona glomerulosa → aldosterone (salt); zona fasciculata → cortisol (sugar/stress); zona reticularis → androgens (sex). The medulla adds epinephrine for the fast fight-or-flight surge. "Salt, sugar, sex, then adrenaline" captures the order from outside in.
Anatomy & Physiology · Chapter 18

The Cardiovascular System: Blood

Blood is the only tissue in the body you can pour into a test tube, and it is easy to underestimate — it looks like a plain red liquid. But that liquid is a connective tissue, a living fleet of cells drifting in a protein-rich sea, and it touches every other cell you own. In this chapter we take a tube of blood apart layer by layer: the straw-colored plasma on top, the packed red cells below, and the thin white line between them. You will learn what each part carries, where blood cells are born, how a red cell ferries oxygen, how a cut seals itself in seconds, and why matching a patient's blood type before a transfusion is a matter of life and death.

By the end of this chapter you can

  • List the major functions of blood — transport, regulation, and protection — and explain why blood counts as a connective tissue.
  • Describe what a spun tube of blood separates into, and define plasma, buffy coat, and hematocrit.
  • Name the three formed elements and the five kinds of white blood cell, and give the main job of each.
  • Trace hematopoiesis from a single stem cell down the myeloid and lymphoid lines, and name the hormone that drives red-cell production.
  • Explain how hemoglobin loads oxygen in the lungs and unloads it in the tissues, and how blood carries carbon dioxide.
  • Walk through the three steps of hemostasis and describe how the clotting cascade builds a fibrin mesh.
  • Predict ABO and Rh compatibility and identify the universal donor and universal recipient.

Section 1What blood is made of

A tissue you can pour

It surprises most students to hear that blood is a connective tissue. Connective tissues are defined by scattered cells sitting in an extracellular matrix, and blood fits the definition perfectly — its "cells" are the red cells, white cells, and platelets, and its matrix is the liquid plasma they float in. What makes blood unusual is that the matrix is a fluid rather than something solid like bone or rubbery like cartilage. An average adult carries about 5 liters of it, a little less in women and a little more in men, and it makes up roughly 8 percent of body weight. Blood is also heavier and stickier than water — about five times as viscous — it runs warm at about 38 °C, and it is slightly alkaline, held in the narrow range of pH 7.35 to 7.45.

Three jobs: transport, regulation, protection

Everything blood does falls into three categories. First is transport: blood delivers O₂ and nutrients to cells, hauls CO₂ and metabolic wastes away, and carries hormones from glands to their distant targets. Second is regulation: by shifting where it flows and how much heat it carries to the skin, blood helps stabilize body temperature; its dissolved buffers steady the body's pH; and its proteins help control fluid balance between blood and tissues. Third is protection: the white cells and antibodies defend against invaders, and the clotting system seals leaks so you do not bleed out from a small wound.

Spin it down and it splits in three

Put a tube of blood in a centrifuge and the heavier parts sink. You end up with three visible layers, and their proportions tell a clinical story:

LayerWhat it containsFraction of blood
Plasma (top)Water, plasma proteins, nutrients, gases, hormones, wastes, ionsAbout 55%
Buffy coat (middle)Leukocytes (white cells) and plateletsLess than 1%
Erythrocytes (bottom)Red blood cells — this fraction is the hematocritAbout 45%

That bottom fraction has its own name: the hematocrit is the percentage of whole-blood volume made up of red cells. Normal values run roughly 37–47 percent in women and 42–52 percent in men. The plasma itself is about 92 percent water; most of the rest is plasma proteins — albumin (the most abundant, which holds water in the vessels and ferries fatty molecules), globulins (which include antibodies and transport proteins), and fibrinogen (the raw material for clots). One term worth pinning down now: if you let blood clot and remove the clot, the pale fluid left behind is serum — plasma minus its clotting proteins.

Hematocrit is a headline number. A low hematocrit means anemia — too few red cells to carry oxygen well, leaving a patient tired and short of breath. A high hematocrit (polycythemia) thickens the blood and strains the heart. One spun tube tells you a great deal about how well oxygen is being delivered.

Spin your own tube and pull apart the layers in the interactive below.

Interactive Spin a tube of blood into its three layers and tap each to see what it holds. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Blood is a fluid connective tissue — cells (formed elements) suspended in a liquid matrix (plasma) — about 5 L and 8% of body weight.
  • Its jobs sort into transport, regulation, and protection.
  • Spun blood gives plasma (~55%), a thin buffy coat of white cells and platelets, and red cells (hematocrit, ~45%).
  • Serum is plasma with the clotting proteins removed.

Section 2The formed elements

Three kinds of passenger

The living cargo of blood — the formed elements — comes in three types: erythrocytes (red blood cells), leukocytes (white blood cells), and platelets (also called thrombocytes). They are wildly unequal in number. Red cells dominate, at roughly 5 million per microliter; platelets number a few hundred thousand; and white cells are the rarest, at only about 5,000 to 10,000 per microliter. That lopsidedness is exactly why the spun tube from Section 1 is almost all red on the bottom with only a whisper of buffy coat.

Red cells: oxygen with everything stripped away

An erythrocyte is a design study in doing one job well. It is a biconcave disc — a flattened circle pinched in on both faces — a shape that gives it lots of surface area for gas exchange and lets it fold to squeeze through capillaries narrower than itself. To make room for as much hemoglobin as possible, the mature red cell throws almost everything else overboard: it has no nucleus and no organelles. That sacrifice has a cost — with no nucleus it cannot repair itself, so it wears out in about 120 days and is recycled by the spleen and liver. We will follow its oxygen work in Section 4.

White cells: the mobile defense force

Unlike red cells, every leukocyte keeps its nucleus, and all of them are soldiers of the immune system. They split into two families by whether they carry visible granules. The granulocytes — neutrophils, eosinophils, and basophils — have grainy cytoplasm; the agranulocytes — lymphocytes and monocytes — do not. Learning them in order of abundance is easiest with a mnemonic: Never Let Monkeys Eat Bananas (Neutrophils, Lymphocytes, Monocytes, Eosinophils, Basophils).

White cellGroupRough shareMain job
NeutrophilGranulocyte50–70%First responder; phagocytoses bacteria
LymphocyteAgranulocyte20–30%Adaptive immunity — T, B, and NK cells
MonocyteAgranulocyte2–8%Becomes a macrophage; big-appetite phagocyte
EosinophilGranulocyte1–4%Attacks parasites; roles in allergy
BasophilGranulocyte<1%Releases histamine and heparin; inflammation

Platelets: fragments that plug leaks

Platelets are not whole cells at all — they are cell fragments pinched off from giant marrow cells called megakaryocytes. They have no nucleus, live only about 10 days, and exist for one purpose: to rush to a wound, pile up, and start a clot. We will watch them do it in Section 5.

Read the "diff" like a story. A complete blood count reports a white-cell differential — the percentages above. A jump in neutrophils points to a bacterial infection; a rise in lymphocytes often means a viral one; and climbing eosinophils suggest a parasite or an allergy. The numbers narrow the diagnosis before any culture comes back.

Meet all three formed elements — and the five white cells — in the interactive.

Interactive Tap each formed element — red cell, five white cells, and platelet — to see its job. Open full ↑
Key points
  • The three formed elements are erythrocytes (oxygen), leukocytes (defense), and platelets (clotting); red cells vastly outnumber the rest.
  • The red cell is a biconcave disc with no nucleus, packed with hemoglobin, lasting ~120 days.
  • Five white cells, most to least: neutrophils, lymphocytes, monocytes, eosinophils, basophils (Never Let Monkeys Eat Bananas).
  • Platelets are megakaryocyte fragments that trigger clotting.

Section 3Where blood cells are born

One stem cell, every blood cell

Red cells wear out in four months and neutrophils in a matter of days, so the body must replace blood cells constantly — on the order of hundreds of billions each day. That factory is the red bone marrow, and the whole process of making blood is called hematopoiesis. Astonishingly, every one of those cells traces back to a single kind of ancestor: the hematopoietic stem cell (also called a hemocytoblast). It is pluripotent, meaning it can become any blood cell, and it renews itself so the supply never runs dry.

Two family lines: myeloid and lymphoid

Early on, the stem cell's descendants commit to one of two branches. The myeloid line is the larger one — it produces red cells, platelets (by way of megakaryocytes), and most white cells: neutrophils, eosinophils, basophils, and monocytes. The lymphoid line is dedicated to the lymphocytes (the T, B, and NK cells of adaptive immunity). Knowing which line a cell comes from is more than trivia — leukemias are classified as myeloid or lymphoid precisely by which branch of this tree went wrong.

LineCells it producesNotice
MyeloidErythrocytes, platelets, neutrophils, eosinophils, basophils, monocytesThe broad branch — nearly everything
LymphoidLymphocytes (T cells, B cells, NK cells)The specialist branch — adaptive immunity

Hormones set the pace

Production is not left to chance; hormones and growth factors tune it to demand. The star is erythropoietin (EPO), released by the kidneys when they sense low oxygen — EPO tells the marrow to make more red cells. Thrombopoietin (from the liver) drives platelet production, and a family of colony-stimulating factors and interleukins ramps up white-cell output during infection. Building a red cell also needs raw materials: iron for hemoglobin, plus vitamin B₁₂ and folate for DNA synthesis. Run short on any of these and you become anemic, which is why the freshly released, not-quite-finished red cells — the reticulocytes — are counted as a gauge of how hard the marrow is working.

Sick kidneys make you anemic. Because the kidney makes EPO, people with chronic kidney disease often run low red counts — the oxygen sensor is broken, so the marrow never gets the memo. This is also the loophole endurance athletes abuse: injecting synthetic EPO forces extra red cells and illegally boosts oxygen delivery.

Climb the family tree node by node in the interactive.

Interactive Follow the tree from one stem cell down the myeloid and lymphoid branches. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Hematopoiesis happens in red bone marrow; every blood cell descends from a pluripotent hematopoietic stem cell.
  • The stem cell branches into the myeloid line (red cells, platelets, most white cells) and the lymphoid line (lymphocytes).
  • Erythropoietin from the kidney drives red-cell production; iron, B₁₂, and folate are the raw materials.

Section 4How blood carries oxygen

The molecule that does the carrying

Red cells matter because of one molecule: hemoglobin. Each hemoglobin is built from four protein chains (two alpha and two beta globins), and every chain cradles a heme group with an atom of iron at its center. Oxygen binds to that iron. Since one hemoglobin has four heme groups, it can carry four O₂ molecules at once — and because a single red cell is stuffed with roughly 270 million hemoglobins, one cell can shuttle over a billion oxygen molecules. Iron is the actual seat oxygen sits on, which is why iron deficiency so directly starves the tissues of oxygen.

Load in the lungs, unload in the tissues

Hemoglobin's genius is that it grabs oxygen where oxygen is plentiful and lets go where it is scarce. In the lungs, where the partial pressure of oxygen is high, hemoglobin binds O₂ to become oxyhemoglobin — and this bright, oxygen-rich blood is a vivid scarlet. Out in the tissues, where cells have burned through their oxygen, the partial pressure is low and hemoglobin releases its cargo, becoming deoxyhemoglobin, a darker brick red. That color swing is why arterial blood looks so much brighter than venous blood. The binding is also cooperative: once the first O₂ is on, the next bind more easily, so loading and unloading both happen fast. Warmth, extra CO₂, and a more acidic environment — exactly the conditions in a hardworking muscle — nudge hemoglobin to release more oxygen right where it is needed.

And the return trip: carbon dioxide

Blood does not just deliver oxygen; it also clears the CO₂ that cells produce. That waste rides home three ways, and the proportions are worth knowing:

GasHow it travelsShare
O₂Bound to hemoglobin as oxyhemoglobin~98.5%
O₂Dissolved in plasma~1.5%
CO₂As bicarbonate (HCO₃⁻) in plasma~70%
CO₂Bound to hemoglobin (carbaminohemoglobin)~23%
CO₂Dissolved in plasma~7%
Carbon monoxide is a thief. CO binds hemoglobin more than 200 times more tightly than oxygen does, locking up the iron seats so O₂ cannot board. A poisoned patient may look deceptively healthy — cherry-red rather than blue — because the blood is saturated, just with the wrong gas. It is a vivid reminder that oxygen delivery depends entirely on hemoglobin being free to bind.

Watch a red cell load up and drop off, on a loop, in the interactive.

Interactive Follow one red cell picking up O₂ in the lungs and dropping it in the tissues. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Hemoglobin = four globin chains, each with an iron-containing heme; it carries up to four O₂ per molecule.
  • It forms oxyhemoglobin (bright red) in the lungs and releases O₂ as deoxyhemoglobin (darker) in the tissues.
  • CO₂ travels home mostly as bicarbonate (~70%), some on hemoglobin, a little dissolved.
  • Carbon monoxide outcompetes oxygen for hemoglobin, blocking delivery.

Section 5How bleeding stops

Three fast steps

Cut a vessel and the body has to seal the leak before too much blood escapes — a process called hemostasis (literally "blood standstill"). It unfolds in three overlapping steps, each buying time for the next. The whole thing can begin within seconds.

StepWhat happensResult
1. Vascular spasmSmooth muscle in the vessel wall contracts near the injuryThe vessel narrows, slowing blood loss
2. Platelet plugPlatelets stick to exposed collagen, activate, and pile upA soft plug fills the hole
3. CoagulationA cascade of clotting factors builds a fibrin meshA firm clot locks the plug in place

The platelet plug snowballs

When the smooth inner lining of a vessel is torn, the tough collagen underneath is exposed. Platelets grab onto it (with the help of a bridging protein, von Willebrand factor), and contact flips them from smooth discs into spiky, sticky activated platelets. Activated platelets then release chemicals — ADP, thromboxane A₂, and serotonin — that summon and activate still more platelets. This is a textbook positive feedback loop: each recruited platelet recruits others, so the plug grows quickly until the breach is covered. This is also exactly the step that aspirin blunts — it blocks thromboxane, which is why low-dose aspirin makes platelets less sticky.

Coagulation: turning liquid into gel

A platelet plug alone is fragile; coagulation reinforces it. A relay of plasma proteins called clotting factors triggers one another in a cascade, along an intrinsic and an extrinsic pathway that merge into a common pathway. The finale has two moves: the enzyme thrombin is switched on, and thrombin converts the soluble plasma protein fibrinogen into threads of insoluble fibrin. Those threads weave a mesh that traps red cells and platelets — the actual clot. Two behind-the-scenes players make it possible: calcium (clotting factor IV) is required at several steps, and vitamin K is needed by the liver to build several of the factors in the first place. Once the wound heals, the enzyme plasmin dissolves the clot (fibrinolysis).

Vitamin K is the reason warfarin works. The anticoagulant warfarin blocks vitamin K, so the liver cannot make its clotting factors and the blood thins — useful for preventing dangerous clots. On the other end, hemophilia is the failure of this cascade: a missing clotting factor (VIII or IX) means even small injuries bleed for a long time.

Step through the seal-and-reinforce sequence in the interactive.

Interactive Step through vascular spasm, the platelet plug, and the fibrin mesh that stops bleeding. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Hemostasis has three steps: vascular spasm → platelet plug → coagulation.
  • The platelet plug grows by positive feedback as activated platelets recruit more; aspirin blunts this.
  • Coagulation ends with thrombin converting fibrinogen to a fibrin mesh; it needs calcium and vitamin K.

Section 6Blood types and transfusion

Antigens on the cell, antibodies in the plasma

Your blood type is decided by molecular flags called antigens (agglutinogens) that stud the surface of your red cells. In the ABO system there are two possible flags, A and B, and your genes decide which you display. Here is the crucial rule: your plasma carries antibodies (agglutinins) against whichever antigen you lack. So a type A person has A antigens and anti-B antibodies; a type B person has B antigens and anti-A; a type AB person has both antigens and no ABO antibodies; and a type O person has neither antigen but both anti-A and anti-B.

Why a mismatch is dangerous

If a patient receives red cells carrying an antigen their plasma has antibodies against, those antibodies clamp onto the donor cells and clump them together — agglutination — and then rupture them (hemolysis). The debris can clog small vessels and overwhelm the kidneys; a serious transfusion reaction can be fatal. This is why blood is always typed and cross-matched before it is given.

TypeAntigen on red cellsAntibody in plasmaCan receive from
AAAnti-BA, O
BBAnti-AB, O
ABA and BNoneA, B, AB, O — universal recipient
ONoneAnti-A and Anti-BO only — universal donor of red cells

The Rh factor

Layered on top of ABO is the Rh system, named for a single important antigen, D. If you carry it you are Rh-positive; if not, Rh-negative. Unlike the ABO antibodies, which are present from early life, an Rh-negative person only makes anti-D antibodies after being exposed to Rh-positive blood. That detail drives one of the most important scenarios in obstetrics: hemolytic disease of the newborn. If an Rh-negative mother carries an Rh-positive baby, she can become sensitized to the D antigen, and in a later Rh-positive pregnancy her antibodies may cross the placenta and attack the fetus's red cells. The fix is elegant — a shot of Rh immune globulin (RhoGAM) clears the fetal cells before the mother's immune system can react.

Give O, take AB. Type O-negative red cells carry no A, B, or D antigen, so they set off no antibodies — the universal donor for emergencies. Type AB-positive plasma carries no ABO antibodies, so it can receive any red cells — the universal recipient. Note the flip: for plasma donation the rule reverses, because plasma is all about the antibodies.

Pick a blood type and see exactly who it can give to and take from in the interactive.

Interactive Choose a blood type and reveal its antigens, antibodies, and who it is compatible with. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Blood type = antigens on your red cells; your plasma holds antibodies against the antigens you lack.
  • Mismatched transfusion causes agglutination and hemolysis, so blood is typed and cross-matched.
  • O-negative is the universal red-cell donor; AB-positive is the universal recipient.
  • Rh-negative mothers with Rh-positive babies risk hemolytic disease of the newborn, prevented by RhoGAM.

The chapter in one breath

  • Blood is a fluid connective tissue — formed elements suspended in plasma — that handles transport, regulation, and protection.
  • Spun blood separates into plasma (~55%), a thin buffy coat, and red cells (hematocrit, ~45%); plasma is mostly water plus albumin, globulins, and fibrinogen.
  • The formed elements are erythrocytes (oxygen), five kinds of leukocyte (defense), and platelets (clotting).
  • Hematopoiesis in red marrow starts from one stem cell that branches into the myeloid and lymphoid lines; the kidney's erythropoietin drives red-cell output.
  • Hemoglobin carries four O₂ on iron-heme seats, loading in the lungs and unloading in the tissues; CO₂ rides home mostly as bicarbonate.
  • Hemostasis seals a wound in three steps — vascular spasm, platelet plug, and coagulation — ending in a fibrin mesh.
  • Blood type is set by red-cell antigens (A, B, Rh); plasma holds antibodies against the ones you lack, making O-negative the universal donor and AB-positive the universal recipient.

ReferenceWords worth knowing

A quick reference for the terms in this chapter — skim it now, or circle back whenever a word trips you up.

Agglutination
The clumping of red blood cells when antibodies bind matching antigens, as in a mismatched transfusion.
Albumin
The most abundant plasma protein; it holds water in the vessels (osmotic pressure) and transports fatty molecules and drugs.
Antibody (agglutinin)
A plasma protein that recognizes and binds a specific antigen; ABO antibodies target the A or B antigen a person lacks.
Antigen (agglutinogen)
A surface molecule on red cells (A, B, or Rh D) that defines blood type and can trigger an immune response.
Buffy coat
The thin layer of white blood cells and platelets sitting between plasma and red cells in a spun sample.
Coagulation
The clotting cascade in which factors activate one another to convert fibrinogen into a fibrin mesh.
Erythrocyte
A red blood cell — a biconcave, anucleate cell packed with hemoglobin to carry oxygen.
Erythropoietin (EPO)
A hormone from the kidney that stimulates red bone marrow to produce more red blood cells when oxygen is low.
Fibrin
The insoluble protein thread, formed from fibrinogen by thrombin, that meshes to trap cells and form a clot.
Formed elements
The cellular components of blood: erythrocytes, leukocytes, and platelets.
Hematocrit
The percentage of whole-blood volume made up of red blood cells (about 37–52%, varying by sex).
Hematopoiesis
The production of blood cells in red bone marrow, all descending from a pluripotent hematopoietic stem cell.
Hemoglobin
The iron-containing protein in red cells (four globin chains, four heme groups) that binds and transports oxygen.
Hemostasis
The stopping of bleeding through vascular spasm, platelet plug formation, and coagulation.
Leukocyte
A white blood cell; the nucleated defenders of the body, divided into granulocytes and agranulocytes.
Plasma
The liquid matrix of blood — about 92% water plus proteins, nutrients, gases, hormones, and wastes.
Platelet (thrombocyte)
A cell fragment from a megakaryocyte that adheres to wounds and initiates clotting.
Rh factor
The D antigen of the Rh blood-group system; present in Rh-positive blood, absent in Rh-negative.
Serum
Plasma with its clotting proteins removed; the fluid left after blood clots.
Thrombin
The enzyme that converts fibrinogen into fibrin, the final step in building a clot.

Check yourself

1. Why is blood classified as a connective tissue, and what serves as its matrix?
Because it is scattered cells (formed elements) suspended in an extracellular matrix — here a liquid, the plasma. Connective tissues are defined by cells in a matrix; blood's matrix simply happens to be fluid rather than solid or gel-like.
2. A spun tube of blood shows three layers. Name them from top to bottom and give the approximate fraction of each.
Plasma (~55%) on top, a thin buffy coat (<1%), and red cells (~45%) on the bottom. The buffy coat holds the white cells and platelets; the red-cell fraction is the hematocrit.
3. What is the hematocrit, and what does an abnormally low value suggest?
The percentage of blood volume that is red cells; a low value suggests anemia. Too few red cells means reduced oxygen-carrying capacity, causing fatigue and shortness of breath. A high value (polycythemia) thickens the blood.
4. Name the three formed elements and the one job each is specialized for.
Erythrocytes carry oxygen, leukocytes defend against invaders, and platelets start clots. Red cells vastly outnumber the other two, which is why spun blood is mostly red on the bottom.
5. A mature red blood cell has thrown out its nucleus and organelles. What does it gain, and what does it lose?
It gains room for more hemoglobin (and a flexible biconcave shape); it loses the ability to repair itself. With no nucleus it cannot make new proteins, so it wears out in about 120 days and is recycled.
6. List the five white blood cells from most to least abundant. What mnemonic helps?
Neutrophils, lymphocytes, monocytes, eosinophils, basophils — "Never Let Monkeys Eat Bananas." Neutrophils are first responders to bacteria; lymphocytes run adaptive immunity; monocytes become macrophages.
7. During hematopoiesis, the stem cell commits to one of two lines. Name them and say which cells each produces.
The myeloid line makes red cells, platelets, and most white cells; the lymphoid line makes lymphocytes. Both descend from a single pluripotent hematopoietic stem cell in red bone marrow.
8. Which hormone drives red-cell production, where is it made, and why are kidney patients often anemic?
Erythropoietin (EPO), made by the kidneys; damaged kidneys make too little of it. Without the EPO signal, the marrow is never told to ramp up red-cell production, so red counts fall.
9. Describe how a single hemoglobin molecule carries oxygen, and where the oxygen actually binds.
Hemoglobin has four globin chains, each with a heme group; oxygen binds the iron atom at the center of each heme, so one molecule carries up to four O₂. Iron is literally the seat oxygen sits on, which is why iron deficiency impairs oxygen transport.
10. In what form does most carbon dioxide travel back to the lungs?
As bicarbonate (HCO₃⁻) dissolved in plasma — roughly 70%. The rest rides on hemoglobin as carbaminohemoglobin (~23%) or is dissolved in plasma (~7%).
11. List the three steps of hemostasis in order, and name the protein mesh that forms in the final step.
Vascular spasm → platelet plug → coagulation; the final mesh is fibrin. Thrombin converts soluble fibrinogen into fibrin threads that trap cells and lock the plug in place. Calcium and vitamin K are required.
12. A patient with type O-negative blood is called the universal donor, while AB-positive is the universal recipient. Explain both, using antigens and antibodies.
O-negative red cells carry no A, B, or D antigen, so no recipient's antibodies attack them; AB-positive plasma has no ABO antibodies, so it accepts any red cells. A reaction occurs only when donor antigens meet matching recipient antibodies — O-negative supplies none, and AB-positive brings none to the fight.
Anatomy & Physiology · Chapter 19

The Cardiovascular System: The Heart

Somewhere behind your breastbone, a muscle the size of your fist is squeezing about once a second, and it has never once taken a break. Over an average lifetime it will beat more than two and a half billion times and move enough blood to fill a small lake. This chapter opens that pump up. You will learn how the heart is built as two pumps in one, trace a single drop of blood on its full loop through the lungs and the body, watch the four valves keep everything flowing forward, and follow the tiny electrical spark the heart makes for itself — the spark that a machine at the bedside can read straight off the skin as an ECG.

By the end of this chapter you can

  • Locate the heart in the mediastinum, name the layers of its wall and the sac around it, and explain why the left ventricle is the thickest chamber.
  • Identify the four chambers and the great vessels, and state which blood (oxygen-rich or oxygen-poor) each one carries.
  • Trace blood flow through the heart in order and distinguish the pulmonary from the systemic circuit.
  • Name the four valves, place each one, and explain how chordae tendineae and pressure keep flow one-way.
  • Describe the conduction system from SA node to Purkinje fibers and explain the purpose of the AV node delay.
  • Break one heartbeat into the phases of the cardiac cycle and say what makes the “lub-dub” heart sounds.
  • Match each wave of the ECG — P, QRS, and T — to the electrical event it records.
  • Calculate cardiac output from heart rate and stroke volume, and predict how it changes.

Section 1The heart as a double pump

A fist-sized pump in the middle of the chest

The heart sits in the mediastinum, the space between the two lungs, resting on the diaphragm and tilted so that about two-thirds of it lies to the left of the midline. Its broad top, where the great vessels attach, is the base; its blunt, downward-and-leftward tip is the apex — the spot where you feel the beat against the chest wall. Around it is a tough double-walled sac, the pericardium. The outer fibrous pericardium anchors the heart and keeps it from overfilling; inside that, a two-layered serous pericardium secretes a slippery fluid into the pericardial cavity so the beating heart glides without friction. When that space fills with fluid or blood and squeezes the heart, the result — cardiac tamponade — is a true emergency.

Two pumps, four chambers

The single most useful idea in this whole chapter is that the heart is really two pumps bolted together, side by side, working in perfect step. The right heart receives oxygen-poor blood returning from the body and pushes it a short distance to the lungs. The left heart receives the freshly oxygenated blood coming back from the lungs and drives it out to the entire rest of the body. A solid muscular wall, the septum, keeps the two sides from mixing. Each pump has two chambers: a thin-walled atrium on top that receives blood, and a thick-walled ventricle below that ejects it. Blood always travels atrium → ventricle → out an artery.

ChamberReceives blood fromSends blood to
Right atriumBody, via the superior & inferior venae cavaeRight ventricle
Right ventricleRight atriumLungs, via the pulmonary trunk & arteries
Left atriumLungs, via the four pulmonary veinsLeft ventricle
Left ventricleLeft atriumWhole body, via the aorta

Three layers, and a heart that feeds itself

The wall itself has three layers. The inner endocardium is a smooth lining continuous with the vessels, so blood never touches rough tissue. The bulky middle myocardium is the cardiac muscle that actually does the pumping — and it is far thicker on the left. The outer epicardium is the visceral layer of the serous pericardium. Notice on the reference figure how the left ventricle wall dwarfs the right: it must generate roughly five times the pressure to push blood through the long systemic circuit, while the right ventricle only has to reach the nearby lungs. Finally, all that hard-working muscle needs its own blood supply, delivered by the coronary arteries that branch off the aorta. Block one and the muscle downstream starves — that is a heart attack, a myocardial infarction.

Thickness follows workload. The left ventricle is the thickest chamber not because it is more important, but because it faces the highest pressure. Anything that raises that pressure long-term — chronic hypertension, a stiff aortic valve — makes the left ventricle thicken further (hypertrophy), just as any muscle grows against a heavier load.

Tap through the chambers, vessels, and wall layers in the interactive below.

Interactive The heart as a double pump — tap each chamber and vessel; blue is low O₂, red is high O₂. Open full ↑
Key points
  • The heart lies in the mediastinum, wrapped in a pericardial sac whose fluid lets it beat friction-free.
  • It is two pumps in one: the right heart serves the lungs, the left heart serves the body, separated by the septum.
  • Atria receive, ventricles eject; the wall is endocardium, myocardium, epicardium; the left ventricle is thickest because it faces the highest pressure.
  • The coronary arteries feed the myocardium; blocking one causes a myocardial infarction.

Section 2Blood flow through the heart

One drop, one full loop

Let us follow a single drop of blood all the way around. Returning from the body tired and oxygen-poor, it empties into the right atrium through the superior and inferior venae cavae. It drops through the tricuspid valve into the right ventricle, which squeezes it through the pulmonary valve into the pulmonary trunk and on to the lungs. In the lungs it unloads carbon dioxide and picks up a fresh load of oxygen. Now bright red, it flows back through the pulmonary veins into the left atrium, passes the mitral valve into the powerful left ventricle, and is launched through the aortic valve into the aorta to begin its journey around the body again.

StepStructureBlood here is
1Venae cavae → right atriumOxygen-poor
2Tricuspid valve → right ventricleOxygen-poor
3Pulmonary valve → pulmonary arteries → lungsOxygen-poor (arriving)
4Pulmonary veins → left atriumOxygen-rich
5Mitral valve → left ventricleOxygen-rich
6Aortic valve → aorta → bodyOxygen-rich

Two circuits, run in series

That single loop is really two loops chained together. The pulmonary circuit runs from the right heart to the lungs and back; its whole purpose is gas exchange. The systemic circuit runs from the left heart out to every other tissue — brain, gut, kidneys, muscles — and back. Because they are wired in series, the same drop must pass through both, and the two ventricles must pump the exact same volume over time. If the right side pumped even a little more than the left for long, blood would pile up in the lungs; if the left outran the right, it would pile up in the body. That balance is why heart failure on one side eventually drags down the other.

The great exception

Here is a rule worth burning in, along with the one place it seems to break. Arteries carry blood away from the heart; veins carry blood back toward it. Notice the definition says nothing about oxygen. Almost everywhere, arteries happen to be oxygen-rich and veins oxygen-poor — but the pulmonary vessels flip that. The pulmonary arteries carry oxygen-poor blood (away from the heart, toward the lungs), and the pulmonary veins carry oxygen-rich blood (back toward the heart). They are the exception that proves the real rule is about direction, not color.

Direction, not color. If a test question shows a blue pulmonary artery, do not call it a mistake. “Artery” means away from the heart — and the pulmonary artery is carrying used blood away, toward the lungs to be refreshed. The same logic makes the pulmonary vein the one vein full of bright red blood.

Play or step a single drop through the whole loop in the interactive.

Interactive Follow one drop of blood on its full loop through the right heart, the lungs, and the left heart. Open full ↑
Key points
  • The forward path: venae cavae → RA → tricuspid → RV → pulmonary valve → lungs → pulmonary veins → LA → mitral → LV → aortic valve → aorta.
  • The pulmonary circuit (right heart ↔ lungs) and systemic circuit (left heart ↔ body) run in series, so both ventricles move equal volumes.
  • Arteries lead away, veins lead back — a rule about direction; the pulmonary vessels are the oxygen-content exception.

Section 3The four one-way valves

Four doors that only open one way

A pump is only as good as its valves, and the heart has four, each a flap-door that opens under forward pressure and slams shut against backward pressure. Two of them, the atrioventricular (AV) valves, sit between each atrium and its ventricle: the tricuspid valve on the right (three cusps) and the mitral or bicuspid valve on the left (two cusps). The other two, the semilunar valves, guard the exits where the great arteries leave: the pulmonary valve at the mouth of the pulmonary trunk and the aortic valve at the mouth of the aorta. Together they make sure that with every squeeze, blood can only go forward.

ValveTypeLocationOpen when
TricuspidAVRight atrium → right ventricleVentricles relaxed (filling)
Mitral (bicuspid)AVLeft atrium → left ventricleVentricles relaxed (filling)
PulmonarySemilunarRight ventricle → pulmonary trunkVentricles contracting (ejecting)
AorticSemilunarLeft ventricle → aortaVentricles contracting (ejecting)

What holds them shut

The two valve types are built to resist backflow in different ways. When the ventricles contract hard, the pressure could blow the AV valve cusps up into the atria — so each cusp is tethered by tough strings, the chordae tendineae (“heart strings”), to little muscle bumps called papillary muscles on the ventricle floor. The papillary muscles tighten just before the squeeze, and the chords hold the cusps like the ropes on a parachute, letting them close flat but not turn inside out. The semilunar valves need no chords: their three cup-shaped pockets simply catch the blood that tries to fall back after ejection, and the weight snaps them shut.

When valves go wrong

Valves fail in exactly two ways, and the words are worth knowing. A stenotic valve is stiff and narrowed — it will not open all the way, so the heart must strain to push blood through a smaller hole. A regurgitant (or insufficient) valve is leaky — it will not close all the way, so blood sloshes backward with each beat. Either fault sets up turbulence, and turbulence you can hear through a stethoscope as a murmur. The normal, healthy sounds — the familiar “lub-dub” — are simply the valves snapping shut, which is the bridge into the cardiac cycle.

LAB · RAT. To remember which AV valve is which: Left Atrium has the Bicuspid; Right Atrium has the Tricuspid. And the heart sounds map cleanly: S1 (“lub”) is the AV valves closing as the ventricles start to squeeze; S2 (“dub”) is the semilunar valves closing as the ventricles relax.

Tap each valve to watch it open and close in the interactive.

Interactive Four one-way valves keep blood moving forward — tap each valve to see it open and close. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Two AV valves (tricuspid right, mitral/bicuspid left) sit between atria and ventricles; two semilunar valves (pulmonary, aortic) guard the artery exits.
  • Chordae tendineae and papillary muscles anchor the AV cusps so they cannot flip backward during the squeeze.
  • Valves fail as stenosis (won’t open) or regurgitation (won’t close), producing murmurs; normal S1/S2 are valves snapping shut.

Section 4The conduction system

A heart that writes its own rhythm

Unlike the muscles in your arm, the heart does not wait for the brain to tell it to beat. A small population of specialized cells can depolarize all on their own, over and over, a property called autorhythmicity. The fastest of these cells — the sinoatrial (SA) node, tucked in the wall of the right atrium — fires about 60 to 100 times a minute and sets the pace for everyone else. That is why the SA node earns the nickname the heart’s pacemaker. Cut every nerve to the heart and it would keep right on beating; the nerves only speed it up or slow it down, as we will see.

The path of the spark

The impulse spreads in a precise, deliberate order so the chambers squeeze in the right sequence — atria first, then ventricles. From the SA node the signal sweeps across both atria, making them contract and top off the ventricles. It then reaches the atrioventricular (AV) node, the only electrical doorway between atria and ventricles, where it deliberately pauses for about a tenth of a second. From the AV node it races down the AV bundle (bundle of His), splits into the right and left bundle branches along the septum, and fans out through the Purkinje fibers into the ventricle walls. The ventricles then contract from the apex up, wringing blood toward the exits like squeezing a tube of toothpaste from the bottom.

ComponentRoleIntrinsic rate
SA nodePacemaker; starts each beat, fires the atria60–100 / min
AV nodeGateway to the ventricles; delays the signal40–60 / min
AV bundle & branchesCarry the signal down the septum—
Purkinje fibersSpread the signal through ventricle walls20–40 / min

Why the pause, and who controls the tempo

That AV-node delay is not a flaw — it is essential. It buys the atria just enough time to finish emptying into the ventricles before the ventricles slam shut and eject. Without it, all four chambers would contract at once and pump far less blood. Notice, too, the built-in backups: if the SA node quits, the AV node can take over at a slower 40 to 60 beats, and the Purkinje fibers slower still — a safety net that keeps a failing heart beating, just too slowly. Finally, the autonomic nervous system is the volume dial: sympathetic signals speed the SA node and strengthen contractions (fear, exercise), while the parasympathetic vagus nerve slows it back down at rest.

The delay is the point. The tenth-of-a-second pause at the AV node is what lets the atria finish filling the ventricles before they fire. On an ECG you can literally measure this pause — it is the flat stretch of the PR segment between the P wave and the QRS complex.

Follow the spark from the SA node all the way down in the interactive.

Interactive Follow the electrical spark from the pacemaker down through the heart in order. Open full ↑
Key points
  • The heart is autorhythmic; the SA node is the pacemaker, firing 60–100 times a minute.
  • The path is SA node → AV node → AV bundle → bundle branches → Purkinje fibers, so atria contract before ventricles.
  • The AV node delay lets the atria finish filling the ventricles; slower backup pacemakers protect against SA failure.
  • Sympathetic nerves speed the heart, parasympathetic (vagus) nerves slow it.

Section 5The cardiac cycle

Squeeze and rest, over and over

One heartbeat is a repeating rhythm of two states. Systole is contraction — a chamber squeezing to push blood out. Diastole is relaxation — a chamber loosening to fill back up. At a resting rate of about 75 beats a minute, the whole cycle takes roughly 0.8 seconds, and, importantly, the heart spends more of that time relaxing than contracting. That rest is not laziness: it is when the ventricles refill, and it is also the only time the coronary arteries can push blood into the thick heart muscle. Race the heart too fast and you steal from diastole — less filling, less coronary flow.

The phases, in order

Walk around the reference wheel and the sequence tells itself. During atrial systole the atria give a gentle squeeze that tops off the already-mostly-full ventricles. Then comes ventricular systole: first an isovolumetric contraction where all four valves are shut and pressure builds with no blood moving, then ventricular ejection as the semilunar valves pop open and blood surges into the arteries. Finally ventricular diastole: an isovolumetric relaxation with all valves shut again, then ventricular filling as the AV valves open and blood pours in — most of it passively, before the atria even contract.

PhaseWhat happensAV valvesSemilunar valves
Atrial systoleAtria squeeze; final 20–30% of fillingOpenClosed
Isovolumetric contractionVentricles tense; pressure builds, no ejectionClosedClosed
Ventricular ejectionVentricles push blood into the arteriesClosedOpen
Isovolumetric relaxationVentricles relax; pressure falls, no fillingClosedClosed
Ventricular fillingBlood pours passively into ventriclesOpenClosed

Lub-dub, and the numbers that matter

The two heart sounds are made by valves closing, not opening. S1, the low “lub,” is the AV valves snapping shut as ventricular systole begins; S2, the sharper “dub,” is the semilunar valves shutting as diastole begins. Between them the heart is ejecting; after S2 it is filling. A few numbers frame the whole thing. The volume in a ventricle at the end of filling is the end-diastolic volume (EDV); what remains after ejection is the end-systolic volume (ESV); the amount actually pumped per beat is the stroke volume (EDV − ESV). Multiply stroke volume by heart rate and you get cardiac output — the liters of blood the heart moves each minute, about 5 L at rest.

Cardiac output = heart rate × stroke volume. Every way the body has of moving more blood — exercise, fear, blood loss — works through one of these two levers. Beat faster, or pump more per beat. And because most ventricular filling is passive, a resting heart can often tolerate losing the atrial “kick” (as in atrial fibrillation) far better than it tolerates a racing rate.

Step through each phase and hear the lub-dub in the interactive.

Interactive Step through the phases of one heartbeat and hear where the lub-dub comes from. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Systole is contraction, diastole is relaxation; the resting cycle is about 0.8 s, mostly diastole.
  • Order of phases: atrial systole → isovolumetric contraction → ejection → isovolumetric relaxation → filling.
  • S1 (“lub”) = AV valves closing; S2 (“dub”) = semilunar valves closing.
  • Stroke volume = EDV − ESV; cardiac output = heart rate × stroke volume (≈5 L/min at rest).

Section 6The ECG

Reading the heart’s electricity off the skin

Every time the heart depolarizes, the wave of electrical change is large enough to reach the body surface, where electrodes on the skin can pick it up. Plotted over time, that signal is an electrocardiogram (ECG or EKG) — one of the most useful, painless, and inexpensive tests in all of medicine. It does not record the heartbeat itself but the electrical events that trigger it, so each bump on the tracing lines up with a step in the conduction system you just met. Learn to read three simple deflections and the whole picture opens up.

P, QRS, and T

The tracing repeats three main shapes. The small P wave is atrial depolarization — the SA node’s signal spreading across the atria just before they contract. The tall, spiky QRS complex is ventricular depolarization — big and sharp because the ventricles are so muscular. (Atrial repolarization happens here too, but it is buried inside the QRS and never gets its own wave.) The rounded T wave is ventricular repolarization, the ventricles electrically resetting so they can relax and fill again. Remember: depolarization triggers contraction, so on the tracing the electrical wave always comes a beat ahead of the mechanical squeeze.

FeatureElectrical eventMechanical partner
P waveAtrial depolarizationAtria contract
PR intervalSA firing through the AV-node delaySignal waits at the AV node
QRS complexVentricular depolarizationVentricles contract
T waveVentricular repolarizationVentricles relax

Intervals, rate, and trouble

The flat stretches between the waves carry as much information as the waves themselves. The PR interval spans the start of the P wave to the start of the QRS — it is the atria firing plus that all-important AV-node pause; stretch it too long and you have a heart block. The QRS duration tells you how fast the ventricles are being activated, and the QT interval covers the whole span of ventricular electrical activity. You can even read the rate straight off the paper: the tracing runs at a fixed speed, so the spacing between two QRS peaks gives beats per minute. A tracing that is too fast is tachycardia, too slow is bradycardia, and a chaotic, quivering line with no real QRS at all — ventricular fibrillation — means the ventricles are twitching uselessly and no blood is moving. That is the rhythm a defibrillator is built to shock back into order.

Watch the flat stretch. The ST segment — the level stretch between the QRS and the T wave, when the whole ventricle is depolarized — normally sits right on the baseline. When a coronary artery is blocked and heart muscle is dying, that segment lifts off the baseline (“ST elevation”), the classic ECG fingerprint of a heart attack in progress.

Tap each wave to link it to its conduction event in the interactive.

Interactive Each bump of the ECG matches an event in the conduction system — tap a wave. Open full ↑
Key points
  • An ECG records the heart’s electrical events from the skin; each deflection maps to a conduction step.
  • P = atrial depolarization, QRS = ventricular depolarization, T = ventricular repolarization; the electrical wave leads the squeeze.
  • The PR interval holds the AV-node delay; a long one signals heart block.
  • Rhythm names: tachycardia (fast), bradycardia (slow), ventricular fibrillation (chaotic, no output); ST elevation flags a heart attack.

The chapter in one breath

  • The heart is a fist-sized double pump in the mediastinum, wrapped in a pericardial sac; the left ventricle is thickest because it faces the highest pressure.
  • Blood flows atrium → ventricle → artery on each side: the right heart serves the pulmonary circuit (lungs), the left heart the systemic circuit (body), and the two run in series.
  • Arteries lead away, veins lead back — a rule about direction; the pulmonary vessels flip the usual oxygen content.
  • Four one-way valves — two AV (tricuspid, mitral), two semilunar (pulmonary, aortic) — keep flow forward; chordae tendineae hold the AV cusps shut.
  • The heart is autorhythmic: the SA node paces it, the AV node delays the signal, and the Purkinje fibers deliver it to the ventricles.
  • One beat = systole and diastole; S1/S2 are valves closing, and cardiac output = heart rate × stroke volume.
  • The ECG reads the electrical events from the skin: P (atrial), QRS (ventricular depolarization), T (ventricular repolarization).

ReferenceWords worth knowing

A quick reference for the terms in this chapter — skim it now, or circle back whenever a word trips you up.

Aorta
The largest artery in the body; it carries oxygen-rich blood out of the left ventricle to begin the systemic circuit.
Atrioventricular (AV) node
The electrical gateway between the atria and ventricles; it delays the impulse about a tenth of a second so the atria can finish filling the ventricles.
Atrioventricular (AV) valve
A valve between an atrium and its ventricle — the tricuspid on the right, the mitral (bicuspid) on the left — held shut by chordae tendineae.
Atrium
An upper, thin-walled receiving chamber of the heart; the right atrium takes blood from the body, the left from the lungs.
Cardiac cycle
One complete heartbeat, made of contraction (systole) and relaxation (diastole) of the chambers.
Cardiac output
The volume of blood pumped by a ventricle each minute; equal to heart rate multiplied by stroke volume (about 5 L/min at rest).
Chordae tendineae
Tough fibrous cords that tie the AV valve cusps to the papillary muscles, keeping the cusps from flipping backward during contraction.
Diastole
The relaxation phase of a heart chamber, during which it fills with blood.
Electrocardiogram (ECG)
A recording of the heart’s electrical activity taken from the skin; its P, QRS, and T deflections map to conduction events.
Endocardium
The smooth inner lining of the heart, continuous with the lining of the blood vessels.
Myocardium
The thick middle layer of cardiac muscle that does the heart’s pumping; thickest in the left ventricle.
Pericardium
The double-walled sac around the heart; its inner serous layers secrete fluid so the beating heart moves without friction.
Pulmonary circuit
The loop of circulation from the right heart to the lungs and back, where blood picks up oxygen and drops off carbon dioxide.
Purkinje fibers
Fast-conducting fibers that spread the electrical impulse through the ventricle walls, triggering contraction from the apex up.
QRS complex
The tall, spiky deflection of the ECG marking ventricular depolarization, just before the ventricles contract.
Semilunar valve
A cup-shaped valve guarding a ventricle’s exit into a great artery — the pulmonary and aortic valves — that snaps shut against backflow.
Sinoatrial (SA) node
The heart’s natural pacemaker, in the wall of the right atrium; it fires 60–100 times a minute and sets the rhythm.
Stroke volume
The amount of blood a ventricle ejects in one beat; the end-diastolic volume minus the end-systolic volume.
Systemic circuit
The loop of circulation from the left heart out to all the body’s tissues and back to the right heart.
Systole
The contraction phase of a heart chamber, during which it ejects blood.
Ventricle
A lower, thick-walled pumping chamber of the heart; the right ejects blood to the lungs, the left to the whole body.

Check yourself

1. A classmate calls the heart “one pump.” Correct them, and explain what the septum has to do with it.
The heart is really two pumps in one — a right heart and a left heart working in step. The right heart pushes oxygen-poor blood to the lungs; the left heart pushes oxygen-rich blood to the body. The muscular septum divides them so the two blood supplies never mix.
2. Why is the wall of the left ventricle so much thicker than the wall of the right ventricle?
Because the left ventricle must generate far higher pressure. It pushes blood through the long, high-resistance systemic circuit to the whole body, while the right ventricle only has to reach the nearby, low-pressure lungs. Thickness follows workload.
3. Trace a drop of blood in order from the superior vena cava to the aorta, naming every chamber and valve it passes.
SVC → right atrium → tricuspid valve → right ventricle → pulmonary valve → lungs → pulmonary veins → left atrium → mitral valve → left ventricle → aortic valve → aorta. The lungs sit in the middle, where the blood is reoxygenated.
4. The pulmonary artery carries dark, oxygen-poor blood. Does this break the rule “arteries carry oxygen-rich blood”? Explain.
No — the real rule is about direction, not oxygen. An artery carries blood away from the heart. The pulmonary artery is carrying oxygen-poor blood away from the heart toward the lungs to be refreshed, so it fits the true definition perfectly.
5. Name the four heart valves, sort them into their two types, and say which type has chordae tendineae.
AV valves: tricuspid and mitral (bicuspid). Semilunar valves: pulmonary and aortic. Only the AV valves have chordae tendineae. The chords tie the AV cusps to the papillary muscles so they cannot flip into the atria during the ventricular squeeze.
6. Distinguish valve stenosis from valve regurgitation.
A stenotic valve is narrowed and will not open fully; a regurgitant (insufficient) valve is leaky and will not close fully. Stenosis forces the heart to strain against a small opening; regurgitation lets blood slosh backward. Both cause turbulence heard as a murmur.
7. Which structure is the heart’s pacemaker, and what does “autorhythmic” mean?
The SA (sinoatrial) node; autorhythmic means the heart generates its own beat without needing a signal from the brain. The SA node fires 60–100 times a minute; nerves only speed it up or slow it down.
8. Why does the impulse pause at the AV node, and what would happen without that delay?
The delay gives the atria time to finish emptying into the ventricles before the ventricles contract. Without it, atria and ventricles would fire almost together, the ventricles would eject before they were fully filled, and each beat would pump much less blood.
9. Put the phases of the cardiac cycle in order, starting from atrial systole.
Atrial systole → isovolumetric contraction → ventricular ejection → isovolumetric relaxation → ventricular filling. During the two “isovolumetric” phases all four valves are shut and no blood moves — the ventricle only changes pressure, not volume.
10. What exactly makes the “lub” (S1) and the “dub” (S2) heart sounds?
S1 (“lub”) is the AV valves snapping shut as ventricular systole begins; S2 (“dub”) is the semilunar valves snapping shut as diastole begins. The sounds come from valves closing, not opening.
11. A patient’s heart rate is 80 beats per minute and each beat ejects 70 mL. What is the cardiac output, and which two levers change it?
Cardiac output = 80 × 70 mL = 5,600 mL/min (about 5.6 L/min). The two levers are heart rate and stroke volume — the body raises output by beating faster, pumping more per beat, or both.
12. On an ECG, what electrical event does each of the P wave, QRS complex, and T wave represent?
P = atrial depolarization; QRS = ventricular depolarization; T = ventricular repolarization. Atrial repolarization also occurs, but it is hidden inside the large QRS complex and has no separate wave. Depolarization triggers contraction, so each wave slightly precedes its mechanical squeeze.
Anatomy & Physiology · Chapter 20

Blood Vessels & Circulation

The heart is only the pump — the real work of the circulation happens in the tubes. In this chapter you will trace a single red blood cell from the moment it is flung out of the heart, through arteries that branch into ever-finer channels, into capillaries so thin that oxygen and nutrients leak across their walls into your tissues, and then back up through the veins against gravity to start the loop again. Along the way you will learn why an artery wall is thick and a capillary wall is one cell thick, what blood pressure really measures, how fluid decides whether to leave the blood or return to it, and the clever tricks your body uses to lift blood out of your feet. Master the vessels and you will understand not just how blood moves, but why swollen ankles, varicose veins, and a stroke all trace back to the same set of rules.

By the end of this chapter you can

  • Name the three tunics of a blood vessel wall and describe the tissue and job of each.
  • Compare arteries, veins, and capillaries by wall thickness, lumen size, pressure, and function.
  • Define blood pressure, distinguish systolic from diastolic, and calculate pulse pressure and mean arterial pressure.
  • Explain how pressure and resistance shape blood flow, and why the arterioles are the body's main resistance vessels.
  • Diagram capillary exchange as a tug-of-war between hydrostatic and colloid osmotic pressure, and explain how the balance can produce edema.
  • Distinguish the pulmonary and systemic circuits and describe special routes such as the hepatic portal system.
  • List the mechanisms of venous return — the skeletal muscle pump, the respiratory pump, and one-way valves — and connect their failure to real disease.

Section 1The wall of a blood vessel

Three layers around a hollow center

Every artery and vein is built the same way: as a set of concentric tunics (from the Latin for "coats") wrapped around a hollow channel called the lumen, the space the blood actually flows through. Reading from the inside out, the three layers are the tunica intima, the tunica media, and the tunica externa. The layers are the same in every vessel; what changes from an artery to a vein to a capillary is simply how thick each layer is and how much muscle or elastic tissue it contains. Learn the three coats once and you can read the wall of any vessel in the body.

Intima, media, externa — from lining to anchor

The innermost coat, the tunica intima, is dominated by a single sheet of flat cells called the endothelium that lines the entire cardiovascular system in one continuous surface. This lining is far more than wallpaper: it is smooth enough that blood cells glide past without clotting, and it releases chemical signals (such as nitric oxide) that tell the muscle underneath to relax or contract. Beneath it sits a thin basement membrane and, in arteries, a springy sheet called the internal elastic lamina.

The middle coat, the tunica media, is usually the thickest and is the muscle of the vessel. It is made of smooth muscle arranged in rings, mixed with sheets of elastic fibers. When the smooth muscle tightens, the lumen narrows — vasoconstriction; when it relaxes, the lumen widens — vasodilation. Because this layer is controlled by sympathetic nerves and by hormones, the tunica media is the dial your nervous system turns to route blood where it is needed and to set blood pressure. The outermost coat, the tunica externa (also called the tunica adventitia), is a tough sleeve of collagen fibers that anchors the vessel to surrounding tissue and keeps it from over-stretching. In large vessels it even carries its own tiny blood supply, the vasa vasorum — "vessels of the vessels" — because the wall is too thick to feed itself by diffusion.

TunicMain tissueJob
Tunica intimaEndothelium + basement membrane (+ internal elastic lamina in arteries)Smooth, non-stick lining; releases signals that relax or constrict the wall
Tunica mediaSmooth muscle + elastic fibersVasoconstriction and vasodilation; sets vessel diameter and blood pressure
Tunica externaCollagen (and elastic) connective tissueAnchors and protects the vessel; carries vasa vasorum in large vessels
The endothelium is the whole story. That single inner layer of cells is where cardiovascular disease begins. When the endothelium is injured — by high blood pressure, smoking, or high cholesterol — cholesterol and inflammatory cells seep into the intima and build the plaque of atherosclerosis. Keep the intima healthy and you keep the arteries young.

Peel the wall apart layer by layer in the interactive below.

Interactive The wall of an artery or vein is built in three layers — tunics — wrapped around a hollow center. Tap each layer from the inside out. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Every vessel wall has three tunics around a central lumen: intima, media, externa.
  • The tunica intima's endothelium is a continuous, non-stick lining that signals the wall to relax or constrict.
  • The tunica media (smooth muscle) sets vessel diameter through vasoconstriction and vasodilation.
  • The tunica externa (collagen) anchors the vessel; atherosclerosis begins in the injured intima.

Section 2Arteries, veins & capillaries

Arteries: the high-pressure pipes

An artery is any vessel that carries blood away from the heart — a definition based on direction, not on oxygen content, which matters later. Because arteries receive blood at high pressure with every heartbeat, they have a thick, muscular tunica media and strong elastic walls. The largest arteries near the heart, such as the aorta, are elastic (conducting) arteries: their walls stretch as the ventricle ejects blood and recoil between beats, smoothing the pulsing flow into a steadier stream. Farther out, muscular (distributing) arteries deliver blood to specific organs, and the smallest branches, the arterioles, act as adjustable nozzles that control how much blood enters each capillary bed.

Veins: the low-pressure reservoirs

A vein carries blood back toward the heart. By the time blood reaches the veins it has spent nearly all its pressure crossing the capillaries, so veins do not need thick muscular walls. Instead they have a thin media, a large floppy lumen, and a prominent tunica externa. Their most distinctive feature is a set of one-way valves — flaps of intima that snap shut to stop blood from sliding backward. Because veins are so distensible, they act as the body's blood reservoir: at rest, roughly two-thirds of your total blood volume is sitting in the veins, ready to be squeezed back into circulation when demand rises. For this reason veins are also called capacitance vessels.

Capillaries: where the exchange happens

Between the smallest arteriole and the smallest venule lie the capillaries — the reason the whole cardiovascular system exists. A capillary wall is nothing but a single layer of endothelium on a thin basement membrane: just the tunica intima, stripped down to its essentials. That paper-thinness is the point, because it lets oxygen, carbon dioxide, glucose, and wastes slip across the wall between blood and tissue. Capillaries are so numerous that no cell in the body sits more than about a tenth of a millimeter from one. They come in three grades of leakiness — continuous (tightest, as in muscle and brain), fenestrated (with pores, as in the kidney and intestine), and sinusoidal (widest gaps, as in the liver and bone marrow) — each tuned to how much needs to cross.

FeatureArteryCapillaryVein
DirectionAway from heartThrough tissuesToward heart
WallThick, muscular, elasticOne endothelial cell thickThin, large lumen
PressureHighFallingLow
ValvesNone (aortic/pulmonary valves are in the heart)NoneYes — one-way
Main jobCarry & distribute pressurized bloodExchange O₂, CO₂, nutrients, wastesReturn blood; store volume (reservoir)
Veins hold the spare blood. Because roughly 60–70% of your blood volume rests in the veins at any moment, a sudden squeeze of the veins (venoconstriction) can pour a large reserve back into the active circulation during exercise or blood loss — a fast, built-in transfusion.

Switch between the three vessel types and watch their jobs change.

Interactive Three vessel types, three very different jobs — carrying blood away, bringing it back, or exchanging with tissues. Switch between them. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Arteries carry blood away from the heart under high pressure and have thick, elastic, muscular walls.
  • Veins carry blood toward the heart, have thin walls, one-way valves, and hold most of the blood as a reservoir.
  • Capillaries are one cell thick — the only place O₂, CO₂, and nutrients are exchanged.
  • Artery vs. vein is defined by direction of flow, not by oxygen content.

Section 3Blood pressure & the pressure gradient

Systolic over diastolic

Blood pressure is simply the force that flowing blood pushes against the vessel walls, measured in millimeters of mercury (mm Hg). In the big arteries this pressure is not steady — it rises and falls with each heartbeat. When the ventricles contract and eject blood, pressure peaks at the systolic pressure; when the ventricles relax and refill, pressure sags to its low point, the diastolic pressure. A typical healthy reading is about 120/80 mm Hg — systolic over diastolic. The gap between them, systolic minus diastolic, is the pulse pressure (here 40 mm Hg), the surge you feel as a pulse at your wrist.

Two numbers worth calculating
