Because the heart spends more of each cycle in diastole than in systole, the true average pressure driving blood forward is not the simple midpoint. This average is the mean arterial pressure (MAP), estimated as the diastolic pressure plus one-third of the pulse pressure. MAP matters clinically because it is the pressure that actually perfuses your organs; a MAP that falls much below about 60 mm Hg starves the brain and kidneys. For a 120/80 reading, MAP is roughly 80 + (40 ÷ 3) ≈ 93 mm Hg.

Pressure falls, and the arterioles set the toll

Follow the blood outward and its pressure drops the whole way — that steady decline is the pressure gradient, and it is precisely what keeps blood flowing (fluid always moves from higher to lower pressure). Pressure is high in the aorta, still pulsatile in the large arteries, and then falls off a cliff across the arterioles. Blood flow through the system follows a simple rule: flow = pressure difference ÷ resistance. Resistance depends most of all on vessel radius, because resistance rises with the fourth power of the radius (r⁴) — halving a vessel's radius multiplies its resistance sixteen-fold. Since arterioles have muscular walls that can constrict and dilate on command, they are the body's chief resistance vessels, the adjustable faucets that set both organ blood flow and overall blood pressure. By the time blood reaches the venae cavae, pressure is near zero.

LocationApprox. pressureWhat is happening
Aorta / large arteries~120/80 mm HgHigh and pulsatile; elastic recoil smooths flow
ArteriolesSharp dropThe biggest fall — the main resistance vessels
Capillaries~35 → 17 mm HgLow and gentle — protects the thin walls; drives exchange
Venules / veinsLowLittle pressure left; other pumps take over
Venae cavaeNear 0 mm HgBlood arrives back at the heart
Small radius, huge effect. Because resistance scales with r⁴, a tiny tightening of the arterioles produces an outsized rise in blood pressure. This is why medications that relax arteriolar smooth muscle (vasodilators) are such powerful tools for lowering high blood pressure.

Tap each segment of the journey to see the pressure fall.

Interactive Blood pressure is highest as it leaves the heart and falls steadily as it travels — the gradient that keeps blood moving. Tap a segment of the journey. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Blood pressure = force of blood on the wall; reported as systolic/diastolic (e.g. 120/80 mm Hg).
  • Pulse pressure = systolic − diastolic; MAP ≈ diastolic + ⅓ of pulse pressure, and it perfuses your organs.
  • Blood flows down a pressure gradient; flow = ΔP ÷ resistance, and resistance rises with r⁴.
  • Arterioles are the main resistance vessels — the dial for both organ flow and blood pressure.

Section 4Capillary exchange

A tug-of-war between two pressures

The entire cardiovascular system is built to deliver blood to the capillaries, and here the crucial trade happens across that one-cell-thick wall. Small molecules cross easily; what is trickier is the movement of fluid in and out, which is governed by a contest between two opposing pressures. The first is hydrostatic pressure — the blood pressure inside the capillary, which physically pushes fluid out through the wall and into the tissue spaces. The second is colloid osmotic pressure (also called oncotic pressure), created mainly by the large plasma protein albumin, which is too big to leave the blood. Because these proteins stay behind, they pull water back in by osmosis. Fluid moves whichever way the stronger pressure dictates.

Out at the start, back in at the end

What makes a capillary elegant is that the balance flips along its length. At the arteriolar end, hydrostatic pressure is relatively high (around 35 mm Hg) and easily overpowers the roughly 25 mm Hg of osmotic pull, so the net force pushes fluid out — this is filtration, delivering water, oxygen, and nutrients to the cells. As blood creeps along the capillary, its hydrostatic pressure bleeds away while the osmotic pull stays steady. By the venule end, hydrostatic pressure has fallen below the osmotic pressure, so the net force now draws fluid back in — this is reabsorption, collecting carbon dioxide and wastes on the way. The net difference between the pushing and pulling forces at any point is called the net filtration pressure.

The lymphatic safety net — and edema

The books do not quite balance: capillaries filter out a little more fluid than they reabsorb, roughly 85% back into the blood and about 15% left behind in the tissues. That leftover fluid is not lost — it is mopped up by the lymphatic vessels and eventually returned to the bloodstream. When any part of this system fails, fluid piles up in the tissues and you get edema, or swelling. Too much hydrostatic pressure (as in heart failure or a standing leg), too little albumin to pull fluid back (as in liver or kidney disease or malnutrition), or blocked lymphatic drainage will each tip the balance toward accumulation.

ForceSourceDirection
Hydrostatic pressureBlood pressure inside the capillaryPushes fluid out of the blood
Colloid osmotic (oncotic) pressurePlasma proteins, mainly albuminPulls fluid into the blood
Net result — arteriolar endHydrostatic > osmoticFiltration (fluid leaves)
Net result — venule endOsmotic > hydrostaticReabsorption (fluid returns)
Low albumin swells the body. Because albumin supplies most of the pull that keeps water in the blood, anything that lowers it — liver disease (less made), kidney disease (more lost in urine), or severe malnutrition (too little protein in) — weakens reabsorption and produces widespread edema. It is why a starving child can have a swollen belly.

Tap each end of the capillary to watch the tug-of-war play out.

Interactive Along a single capillary, two opposing forces decide whether fluid leaves the blood or returns to it. Tap each end to see the tug-of-war. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Fluid exchange is a contest between hydrostatic pressure (pushes out) and colloid osmotic pressure (pulls in).
  • Filtration dominates at the arteriolar end; reabsorption dominates at the venule end.
  • Capillaries filter slightly more than they reabsorb; the lymphatics return the surplus.
  • Imbalance — high hydrostatic pressure, low albumin, or blocked lymphatics — causes edema.

Section 5The circulatory routes

Two great circuits

Blood does not wander — it runs in fixed loops called circuits, and the whole system is really two circuits joined at the heart. The pulmonary circuit is the short loop to the lungs: the right side of the heart pumps oxygen-poor blood out through the pulmonary arteries, the blood picks up oxygen and dumps carbon dioxide in the lung capillaries, and the pulmonary veins carry the now oxygen-rich blood back to the left side of the heart. The systemic circuit is the long loop to everything else: the left side of the heart pumps oxygen-rich blood out through the aorta to every organ and limb, and the veins bring the oxygen-poor blood back to the right side to begin again. The two circuits run in series, so the same volume must pass through both — the right and left hearts pump in perfect lockstep.

The pulmonary vessels break the usual rule. Everywhere else, arteries carry oxygen-rich blood and veins carry oxygen-poor blood. The pulmonary circuit reverses it: the pulmonary arteries carry oxygen-poor blood to the lungs, and the pulmonary veins carry oxygen-rich blood back. Remember: artery = away from the heart, regardless of oxygen.

Portal systems: two capillary beds in a row

Most blood passes through only one capillary bed between leaving and returning to the heart. A portal system is the exception: blood flows through two capillary beds in series, linked by a vein. The most important example is the hepatic portal system. Blood draining the stomach and intestines is loaded with freshly absorbed nutrients (and any swallowed drugs or toxins). Rather than heading straight back to the heart, it is funneled through the hepatic portal vein into a second capillary bed inside the liver, which stores nutrients, detoxifies chemicals, and adjusts blood sugar before releasing the blood onward. Only then does it join the general circulation through the hepatic veins.

Special deliveries

A few organs have their own dedicated arrangements. The coronary circulation is the heart feeding itself, branching off the aorta the instant blood leaves the heart. The cerebral circulation supplies the brain through a protective ring of connected arteries, the circle of Willis, so that if one supply artery is blocked, others can reroute blood around the gap. Such connections between vessels are called anastomoses — natural detours that provide a backup blood supply. Where anastomoses are sparse, a blockage is far more dangerous, which is one reason a clot in a coronary artery so reliably causes a heart attack.

FeaturePulmonary circuitSystemic circuit
Pumped byRight side of the heartLeft side of the heart
Sends blood toThe lungsThe rest of the body
Blood leaving heartOxygen-poor (in pulmonary arteries)Oxygen-rich (in the aorta)
Blood returningOxygen-rich (in pulmonary veins)Oxygen-poor (in the venae cavae)
Main purposeLoad O₂, unload CO₂Deliver O₂ & nutrients, collect wastes

Tap each route to trace where the blood goes.

Interactive Blood travels in circuits. Two big ones move blood to the lungs and to the body; a few special routes serve particular organs. Tap each route. Open full ↑
Key points
  • The pulmonary circuit (right heart → lungs) loads O₂; the systemic circuit (left heart → body) delivers it.
  • In the pulmonary circuit, arteries carry oxygen-poor blood and veins carry oxygen-rich blood.
  • The hepatic portal system routes gut blood through the liver — two capillary beds in series — before it returns to the heart.
  • Anastomoses (as in the circle of Willis) give backup routes; where they are sparse, a blockage is dangerous.

Section 6Venous return

An uphill problem

By the time blood reaches the veins, it has almost no pressure left — often around 15 mm Hg, falling toward zero at the heart. Yet the blood in your feet must somehow climb more than a meter, against gravity, back to the right side of the heart. The volume of blood returning to the heart each minute is called the venous return, and over any stretch of time it must exactly equal the cardiac output — whatever the heart pumps out, the veins must bring back. With so little pressure to work with, the veins cannot do this alone. They rely on a set of external pumps and clever one-way plumbing.

The muscle pump and the valves

The workhorse is the skeletal muscle pump. Deep veins run between your muscles, so every time you contract a muscle — each step you take — it squeezes the veins and forces blood along. The trick that makes the squeeze productive is the one-way valves: when a muscle presses on a vein, the valve above snaps open to let blood rush upward while the valve below slams shut to stop it falling back. Contract, and blood is pushed toward the heart; relax, and the valves hold the ground you gained. Walk across a room and your calf muscles are, quite literally, a second heart pumping blood up your legs.

The respiratory pump, and what fails

A second helper is the respiratory pump. Each time you inhale, the chest cavity expands and its pressure drops while pressure in the abdomen rises; this squeezes abdominal veins and draws blood upward into the low-pressure thoracic veins toward the heart. A third contributor is venoconstriction — the sympathetic nervous system tightening the vein walls to push stored blood back into circulation when demand rises. When these mechanisms falter, the consequences are familiar. Stand still for a long time and the muscle pump stops, blood pools in the legs, and you may faint. Weakened or leaky valves let blood collect and stretch the veins into bulging varicose veins. And blood that sits motionless — on a long flight, or after surgery — can clot in a deep vein, a dangerous deep vein thrombosis.

MechanismHow it worksWhen it matters most
Skeletal muscle pumpContracting muscles squeeze deep veins; valves keep flow one-wayWalking, running — any leg movement
Respiratory pumpInhaling lowers chest pressure and raises abdominal pressure, drawing blood upwardEvery breath; deeper with exercise
Venous valvesOne-way flaps prevent backflow between pumpsStanding upright, against gravity
VenoconstrictionSympathetic tightening of vein walls pushes reserve blood forwardExercise, blood loss, standing up
Your calves are a second heart. This is why walking after surgery or getting up during a long flight is real medicine: contracting the calf muscles keeps blood from stagnating and clotting. Motionless blood is one leg of Virchow's triad — the classic recipe for a deep vein thrombosis.

Watch the muscle pump and valves lift blood against gravity in the interactive.

Interactive Blood in your legs has to climb back up to the heart against gravity, with almost no pressure behind it. Watch the muscle pump and one-way valves do the work. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Venous return is the blood coming back to the heart; over time it must equal cardiac output.
  • With little pressure left, veins rely on the skeletal muscle pump, the respiratory pump, and one-way valves.
  • Venoconstriction pushes reserve blood forward during exercise or blood loss.
  • Failure produces fainting, varicose veins, and deep vein thrombosis — which is why movement matters.

The chapter in one breath

  • Every vessel wall has three tunics — intima, media, externa — around a central lumen; the endothelium lining is where vascular disease begins.
  • Arteries carry blood away under high pressure with thick elastic walls; veins return it under low pressure with valves and hold most of the blood; capillaries are one cell thick and do all the exchanging.
  • Blood pressure is reported as systolic/diastolic; pulse pressure is their difference and MAP perfuses the organs.
  • Blood flows down a pressure gradient (flow = ΔP ÷ resistance); the muscular arterioles are the main resistance vessels because resistance scales with r⁴.
  • Capillary exchange is a tug-of-war between hydrostatic (pushes out) and colloid osmotic (pulls in) pressure — filtration at the arteriolar end, reabsorption at the venule end; imbalance causes edema.
  • Blood runs in a pulmonary circuit (to the lungs) and a systemic circuit (to the body); special routes like the hepatic portal system send blood through two capillary beds.
  • Venous return against gravity depends on the skeletal muscle pump, the respiratory pump, and one-way valves — failure brings fainting, varicose veins, and clots.

ReferenceWords worth knowing

A quick reference for the terms in this chapter — skim it now, or circle back whenever a word trips you up.

Arteriole
A very small artery with a muscular wall that regulates flow into a capillary bed; the body's main resistance vessel.
Artery
A vessel that carries blood away from the heart, defined by direction rather than by oxygen content.
Blood pressure
The force that flowing blood exerts against the vessel wall, measured in millimeters of mercury (mm Hg).
Capillary
The smallest vessel, one endothelial cell thick, where oxygen, carbon dioxide, nutrients, and wastes are exchanged with tissues.
Colloid osmotic (oncotic) pressure
The inward pull created by plasma proteins (mainly albumin) that draws water back into the capillary.
Diastolic pressure
The lower arterial pressure, measured while the ventricles relax and refill.
Edema
Swelling from excess fluid accumulating in the tissues when capillary filtration and reabsorption fall out of balance.
Endothelium
The single layer of flat cells lining the entire cardiovascular system; the inner surface of the tunica intima.
Filtration
The movement of fluid out of a capillary into the tissues, dominant at the arteriolar end where hydrostatic pressure is highest.
Hepatic portal system
A route in which nutrient-rich blood from the gut passes through the liver's capillaries before returning to the heart.
Hydrostatic pressure
The blood pressure inside a capillary that pushes fluid outward through the wall.
Mean arterial pressure (MAP)
The average pressure driving blood through the systemic circuit; roughly diastolic pressure plus one-third of the pulse pressure.
Pulmonary circuit
The loop that carries blood from the right heart to the lungs and back, loading oxygen and unloading carbon dioxide.
Pulse pressure
The difference between systolic and diastolic pressure; the pressure surge felt as a pulse.
Reabsorption
The return of fluid into a capillary from the tissues, dominant at the venule end where osmotic pressure exceeds hydrostatic pressure.
Resistance
The opposition to blood flow, determined mainly by vessel radius (it rises with the fourth power of the radius).
Skeletal muscle pump
The squeezing of deep veins by contracting muscles, which — with one-way valves — drives blood back toward the heart.
Systemic circuit
The loop that carries blood from the left heart to the whole body and back, delivering oxygen and collecting wastes.
Systolic pressure
The higher arterial pressure, measured while the ventricles contract and eject blood.
Tunica media
The middle vessel coat of smooth muscle and elastic fibers that sets vessel diameter through vasoconstriction and vasodilation.
Vein
A vessel that carries blood toward the heart; thin-walled, valved, and the body's main blood reservoir.
Venous return
The volume of blood returning to the heart per minute; over time it must equal cardiac output.

Check yourself

1. Name the three tunics of a blood vessel from inside out, and give the main tissue and job of each.
Tunica intima (endothelium) → tunica media (smooth muscle) → tunica externa (collagen). The intima is a smooth, signaling lining; the media constricts and dilates the vessel to set diameter and pressure; the externa anchors and protects the vessel.
2. Why is the definition of an artery based on the direction of flow rather than on oxygen content?
Because arteries always carry blood away from the heart, but they do not always carry oxygen-rich blood. The pulmonary arteries carry oxygen-poor blood to the lungs, and the pulmonary veins carry oxygen-rich blood back — the opposite of the usual pattern.
3. A capillary wall is described as being ideal for exchange. What single structural feature makes it so, and what would happen if the wall were thicker?
It is only one endothelial cell thick. That thinness lets gases, nutrients, and wastes cross almost instantly; a thicker wall would slow or block diffusion and starve the tissue — another case of structure fitting function.
4. A blood pressure reads 130/85 mm Hg. Calculate the pulse pressure and estimate the mean arterial pressure.
Pulse pressure = 45 mm Hg; MAP ≈ 100 mm Hg. Pulse pressure is systolic minus diastolic (130 − 85 = 45). MAP ≈ diastolic + ⅓ of pulse pressure = 85 + (45 ÷ 3) = 85 + 15 = 100 mm Hg.
5. Where along the vascular tree does blood pressure fall the most, and why are the vessels there called the resistance vessels?
Across the arterioles. Their muscular walls can constrict and dilate, and because resistance rises with the fourth power of the radius (r⁴), small diameter changes cause large pressure drops — making arterioles the main controllers of blood pressure and organ flow.
6. Name the two opposing forces in capillary exchange and state which direction each moves fluid.
Hydrostatic pressure pushes fluid out; colloid osmotic (oncotic) pressure pulls fluid in. Hydrostatic pressure is the blood pressure inside the capillary; osmotic pressure comes from plasma proteins, mainly albumin, that are too large to leave the blood.
7. Why does filtration dominate at the arteriolar end of a capillary but reabsorption dominate at the venule end?
Hydrostatic pressure falls along the capillary while osmotic pressure stays roughly constant. At the start, high hydrostatic pressure beats osmotic pull, so fluid leaves (filtration); by the end, hydrostatic pressure has dropped below osmotic pressure, so fluid returns (reabsorption).
8. A patient with liver disease has a very low blood albumin level and develops widespread edema. Explain the mechanism.
Low albumin lowers the colloid osmotic (oncotic) pressure, so less fluid is pulled back into the capillaries. With reabsorption weakened, filtered fluid accumulates in the tissues faster than it can be returned, producing edema.
9. Trace a red blood cell through the pulmonary circuit, naming the chamber it leaves, the vessels it uses, and whether it is oxygen-rich or oxygen-poor at each step.
Right heart → pulmonary arteries (oxygen-poor) → lung capillaries (picks up O₂) → pulmonary veins (oxygen-rich) → left heart. This is the loop that reverses the usual artery/vein oxygen convention.
10. What is special about the hepatic portal system, and why does it matter for a drug taken by mouth?
Blood passes through two capillary beds in series — the gut, then the liver — before returning to the heart. An oral drug absorbed from the intestine is carried straight to the liver, which can metabolize much of it before it ever reaches the rest of the body.
11. Blood in the feet has almost no pressure behind it yet returns to the heart against gravity. Name the three mechanisms that make this possible.
The skeletal muscle pump, the respiratory pump, and one-way venous valves (aided by venoconstriction). Contracting muscles squeeze the veins, breathing changes chest and abdominal pressures to draw blood upward, and valves stop it from sliding back between squeezes.
12. Why does a nurse encourage a post-surgical patient to walk, and what venous complication is this preventing?
Walking activates the skeletal muscle pump, keeping blood moving so it does not stagnate and clot — preventing deep vein thrombosis (DVT). Motionless blood in the deep leg veins is one of the main triggers for a clot, so movement is genuine therapy.
Anatomy & Physiology · Chapter 21

The Lymphatic & Immune System

Every day, trillions of bacteria, viruses, and fungi test your borders — and most days you never notice, because a layered defense force turns them away before they can take hold. This chapter follows that force from its plumbing to its most precise weapons. First you will meet the lymphatic system, the quiet network that drains your tissues, ferries fat from your meals, and houses your immune army. Then you will watch immunity itself unfold in three lines of defense — from the skin on the outside, through the fast fury of inflammation, to the slow, specific, unforgettable power of T cells and B cells. By the end you will understand not just how you fight infection, but why a vaccine protects you and a snakebite antivenom does not last.

By the end of this chapter you can

  • Describe the three functions of the lymphatic system and trace the path of lymph from a tissue back to the bloodstream.
  • Distinguish the primary and secondary lymphoid organs and state what each one does.
  • Contrast innate and adaptive immunity and place the body's defenses into the correct line of defense.
  • Explain the inflammatory response, name its four cardinal signs, and connect each sign to the event that causes it.
  • Compare the roles of helper and cytotoxic T cells and explain why T cells require antigen presentation.
  • Describe how a B cell becomes a plasma cell, the structure of an antibody, and how antibodies disable a pathogen.
  • Distinguish a primary from a secondary immune response and explain why memory cells make vaccines work.
  • Classify immunity as active or passive and natural or artificial, and give a real example of each of the four types.

Section 1The lymphatic system

A drainage network with three jobs

Your bloodstream is not perfectly sealed. As blood pushes through the tiniest capillaries, a little fluid is squeezed out into the spaces between cells — roughly three liters a day more leaves than comes back. If that fluid simply pooled, you would swell up and your blood volume would crash within hours. The lymphatic system is the return line that prevents this. It is a one-way network of vessels that collects the leaked interstitial fluid, renames it lymph once it is inside a lymphatic vessel, and hands it back to the bloodstream near the heart. That is its first and most basic job: fluid balance.

It also does two more things. In the lining of the small intestine, specialized lymphatic capillaries called lacteals absorb dietary fats and fat-soluble vitamins that are too large to enter the blood capillaries directly — the milky fat-laden lymph they carry is called chyle. And, most importantly for the rest of this chapter, the lymphatic system is the home and highway of the immune system: it houses the lymphocytes and macrophages that fight infection and it filters lymph through checkpoints where those cells can catch invaders in the act.

FunctionWhat happensWhere
Fluid balanceReturns ~3 L/day of leaked interstitial fluid to the bloodLymphatic capillaries throughout the body
Fat absorptionTakes up dietary fats and fat-soluble vitamins as chyleLacteals in the small intestine
Immune defenseHouses and transports lymphocytes; filters lymph for pathogensLymph nodes, spleen, and other lymphoid organs

How lymph flows — with no pump

Lymph starts its journey in lymphatic capillaries, blind-ended tubes whose overlapping endothelial cells act like tiny one-way flap valves: interstitial fluid pushes them open to get in, but cannot push them back out. From there lymph flows into larger lymphatic vessels, which look and behave much like veins — thin-walled, with internal valves to stop backflow. The vessels pass their lymph through bean-shaped lymph nodes, then merge into large lymphatic trunks and finally two collecting ducts: the small right lymphatic duct (which drains the right arm and the right side of the head and chest) and the large thoracic duct (which drains everything else). Both empty into the subclavian veins at the base of the neck, and the fluid is blood again.

Here is the catch: the lymphatic system has no heart of its own. It moves lymph the same way veins move blood back uphill — by the skeletal muscle pump (contracting muscles squeeze the vessels), the respiratory pump (pressure changes with each breath), and rhythmic contraction of smooth muscle in the vessel walls, all made one-directional by those valves. This is why gentle movement reduces swelling and why a limb that cannot move tends to puff up.

Blocked drainage means swelling. When lymphatic vessels are damaged or removed — for example, when axillary lymph nodes are taken out during breast-cancer surgery — fluid can no longer drain, and the limb swells with protein-rich fluid. This is lymphedema. The same principle explains why swollen, tender lymph nodes (lymphadenopathy) are a classic sign that the immune system is fighting an infection nearby.

The lymphoid organs

The organs of the immune system are sorted into two groups by what they do. Primary lymphoid organs are where lymphocytes are born and mature: the red bone marrow, where all blood cells originate and where B lymphocytes mature, and the thymus, a bilobed organ in the chest where T lymphocytes finish maturing and learn not to attack the body's own tissues. The thymus is largest in childhood and slowly shrinks (involutes) after puberty. Secondary lymphoid organs are where mature lymphocytes actually meet antigens and launch responses: the lymph nodes that filter lymph, the spleen that filters blood (removing worn-out red blood cells in its red pulp and screening blood for pathogens in its white pulp), plus the tonsils, Peyer's patches of the intestine, and the diffuse MALT (mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue) that guards every wet surface.

Interactive Tap each organ to meet it — the lymphatic system drains fluid, absorbs fats, and houses the cells that fight infection. Open full ↑
Key points
  • The lymphatic system does three jobs: fluid balance (returns leaked fluid), fat absorption (lacteals make chyle), and immune defense.
  • Lymph flows one way — capillaries → vessels → nodes → ducts → subclavian veins — driven by muscle and breathing, not a pump, and kept one-directional by valves.
  • Primary lymphoid organs (bone marrow, thymus) make lymphocytes; secondary organs (lymph nodes, spleen, tonsils, MALT) are where they fight.

Section 2The three lines of defense

Two kinds of immunity, three layers of defense

Immunity comes in two broad styles. Innate (nonspecific) immunity is the defense you are born with: it treats every intruder the same way, responds within minutes to hours, and keeps no memory of past attackers. Adaptive (specific) immunity is the defense you develop over a lifetime: it targets one particular pathogen with exquisite precision, takes days to gear up the first time, and — crucially — remembers. These two styles are organized into three lines of defense, each one taking over if the one before it is breached.

The first line is the body's outer wall: the skin and mucous membranes and their secretions. Intact skin is a dry, tough, keratinized barrier that few microbes can cross. Mucous membranes trap invaders in sticky mucus, then cilia sweep them out. Chemical defenses back up the wall: the acid mantle of sweat and sebum, the enzyme lysozyme in tears and saliva that digests bacterial walls, harsh stomach acid, and the normal flora of harmless bacteria that crowd out dangerous ones. Nothing here is aimed at a specific microbe — it is a barrier against all of them.

When the wall is breached

If a pathogen gets past the barrier — through a cut, a burn, an inhaled droplet — the second line meets it inside the body. This is still innate and still nonspecific, but now it is cellular and chemical warfare: phagocytes (neutrophils and macrophages) that engulf and digest invaders, natural killer (NK) cells that destroy virus-infected and tumor cells on sight, the inflammatory response, fever, and antimicrobial proteins such as interferons (which warn neighboring cells about viruses) and complement (a cascade of blood proteins that punches holes in microbes and tags them for destruction). The second line does not care which microbe it is fighting — only that something does not belong.

The third line is adaptive immunity: the lymphocytes — T cells and B cells — and the antibodies the B cells produce. This line is slow to start but devastatingly specific, and it leaves behind memory cells so the next encounter is faster and stronger. The rest of this chapter is mostly about the second and third lines.

LineTypeDefenses
First lineInnate — surface barriersSkin, mucous membranes, secretions (acid, lysozyme, mucus, normal flora)
Second lineInnate — internalPhagocytes, NK cells, inflammation, fever, interferons, complement
Third lineAdaptive — specificT lymphocytes, B lymphocytes, antibodies, memory cells
Innate buys time; adaptive wins the war. The first two lines act immediately and hold the invader back for the several days the third line needs to build a custom weapon. And because only the third line has memory, it alone explains why you catch measles once and why a vaccine can protect you for decades.
Interactive Tap each line — a wall to keep invaders out, fast general-purpose troops, and a slow but precise specialist force. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Innate immunity is fast, general, and memoryless; adaptive immunity is slow at first, specific, and remembers.
  • First line = surface barriers; second line = internal innate defenses (phagocytes, NK cells, inflammation, fever, complement); third line = lymphocytes and antibodies.
  • Only the third line has memory — the basis of lifelong immunity and vaccination.

Section 3The inflammatory response

Four signs you already know

Cut your finger and within minutes it turns red, warm, swollen, and sore. Those four cardinal signs — the Latin rubor, calor, tumor, and dolor — are not the infection; they are your body's inflammatory response, a central weapon of the second line of defense. Inflammation is triggered whenever tissue is damaged or invaded, and although it feels like something going wrong, it is a tightly choreographed rescue operation. Its goals are to dispose of debris and pathogens, wall off the injured area so infection cannot spread, and set the stage for repair.

What actually happens

The moment tissue is injured, damaged cells and resident mast cells release a flood of chemical alarm signals — most famously histamine, along with prostaglandins and other mediators. These chemicals do two things at once. They cause vasodilation, widening local blood vessels so more blood floods the area — that is the redness and the heat. And they make the capillary walls more permeable, so fluid, clotting proteins, and defensive cells leak out into the tissue — that is the swelling (edema), and the pressure of that swelling plus the mediators themselves cause the pain.

Now the cleanup crew arrives. Chemical signals from the injury draw phagocytes toward it, a process called chemotaxis. Neutrophils come first, squeezing out of the bloodstream through the vessel wall (diapedesis) and engulfing microbes; longer-lived macrophages follow to devour pathogens and dead cells. The creamy pus that may collect is simply the debris of this battle — dead neutrophils, broken tissue, and destroyed microbes. Once the threat is cleared, the same site becomes the workshop where repair begins.

Cardinal signLatinWhat causes it
RednessRuborVasodilation brings more blood to the area
HeatCalorIncreased blood flow warms the tissue
SwellingTumorIncreased capillary permeability lets fluid leak into tissue
PainDolorPressure of edema plus chemical mediators on nerve endings

Inflammation can also go systemic. When infection is widespread, chemicals called pyrogens raise the hypothalamic set point, producing fever. A moderate fever is protective — it slows the reproduction of many microbes and speeds up the body's repair reactions. The bone marrow also ramps up white-cell production (leukocytosis), which is why a high white-cell count on a blood test suggests infection.

Fever is a feature, not just a bug. Within limits, a modest fever helps you fight infection, which is why it is not always wise to knock every fever down. Note too that anti-inflammatory drugs like NSAIDs and aspirin work by blocking prostaglandins — which is exactly why they reduce redness, swelling, pain, and fever all at once.
Interactive Step through inflammation — the reason a cut turns red, warm, swollen, and sore. Open full ↑
Key points
  • The four cardinal signs — redness, heat, swelling, pain — come from vasodilation and increased capillary permeability.
  • Histamine from mast cells starts it; neutrophils then macrophages arrive by chemotaxis and diapedesis to phagocytose debris and pathogens.
  • Inflammation disposes of threats, walls off the area, and prepares repair; systemically it can produce protective fever and leukocytosis.

Section 4T cells & cell-mediated immunity

Immunity that acts cell-to-cell

The third line splits into two branches. Cell-mediated immunity, run by T lymphocytes, deals with threats that hide inside your cells — viruses, some bacteria, and cancerous cells — and it does its work by direct cell-to-cell contact rather than by releasing antibodies. T cells are born in the bone marrow but mature in the thymus (the "T" is for thymus), where they are screened so that only cells which ignore your own tissues are allowed to graduate.

Here is the defining rule of T cells: they are blind to free-floating antigen. A T cell can only recognize an antigen when it is presented to it, held up on the surface of another cell in a special display molecule called an MHC (major histocompatibility complex). Every nucleated cell carries MHC class I, on which it displays samples of the proteins being made inside it — a healthy cell shows self-peptides, but a virus-infected cell displays viral fragments, in effect raising a flag that says "I am infected." Antigen-presenting cells (APCs) — dendritic cells, macrophages, and B cells — also carry MHC class II, on which they display bits of pathogens they have engulfed. This presentation requirement is the safety switch that keeps the powerful T-cell response from firing at random.

The T-cell lineup

When a T cell whose receptor happens to match the presented antigen finds it, that one cell is selected to multiply into an army of identical clones — a process called clonal selection. Those clones mature into several specialized types:

T cell typeMarkerJob
Helper T cellCD4The coordinator — reads antigen on MHC II and releases cytokines that activate cytotoxic T cells, B cells, and macrophages
Cytotoxic T cellCD8The killer — reads antigen on MHC I and destroys infected or cancerous cells with perforin and granzymes
Regulatory T cellCD4The brake — dampens and shuts down the response, preventing autoimmunity
Memory T cell—The archive — persists for years to mount a fast response on re-exposure

The helper T cell is the quarterback of the entire adaptive response: without its cytokine signals, neither the cytotoxic T cells nor the B cells switch fully on. The cytotoxic T cell is the assassin: when it recognizes an infected cell, it releases perforin to punch a pore in the target and granzymes that trigger the cell to self-destruct by apoptosis, killing the pathogen along with its hiding place.

Why HIV is so devastating. The human immunodeficiency virus specifically infects and destroys helper T (CD4) cells. Take out the quarterback and the whole adaptive team falls apart — both cytotoxic T cells and antibody production collapse. That is why clinicians track the CD4 count to follow the progression toward AIDS.
Interactive Tap each type — T cells run cell-mediated immunity, coordinating and killing only when an antigen is presented to them. Open full ↑
Key points
  • T cells mature in the thymus and run cell-mediated immunity by direct contact, not antibodies.
  • T cells act only when antigen is presented on an MHC molecule — MHC I shows what is inside any cell, MHC II is used by APCs.
  • Helper (CD4) cells coordinate the whole response; cytotoxic (CD8) cells kill infected cells; regulatory cells brake it; memory cells remember.

Section 5B cells & antibodies

The antibody factories

The other branch of adaptive immunity is humoral immunity (from humor, meaning body fluid), and it is the job of B lymphocytes. Where T cells attack threats hidden inside cells, B cells fight pathogens floating free in the blood, lymph, and tissue fluid — and they do it not by direct combat but by manufacturing antibodies. B cells mature in the red bone marrow (a handy memory hook: B for bone marrow) and then wait in the lymph nodes and spleen for their matching antigen.

When a B cell's surface receptor binds its specific antigen — usually with a confirming cytokine push from a helper T cell — that B cell is activated and undergoes clonal selection, dividing rapidly into two products. Most become plasma cells, short-lived but astonishing antibody factories that can pump out thousands of identical antibodies every second. The rest become memory B cells that linger for years, primed to respond instantly if the same pathogen ever returns.

What an antibody is and how it fights

An antibody (also called an immunoglobulin, Ig) is a Y-shaped protein built from four chains — two heavy and two light. The two tips of the Y form the variable region, a custom-shaped antigen-binding site that fits one specific antigen like a lock and key; the stem is the constant region that determines the antibody's class. Antibodies do not usually kill a pathogen outright. Instead they tag and disable it: they neutralize toxins and viruses by covering the part that would bind your cells, agglutinate (clump) many pathogens together for easy disposal, activate complement to lyse the invader, and opsonize — coat the target so phagocytes grab it more readily.

ClassWhere / whenNotable job
IgGMost abundant; the secondary responseLong-term immunity; the only class that crosses the placenta
IgMFirst antibody made in a new infectionLarge pentamer; powerful complement activator
IgASecretions — saliva, tears, mucus, breast milkGuards mucous membranes and passes immunity in milk
IgEBound to mast cells and basophilsDrives allergies and fights parasitic worms
IgDOn the B-cell surfaceActs as an antigen receptor that helps activate the B cell

Why the second exposure is different

The first time you meet a pathogen, the primary response is slow: it takes several days for the right B cell to be found, multiply, and start producing antibody (mostly IgM at first, then IgG), and you may get sick in the meantime. But the encounter leaves behind memory B cells. If that same pathogen returns, the secondary response is far faster and stronger — memory cells launch a flood of high-quality IgG within a day or two, often clearing the invader before you ever feel ill. That difference is the entire logic of vaccination: safely create the memory now, so the real infection meets a secondary response.

Memory is why one exposure protects. A newborn is shielded for months because IgG crosses the placenta and IgA arrives in breast milk — the mother lends her antibodies. But that borrowed protection fades, because the baby made no memory cells of its own. Only an active response — from illness or a vaccine — builds lasting memory.
Interactive Step through how one B cell becomes an antibody factory in the humoral response. Open full ↑
Key points
  • B cells mature in bone marrow and run humoral immunity, becoming plasma cells that mass-produce antibodies and memory B cells that remember.
  • An antibody is a Y-shaped protein with a variable antigen-binding region; it disables pathogens by neutralization, agglutination, complement activation, and opsonization.
  • The secondary response is fast and strong because of memory cells — the principle behind every vaccine.

Section 6The four types of immunity

Two questions sort every kind of immunity

Acquired immunity is classified by asking two simple questions. First: who made the antibodies? If your own immune system made them, the immunity is active — it is slow to develop but long-lasting, because it comes with memory cells. If you received ready-made antibodies from someone else, the immunity is passive — it protects you immediately but temporarily, because when those borrowed antibodies break down there are no memory cells to replace them. Second: how did the exposure happen? If it came through the ordinary course of life, it is natural; if it came through medicine, it is artificial. Cross the two questions and you get four categories.

Active (you make it)Passive (you receive it)
NaturalCatch an infection, recover, and make your own antibodies and memoryAntibodies passed mother → child across the placenta (IgG) or in breast milk (IgA)
ArtificialA vaccine exposes you to a harmless form of the antigen so you build antibodies and memoryAn injection of ready-made antibodies (antiserum) — antivenom, rabies or tetanus immunoglobulin

When each one is used

Active immunity is the goal of prevention. Whether you earn it the hard way by surviving an illness (naturally acquired active) or the safe way through a vaccine (artificially acquired active), the payoff is the same: a durable supply of memory cells ready to crush the pathogen on any future encounter. This is why childhood vaccines can protect for decades.

Passive immunity is the tool of emergencies. A fetus cannot wait to build its own defenses, so it borrows its mother's antibodies (naturally acquired passive); a person already bitten by a venomous snake or an animal with rabies cannot wait the weeks a vaccine would take, so they are given an injection of pre-formed antibodies for instant protection (artificially acquired passive). The trade-off is always the same: passive immunity works right now but fades in weeks to months and never leaves memory behind.

Active prevents; passive rescues. If you have time and want lasting protection, you want active immunity — a vaccine. If you have already been exposed and need protection this minute, you want passive immunity — an antibody injection. One builds memory slowly; the other lends protection instantly and briefly.
Interactive Tap a box — immunity sorted by who makes the antibodies (active vs passive) and how you were exposed (natural vs artificial). Open full ↑
Key points
  • Active immunity = your body makes the antibodies (slow, lasting, has memory); passive = you receive antibodies from another source (instant, temporary, no memory).
  • Natural exposure happens through life; artificial exposure happens through medicine — giving four types in all.
  • Vaccines give artificially acquired active immunity; antivenom and mother-to-baby antibodies are passive.

The chapter in one breath

  • The lymphatic system returns leaked fluid to the blood, absorbs dietary fat as chyle, and houses the immune cells — moving lymph one way with no pump, using valves and muscle.
  • Primary lymphoid organs (bone marrow, thymus) build lymphocytes; secondary organs (lymph nodes, spleen, tonsils, MALT) are where they fight.
  • Defense is layered into three lines: surface barriers, then internal innate defenses, then specific adaptive immunity — and only the last has memory.
  • Inflammation (redness, heat, swelling, pain) is the second line at work: vasodilation and leaky capillaries bring phagocytes to dispose of threats and start repair.
  • T cells run cell-mediated immunity and act only on presented antigen — helpers coordinate, cytotoxic cells kill infected cells.
  • B cells run humoral immunity, becoming plasma cells that make antibodies and memory cells that make the secondary response fast — the basis of vaccines.
  • Immunity is active or passive and natural or artificial; active is slow and lasting with memory, passive is instant and temporary without it.

ReferenceWords worth knowing

A quick reference for the terms in this chapter — skim it now, or circle back whenever a word trips you up.

Active immunity
Long-lasting immunity in which your own body produces the antibodies and memory cells, acquired naturally through infection or artificially through a vaccine.
Antibody (immunoglobulin)
A Y-shaped protein made by plasma cells that binds one specific antigen and tags it for destruction by neutralization, agglutination, complement, or opsonization.
Antigen
Any molecule the immune system recognizes as foreign and can respond to, usually a fragment of a pathogen.
Antigen-presenting cell (APC)
A cell — dendritic cell, macrophage, or B cell — that displays engulfed antigen on MHC class II to activate helper T cells.
B lymphocyte (B cell)
A lymphocyte that matures in bone marrow and runs humoral immunity by differentiating into antibody-producing plasma cells and memory cells.
Complement
A cascade of blood proteins of the innate second line that lyses microbes, promotes inflammation, and opsonizes pathogens for phagocytosis.
Cytotoxic T cell (CD8)
A T lymphocyte that recognizes antigen on MHC I and kills infected or cancerous cells using perforin and granzymes.
Helper T cell (CD4)
A T lymphocyte that recognizes antigen on MHC II and coordinates the adaptive response by releasing cytokines; the main target of HIV.
Humoral immunity
Adaptive immunity carried out by antibodies in the body fluids, run by B cells; effective against free-floating (extracellular) pathogens.
Inflammation
An innate response to injury or infection marked by redness, heat, swelling, and pain that disposes of threats and prepares tissue repair.
Innate immunity
The nonspecific defenses present from birth — the first and second lines — that respond fast to any threat and keep no memory.
Lymph
The interstitial fluid once it has entered a lymphatic vessel, on its way back to the bloodstream.
Lymph node
A small, bean-shaped secondary lymphoid organ that filters lymph and houses lymphocytes and macrophages.
Lymphocyte
A white blood cell of the adaptive immune system; the T cells and B cells.
Macrophage
A large, long-lived phagocyte that engulfs pathogens and debris and can present antigen to T cells.
Memory cell
A long-lived T or B cell left behind after an infection that enables a fast, strong secondary response on re-exposure.
MHC (major histocompatibility complex)
Surface molecules that display antigen for T cells — MHC I on all nucleated cells, MHC II on antigen-presenting cells.
Passive immunity
Temporary immunity from ready-made antibodies received from another source (placenta, breast milk, or injection); immediate but leaves no memory.
Plasma cell
An activated B cell that mass-produces and secretes antibodies, thousands per second.
Thymus
A primary lymphoid organ in the chest where T lymphocytes mature and are screened against attacking the body's own tissues.
Vaccine
A preparation of harmless antigen that triggers artificially acquired active immunity, building antibodies and memory without causing disease.

Check yourself

1. Name the three functions of the lymphatic system, and identify which one prevents you from swelling up over the course of a day.
Fluid balance, fat absorption, and immune defense; fluid balance prevents swelling. About 3 L of fluid leaks from the blood capillaries each day; the lymphatic system returns it to the bloodstream, which is what keeps tissues from becoming waterlogged.
2. The lymphatic system has no pump of its own. How, then, does lymph move, and what keeps it flowing in only one direction?
The skeletal muscle pump, the respiratory pump, and smooth-muscle contractions move it; one-way valves keep it directional. Like veins, lymphatic vessels rely on surrounding movement rather than a central pump, and internal valves prevent backflow.
3. Sort these into primary versus secondary lymphoid organs and say what defines each group: thymus, spleen, red bone marrow, lymph nodes.
Primary: thymus and red bone marrow (where lymphocytes form and mature); secondary: spleen and lymph nodes (where lymphocytes meet antigen and respond). B cells mature in bone marrow, T cells in the thymus; the spleen filters blood while lymph nodes filter lymph.
4. What is the single most important difference between innate and adaptive immunity, and which line of defense provides each?
Adaptive immunity is specific and has memory; innate immunity is general and has none. The first and second lines (barriers, then phagocytes, inflammation, fever, complement) are innate; the third line (T cells, B cells, antibodies) is adaptive.
5. A microbe is inhaled and slips past the mucous membranes. Name three second-line defenses it now faces.
Any three of: phagocytes (neutrophils/macrophages), natural killer cells, inflammation, fever, interferons, complement. These internal innate defenses act fast and nonspecifically once the surface barrier is breached, buying time for the adaptive third line.
6. A cut turns red, warm, swollen, and painful. Link each of these four cardinal signs to the vascular event that produces it.
Redness and heat come from vasodilation (more blood flow); swelling comes from increased capillary permeability (fluid leaks out); pain comes from that edema plus chemical mediators pressing on nerve endings. Histamine from mast cells triggers both the dilation and the leakiness.
7. Why can a moderate fever actually help you, and by what mechanism does an NSAID like aspirin reduce inflammation?
A moderate fever slows microbial reproduction and speeds tissue repair; NSAIDs work by blocking prostaglandins. Pyrogens raise the hypothalamic set point to create fever, while prostaglandins drive pain and swelling — so blocking them relieves several signs at once.
8. Why can a T cell never respond to a free-floating antigen on its own, and what molecule solves this?
A T cell only recognizes antigen when it is presented on an MHC molecule. MHC I on any nucleated cell displays what is being made inside it, and MHC II on antigen-presenting cells displays engulfed material — this presentation requirement keeps T cells from firing at random.
9. Contrast the jobs of a helper T cell and a cytotoxic T cell, and explain why losing helper T cells (as in HIV) is so catastrophic.
Helper (CD4) cells coordinate the response with cytokines; cytotoxic (CD8) cells directly kill infected cells with perforin and granzymes. Because helper cells switch on both cytotoxic T cells and B cells, destroying them collapses the entire adaptive response — the essence of AIDS.
10. Trace what an activated B cell becomes, and describe the parts of the antibody it produces.
It divides into plasma cells (antibody factories) and memory B cells; the antibody is a Y-shaped protein with a variable antigen-binding region at the tips and a constant region forming the stem. The variable region gives the antibody its lock-and-key specificity for one antigen.
11. Why is the secondary immune response so much faster than the primary, and how do vaccines exploit this?
Memory cells left over from the first exposure launch a rapid, strong antibody flood on re-exposure. A vaccine safely creates those memory cells in advance, so that the real pathogen is met by a fast secondary response rather than a slow primary one.
12. Classify each as active or passive, natural or artificial: (a) a childhood measles vaccine; (b) antibodies passed to a fetus across the placenta; (c) an antivenom injection after a snakebite; (d) recovering from chickenpox.
(a) artificial active; (b) natural passive; (c) artificial passive; (d) natural active. Active types (a and d) build your own long-lasting memory; passive types (b and c) lend ready-made antibodies for instant but temporary protection.
Anatomy & Physiology · Chapter 22

The Respiratory System

Every cell in your body burns oxygen to make energy, and every one of them produces carbon dioxide as waste — so twelve to sixteen times a minute, without a single conscious command, your body trades one gas for the other. This chapter follows a breath from the nostril to the microscopic air sac where the exchange actually happens, then explains the physics that pulls air in, the diffusion that swaps the gases, the clever chemistry that ferries them through the blood, and the volumes a clinician measures to tell a healthy lung from a sick one. By the end you will understand not just how you breathe but why the whole elegant machine works the way it does.

By the end of this chapter you can

  • Trace the path of air through the conducting and respiratory zones and name each structure it passes.
  • Describe how the airway warms, humidifies, and filters incoming air, and explain the mucociliary escalator.
  • Diagram the respiratory membrane and explain why its thinness and huge surface area make it ideal for gas exchange.
  • Explain the job of surfactant and predict what happens when it is missing.
  • Use Boyle’s law to explain inspiration and expiration, and name the muscles and pressures involved in each.
  • Apply partial-pressure gradients to external and internal respiration, saying which way each gas moves and why.
  • Describe how oxygen and carbon dioxide are carried in the blood, including hemoglobin and the bicarbonate system.
  • Define the lung volumes and capacities and interpret how spirometry separates obstructive from restrictive disease.

Section 1The respiratory tract

Two zones: a highway and a destination

The respiratory system does one headline job — move oxygen in and carbon dioxide out — but it also warms and humidifies air, filters out debris, gives you the sense of smell, produces your voice, and helps regulate blood pH. To do all this, the airway is split into two functional zones. The conducting zone is plumbing: the nose, pharynx, larynx, trachea, bronchi, and bronchioles that carry air but never exchange gas. The respiratory zone — the respiratory bronchioles, alveolar ducts, and alveoli — is where the actual trade of oxygen for carbon dioxide takes place. Think of the conducting zone as the highway and the respiratory zone as the destination; air only does its real work once it arrives.

Clinicians also divide the tract by location. The upper respiratory tract is everything above the larynx (nose, nasal cavity, sinuses, pharynx), and the lower respiratory tract is the larynx on down (trachea, bronchi, lungs). This is why a "cold" (an upper-tract infection) feels so different from bronchitis or pneumonia (lower-tract problems).

The journey of a breath

Follow one inhalation. Air enters the nostrils, swirls through the nasal cavity, and passes into the three-part pharynx (throat). It slips through the larynx — the voice box, guarded by the epiglottis that flips down during swallowing so food goes to the esophagus instead of the lungs — and enters the trachea (windpipe), held permanently open by C-shaped rings of cartilage. The trachea forks into the right and left primary bronchi, which branch again and again like an upside-down tree into ever-smaller bronchi and then bronchioles, until the tubes end in clusters of alveoli. The whole branching pattern is called the bronchial tree.

StructureZoneKey feature
Nasal cavityConductingWarms, humidifies, filters; lined by mucous membrane
PharynxConductingShared passage for air and food (naso-, oro-, laryngo-)
LarynxConductingVoice box; epiglottis guards the airway
TracheaConductingC-shaped cartilage rings keep it open
BronchiConductingBranch into each lung, then lobes and segments
BronchiolesConductingNo cartilage; smooth muscle sets their diameter
AlveoliRespiratoryThin-walled air sacs where gas exchange occurs

The air that arrives is conditioned

By the time air reaches the alveoli it has been transformed: warmed to body temperature, saturated with water vapor, and scrubbed nearly clean. The nasal cavity does most of this conditioning, its rich blood supply radiating heat and its mucous lining trapping dust and microbes. Beneath that mucus sits a carpet of microscopic cilia beating in waves toward the throat — the mucociliary escalator — which sweeps trapped debris up and out to be swallowed or coughed away. It is a tireless housekeeping system that protects the delicate air sacs from the world.

Smoke stalls the escalator. Cigarette smoke paralyzes and eventually destroys the cilia, so the mucociliary escalator quits. Trapped tar and germs sit in the airway instead of being cleared, which is exactly why smokers develop the chronic "morning cough" — the lungs resort to coughing because their built-in escalator has stopped running.

Walk the whole tract, structure by structure, in the interactive below.

Interactive Follow a breath from the nose down to the tiny air sacs, tapping each structure along the way. Open full ↑
Key points
  • The conducting zone (nose → bronchioles) moves air; the respiratory zone (respiratory bronchioles → alveoli) exchanges gas.
  • Air path: nose → pharynx → larynx → trachea → bronchi → bronchioles → alveoli.
  • Incoming air is warmed, humidified, and filtered; the mucociliary escalator sweeps debris up and out.

Section 2The alveolus & respiratory membrane

A surface the size of a room

The lungs hold roughly 300 million alveoli, each a hollow, bubble-like sac wrapped in a mesh of capillaries. Individually they are tiny, but together their walls unfold into an astonishing 70 square meters of surface — about the floor area of a small apartment — packed inside your chest. This enormous area is the whole point: the more membrane you spread out, the more gas can cross it at once. Structure fits function, and here the function is to make oxygen and carbon dioxide diffusion as fast and abundant as physically possible.

The respiratory membrane: three thin layers

Gas exchange happens across the respiratory membrane, the barrier separating alveolar air from capillary blood. Despite the important-sounding name, it is almost nothing at all — about 0.5 micrometers thick, thinner than a soap bubble. It has just three layers: the alveolar wall (a single flat layer of type I cells), the capillary wall (a single flat layer of endothelial cells), and their fused basement membranes glued between them. Oxygen crosses all three and enters the blood in a fraction of a second; carbon dioxide crosses the other way just as fast.

Cell / layerWhat it isJob
Type I alveolar cellSimple squamous (flat) cellForms most of the wall; thin enough for gases to cross
Type II alveolar cellRounded cell scattered in the wallSecretes surfactant
Alveolar macrophageRoaming immune cell ("dust cell")Engulfs debris and microbes that reach the sac
Capillary endotheliumFlat cells lining the capillaryThe blood side of the respiratory membrane

Why does the thinness matter so much? Because diffusion slows sharply as the barrier thickens. In diseases that thicken this membrane — pulmonary edema floods it with fluid, pulmonary fibrosis stiffens it with scar — oxygen suddenly has farther to travel, and the patient becomes short of breath even though the air and the blood are both right there.

Surfactant: keeping the sacs from collapsing

A wet-walled bubble wants to collapse, because water molecules lining it pull toward one another — a force called surface tension. Left unchecked, that tension would snap the smallest alveoli shut with every exhalation. To prevent this, the type II alveolar cells secrete surfactant, a soapy mix of lipids and proteins that coats the inner surface and breaks up the water’s pull, lowering surface tension and keeping the sacs open and easy to inflate.

Why premature babies struggle to breathe. A fetus does not make enough surfactant until late in pregnancy (around 28–34 weeks). Born too early, the infant’s alveoli collapse with each breath — infant respiratory distress syndrome — which is why premature newborns may be given artificial surfactant and why steroids are given to speed a threatened baby’s lung maturity.

Zoom into a single sac and its capillary in the interactive below.

Interactive Zoom into one air sac and the capillary hugging it, and tap each part of the wafer-thin respiratory membrane. Open full ↑
Key points
  • ~300 million alveoli give ~70 m² of surface for gas exchange.
  • The respiratory membrane is ~0.5 µm thick and has three layers: alveolar wall, fused basement membranes, and capillary wall.
  • Type II cells make surfactant, which lowers surface tension and keeps alveoli from collapsing.

Section 3Breathing mechanics

You make room, and air follows (Boyle’s law)

Here is the surprise most students never expect: you do not actively suck air into your lungs. You make the chest bigger, and air rushes in on its own to fill the extra space. The physics behind this is Boyle’s law — at a fixed temperature, the pressure of a gas rises as its volume falls, and falls as its volume rises. Squeeze a container and the pressure inside climbs; enlarge it and the pressure drops. Air always flows from higher pressure to lower pressure, so all your muscles have to do is change the volume of the chest, and the pressure difference moves the air for you.

Inspiration: an active pull

Breathing in is active — it takes muscle work. The diaphragm, a dome of muscle under the lungs, contracts and flattens downward, while the external intercostal muscles lift the ribs up and out. Both actions enlarge the thoracic cavity. By Boyle’s law, that larger volume drops the pressure inside the lungs (the intrapulmonary pressure) just below atmospheric pressure, and air flows in until the two pressures equalize. The lungs themselves are not muscular; they follow the chest wall because a thin film of fluid and a permanently negative intrapleural pressure keep them stuck to it, so when the chest expands, the lungs expand with it.

Expiration: mostly letting go

Breathing out at rest is the opposite — and easier, because it is usually passive. The diaphragm and external intercostals simply relax, the stretched lungs and chest wall recoil like a released rubber band, thoracic volume shrinks, intrapulmonary pressure rises above atmospheric, and air flows out. No muscle contraction is required for a quiet exhale. Only when you need to force air out — blowing out candles, coughing, exercising hard — do the internal intercostals and abdominal muscles pitch in to actively squeeze the chest.

InspirationExpiration (quiet)
EffortActive (muscles contract)Passive (muscles relax)
Main musclesDiaphragm, external intercostalsNone — elastic recoil
Thoracic volumeIncreasesDecreases
Intrapulmonary pressureFalls below atmosphericRises above atmospheric
Air movementInOut
A punctured seal: pneumothorax. The negative intrapleural pressure is what keeps the lung glued to the chest wall. If a wound or ruptured air sac lets air into the pleural space, that seal breaks, the pressure equalizes, and the lung collapses inward — a pneumothorax. It is a vivid reminder that the lung has no muscle of its own; it inflates only because it is held tightly against a moving chest wall.

Watch the diaphragm change the chest’s volume and pressure in the interactive below.

Interactive The diaphragm changes the chest’s volume, which changes its pressure (Boyle’s law), and air follows. Watch it breathe. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Boyle’s law: enlarging the chest lowers lung pressure so air flows in; shrinking it raises pressure so air flows out.
  • Inspiration is active (diaphragm + external intercostals); quiet expiration is passive (elastic recoil).
  • A negative intrapleural pressure keeps the lungs adhered to the chest wall; break the seal and the lung collapses (pneumothorax).

Section 4Gas exchange

Partial pressure: each gas pushes on its own

Air is a mixture, and in a mixture every gas exerts its own share of the total pressure — its partial pressure — as if the others were not there. Oxygen makes up about 21% of air, so at sea level its partial pressure (written PO₂) is roughly 160 mmHg in the atmosphere and about 104 mmHg once diluted inside the alveoli. This matters because gases, like everything else in diffusion, move from where their partial pressure is high to where it is low, and they keep moving until the two sides even out. Every gas exchange in the body is just this one rule applied over and over.

External respiration: loading O₂, dumping CO₂

External respiration is the swap that happens in the lungs, between alveolar air and the blood. Blood arriving from the body is oxygen-poor (PO₂ ≈ 40 mmHg) and carbon-dioxide-rich (PCO₂ ≈ 45 mmHg). The alveolar air is the reverse — high in oxygen (≈ 104 mmHg) and low in carbon dioxide (≈ 40 mmHg). So oxygen diffuses down its gradient into the blood, and carbon dioxide diffuses the other way out into the alveoli to be exhaled. Blood leaves the lungs freshly loaded with oxygen and stripped of most of its carbon dioxide.

Internal respiration: the reverse at the tissues

Internal respiration is the same physics run backward, out in the body’s tissues. Cells constantly burn oxygen and pour out carbon dioxide, so the tissue PO₂ is low (≈ 40 mmHg) and its PCO₂ is high (≈ 45 mmHg). Blood arriving in the tissue capillaries is the opposite, so oxygen diffuses out of the blood into the cells and carbon dioxide diffuses into the blood to be hauled back to the lungs. Same rule, opposite direction — and the cycle repeats with every heartbeat.

GasAlveoli (air)Deoxygenated bloodTissues
PO₂~104 mmHg~40 mmHg~40 mmHg
PCO₂~40 mmHg~45 mmHg~45 mmHg
Net movementO₂ in, CO₂ out (external)—O₂ out, CO₂ in (internal)
Small gradient, big mover. Notice that carbon dioxide’s partial-pressure gradient (45 vs 40 mmHg) is far smaller than oxygen’s (104 vs 40). CO₂ still keeps up because it is about 20 times more soluble than O₂ and crosses the membrane easily — a good example that solubility, not just the gradient, sets how fast a gas diffuses.

Switch between the lungs and the tissues in the interactive below.

Interactive Gases diffuse from high to low partial pressure — one way in the lungs, the opposite way in the tissues. Switch between them. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Each gas diffuses from high to low partial pressure and stops when the pressures equalize.
  • External respiration (lungs): O₂ into blood, CO₂ out to be exhaled.
  • Internal respiration (tissues): O₂ out to cells, CO₂ into blood — the same rule reversed.

Section 5Gas transport in the blood

Oxygen rides on hemoglobin

Oxygen barely dissolves in water, so blood plasma alone could never carry enough of it to keep you alive — only about 1.5% of the oxygen in blood is dissolved. The other 98.5% is bound to hemoglobin, the iron-containing protein packed inside red blood cells. Each hemoglobin molecule grips up to four oxygen molecules on its iron sites, forming oxyhemoglobin. This is the whole reason red blood cells exist: hemoglobin lets blood carry roughly seventy times more oxygen than it otherwise could.

The oxygen–hemoglobin dissociation curve

Hemoglobin has a wonderful property: it grabs oxygen tightly where oxygen is plentiful (the lungs) and releases it where oxygen is scarce (the tissues). Plot how saturated hemoglobin is against the surrounding PO₂ and you get the S-shaped oxygen–hemoglobin dissociation curve. Better still, hemoglobin reads the tissue’s needs: in an active, hard-working tissue — warmer, more acidic, richer in carbon dioxide — hemoglobin loosens its grip and hands off more oxygen right where it is needed most. This shift, driven by low pH and high CO₂, is the Bohr effect. It is homeostasis in miniature: the busiest cells automatically get the biggest oxygen delivery.

Carbon dioxide travels three ways

Carbon dioxide is more sociable than oxygen and hitches three different rides back to the lungs. A small share (~7%) simply dissolves in plasma; about 23% binds to hemoglobin (as carbaminohemoglobin, on a different site than oxygen); and the majority — about 70% — is converted inside red blood cells into bicarbonate. The enzyme carbonic anhydrase combines CO₂ with water into carbonic acid, which splits into a hydrogen ion and a bicarbonate ion (HCO₃⁻) that slips out into the plasma. At the lungs the whole reaction runs in reverse, releasing CO₂ to be breathed out.

GasForm in bloodShare
OxygenBound to hemoglobin (oxyhemoglobin)~98.5%
OxygenDissolved in plasma~1.5%
Carbon dioxideAs bicarbonate (HCO₃⁻)~70%
Carbon dioxideBound to hemoglobin (carbaminohemoglobin)~23%
Carbon dioxideDissolved in plasma~7%
Breathing is also pH control. Because most CO₂ rides as bicarbonate and releases a hydrogen ion when it forms, your breathing rate quietly steers your blood pH. Breathe faster and you blow off CO₂, pulling the reaction backward and raising pH; hold CO₂ in and pH falls. This is why a patient hyperventilating from anxiety can turn their blood alkaline — the respiratory and buffer systems are two sides of one coin.

Switch between oxygen and carbon dioxide transport in the interactive below.

Interactive Once gases cross into the blood they must be carried — and oxygen and carbon dioxide travel very differently. Switch between them. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Oxygen is carried mostly on hemoglobin (~98.5%); each molecule binds four O₂.
  • The Bohr effect makes hemoglobin release more oxygen in warm, acidic, CO₂-rich (hard-working) tissues.
  • Carbon dioxide travels as bicarbonate (~70%), on hemoglobin (~23%), and dissolved (~7%); this links breathing to blood pH.

Section 6Lung volumes & capacities

Four volumes

A spirometer records how much air you move as you breathe, and its trace is divided into four basic volumes. Tidal volume (TV) is the modest amount — about 500 mL — you move in a normal quiet breath. On top of that, inspiratory reserve volume (IRV, ~3000 mL) is the extra you can pull in with a maximal inhale, and expiratory reserve volume (ERV, ~1100 mL) is the extra you can force out after a normal exhale. Finally, residual volume (RV, ~1200 mL) is the air that always stays behind — you can never fully empty your lungs, and that leftover air keeps the alveoli from collapsing between breaths.

Four capacities (volumes added together)

A capacity is simply two or more volumes added together, and each one answers a practical question. Vital capacity (VC) — the total you can move from a maximal inhale to a maximal exhale (TV + IRV + ERV, ~4600 mL) — is the number clinicians care about most. Total lung capacity (TLC) adds the residual volume back in for everything the lungs can hold (~5800 mL). Inspiratory capacity (IC = TV + IRV) is all you can breathe in from rest, and functional residual capacity (FRC = ERV + RV) is what remains after a normal exhale.

MeasureDefinitionTypical value
Tidal volume (TV)Air per normal quiet breath~500 mL
Inspiratory reserve (IRV)Extra air forcibly inhaled~3000 mL
Expiratory reserve (ERV)Extra air forcibly exhaled~1100 mL
Residual volume (RV)Air that cannot be exhaled~1200 mL
Vital capacity (VC)TV + IRV + ERV~4600 mL
Total lung capacity (TLC)VC + RV (everything)~5800 mL

Dead space & what spirometry reveals

Not every milliliter you inhale reaches the alveoli. About 150 mL stays in the conducting airways — the anatomical dead space — where no gas exchange happens, so of a 500 mL breath only ~350 mL is fresh air that actually does work. Spirometry becomes truly powerful when timed: the FEV₁/FVC ratio (how much of your vital capacity you can blow out in the first second) separates the two great families of lung disease. In obstructive disease (asthma, COPD) the airways are narrowed, so air comes out slowly and the ratio drops. In restrictive disease (fibrosis) the lungs are stiff and small, so volumes shrink but the air that is there comes out briskly, keeping the ratio normal or high.

You can never fully deflate. Because the residual volume never leaves the lungs, a simple spirometer — which only measures air that moves in and out — cannot capture RV, FRC, or TLC directly; those require special techniques. It is also why a lung, even after your hardest exhale, still floats in water: there is always air trapped inside.

Trace each volume on a spirometer in the interactive below.

Interactive A spirometer traces how much air you move — quiet breathing uses only a sliver of capacity. Tap each volume to see it. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Four volumes: tidal, inspiratory reserve, expiratory reserve, and residual; capacities are volumes summed (VC, TLC, IC, FRC).
  • Residual volume always stays behind and keeps alveoli open; it cannot be measured by simple spirometry.
  • A low FEV₁/FVC ratio signals obstructive disease; shrunken volumes with a normal ratio signal restrictive disease.

The chapter in one breath

  • The airway has a conducting zone that moves and conditions air and a respiratory zone where gas is actually exchanged; air travels nose → pharynx → larynx → trachea → bronchi → bronchioles → alveoli.
  • Gas exchange occurs across the paper-thin respiratory membrane of ~300 million alveoli; surfactant from type II cells keeps the sacs from collapsing.
  • Breathing obeys Boyle’s law: inspiration is active (diaphragm + external intercostals enlarge the chest), while quiet expiration is passive elastic recoil.
  • Gases diffuse from high to low partial pressure — O₂ in and CO₂ out at the lungs (external respiration), and the reverse at the tissues (internal respiration).
  • Oxygen is carried mostly on hemoglobin; carbon dioxide travels mostly as bicarbonate, tying breathing to blood pH.
  • The lung’s volumes and capacities quantify how much air you move; a low FEV₁/FVC ratio flags obstructive disease and shrunken volumes flag restrictive disease.

ReferenceWords worth knowing

A quick reference for the terms in this chapter — skim it now, or circle back whenever a word trips you up.

Alveolus
A microscopic, thin-walled air sac (plural: alveoli) where oxygen and carbon dioxide are exchanged with the blood.
Bicarbonate (HCO₃⁻)
The ion into which most carbon dioxide is converted for transport in the blood; also a key player in acid–base balance.
Bohr effect
The tendency of hemoglobin to release more oxygen when blood is warmer, more acidic, and richer in carbon dioxide — conditions found in active tissues.
Boyle’s law
At constant temperature, the pressure of a gas is inversely related to its volume; the basis of how the chest moves air.
Bronchiole
A small airway of the conducting zone with no cartilage, whose diameter is set by smooth muscle.
Conducting zone
The airways (nose through bronchioles) that carry, warm, humidify, and filter air but do not exchange gas.
Dead space (anatomical)
The ~150 mL of inhaled air that stays in the conducting airways and never reaches the alveoli for exchange.
Diaphragm
The dome-shaped muscle beneath the lungs; its contraction is the main driver of inspiration.
Expiration
Breathing out; passive at rest (elastic recoil) and active when forced.
External respiration
Gas exchange in the lungs between alveolar air and blood — oxygen in, carbon dioxide out.
Hemoglobin
The iron-containing protein in red blood cells that binds and carries up to four oxygen molecules.
Inspiration
Breathing in; an active process in which the diaphragm and external intercostals enlarge the chest.
Internal respiration
Gas exchange in the tissues between blood and cells — oxygen out to cells, carbon dioxide into blood.
Intrapleural pressure
The pressure in the fluid-filled space between the lung and chest wall; kept negative so the lung stays inflated against the wall.
Partial pressure
The share of total gas pressure contributed by one gas in a mixture; gases diffuse from high to low partial pressure.
Residual volume
The air (~1200 mL) that remains in the lungs after the most forceful exhale and cannot be voluntarily expelled.
Respiratory membrane
The ~0.5 µm barrier — alveolar wall, fused basement membranes, and capillary wall — across which gases diffuse.
Spirometry
A test that measures the volumes and flow rates of air a person breathes, used to diagnose lung disease.
Surfactant
A lipid-and-protein mixture from type II alveolar cells that lowers surface tension and keeps alveoli from collapsing.
Tidal volume
The volume of air (~500 mL) moved in a single normal, quiet breath.
Vital capacity
The maximum air a person can exhale after a maximal inhale (tidal + inspiratory reserve + expiratory reserve volumes).

Check yourself

1. Put these in the order air passes through them and say which is the first structure of the respiratory (not conducting) zone: trachea, alveolus, larynx, bronchiole, pharynx.
Pharynx → larynx → trachea → bronchiole → alveolus; the alveolus is the respiratory-zone structure. Everything from the nose down through the bronchioles is the conducting zone that only moves air; gas exchange begins in the respiratory zone at the alveoli.
2. A longtime smoker develops a chronic productive morning cough. Which airway defense has failed, and why does that cause coughing?
The mucociliary escalator. Smoke paralyzes and destroys the cilia that normally sweep mucus and trapped debris up and out, so material accumulates in the airway and the lungs fall back on coughing to clear it.
3. Name the three layers of the respiratory membrane and explain why pulmonary edema causes shortness of breath.
The alveolar wall, the fused basement membranes, and the capillary wall. Edema adds a layer of fluid, thickening the barrier so oxygen must diffuse farther and more slowly — less oxygen reaches the blood, and the patient feels short of breath.
4. What do type II alveolar cells secrete, what does it do, and why are very premature babies especially at risk?
