Surfactant, which lowers surface tension so alveoli stay open. A fetus makes little surfactant until late pregnancy, so a very premature infant’s alveoli tend to collapse with each breath — infant respiratory distress syndrome.
5. Using Boyle’s law, explain what happens to thoracic volume, intrapulmonary pressure, and airflow during inspiration.
Volume increases, pressure falls below atmospheric, and air flows in. The diaphragm and external intercostals enlarge the chest; by Boyle’s law the larger volume lowers the pressure inside the lungs, and air moves from higher (outside) to lower (inside) pressure.
6. Which phase of quiet breathing is passive, and what supplies the force for it?
Quiet expiration is passive. The inspiratory muscles simply relax and the stretched lungs and chest wall recoil elastically, shrinking thoracic volume and pushing air out — no muscle contraction is needed unless expiration is forced.
7. A stab wound lets air into the pleural space and the lung collapses. What normal pressure was lost, and what does this reveal about how the lung inflates?
The negative intrapleural pressure was lost, causing a pneumothorax. That negative pressure is what glues the lung to the chest wall; since the lung has no muscle of its own, it inflates only by being held against a moving chest wall, and breaking the seal lets it recoil shut.
8. In the lungs, alveolar PO₂ is ~104 mmHg and incoming blood PO₂ is ~40 mmHg. Which way does oxygen move, and what is this process called?
Oxygen diffuses from the alveoli into the blood (high to low partial pressure); this is external respiration. At the same time carbon dioxide moves the opposite way, out of the blood into the alveoli to be exhaled.
9. Carbon dioxide’s partial-pressure gradient across the respiratory membrane is much smaller than oxygen’s, yet it keeps pace. Why?
Carbon dioxide is about 20 times more soluble than oxygen. Diffusion depends on solubility as well as the pressure gradient, so CO₂’s high solubility lets it cross the membrane quickly despite the modest 45-vs-40 mmHg difference.
10. How is most oxygen carried in the blood, and what does the Bohr effect accomplish in an exercising muscle?
About 98.5% of oxygen is bound to hemoglobin; the Bohr effect makes hemoglobin release more of it in active tissue. Exercising muscle is warmer, more acidic, and CO₂-rich, which loosens hemoglobin’s grip so more oxygen is delivered exactly where demand is highest.
11. Name the three ways carbon dioxide travels in the blood, roughly in order of amount, and name the enzyme behind the largest one.
As bicarbonate (~70%), bound to hemoglobin (~23%), and dissolved (~7%); carbonic anhydrase drives the bicarbonate route. Inside red cells, carbonic anhydrase joins CO₂ and water into carbonic acid, which splits into a hydrogen ion and bicarbonate — a reaction reversed at the lungs.
12. Two patients have abnormal spirometry: one has a low FEV₁/FVC ratio, the other has small volumes but a normal ratio. Which pattern is obstructive and which is restrictive?
The low FEV₁/FVC ratio is obstructive; small volumes with a normal ratio is restrictive. In obstruction (asthma, COPD) narrowed airways slow airflow, so less comes out in the first second; in restriction (fibrosis) stiff lungs shrink the volumes but the air that is present still exits briskly.
Anatomy & Physiology · Chapter 23

The Digestive System

Every meal you have ever eaten was, in a sense, still outside your body until this system took it apart. The digestive system is a long muscular tube — roughly nine meters from mouth to anus — that ingests food, physically and chemically breaks it into molecules small enough to cross a cell membrane, absorbs those molecules into the blood, and packages the leftovers for disposal. In this chapter we will walk that tube from one end to the other, watch the muscle in its walls push and mix, step inside the acid bath of the stomach and the velvet lining of the small intestine, meet the accessory glands that pour in their juices, and finally follow a bite of food down to the individual building blocks your cells can burn. By the end you should be able to trace a sandwich from lips to bloodstream and name what happens at every stop.

By the end of this chapter you can

  • Distinguish the alimentary canal from the accessory organs and list the organs in each group.
  • Name the six essential digestive processes and the four layers of the gut wall.
  • Contrast peristalsis and segmentation and explain what each accomplishes.
  • Describe the regions of the stomach and the gastric gland cells along with what each secretes.
  • Explain how the small intestine multiplies its surface area and how a single villus absorbs nutrients.
  • State what the salivary glands, liver, gallbladder, and pancreas contribute and how their secretions reach the gut.
  • Follow the chemical digestion of carbohydrates, proteins, fats, and nucleic acids to their absorbable end products.
  • Connect common disorders — ulcers, celiac disease, gallstones, lactose intolerance — to the anatomy that explains them.

Section 1The digestive tract

One long tube, two kinds of organs

The digestive system has two divisions that are easy to keep straight once you see the logic. The alimentary canal — also called the gastrointestinal (GI) tract — is the continuous muscular tube that food actually passes through: the mouth, pharynx, esophagus, stomach, small intestine, large intestine, and finally the anus. Laid out end to end it runs about nine meters in a cadaver, though muscle tone shortens it considerably in life. The second division is the accessory organs — the teeth, tongue, salivary glands, liver, gallbladder, and pancreas. These never see the food pass through them; instead they either help mechanically (teeth, tongue) or pour secretions into the canal through ducts. Keeping "tube" and "helpers" separate is the first organizing idea of the whole chapter.

Six jobs, start to finish

Everything the system does can be sorted into six essential processes, and it helps to name them in order. Ingestion is taking food in through the mouth. Propulsion moves it along, first by voluntary swallowing and then by involuntary peristalsis. Mechanical digestion physically breaks food apart — chewing, the churning of the stomach, and segmentation in the intestine — increasing the surface area exposed to enzymes. Chemical digestion uses those enzymes to split large molecules into small ones. Absorption moves the small molecules across the gut wall into blood or lymph. And defecation eliminates whatever cannot be absorbed. A seventh word, secretion, threads through all of them: water, acid, enzymes, bile, and mucus are released along the way to make digestion possible.

The wall has four layers

From the esophagus to the anus the tube is built from the same four concentric layers, and knowing them lets you read any part of the tract. Innermost is the mucosa, the moist lining that secretes and absorbs and faces the hollow center, or lumen. Around it sits the submucosa, a layer of connective tissue packed with blood vessels, lymphatics, and a nerve network. Outside that is the muscularis externa, usually two sheets of smooth muscle — an inner circular layer and an outer longitudinal layer — whose contractions do the propelling and mixing. The outermost layer is the serosa (the visceral peritoneum) where the organ hangs free in the abdomen, or an adventitia of fibrous tissue where it is anchored to the body wall, as in the esophagus.

LayerWhat it isMain job
MucosaEpithelium + thin connective tissue + a wisp of muscle, facing the lumenSecretion, absorption, and defense at the surface
SubmucosaLoose connective tissue with vessels, lymphatics, glands, nervesSupplies the mucosa; houses the submucosal nerve plexus
Muscularis externaInner circular and outer longitudinal smooth musclePeristalsis and segmentation; forms sphincters
Serosa / AdventitiaOuter visceral peritoneum, or fibrous anchoring layerWraps and suspends, or tethers, the organ
The lumen is really the outside world. The hollow channel through the middle of the tract is continuous with the outside at both ends, so food in your gut has not truly entered your body until it is absorbed across the wall. That is why the gut lining must be both an absorptive surface and a defensive barrier — it is the border checkpoint between you and everything you swallow.

Tap each organ in the interactive below to see where it sits and what job it does.

Interactive Follow food through the ~9-meter tube and tap each organ to see its job. Open full ↑
Key points
  • The alimentary canal is the tube food passes through; the accessory organs (teeth, tongue, salivary glands, liver, gallbladder, pancreas) help from the side.
  • The six processes are ingestion → propulsion → mechanical digestion → chemical digestion → absorption → defecation, with secretion throughout.
  • The gut wall has four layers: mucosa, submucosa, muscularis externa, serosa/adventitia.
  • The lumen is topologically outside the body; nutrients only truly enter once absorbed across the wall.

Section 2Moving and mixing

Two muscle layers, two kinds of motion

The smooth muscle of the muscularis externa produces two very different movements, and they solve two different problems. When the muscle works to move food forward, that is propulsion; when it works to mix food in place, that is mechanical digestion. The inner circular layer narrows the tube when it contracts; the outer longitudinal layer shortens it. By timing these two sheets against each other, the gut can either march a bolus along or churn it like a washing machine. Almost all of this happens automatically, without a thought, orchestrated by the gut's own nerve network with only background input from the brain.

Peristalsis: the wave that pushes

Peristalsis is the traveling wave of contraction that propels material along the tract. Just behind the bolus the circular muscle contracts to squeeze, while just ahead of it the muscle relaxes to receive — so the food is milked forward, the way you might squeeze the last of the toothpaste up the tube. Peristalsis is what carries a swallowed bite down the esophagus in a few seconds, and it is powerful enough to work against gravity: you can swallow while lying down, or even upside down, and the bolus still arrives at the stomach because the muscle, not gravity, is doing the work.

Segmentation: mixing in place

Segmentation is a nonmoving rhythm seen mainly in the small intestine. Rings of circular muscle contract at intervals, chopping the contents into segments, then relax as neighboring rings contract — sloshing the chyme back and forth without sending it anywhere in particular. This does two useful things at once: it blends food thoroughly with digestive enzymes, and it repeatedly presses the mixture against the absorptive wall so nutrients can be taken up. Only as absorption nears completion does segmentation give way to peristalsis, which finally moves the residue onward. The whole tempo is set by pacemaker cells — the interstitial cells of Cajal — that fire slow electrical waves the muscle follows.

FeaturePeristalsisSegmentation
Net effectMoves food forward along the tractMixes food in place, little net movement
Muscle actionContract behind, relax ahead — a traveling waveAlternating stationary rings of contraction
Main locationWhole tract — esophagus, stomach, intestinesSmall intestine (and colon)
Main purposePropulsionMechanical mixing and absorption contact
Sphincters are one-way gates. Where the tube needs a valve, the circular muscle thickens into a ring called a sphincter — the lower esophageal sphincter guarding the stomach, the pyloric sphincter releasing chyme in squirts, the ileocecal valve, and the anal sphincters. When the lower esophageal sphincter leaks, acid washes up into the esophagus and you feel it as heartburn (reflux).

Switch between the two motions in the interactive and watch how each one moves the contents.

Interactive Switch between peristalsis (pushes food along) and segmentation (mixes it in place). Open full ↑
Key points
  • Peristalsis propels: contract behind the bolus, relax ahead, so it travels forward — even against gravity.
  • Segmentation mixes chyme in place and presses it against the wall for absorption; it dominates the small intestine.
  • Interstitial cells of Cajal set the rhythm both motions follow.
  • Thickened circular muscle forms sphincters that act as one-way valves between regions.

Section 3The stomach

A muscular bag with regions and folds

The stomach is a J-shaped expansion of the tract that acts as a storage tank, a mixing vat, and a chemical reactor all at once. It has four regions: the cardia, where the esophagus enters; the domed fundus that bulges up beside it; the large central body; and the funnel-shaped pylorus that narrows to the pyloric sphincter guarding the exit into the small intestine. When empty its lining is thrown into deep folds called rugae that flatten out as the stomach fills, letting it stretch to hold a liter or more. The stomach also has a third muscle layer the rest of the tract lacks — an inner oblique layer beneath the circular and longitudinal sheets — which lets it churn food in several directions at once, grinding a meal into the creamy, acidic soup called chyme.

The gastric glands and their cells

The stomach lining is dotted with millions of gastric pits that lead down into gastric glands, and the different cells lining those glands each make one signature product. Mucous neck cells secrete acidic mucus. Parietal cells are the workhorses that pump out hydrochloric acid (HCl) and, just as importantly, intrinsic factor. Chief cells release pepsinogen, the inactive precursor of the protein-splitting enzyme pepsin, plus a little gastric lipase. Scattered among them are enteroendocrine cells such as the G cells that release the hormone gastrin into the blood, which in turn drives the other cells to secrete. Together these cells produce two to three liters of gastric juice a day.

CellSecretesWhat it does
Mucous neck cellAcidic mucusCoats and protects the lining
Parietal cellHCl and intrinsic factorAcidifies chyme, activates pepsin, kills microbes; intrinsic factor is needed to absorb vitamin B₁₂
Chief cellPepsinogen, gastric lipasePepsinogen becomes pepsin in acid and digests protein
Enteroendocrine (G) cellGastrin (a hormone)Stimulates gastric secretion and motility

Making acid — and surviving it

Parietal cells drive the stomach's pH down to about 1.5 to 2, acidic enough to unravel proteins and to convert pepsinogen into active pepsin. Secretion is switched on in three overlapping phases: the cephalic phase (the sight, smell, and thought of food, via the brain), the gastric phase (stretch and protein in the stomach itself, the largest phase), and the intestinal phase that first briefly encourages and then dampens secretion as chyme moves on. The obvious question is how the stomach avoids digesting itself. Its mucosal barrier answers this three ways: a thick coat of bicarbonate-rich mucus, tight junctions sealing the epithelial cells to one another, and rapid replacement of surface cells every few days, so any damaged patch is quickly resurfaced.

No intrinsic factor, no B₁₂. Vitamin B₁₂ can only be absorbed (far downstream, in the ileum) while bound to intrinsic factor from the parietal cells. If those cells are destroyed — as in autoimmune gastritis — B₁₂ absorption fails and the person develops pernicious anemia, no matter how much B₁₂ is in the diet. It is a neat example of one organ's secretion mattering an organ away.

Switch between the stomach's regions and its secreting cells in the interactive.

Interactive Explore the stomach's regions and the gland cells that pour out acid and enzymes. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Regions: cardia, fundus, body, pylorus; rugae let it stretch; a third oblique muscle layer churns food into chyme.
  • Gland cells: parietal (HCl + intrinsic factor), chief (pepsinogen), mucous neck (mucus), G cells (gastrin).
  • Acid pH ~1.5–2 activates pepsin; secretion runs in cephalic, gastric, and intestinal phases.
  • The mucosal barrier (bicarbonate mucus, tight junctions, fast cell turnover) keeps the stomach from digesting itself.

Section 4The small intestine

Three segments, one enormous surface

The small intestine is where nearly all chemical digestion finishes and nearly all absorption happens, so almost everything about it is built to maximize the surface exposed to chyme. It has three segments in sequence: the short, curved duodenum that receives chyme and the bile and pancreatic juices; the long jejunum, the main site of absorption; and the ileum, which finishes the job and empties into the large intestine at the ileocecal valve. Although it is only about an inch wide, it is by far the longest part of the tract, and it packs three levels of folding to turn that length into a surface roughly the area of a tennis court — on the order of 200 square meters.

Three amplifiers of surface area

The magnification comes in three nested tiers, each one folding the layer above it. First, the wall itself is thrown into permanent circular folds (plicae circulares) that force chyme to spiral slowly along, giving it more time and contact. Second, the surface of every fold is carpeted with millions of fingerlike villi, each a projection of mucosa about a millimeter tall. Third, the apical membrane of every absorptive cell on a villus is fringed with thousands of tiny microvilli, so densely packed they look furry under the microscope — the brush border. Together these three amplifiers multiply the flat tube's area more than 500-fold.

AmplifierWhat it isRough effect
Circular foldsPermanent ridges of the whole wallSlow and spiral the chyme; ~3× area
VilliFingerlike mucosal projections, ~1 mmCarpet every fold; ~10× area
Microvilli (brush border)Membrane fringe on each cell's apexVast final surface; ~20× area; hold digestive enzymes

Inside a single villus

Zoom into one villus and you find the whole absorption machine in miniature. Its outer layer is a sheet of absorptive epithelial cells (enterocytes) carrying the brush border, whose membrane enzymes finish digesting sugars and peptides right at the point of uptake. Inside the villus sits a network of capillaries that carry off absorbed sugars and amino acids to the liver, and a single blind lymphatic vessel called a lacteal that absorbs digested fats. Between the villi, deep pits called intestinal crypts continually manufacture fresh cells to replace the ones worn off the tips, which is why the lining renews itself every few days.

Flatten the villi and absorption collapses. In celiac disease, an immune reaction to gluten attacks and blunts the villi until the lining goes nearly flat. With the amplifying surface gone, nutrients pass through unabsorbed, causing diarrhea, weight loss, and deficiencies — a vivid demonstration that in this organ, surface area is function.

Tap each level of folding, then explore the parts of a single villus, in the interactive.

Interactive Peel through the three levels of folding, then tap the parts of a single villus. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Segments in order: duodenum → jejunum → ileum; this is where most digestion and absorption happen.
  • Three surface amplifiers: circular folds → villi → microvilli (brush border), giving ~200 m².
  • A villus contains a capillary network (sugars, amino acids) and a lacteal (fats), fed by cells born in the crypts.
  • Destroying the villi — as in celiac disease — wrecks absorption.

Section 5The accessory organs

Glands that pour in from the side

The accessory glands never let food pass through them; instead they manufacture secretions and deliver them into the canal through ducts, usually into the mouth or the duodenum. Three sets matter most. The salivary glands — the parotid in front of each ear, the submandibular under the jaw, and the sublingual under the tongue — release up to a liter and a half of saliva a day. Saliva moistens food into a swallowable bolus, begins starch digestion with salivary amylase, and defends the mouth with the antibacterial enzyme lysozyme and antibodies.

The liver and gallbladder

The liver is the body's largest gland and a chemical factory with dozens of jobs, but its one digestive contribution is bile. Bile is a greenish fluid containing bile salts that act on fat the way dish soap acts on grease: they emulsify it, breaking large fat globules into a fine suspension of tiny droplets so that fat-digesting enzymes have vastly more surface to attack. Between meals bile is diverted into the gallbladder, a small muscular pouch that stores and concentrates it. When a fatty meal reaches the duodenum, the hormone cholecystokinin (CCK) signals the gallbladder to squeeze, sending bile down the common bile duct into the small intestine.

The pancreas: two glands in one

The pancreas lies behind the stomach and does double duty. Its exocrine tissue — clusters of acinar cells — produces pancreatic juice, the most complete digestive cocktail in the body: enzymes for every food group (pancreatic amylase, lipase, the protein-splitters trypsin and chymotrypsin, and nucleases) plus a flood of bicarbonate that neutralizes the acidic chyme arriving from the stomach so the intestinal enzymes can work near neutral pH. Its endocrine tissue — the pancreatic islets — instead secretes insulin and glucagon into the blood to regulate blood sugar. The pancreatic duct joins the common bile duct at the hepatopancreatic ampulla, and their combined flow enters the duodenum through the sphincter of Oddi.

OrganSecretionDigestive contribution
Salivary glandsSaliva (amylase, lysozyme, mucus)Moisten food; start starch digestion; defend the mouth
LiverBile (bile salts)Emulsifies fats into small droplets
Gallbladder— (stores & concentrates bile)Releases bile on demand via CCK
Pancreas (exocrine)Pancreatic juice (enzymes + bicarbonate)Digests all food groups; neutralizes acid
Bile is a detergent, not an enzyme. Bile salts chemically break nothing — they only physically scatter fat into a fine emulsion so pancreatic lipase can reach it. If bile flow is blocked (say, a gallstone lodged in the bile duct), fat digestion falters, stools turn pale and greasy, and the trapped bile pigment backs up to yellow the skin as jaundice.

Tap each accessory organ in the interactive to trace where its secretion goes.

Interactive Tap each accessory organ to see the secretion it pours into the small intestine. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Salivary glands (parotid, submandibular, sublingual) moisten food and start starch digestion with amylase.
  • The liver makes bile, which emulsifies fat; the gallbladder stores it and releases it via CCK.
  • The pancreas supplies enzymes for every food group plus bicarbonate to neutralize acid, and separately makes insulin/glucagon.
  • Pancreatic and bile ducts merge at the hepatopancreatic ampulla to enter the duodenum.

Section 6Chemical digestion

Why big molecules must be cut down

The nutrients in food arrive as large polymers — starch, proteins, fats, nucleic acids — and they are simply too big to cross the cell membranes of the gut lining. Chemical digestion solves this by hydrolysis: enzymes split the bonds holding each polymer together, adding a molecule of water at every cut, until only the small absorbable building blocks remain. Each food group has its own team of enzymes and its own set of end products, and it is worth learning them side by side because the exam and the clinic both ask you to trace a nutrient from plate to bloodstream.

Carbohydrates, proteins, and nucleic acids

Carbohydrate digestion starts in the mouth with salivary amylase and resumes in the small intestine with pancreatic amylase, both breaking starch into the disaccharide maltose and related fragments. Brush-border enzymes — maltase, sucrase, and lactase — then split those disaccharides into the monosaccharides glucose, fructose, and galactose, which are absorbed. Protein digestion begins in the stomach with pepsin and continues in the intestine with the pancreatic proteases trypsin and chymotrypsin, finishing with brush-border peptidases that release single amino acids. Nucleic acids are handled by pancreatic nucleases and brush-border enzymes down to nucleotides.

Fats take a different route

Fat digestion is the special case. Because fat will not dissolve in the watery chyme, bile salts must first emulsify it into tiny droplets; only then can pancreatic lipase clip each triglyceride into monoglycerides and free fatty acids. These products are ferried to the villus surface in little bile-salt packages called micelles, absorbed into the enterocyte, and there reassembled into triglycerides, coated in protein, and shipped out as droplets called chylomicrons. Too big for capillaries, chylomicrons enter the lacteal instead — which is why dietary fat travels first through the lymph, not straight to the liver like sugars and amino acids.

NutrientKey enzymesAbsorbed as
CarbohydrateSalivary & pancreatic amylase; brush-border maltase, sucrase, lactaseMonosaccharides (glucose, fructose, galactose)
ProteinPepsin (stomach); trypsin, chymotrypsin; brush-border peptidasesAmino acids
FatBile salts (emulsify) + pancreatic lipaseMonoglycerides & fatty acids → chylomicrons
Nucleic acidPancreatic nucleases; brush-border enzymesNucleotides
Lactose intolerance is a missing brush-border enzyme. When the enzyme lactase is low, the milk sugar lactose passes undigested into the colon, where bacteria ferment it into gas and draw in water — producing the bloating, cramps, and diarrhea of lactose intolerance. It is chemical digestion failing at a single, final step on the brush border.

Tap a nutrient in the interactive to follow it down to its absorbable building blocks.

Interactive Tap a nutrient and follow its enzymes down to the building blocks your cells absorb. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Chemical digestion is hydrolysis — enzymes cut polymers into absorbable monomers.
  • Carbs → monosaccharides (amylase then brush-border maltase/sucrase/lactase); proteins → amino acids (pepsin, trypsin, chymotrypsin, peptidases); nucleic acids → nucleotides.
  • Fats need bile emulsification before lipase, then travel as chylomicrons into the lacteal.
  • A missing brush-border enzyme (lactase) explains lactose intolerance.

The chapter in one breath

  • The digestive system is an alimentary canal (mouth → anus) plus accessory organs, carrying out ingestion, propulsion, mechanical and chemical digestion, absorption, and defecation.
  • The gut wall's four layers — mucosa, submucosa, muscularis externa, serosa/adventitia — repeat along the whole tube, and the lumen is topologically outside the body.
  • Smooth muscle produces peristalsis (propels food forward) and segmentation (mixes it for absorption), with sphincters as one-way gates.
  • The stomach churns food into chyme and secretes acid, pepsinogen, and intrinsic factor from parietal, chief, mucous, and G cells, protected by its mucosal barrier.
  • The small intestine absorbs almost everything, using circular folds, villi, and microvilli to build a huge surface; each villus holds capillaries and a lacteal.
  • The salivary glands, liver, gallbladder, and pancreas pour saliva, bile, and enzyme-rich, bicarbonate-buffered pancreatic juice into the canal.
  • Chemical digestion hydrolyzes carbs to monosaccharides, proteins to amino acids, fats (after bile emulsification) to fatty acids and chylomicrons, and nucleic acids to nucleotides.

ReferenceWords worth knowing

A quick reference for the terms in this chapter — skim it now, or circle back whenever a word trips you up.

Absorption
The movement of digested nutrients across the gut wall into the blood or lymph.
Accessory organs
Digestive structures — teeth, tongue, salivary glands, liver, gallbladder, pancreas — that aid digestion without food passing through them.
Alimentary canal
The continuous muscular tube (mouth, pharynx, esophagus, stomach, small and large intestine, anus) that food travels through; the GI tract.
Bile
A liver secretion containing bile salts that emulsifies fats; stored and concentrated in the gallbladder.
Chylomicron
A protein-coated droplet of reassembled fat that carries absorbed lipids from the intestine into the lacteal and lymph.
Chyme
The soupy, acidic mixture of partly digested food and gastric juice that leaves the stomach.
Emulsification
The physical breakup of large fat globules into tiny droplets by bile salts, increasing the surface available to lipase.
Hydrolysis
The enzyme-driven splitting of a large molecule by adding water; the chemistry of chemical digestion.
Intrinsic factor
A parietal-cell protein required for absorption of vitamin B₁₂ in the ileum; its loss causes pernicious anemia.
Lacteal
The blind lymphatic vessel in the center of a villus that absorbs digested fats (chylomicrons).
Mucosa
The innermost gut layer, facing the lumen, that secretes and absorbs.
Parietal cell
A gastric gland cell that secretes hydrochloric acid and intrinsic factor.
Pepsin
The stomach's protein-digesting enzyme, formed when acid activates pepsinogen from chief cells.
Peristalsis
A traveling wave of smooth-muscle contraction that propels material forward along the tract.
Rugae
Deep folds of the empty stomach lining that flatten as it fills, allowing it to stretch.
Segmentation
Stationary rings of contraction that mix chyme in place and press it against the absorptive wall.
Serosa
The outer visceral-peritoneum layer of a gut organ that hangs free in the abdomen.
Sphincter
A thickened ring of circular muscle that acts as a valve between regions of the tract.
Villus
A fingerlike mucosal projection of the small intestine, carpeted with microvilli, that absorbs nutrients.

Check yourself

1. A classmate lists the liver, stomach, pancreas, and esophagus together as "the tube food passes through." Which of these does not belong, and what is the correct term for the group it belongs to instead?
The liver and the pancreas do not belong; they are accessory organs. Food actually passes through the esophagus and stomach (the alimentary canal), but the liver and pancreas only deliver secretions into the canal through ducts.
2. Name the six essential digestive processes in order, and say which one physically increases the surface area exposed to enzymes.
Ingestion → propulsion → mechanical digestion → chemical digestion → absorption → defecation; mechanical digestion increases surface area. Chewing and churning break food into smaller pieces so enzymes have more surface to act on.
3. List the four layers of the gut wall from the lumen outward, and name the layer whose two muscle sheets produce peristalsis.
Mucosa → submucosa → muscularis externa → serosa/adventitia; the muscularis externa does the propelling. Its inner circular and outer longitudinal smooth-muscle layers generate both peristalsis and segmentation.
4. Contrast peristalsis and segmentation: what does each accomplish, and where does segmentation mainly occur?
Peristalsis propels food forward; segmentation mixes it in place and occurs mainly in the small intestine. Peristalsis is a traveling wave, while segmentation is alternating stationary rings that blend chyme with enzymes and press it against the wall for absorption.
5. Why can you swallow a drink of water while lying flat or even upside down?
Because peristalsis, not gravity, moves the bolus. The circular muscle contracts behind the bolus and relaxes ahead of it, milking it toward the stomach regardless of body position.
6. Match each gastric gland cell to its secretion: parietal cell, chief cell, G cell, mucous neck cell.
Parietal → HCl and intrinsic factor; chief → pepsinogen; G cell → gastrin; mucous neck → acidic mucus. Pepsinogen becomes active pepsin in the acid the parietal cells create, and gastrin (a hormone) stimulates the whole gland.
7. A patient's parietal cells have been destroyed by autoimmune gastritis. Which vitamin will they fail to absorb, and what condition results?
Vitamin B₁₂, causing pernicious anemia. Parietal cells make intrinsic factor, which B₁₂ must bind to in order to be absorbed in the ileum; without it, no amount of dietary B₁₂ is taken up.
8. Name the three structural amplifiers that give the small intestine its huge absorptive surface, from largest to smallest.
Circular folds → villi → microvilli (the brush border). Each tier folds the one above it, multiplying the flat surface more than 500-fold to roughly 200 square meters.
9. Inside one villus, which vessel absorbs digested sugars and amino acids, and which absorbs digested fat?
The capillary network absorbs sugars and amino acids; the lacteal absorbs fat. Fats leave as chylomicrons, which are too big for capillaries and enter the lymphatic lacteal instead.
10. The pancreas contributes two things that make the small intestine work. Name both, and explain why the second is needed.
A full set of digestive enzymes and a flood of bicarbonate. Bicarbonate neutralizes the acidic chyme arriving from the stomach so that the intestinal and pancreatic enzymes can work near their neutral optimum pH.
11. Bile is essential for fat digestion, yet it contains no enzymes. What does it actually do, and what happens if a gallstone blocks the bile duct?
Bile salts emulsify fat — physically scattering it into tiny droplets so lipase can reach it. A blocked duct means fat is poorly digested (pale, greasy stools) and bile pigment backs up, yellowing the skin as jaundice.
12. Trace each nutrient to its absorbable end product: starch, protein, triglyceride. For the fat, name the form in which it finally leaves the intestinal cell.
Starch → monosaccharides; protein → amino acids; triglyceride → monoglycerides and fatty acids, reassembled and exported as chylomicrons. Carbs and amino acids enter the blood capillaries, while chylomicrons enter the lacteal and travel through the lymph first.
Anatomy & Physiology · Chapter 24

Metabolism & Nutrition

Every second you are alive, thousands of chemical reactions are running inside your cells — some building, some breaking down, all of them handling energy. That entire web of reactions is metabolism, and this chapter is where the food on your plate finally meets the machinery of your cells. You will follow a molecule of glucose as it is dismantled to power an ATP, watch your body flip between storing fuel after a meal and spending it between meals, and learn what the six classes of nutrients actually do once they are inside you. By the end, the deceptively simple question — why do we eat? — will have a precise, satisfying answer.

By the end of this chapter you can

  • Define metabolism and distinguish anabolism from catabolism by their direction and their handling of energy.
  • Explain how ATP stores and delivers energy, and how catabolism is coupled to anabolism.
  • Trace glucose through the four steps of cellular respiration and account for the ATP produced at each stage.
  • Explain why oxygen is required for aerobic respiration and what the cell does when it runs short.
  • Contrast the absorptive and postabsorptive metabolic states and the roles of insulin and glucagon.
  • Name the six classes of nutrients, state which yield energy, and give each one's caloric value.
  • Separate the fat-soluble from the water-soluble vitamins and predict which are stored and which must be eaten regularly.
  • Describe energy balance and the three parts of total energy expenditure.

Section 1Anabolism & catabolism

One word for every reaction

Metabolism is the sum of every chemical reaction in the body — and there are staggering numbers of them running at once. To make that ocean learnable, we sort every reaction into one of two directions. Anabolism is the building direction: small molecules are joined into larger ones, usually by dehydration synthesis (removing a water molecule to form a bond). Amino acids become proteins, glucose units become glycogen, fatty acids and glycerol become fat. Building costs energy, so anabolic reactions are endergonic — they must be paid for. Catabolism is the breaking-down direction: large molecules are split into smaller ones, usually by hydrolysis (adding water to break a bond). Catabolism releases the energy that was locked in those chemical bonds, so these reactions are exergonic — they pay out. The two directions are not separate businesses; the energy released by catabolism is precisely what funds anabolism.

ATP: the cell's rechargeable battery

The body does not spend food energy directly. Instead it converts that energy into a single, universal currency — ATP (adenosine triphosphate). ATP carries its energy in the bonds between its three phosphate groups. Snap off the last phosphate and you get ADP plus a free phosphate plus a burst of usable energy; the cell uses that burst to power muscle contraction, nerve signaling, and anabolic building. Catabolism then recharges the battery by bolting the phosphate back on, turning ADP back into ATP. This constant charge-and-discharge is called energy coupling: an exergonic reaction (breaking a bond) is linked to an endergonic one (doing work) so the energy is handed off rather than lost as heat. You cycle your entire body weight in ATP roughly every day — not because you store that much, but because each molecule is recharged thousands of times.

Following the electrons

Many metabolic reactions are oxidation-reduction (redox) reactions, in which electrons are transferred from one molecule to another. A molecule that loses electrons is oxidized; one that gains them is reduced. This matters because electrons carry energy, and the cell moves them around using shuttle molecules called electron carriers — chiefly NAD⁺ and FAD. When these carriers pick up electrons (becoming NADH and FADH₂), they are essentially loading up on energy to deliver later. Hold onto that idea; in the next section those loaded carriers become the key to making most of your ATP.

FeatureAnabolismCatabolism
DirectionBuilds large molecules from smallBreaks large molecules into small
Reaction typeDehydration synthesisHydrolysis
EnergyRequires energy (endergonic); uses ATPReleases energy (exergonic); makes ATP
ExampleAmino acids → protein; glucose → glycogenProtein → amino acids; glycogen → glucose
Anabolic builds, catabolic breaks. The gym word gives it away: "anabolic" steroids are used to build muscle. Any time you see "ana-", think up and together; any time you see "cata-", think down and apart. And remember the payoff line — catabolism releases the energy that anabolism spends.

Switch between the building and breaking directions in the interactive below.

Interactive Metabolism runs in two directions — one builds molecules and stores energy, the other breaks them down and releases it. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Metabolism = all body reactions; anabolism builds (endergonic, uses ATP), catabolism breaks down (exergonic, makes ATP).
  • ATP is the cell's energy currency — it discharges to ADP + phosphate to do work, and catabolism recharges it.
  • Energy coupling links energy-releasing reactions to energy-requiring ones so the energy is used, not wasted.
  • Electron carriers NAD⁺ and FAD shuttle energy-rich electrons from catabolism to ATP production.

Section 2Cellular respiration

Burning glucose, carefully

Cellular respiration is the controlled catabolism of glucose to make ATP. It is a kind of slow, safe burning — instead of releasing all of glucose's energy at once as a destructive flash, the cell strips it away in small steps, capturing the energy in ATP as it goes. The whole process happens in four stages across two locations: the first stage in the cytoplasm, the last three inside the mitochondrion. When enough oxygen is present, a single glucose molecule can yield up to roughly 30 to 32 ATP. Follow it stage by stage.

The four stages

1. Glycolysis happens in the cytoplasm and needs no oxygen. One six-carbon glucose is split into two three-carbon pyruvate molecules. The cell spends two ATP to start the process and harvests four, for a net gain of 2 ATP, plus 2 NADH. 2. Pyruvate oxidation (the transition reaction) moves each pyruvate into the mitochondrial matrix, snips off a carbon as CO₂, and attaches the remainder to a carrier to form acetyl CoA, making more NADH. 3. The citric acid cycle (Krebs cycle), also in the matrix, feeds acetyl CoA around a loop that releases the rest of the carbons as CO₂, yields a little more ATP directly, and — most importantly — loads up many molecules of NADH and FADH₂. 4. Oxidative phosphorylation on the inner mitochondrial membrane is where the real payoff comes. The loaded carriers drop their electrons into the electron transport chain, which uses that energy to pump H⁺ ions and build a gradient; the ions then rush back through an enzyme that spins like a turbine, snapping phosphates onto ADP. This step alone makes the large majority of the ATP.

StageLocationNeeds O₂?Main products
GlycolysisCytoplasmNo2 pyruvate, net 2 ATP, 2 NADH
Pyruvate oxidationMitochondrial matrixYes (aerobic)Acetyl CoA, CO₂, NADH
Citric acid cycleMitochondrial matrixYes (aerobic)CO₂, 2 ATP, NADH, FADH₂
Oxidative phosphorylationInner mitochondrial membraneYes~26–28 ATP, H₂O

Why oxygen, and what happens without it

Here is the punchline of the whole process: oxygen is the final electron acceptor. At the very end of the electron transport chain, the spent electrons and some H⁺ ions are handed to O₂, forming H₂O (the water you make from breathing). If no oxygen is waiting there to accept them, the chain backs up like a clogged drain, the carriers stay loaded, and stages 2 through 4 grind to a halt. When that happens, the cell falls back on anaerobic fermentation: it keeps glycolysis limping along by converting pyruvate to lactic acid, which regenerates the NAD⁺ glycolysis needs. This buys a fast trickle of 2 ATP with no oxygen — useful for a sprinting muscle — but it is wildly inefficient and the lactic acid builds up, which is part of why hard exercise burns.

No oxygen, no chain. This is why suffocation, drowning, and a blocked coronary artery are so quickly fatal — without O₂ to accept electrons, oxidative phosphorylation stops and ATP output collapses to the tiny anaerobic trickle. The poison cyanide kills the same way, by jamming the electron transport chain even when oxygen is plentiful.

Step through all four stages and watch the ATP add up in the interactive.

Interactive How one glucose becomes up to ~32 ATP — four stages, two locations. Step through and watch the total climb. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Cellular respiration = controlled glucose catabolism yielding up to ~30–32 ATP.
  • Four stages: glycolysis (cytoplasm) → pyruvate oxidation → citric acid cycle (matrix) → oxidative phosphorylation (inner membrane).
  • Oxidative phosphorylation makes most of the ATP, and O₂ is the final electron acceptor, forming water.
  • Without oxygen, cells use anaerobic fermentation — only 2 ATP per glucose, with lactic acid as the byproduct.

Section 3Metabolic states: fed vs fasting

Two modes: store or spend

Your fuel supply is not constant — it arrives in bursts at mealtimes and then dwindles for hours afterward. To cope, the body runs in two distinct metabolic states. The absorptive (fed) state is the few hours after a meal, when nutrients are pouring in from the gut and blood glucose rises. The task now is to store the surplus: extra glucose is packed into glycogen (glycogenesis) and fat (lipogenesis), and amino acids build protein. The postabsorptive (fasting) state takes over once absorption is done — overnight, between meals, or during a fast. Now the task flips to releasing stored fuel to keep blood glucose steady: glycogen is broken back to glucose (glycogenolysis), fat is broken down (lipolysis), and the liver even manufactures brand-new glucose from non-carbohydrate sources like amino acids (gluconeogenesis).

Two hormones run the switch

The state you are in is set mainly by two opposing pancreatic hormones. Insulin is the storage hormone; it rises after a meal, drives glucose into cells, and turns on all the building pathways. Glucagon is the mobilizing hormone; it rises during fasting and turns on the releasing pathways. Think of them as a seesaw: insulin high means store, glucagon high means spend. One organ is the hub for all of it — the liver, which stores glycogen after meals and pumps out glucose between them, keeping blood sugar within its narrow homeostatic range around the clock.

Protecting the brain

Why go to all this trouble? Because the brain runs almost exclusively on glucose and cannot store its own fuel, so blood glucose must never crash. During a long fast the body practices glucose sparing: most tissues switch to burning fatty acids, leaving the scarce glucose for the brain. In prolonged starvation the liver converts fat into ketone bodies, an alternative fuel the brain can partly use — a clever adaptation, though a heavy buildup of these acidic ketones can dangerously lower blood pH.

FeatureAbsorptive (fed)Postabsorptive (fasting)
WhenHours after a mealBetween meals, overnight, fasting
GoalStore surplus fuelRelease stored fuel
Dominant hormoneInsulinGlucagon
Key processesGlycogenesis, lipogenesis, protein synthesisGlycogenolysis, lipolysis, gluconeogenesis, ketogenesis
Blood glucose trendRising → storedFalling → defended
Insulin stores, glucagon spends. This single pair explains diabetes: without working insulin, cells cannot take up glucose after a meal, so blood sugar climbs while the cells starve — the body behaves as if it were fasting even while overflowing with fuel, which is why untreated diabetics can slip into dangerous ketosis.

Flip between the fed and fasting states in the interactive below.

Interactive Your body switches modes depending on whether you have just eaten or are between meals — storing fuel or releasing it. Open full ↑
Key points
  • The absorptive state stores surplus fuel (glycogenesis, lipogenesis); the postabsorptive state releases it (glycogenolysis, gluconeogenesis, lipolysis).
  • Insulin drives storage; glucagon drives mobilization — a seesaw centered on the liver.
  • The brain depends on glucose, so fasting metabolism defends blood sugar via glucose sparing and, if prolonged, ketone bodies.

Section 4The six nutrient classes

Three that fuel you, three that don't

Everything worth eating falls into six classes of nutrients. Three of them supply usable energy and are eaten in large amounts — the macronutrients: carbohydrates, lipids (fats), and proteins. The other three yield no energy at all but are absolutely required in smaller amounts — vitamins, minerals, and water. "No energy" does not mean "optional": you would die without water or without trace minerals long before you starved. Each class has a job. Carbohydrates are the body's preferred quick fuel. Lipids are the densest energy store and build every cell membrane. Proteins are mainly structural and functional — enzymes, muscle, antibodies — and are burned for energy only as a last resort.

How much energy is in food?

The energy in food is measured in kilocalories (written "Calories" with a capital C on food labels). Only the three macronutrients carry calories, and they are not equal. Carbohydrate and protein each release about 4 kcal per gram; fat packs more than twice that at about 9 kcal per gram, which is exactly why fatty foods are so calorie-dense and why fat is the body's chosen long-term energy store. Alcohol, though not a nutrient, also carries energy at about 7 kcal per gram.

Essential nutrients: the ones you must eat

An essential nutrient is one your body cannot make (or cannot make in sufficient amounts), so it must come from the diet. These include roughly nine essential amino acids, a couple of essential fatty acids, and all the vitamins and minerals. This is why protein quality matters: a complete protein (from meat, eggs, dairy, soy) supplies all the essential amino acids, while most single plant foods are incomplete — but combining complementary plants, such as beans with rice, covers the full set.

Nutrient classYields energy?Main roleEnergy value
CarbohydratesYesPreferred quick fuel4 kcal/g
ProteinsYesStructure, enzymes, defense; last-resort fuel4 kcal/g
Lipids (fats)YesDense energy store; membranes; hormones9 kcal/g
VitaminsNoCoenzymes and regulators—
MineralsNoIons, bone, cofactors (Na⁺, Ca²⁺, Fe)—
WaterNoSolvent, transport, temperature control—
Fat is 9, the rest are 4. Memorize just those two numbers and you can estimate the calories in almost any food. The gap explains a lot: one gram of fat holds more than double the energy of a gram of sugar or protein, so trimming fat is the fastest way to cut calories — and storing fat is the most compact way for the body to bank them.

Tap each nutrient class to see its role in the interactive.

Interactive A healthy diet supplies six kinds of nutrients — three give energy, three don't but are still essential. Tap each to see its role. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Six nutrient classes: carbohydrates, lipids, proteins (energy-yielding) and vitamins, minerals, water (no energy but essential).
  • Caloric values: carbohydrate and protein 4 kcal/g, fat 9 kcal/g.
  • Essential nutrients (some amino acids and fatty acids, all vitamins and minerals) must be supplied by the diet.
  • Complete proteins provide all essential amino acids; complementary plant foods can be combined to do the same.

Section 5Vitamins

Tiny amounts, huge consequences

Vitamins are organic molecules the body needs in very small amounts to run its metabolism, mostly by acting as coenzymes — helper molecules that enzymes need in order to work. You need mere milligrams or micrograms a day, yet a shortfall of even one can cause a specific, sometimes deadly, deficiency disease. What makes a vitamin behave the way it does is a single physical property: how it dissolves. That splits all vitamins into two camps — fat-soluble and water-soluble — and that one fact predicts how each is absorbed, whether it is stored, and whether it can build up to toxic levels.

Fat-soluble: stored, and able to overdose

The fat-soluble vitamins are A, D, E, and K — remember them as the letters that "ADEK" up. Because they dissolve in fat, they are absorbed alongside dietary fat and then stored in the liver and fatty tissue. That storage is a double-edged sword: you can go a while without them because you have a reserve, but because the body cannot easily flush the excess, megadoses of A or D can accumulate to toxic levels. Their jobs are varied — A for vision, D for calcium balance and bone, E as an antioxidant, K for blood clotting.

Water-soluble: not stored, needed steadily

The water-soluble vitamins are the B-complex and vitamin C. Because they dissolve in water, the body does not stockpile them — any surplus is simply filtered out by the kidneys and lost in urine (which is why very high doses mostly make expensive urine). The flip side is that you need a steady daily supply, and deficiencies appear relatively quickly. Most B vitamins serve as coenzymes in energy metabolism — the very pathways from Sections 1 and 2. The classic deficiency diseases are worth knowing: scurvy from lack of vitamin C, rickets from vitamin D, beriberi from B₁, pellagra from B₃, and pernicious anemia from B₁₂.

PropertyFat-soluble (A, D, E, K)Water-soluble (B-complex, C)
Absorbed withDietary fatWater
Stored?Yes — liver and fatNo (B₁₂ is the exception)
ExcessAccumulates → toxicity riskExcreted in urine
Supply neededLess frequent (reserves exist)Regular, near-daily
Deficiency exampleRickets (D), night blindness (A)Scurvy (C), beriberi (B₁)
"ADEK" are stored; the rest wash out. This one distinction does a lot of clinical work. Fat-soluble vitamins can be taken less often but can be overdosed, so caution with high-dose A and D supplements. Water-soluble vitamins are hard to overdose but must be replenished constantly — miss vitamin C for a couple of months and scurvy sets in.

Switch between the two vitamin groups and tap each one in the interactive.

Interactive Vitamins split into two camps by how they dissolve — which decides whether your body stores them or needs a steady supply. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Vitamins are needed in tiny amounts and mostly act as coenzymes; solubility governs their behavior.
  • Fat-soluble (A, D, E, K) are absorbed with fat, stored, and can reach toxic levels.
  • Water-soluble (B-complex, C) are not stored, excess is excreted, and they need a steady daily supply.
  • Each deficiency has a signature disease — scurvy (C), rickets (D), beriberi (B₁), pellagra (B₃), pernicious anemia (B₁₂).

Section 6Energy balance

A ledger, not a mystery

Body weight, at its foundation, follows a simple accounting rule called energy balance: the calories you take in as food versus the calories you spend out. When intake equals expenditure, weight holds steady (neutral balance). When intake exceeds expenditure, the surplus is stored, mostly as fat, and weight rises (positive balance). When expenditure exceeds intake, the body draws on its stores and weight falls (negative balance). The rule is straightforward; the reason weight is hard to control is that both sides of the ledger are governed by biology, not willpower alone.

Where the calories go

"Energy out" is not just exercise. Total energy expenditure has three parts. By far the largest is the basal metabolic rate (BMR) — the energy your body burns just staying alive at rest, keeping the heart beating, the kidneys filtering, and body temperature up. BMR accounts for roughly 60 to 75% of what you burn in a day, which surprises most people. A second slice, about 10%, is the thermic effect of food — the energy spent digesting and absorbing a meal. The remaining, most variable part is physical activity, from fidgeting to running a marathon. Because BMR dominates, factors that change it — muscle mass, age, thyroid hormone, body size — strongly influence how easily a person gains or loses weight.

The body defends its weight

Energy balance is not left to chance; it is regulated by the same homeostatic logic as everything else, with the hypothalamus as the control center reading hormonal signals about the body's fuel stores. Leptin, released by fat tissue, signals that stores are full and dampens appetite; ghrelin, from the empty stomach, signals hunger and stimulates eating. As a useful rule of thumb, a pound of body fat stores roughly 3,500 kcal — so a sustained deficit of about 500 kcal a day trims close to a pound a week, at least until the body adjusts its BMR downward to defend against the loss.

BalanceIntake vs outputResult
NeutralIn = outWeight stable
PositiveIn > outSurplus stored as fat → weight gain
NegativeIn < outStores drawn down → weight loss
BMR is the biggest spender. Most of your daily calorie burn happens while you do nothing at all — keeping you warm and running your organs. That is why crash dieting backfires: cut intake too hard and the body lowers its BMR to protect its stores, making the last pounds stubborn and rapid regain likely.

Tip the balance one way or the other and see what happens in the interactive.

Interactive Body weight comes down to a simple ledger: energy in (food) versus energy out (everything you burn). Tip the balance and see what happens. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Energy balance compares calories in (food) with calories out; positive balance stores fat, negative balance draws it down.
  • Total expenditure = BMR (~60–75%, the largest part) + thermic effect of food (~10%) + physical activity.
  • Appetite and stores are regulated by the hypothalamus using signals like leptin (full) and ghrelin (hungry).
  • Roughly 3,500 kcal equals a pound of fat, but the body defends its weight by adjusting BMR.

The chapter in one breath

  • Metabolism is every reaction in the body; anabolism builds and uses energy, catabolism breaks down and releases it, with ATP as the shared currency.
  • Cellular respiration catabolizes glucose in four stages — glycolysis, pyruvate oxidation, the citric acid cycle, and oxidative phosphorylation — yielding up to ~32 ATP.
  • Oxygen is the final electron acceptor; without it cells fall back on anaerobic fermentation for a mere 2 ATP plus lactic acid.
  • The body alternates between an absorptive (store, insulin) and a postabsorptive (release, glucagon) state, with the liver keeping blood glucose steady for the brain.
  • Diet supplies six nutrient classes: energy-yielding carbohydrates, lipids, and proteins (4, 9, and 4 kcal/g) plus non-energy vitamins, minerals, and water.
  • Vitamins split by solubility: fat-soluble A, D, E, K are stored and can be toxic; water-soluble B and C are excreted and needed daily.
  • Energy balance weighs calories in against calories out — BMR, the thermic effect of food, and activity — and the body actively defends its weight.

ReferenceWords worth knowing

A quick reference for the terms in this chapter — skim it now, or circle back whenever a word trips you up.

Absorptive (fed) state
The hours after a meal when nutrients are being absorbed, blood glucose rises, and the body stores surplus fuel under the direction of insulin.
Anabolism
The building side of metabolism — small molecules are joined into larger ones, requiring energy (endergonic).
ATP (adenosine triphosphate)
The cell's universal energy currency; energy is released when its terminal phosphate is removed to form ADP, and stored again when the phosphate is reattached.
Basal metabolic rate (BMR)
The energy the body burns at complete rest just to stay alive; the largest component of daily energy expenditure.
Catabolism
The breaking-down side of metabolism — large molecules are split into smaller ones, releasing energy (exergonic).
Cellular respiration
The controlled, stepwise catabolism of glucose that captures its energy in ATP.
Citric acid cycle (Krebs cycle)
The mitochondrial pathway that oxidizes acetyl CoA, releasing CO₂ and loading electron carriers (NADH, FADH₂) for the electron transport chain.
Electron transport chain
A series of carriers in the inner mitochondrial membrane that passes electrons to build an H⁺ gradient, powering most ATP production.
Energy balance
The relationship between calories taken in as food and calories expended; determines whether body weight rises, falls, or holds steady.
Essential nutrient
A nutrient the body cannot make in adequate amounts and therefore must obtain from the diet (certain amino acids and fatty acids, all vitamins and minerals).
Fat-soluble vitamin
A vitamin (A, D, E, or K) absorbed with dietary fat and stored in the body, carrying a risk of toxicity in excess.
Gluconeogenesis
The liver's manufacture of new glucose from non-carbohydrate sources such as amino acids, used during fasting.
Glycogenolysis
The breakdown of stored glycogen back into glucose to raise blood sugar between meals.
Glycolysis
The first stage of respiration, in the cytoplasm, splitting glucose into two pyruvate and netting 2 ATP without oxygen.
Ketone bodies
Acidic fuel molecules made by the liver from fat during prolonged fasting; usable by the brain but able to lower blood pH.
Kilocalorie (Calorie)
The unit of food energy; carbohydrate and protein yield ~4 kcal/g, fat ~9 kcal/g.
Metabolism
The sum of all chemical reactions in the body, comprising anabolism and catabolism.
Oxidative phosphorylation
The final stage of respiration on the inner mitochondrial membrane, using the electron transport chain and oxygen to generate the bulk of ATP.
Postabsorptive (fasting) state
The period between meals when stored fuel is released to maintain blood glucose, directed by glucagon.
Vitamin
An organic molecule required in tiny amounts, usually acting as a coenzyme in metabolism.
Water-soluble vitamin
A vitamin (the B-complex or C) that dissolves in water, is not stored, is excreted in excess, and must be supplied regularly.

Check yourself

1. A cell links glycogen breakdown to muscle contraction so the energy is used rather than lost as heat. What is this arrangement called, and which molecule carries the energy between the two events?
Energy coupling, carried by ATP. The exergonic reaction (breaking down glycogen) recharges ADP into ATP, and ATP then discharges to power the endergonic work of contraction — the energy is handed off, not wasted.
2. Classify each as anabolic or catabolic: (a) amino acids joined into a protein; (b) glycogen split into glucose. Which one releases energy?
(a) Anabolic; (b) catabolic, and (b) releases energy. Anabolism builds and costs energy (endergonic); catabolism breaks down and releases energy (exergonic). "Anabolic" builds, like anabolic steroids build muscle.
3. Put the four stages of cellular respiration in order and name where each occurs.
Glycolysis (cytoplasm) → pyruvate oxidation (mitochondrial matrix) → citric acid cycle (matrix) → oxidative phosphorylation (inner mitochondrial membrane). The first stage is in the cytoplasm and needs no oxygen; the last three are inside the mitochondrion and require it.
4. Which stage of respiration produces most of the ATP, and what molecule serves as the final electron acceptor there?
Oxidative phosphorylation; oxygen (O₂) is the final electron acceptor. Electrons from NADH and FADH₂ pass down the chain and are handed to O₂, forming water; this step makes roughly 26–28 of the ~32 ATP.
5. A sprinter's muscles run short of oxygen. What pathway keeps a trickle of ATP coming, how many ATP per glucose does it yield, and what byproduct accumulates?
Anaerobic fermentation; net 2 ATP per glucose; lactic acid. Converting pyruvate to lactic acid regenerates the NAD⁺ that glycolysis needs, keeping it running — fast but inefficient, and the lactic acid contributes to muscle burning.
6. Which metabolic state dominates two hours after a large lunch, which hormone drives it, and what is the body doing with the incoming fuel?
The absorptive (fed) state, driven by insulin, storing surplus fuel. Insulin pushes glucose into cells and turns on glycogenesis and lipogenesis, banking the surplus as glycogen and fat.
7. Overnight, blood glucose is defended without any food coming in. Name the hormone in charge and two processes it turns on to keep blood sugar up.
Glucagon; glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis. Glucagon triggers the liver to break stored glycogen into glucose and to manufacture new glucose from amino acids, keeping the glucose-dependent brain supplied.
8. List the six nutrient classes and state which three yield energy.
Carbohydrates, lipids, proteins, vitamins, minerals, water; the first three (carbohydrates, lipids, proteins) yield energy. Vitamins, minerals, and water supply no calories but are still essential to life.
9. A snack has 20 g carbohydrate, 10 g fat, and 5 g protein. Roughly how many kilocalories is that?
About 190 kcal. Carbohydrate and protein are 4 kcal/g and fat is 9 kcal/g: (20 × 4) + (10 × 9) + (5 × 4) = 80 + 90 + 20 = 190 kcal.
10. Why can fat-soluble vitamins reach toxic levels while water-soluble vitamins rarely do? Name the members of each group.
Fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) are stored in liver and fat, so excess accumulates; water-soluble vitamins (B-complex and C) are excreted in urine, so excess washes out. The stored group needs less frequent intake but must be dosed cautiously; the excreted group needs steady daily replenishment.
11. Match each deficiency to its missing vitamin: scurvy, rickets, beriberi.
Scurvy → vitamin C; rickets → vitamin D; beriberi → vitamin B₁ (thiamine). Each vitamin has a signature deficiency disease, which is why a varied diet matters — a shortfall of even one micronutrient produces a specific illness.
12. Name the three components of total energy expenditure and state which is usually the largest.
Basal metabolic rate (BMR), the thermic effect of food, and physical activity; BMR is usually the largest (~60–75%). Most of your calories are burned at rest keeping organs running, which is why BMR — not exercise — dominates the daily total.
Anatomy & Physiology · Chapter 25

The Urinary System

Every minute, about a fifth of the blood your heart pumps takes a detour through two fist-sized organs tucked against your back — and comes out cleaner, better balanced, and more precisely tuned than it went in. This chapter follows that journey. You will meet the four organs of the urinary system, cut open a kidney to see how it is built, shrink down to the microscopic nephron where urine is actually made, and watch the three steps — filter, reclaim, and fine-tune — that turn 180 liters of filtered plasma a day into barely a liter and a half of urine. Along the way you will see how a handful of hormones let the kidney set your blood volume and blood pressure, and how a simple spinal reflex — one you learned to override as a toddler — empties the bladder. If homeostasis is the theme of the whole course, the kidney is its master craftsman.

By the end of this chapter you can

  • List the four organs of the urinary system and state the job of each, and describe the many homeostatic roles the kidneys play beyond making urine.
  • Identify the major gross structures of the kidney — cortex, medulla, pyramids, calyces, and renal pelvis — and trace the path urine takes to leave.
  • Describe the nephron as the functional unit of the kidney and name its segments in order from renal corpuscle to collecting duct.
  • Explain the filtration membrane and how the renal corpuscle produces a protein-free filtrate.
  • Distinguish the three processes of urine formation — filtration, reabsorption, and secretion — and say where each happens.
  • Explain how ADH, aldosterone, the renin–angiotensin–aldosterone system, and ANP adjust water and salt to control blood volume and pressure.
  • Describe the micturition reflex and how voluntary control of the external sphincter lets you decide when to void.

Section 1What the urinary system does

Not just a waste line

It is tempting to file the urinary system under "waste disposal" and move on — but that badly undersells it. Yes, the kidneys clear nitrogen wastes like urea (from protein breakdown) and creatinine (from muscle). But their deeper job is homeostasis: minute by minute, they decide exactly how much water, sodium, potassium, calcium, and acid to keep and how much to let go, holding the blood's composition inside the narrow ranges cells need to survive. The kidney is where the abstract idea of a steady internal environment becomes a real, physical machine. When you drink a large glass of water, the kidney sheds the excess within the hour; when you are dehydrated, it clamps down and concentrates the urine to save every drop. It also does jobs that have nothing to do with urine at all — it helps set blood pressure, tells the bone marrow to make red blood cells, and activates vitamin D.

The four organs, in order

The plumbing is refreshingly simple. Two kidneys make urine. Two ureters carry it downhill. One urinary bladder stores it. One urethra carries it out. Everything upstream of the ureters is about chemistry — adjusting the blood — and everything from the ureters down is about transport and storage. The kidneys sit high on the back wall of the abdomen, one on each side of the spine, retroperitoneal (behind the lining of the abdominal cavity rather than inside it). The right kidney rides a little lower than the left because the large liver sits above it.

OrganNumberMain job
Kidneys2Filter blood and form urine; balance water, salts, and pH; help set blood pressure and red-cell count
Ureters2Carry urine from kidney to bladder by peristalsis (muscular waves)
Urinary bladder1Stores urine until it is convenient to release it
Urethra1Carries urine from bladder to the outside of the body

A river of blood, a trickle of urine

The scale of the kidney's work is startling. Together the kidneys receive roughly 20–25% of the entire cardiac output — more blood per gram than almost any other organ — and from it they filter about 180 liters of fluid every day. If that all left as urine you would be in serious trouble, so the kidney reclaims more than 99% of it, and only about 1–2 liters actually leaves as urine. That enormous filter-then-reclaim strategy is the secret to the kidney's precision: by pushing a huge volume out and then choosing exactly what to take back, it can regulate dozens of substances independently.

Follow the blood, not the urine. Because the kidneys are downstream of one-fifth of every heartbeat, a change in blood pressure, blood volume, or a drug in the bloodstream reaches them fast — which is why kidneys are both powerful regulators of the body and vulnerable to whatever the blood carries, from high glucose to toxins to medications.

Tap each of the four organs in the interactive to see where it sits and what it does.

Interactive Filter, balance, and drain — tap each of the four organs of the urinary system. Open full ↑
Key points
  • The urinary system has four organs: two kidneys (make urine), two ureters (transport), one bladder (store), one urethra (void).
  • The kidneys do far more than clear waste — they regulate water, electrolytes, and pH, and help control blood pressure, red-cell production, and vitamin D.
  • The kidneys are retroperitoneal and receive ~20–25% of cardiac output, filtering ~180 L/day but excreting only 1–2 L of urine.

Section 2Inside the kidney

The layers from outside in

Slice a kidney down the middle like a bean and it reveals a beautifully organized set of layers. A tough fibrous renal capsule wraps the surface. Just beneath it is the renal cortex, the granular outer working layer where filtration begins. Deeper still is the renal medulla, arranged into 8–18 cone-shaped renal pyramids. The pyramids point inward, and the tip of each cone — the renal papilla — drips finished urine into the collecting spaces. Stripes of cortex called renal columns dip down between the pyramids, carrying blood vessels. The whole apparatus curves around a notch on the medial border, the hilum, where the renal artery enters, and the renal vein and ureter leave.

The drainage route

Once urine drips off a papilla it never touches kidney tissue again — it is simply collected and drained. The route is worth memorizing because it names the structures a kidney stone travels through: urine leaves the papilla into a cup-shaped minor calyx, several of which merge into a major calyx, and the major calyces empty into the funnel-shaped renal pelvis. The renal pelvis narrows at the hilum to become the ureter. From papilla to bladder, then, the path is: papilla → minor calyx → major calyx → renal pelvis → ureter.

StructureLocationWhat happens there
Renal capsuleOuter surfaceFibrous coat that protects the kidney
Renal cortexOuter working layerHolds the renal corpuscles; filtration begins here
Renal medulla (pyramids)Deep to the cortexHolds the loops and collecting ducts that concentrate urine
Renal papillaTip of each pyramidDrips finished urine into a minor calyx
Calyces & renal pelvisCentral collecting spaceFunnel urine toward the ureter (no further processing)
HilumMedial notchEntry/exit for renal artery, renal vein, and ureter

Two capillary beds in a row

The kidney's blood supply has one unusual feature that makes everything else work. Most organs have a single capillary bed; the kidney has two capillary beds in series. Blood arrives through the renal artery, branches down to a tiny afferent arteriole, and enters the first bed — a high-pressure knot of capillaries called the glomerulus, where filtration happens. Instead of draining into a vein, blood leaves the glomerulus through an efferent arteriole and enters a second bed, the peritubular capillaries that wrap around the tubules. This two-bed design lets the kidney filter under high pressure in the first bed and then reabsorb under low pressure in the second — two opposite jobs, done in sequence.

Cortex filters, medulla concentrates. Nearly all the filtering happens in the cortex, while the medulla's pyramids house the long loops and collecting ducts that pull water back and concentrate the urine. If you remember which layer does which job, the rest of nephron function falls into place.

Explore each zone by slicing the kidney in the interactive below.

Interactive Slice a kidney in half and tap each zone — from cortex to renal pelvis. Open full ↑
Key points
  • From outside in: capsule → cortex → medulla (pyramids), with cortex-filled renal columns between the pyramids.
  • Urine drains: papilla → minor calyx → major calyx → renal pelvis → ureter, with no further processing after the papilla.
  • The kidney has two capillary beds in series — the high-pressure glomerulus (filtration) and the low-pressure peritubular capillaries (reabsorption).

Section 3The nephron

A million tiny factories

The kidney does not filter as one big sieve. Its work is divided among roughly one million microscopic units per kidney called nephrons, and each is a complete little urine factory. A nephron has two parts: the renal corpuscle, where filtrate is made, and a long, winding renal tubule, where that filtrate is processed into urine. Understanding one nephron means understanding the whole kidney — the organ is just this same unit repeated a million times over.

The renal corpuscle and the filtration membrane

The renal corpuscle is where blood becomes filtrate. It has two components: the glomerulus (that high-pressure ball of capillaries) sitting inside a cup called the glomerular capsule (Bowman's capsule). Fluid squeezes out of the glomerular blood, across a three-layered filtration membrane, into the capsule. The membrane is exquisitely selective. Its first layer is the fenestrated ("windowed") capillary wall, riddled with pores. Its middle layer is a basement membrane whose negative charge repels proteins. Its third layer is made of octopus-like cells called podocytes, whose interlocking foot processes leave narrow filtration slits. Together these layers let water and small solutes through freely while holding back blood cells and plasma proteins — so normal filtrate is essentially protein-free plasma. Finding protein or blood in the urine is a red flag that this membrane is damaged.

Following the tubule

From the capsule, filtrate flows through the tubule's named segments in a fixed order, and each segment has its own personality: the proximal convoluted tubule (PCT), the hairpin loop of Henle (a descending limb and an ascending limb), the distal convoluted tubule (DCT), and finally the collecting duct, which gathers filtrate from many nephrons and carries it toward the papilla. Some nephrons sit almost entirely in the cortex (cortical nephrons); a smaller group, the juxtamedullary nephrons, have long loops that plunge deep into the medulla and are what make concentrated urine possible.

SegmentLocationMain role
Renal corpuscleCortexFiltration — makes protein-free filtrate
Proximal convoluted tubule (PCT)CortexBulk reabsorption of water, Na⁺, glucose, amino acids
Loop of HenleMedullaSets up the salty gradient that lets the kidney concentrate urine
Distal convoluted tubule (DCT)CortexFine-tuning under hormone control (Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺)
Collecting ductCortex & medullaFinal water reabsorption (ADH-controlled); drains to papilla
The juxtaglomerular apparatus is the kidney's control panel. Where each nephron's ascending limb brushes past its own afferent arteriole, specialized cells form the juxtaglomerular apparatus: the macula densa senses the filtrate's salt content, and the granular cells release renin when blood pressure falls. This tiny junction is where the kidney takes charge of the body's blood pressure.

Trace the filtrate through every twist of the nephron in the interactive.

Interactive Follow filtrate from the glomerulus through the loop to the collecting duct — tap each part. Open full ↑
Key points
  • The nephron is the functional unit of the kidney (~1 million per kidney), made of a renal corpuscle plus a renal tubule.
  • The filtration membrane — fenestrated capillary, basement membrane, podocyte slits — passes water and small solutes but holds back cells and proteins.
  • Tubule order: corpuscle → PCT → loop of Henle → DCT → collecting duct; long-looped juxtamedullary nephrons concentrate the urine.

Section 4How urine is made

Three steps: dump, reclaim, fine-tune

The kidney makes urine using a strategy that seems wasteful but is actually genius: dump almost everything out, then take back what you want. This happens in three steps, in order. First, glomerular filtration pushes a large volume of fluid out of the blood into the tubule. Second, tubular reabsorption reclaims the water and useful solutes, returning them to the blood. Third, tubular secretion adds a few extra wastes and ions from the blood into the tubule for disposal. Whatever remains in the tubule after all three steps is the urine. The genius is control: by filtering a huge amount and then reabsorbing selectively, the kidney can adjust each substance independently.

Step one: glomerular filtration

Filtration is driven by pure pressure. Blood enters the glomerulus under high hydrostatic pressure (thanks to that wide afferent arteriole feeding a narrow efferent one), and that pressure forces fluid across the filtration membrane into the capsule. The rate at which all the glomeruli together produce filtrate is the glomerular filtration rate (GFR) — normally about 125 mL per minute, which adds up to roughly 180 L a day. GFR is a vital sign for the kidney; a falling GFR is the central measure of kidney failure. The kidney guards its GFR fiercely, adjusting the diameter of the afferent and efferent arterioles to keep filtration steady even as blood pressure swings.

Steps two and three: reabsorption and secretion

If filtration were the whole story you would urinate 180 liters a day and die of dehydration in an hour. Reabsorption saves you. The PCT alone reclaims about 65% of the filtered water and sodium, and essentially 100% of the glucose and amino acids — nutrients are too valuable to waste. The loop of Henle builds a salty medulla so that, later, water can be pulled back osmotically. By the time filtrate reaches the collecting duct, only fine adjustments remain. Meanwhile secretion runs the opposite direction, moving substances from the blood into the tubule — hydrogen ions (H⁺) to control pH, excess potassium (K⁺), and many drugs and toxins. Secretion is the kidney's way of clearing things too big or too slow to filter well.

ProcessDirectionWhereWhat moves
FiltrationBlood → tubuleRenal corpuscleWater & small solutes (pressure-driven, ~125 mL/min)
ReabsorptionTubule → bloodWhole tubule (mostly PCT)Water, Na⁺, glucose, amino acids — the useful stuff
SecretionBlood → tubulePCT, DCT, collecting ductH⁺, K⁺, drugs, toxins — extra waste
Glucose in the urine is a clue, not normal. The tubule's glucose carriers have a maximum rate (a "transport maximum"). Below it, all glucose is reabsorbed and urine is glucose-free. In uncontrolled diabetes, blood glucose overwhelms the carriers, glucose spills into the urine, and it drags water with it — which is why excessive thirst and urination are classic early signs.

Step through filtration, reabsorption, and secretion in the interactive below.

Interactive Dump a lot, take back what's useful, add a little waste — step through the three stages. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Urine formation has three steps: filtration (blood→tubule), reabsorption (tubule→blood), and secretion (blood→tubule).
