  • GFR is about 125 mL/min and is the key measure of kidney function; the kidney regulates arteriole diameter to keep it steady.
  • The PCT reabsorbs the bulk (~65% of water and Na⁺, ~all glucose); secretion clears H⁺, K⁺, and drugs.

Section 5Hormonal control

Water: antidiuretic hormone

The kidney does not decide alone how much water to keep — the body signals it with hormones, and the most important water hormone is antidiuretic hormone (ADH), also called vasopressin. When you are dehydrated and blood becomes too concentrated, the hypothalamus senses it and the posterior pituitary releases ADH. ADH acts on the collecting duct, inserting water channels called aquaporins into its wall so that water is pulled back into the salty medulla. The result is a small volume of concentrated urine and conserved body water. When you are well hydrated, ADH falls, the channels are removed, water stays in the tubule, and you produce a large volume of dilute urine. Alcohol suppresses ADH — which is why a night of drinking leaves you dehydrated.

Salt and pressure: the renin–angiotensin–aldosterone system

To control blood pressure, the kidney manages sodium — because where sodium goes, water follows, and water is volume, and volume is pressure. The master circuit is the renin–angiotensin–aldosterone system (RAAS). When blood pressure drops, the kidney's granular cells release renin. Renin converts a liver protein into angiotensin I, which an enzyme in the lungs (ACE) converts to angiotensin II. Angiotensin II is a powerful actor: it constricts blood vessels directly (raising pressure at once) and it stimulates the adrenal gland to release aldosterone, which tells the DCT and collecting duct to reabsorb more sodium (and water with it) while dumping potassium. More sodium reabsorbed means more water retained, more blood volume, and higher pressure — a slower but sustained boost.

Letting go: atrial natriuretic peptide

Every regulator needs an opposite. When blood volume runs high, the stretched walls of the heart's atria release atrial natriuretic peptide (ANP), which does the reverse of RAAS: it promotes the loss of sodium and water in the urine, lowering blood volume and pressure. Between ADH tuning water, RAAS raising pressure, and ANP lowering it, the kidney sits at the center of a constant push-and-pull that keeps your blood volume — and therefore your blood pressure — remarkably steady.

HormoneTriggerEffect on the kidneyNet result
ADH (vasopressin)Concentrated / low-volume bloodAdds aquaporins to collecting duct → reabsorb waterLess, more concentrated urine; water saved
AldosteroneAngiotensin II; high K⁺Reabsorb Na⁺ (and water), secrete K⁺↑ blood volume & pressure
Renin / angiotensin IILow blood pressureConstricts vessels; drives aldosterone release↑ blood pressure (fast & slow)
ANPHigh blood volume (atrial stretch)Promotes Na⁺ and water excretion↓ blood volume & pressure
Why blood-pressure pills target the kidney. Many common blood-pressure medicines work on this exact system: ACE inhibitors block the conversion to angiotensin II, and diuretics ("water pills") make the kidney excrete more sodium and water. Both lower blood pressure by reducing the volume and vessel tone the kidney otherwise defends.

Tap each hormone in the interactive to see how it shifts water and salt.

Interactive How hormones tell the kidney how much water and salt to keep — tap each hormone. Open full ↑
Key points
  • ADH controls water: it inserts aquaporins in the collecting duct to concentrate urine and conserve water.
  • The RAAS controls sodium and pressure: low pressure → renin → angiotensin II → aldosterone → Na⁺ and water reabsorption → higher blood volume and pressure.
  • ANP opposes RAAS, shedding Na⁺ and water when blood volume is high; together these hormones stabilize blood pressure.

Section 6Storing and voiding urine

The storage plumbing

Once urine leaves the kidney it is finished — the rest is transport and storage. The ureters squeeze urine downhill to the bladder with waves of smooth-muscle peristalsis, so urine keeps moving even when you lie down. The urinary bladder is a muscular, expandable bag lined by transitional epithelium that can stretch flat as the bladder fills, and thrown into folds called rugae when it is empty. Its muscular wall is the detrusor muscle, and a smooth triangular region on its floor, the trigone, funnels urine toward the exit. Two gates guard the outlet: an involuntary internal urethral sphincter (smooth muscle, works automatically) and a voluntary external urethral sphincter (skeletal muscle, under your conscious control). The urethra then carries urine out — short in females (about 4 cm) and longer in males (about 20 cm, because it also passes through the penis).

The micturition reflex

Emptying the bladder starts as a simple spinal reflex. As the bladder fills to around 200–400 mL, stretch receptors in the detrusor wall fire and send signals to the sacral region of the spinal cord. The cord answers with a parasympathetic command that does two things at once: it contracts the detrusor muscle to squeeze the bladder, and it relaxes the internal sphincter to open the first gate. This is the micturition reflex, and in an infant — whose nervous system has not yet learned control — it simply proceeds to the end, and the baby voids.

The voluntary override

What makes you different from an infant is the second gate. The external urethral sphincter is skeletal muscle you can consciously hold shut, and higher brain centers (a control area in the pons, guided by the cortex) can either permit or postpone urination. When the reflex fires but the moment is wrong, your brain keeps the external sphincter contracted and the urge passes — for a while. When you decide it is time, the brain relaxes the external sphincter, the reflex completes, and the bladder empties. Learning this override is exactly what "potty training" teaches, and losing it — through spinal injury, stroke, or age — is what causes many forms of incontinence.

StepWhat happens
1. FillUrine collects; the detrusor stretches as volume nears 200–400 mL
2. SenseStretch receptors fire and signal the sacral spinal cord
3. ReflexParasympathetic output contracts the detrusor and relaxes the internal sphincter
4. DecideThe brain (pons/cortex) holds or releases the external sphincter — the voluntary gate
5. VoidExternal sphincter relaxes; bladder empties through the urethra
The short female urethra invites infection. Because a woman's urethra is only about 4 cm long and opens close to the anus, bacteria reach the bladder more easily — which is why urinary tract infections are far more common in women. The same short distance is why a UTI can climb to the kidneys if it is not treated.

Fill the bladder and release it in the interactive to watch the reflex play out.

Interactive Fill the bladder until stretch receptors fire, then override or void — watch the reflex. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Ureters move urine by peristalsis; the bladder (detrusor muscle, transitional epithelium, trigone) stores it behind two sphincters.
  • The micturition reflex is a sacral spinal reflex: stretch → parasympathetic → detrusor contracts and the internal sphincter opens.
  • The external sphincter is skeletal muscle under conscious (pons/cortex) control — the voluntary override that lets you choose when to void.

The chapter in one breath

  • The urinary system's four organs — two kidneys, two ureters, one bladder, one urethra — filter blood, balance water and salts, and drain urine; the kidneys also help set blood pressure, red-cell count, and vitamin D.
  • A kidney is layered cortex over medulla (pyramids); urine drains papilla → calyces → renal pelvis → ureter, and blood passes through two capillary beds in series.
  • The nephron (~1 million per kidney) is the functional unit: a renal corpuscle that filters plus a tubule (PCT → loop of Henle → DCT → collecting duct) that processes.
  • Urine forms in three steps — filtration (blood→tubule), reabsorption (tubule→blood), and secretion (blood→tubule); GFR is about 125 mL/min.
  • ADH conserves water, the RAAS retains sodium to raise blood pressure, and ANP sheds sodium and water to lower it.
  • Urine is stored in the bladder and released by the micturition reflex, with the voluntary external sphincter letting you decide when to void.
  • Above all, the kidney is the body's great homeostatic organ — it turns the idea of a stable internal environment into a working machine.

ReferenceWords worth knowing

A quick reference for the terms in this chapter — skim it now, or circle back whenever a word trips you up.

Aldosterone
An adrenal hormone that tells the distal tubule and collecting duct to reabsorb sodium (and water with it) while secreting potassium, raising blood volume and pressure.
Antidiuretic hormone (ADH)
A posterior-pituitary hormone that inserts aquaporins into the collecting duct so water is reabsorbed, producing concentrated urine and conserving body water.
Calyx
A cup-shaped chamber that collects urine dripping from a renal papilla; minor calyces merge into major calyces, which drain into the renal pelvis.
Collecting duct
The final tubule segment, shared by many nephrons, where ADH controls the last of water reabsorption before urine reaches the papilla.
Detrusor muscle
The smooth muscle of the bladder wall that contracts to expel urine during the micturition reflex.
Glomerular filtration rate (GFR)
The volume of filtrate all the glomeruli produce per minute (normally ~125 mL/min); the central measure of kidney function.
Glomerulus
The high-pressure ball of capillaries inside the renal corpuscle where blood is filtered into the glomerular capsule.
Juxtaglomerular apparatus
The junction where a nephron's ascending limb meets its afferent arteriole; its cells sense salt and pressure and release renin.
Loop of Henle
The hairpin tubule segment (descending and ascending limbs) that builds the salty medullary gradient needed to concentrate urine.
Micturition
Urination — the emptying of the bladder, triggered by a spinal stretch reflex and permitted or delayed by voluntary control.
Nephron
The microscopic functional unit of the kidney (~1 million per kidney), consisting of a renal corpuscle and a renal tubule.
Podocyte
An octopus-shaped cell of the filtration membrane whose foot processes form narrow filtration slits that help hold back proteins.
Proximal convoluted tubule (PCT)
The first tubule segment after the corpuscle; site of bulk reabsorption of water, sodium, glucose, and amino acids.
Reabsorption (tubular)
The movement of water and useful solutes out of the tubule and back into the blood.
Renal corpuscle
The filtering component of the nephron — the glomerulus plus its surrounding glomerular (Bowman's) capsule.
Renin
An enzyme released by the kidney when blood pressure falls; it starts the cascade that produces angiotensin II and aldosterone.
Retroperitoneal
Positioned behind the peritoneum, against the posterior abdominal wall — the location of the kidneys and ureters.
Secretion (tubular)
The movement of wastes and ions (H⁺, K⁺, drugs) out of the blood and into the tubule for disposal.
Trigone
The smooth triangular region on the bladder floor, marked by the two ureter openings and the urethral outlet.
Ureter
The muscular tube that carries urine from a kidney to the bladder by peristalsis.
Urethra
The tube that carries urine from the bladder to the outside; short in females, longer in males.

Check yourself

1. Name the four organs of the urinary system and give the main job of each.
Kidneys (make urine and balance the blood), ureters (transport), bladder (store), urethra (void). Everything upstream of the ureters is about adjusting blood chemistry; everything from the ureters down is transport and storage.
2. Beyond removing waste, list three homeostatic jobs the kidneys perform.
Any three of: regulating water and electrolyte balance, controlling blood pH, setting blood pressure (renin), stimulating red-cell production, and activating vitamin D. The kidney is the body's master homeostatic organ, not merely a filter.
3. Trace the path a drop of urine takes from where it leaves a renal pyramid to the ureter.
Papilla → minor calyx → major calyx → renal pelvis → ureter. After it drips off the papilla, urine is only collected and drained — no further processing occurs in these spaces.
4. The kidney has two capillary beds in series. Name them and say what each does.
The glomerulus (high pressure, filtration) and the peritubular capillaries (low pressure, reabsorption). Blood passes from glomerulus → efferent arteriole → peritubular capillaries, letting the kidney filter and then reabsorb in sequence.
5. What is the functional unit of the kidney, and what are its two main parts?
The nephron; its two parts are the renal corpuscle (filters) and the renal tubule (processes filtrate into urine). There are about a million nephrons per kidney, and each is a complete little urine factory.
6. Name the three layers of the filtration membrane and explain why normal urine has no protein.
Fenestrated capillary endothelium, basement membrane, and podocyte filtration slits. The pores and slits let water and small solutes through but block cells, and the negatively charged basement membrane repels plasma proteins — so protein in the urine signals membrane damage.
7. List the nephron tubule segments in the order filtrate flows through them.
Renal corpuscle → proximal convoluted tubule → loop of Henle → distal convoluted tubule → collecting duct. The PCT does bulk reabsorption, the loop concentrates, the DCT fine-tunes, and the collecting duct handles ADH-controlled water.
8. Name the three processes of urine formation and give the direction (blood-to-tubule or tubule-to-blood) of each.
Filtration (blood → tubule), reabsorption (tubule → blood), secretion (blood → tubule). Filtration dumps a large volume out, reabsorption reclaims the useful water and solutes, and secretion adds extra wastes for disposal.
9. A patient's urine tests positive for glucose. Why is this abnormal, and what condition should you suspect?
Normally 100% of filtered glucose is reabsorbed, so any glucose in urine suggests the reabsorption carriers were overwhelmed — classically in diabetes. When blood glucose exceeds the transport maximum, the excess spills into the urine and pulls water with it, causing thirst and frequent urination.
10. Blood pressure drops. Walk through how the renin–angiotensin–aldosterone system brings it back up.
Low pressure → kidney releases renin → angiotensin I → (ACE in lungs) → angiotensin II → vasoconstriction plus aldosterone release → sodium and water reabsorption. Vasoconstriction raises pressure quickly, and sodium/water retention raises blood volume and pressure over time.
11. Contrast what ADH and ANP do to the amount of water in the urine, and name the trigger for each.
ADH (triggered by concentrated/low-volume blood) reabsorbs water, giving less, more concentrated urine; ANP (triggered by high blood volume/atrial stretch) promotes water and sodium loss, giving more urine. They are opposing regulators that together keep blood volume steady.
12. Describe the micturition reflex, then explain what lets an adult postpone urination when an infant cannot.
Bladder stretch fires receptors that signal the sacral cord, which sends parasympathetic output to contract the detrusor and relax the internal sphincter. Adults can consciously hold the skeletal external urethral sphincter shut (via the pons and cortex) to delay voiding until it is convenient — the voluntary override that toilet training establishes.
Anatomy & Physiology · Chapter 26

Fluid, Electrolyte & Acid-Base Balance

You are, quite literally, mostly water — and the story of staying alive is largely the story of keeping that water, the salts dissolved in it, and its acidity all pinned inside astonishingly narrow ranges. This chapter follows the fluid from the compartments where it lives, through the daily balance of what comes in and what goes out, to the charged electrolytes that ride within it and the delicate pH that every enzyme depends on. You will meet the buffers that catch stray acid in an instant, the lungs and kidneys that finish the job over minutes and days, and the four classic acid-base disorders that appear when the whole system slips. By the end you will be able to read a set of blood numbers and say, plainly, what has gone wrong and how the body is fighting back.

By the end of this chapter you can

  • Name the body's fluid compartments, estimate the fraction of water in each, and describe how their ionic composition differs.
  • Balance a day's water intake against output and identify the routes of each, including insensible loss.
  • Explain how thirst and antidiuretic hormone keep total body water steady.
  • List the major electrolytes, state where each is concentrated, what it does, and which hormone controls it.
  • Write the bicarbonate buffer equation and explain how a buffer resists a change in pH.
  • Rank the body's three lines of acid-base defense by speed and describe the mechanism of each.
  • Classify the four acid-base disorders by cause and direction, and predict the compensation for each.
  • Interpret a simple set of blood-gas values to name an acid-base disturbance.

Section 1The fluid compartments

How much of you is water

In a healthy adult, roughly 60% of body mass is water — call it about 42 liters in a 70-kilogram person. That number is not fixed. It runs higher in infants (near 75%) and lower in older adults, and because fat tissue holds almost no water, a leaner body is a wetter one and women, who carry proportionally more adipose tissue on average, sit a few points lower than men. This total body water is not one puddle. It is parceled into compartments separated by living membranes, and the whole subject of fluid balance is really about how water and solutes move between those compartments.

Two great pools, and a smaller subdivision

The first and largest division is by the cell membrane. About two-thirds of your water sits inside your cells — the intracellular fluid, or ICF. The remaining one-third lies outside the cells — the extracellular fluid, or ECF. The ECF is then split again by the walls of the blood vessels: most of it bathes the tissues directly as interstitial fluid (the water in the gaps between cells), and a smaller share is the plasma traveling inside the vessels. A tiny extra fraction, the transcellular fluid, includes specialized pools like cerebrospinal fluid, joint fluid, and the aqueous humor of the eye.

CompartmentShare of total body waterWhere it is
Intracellular (ICF)~2/3 (about 40% of body weight)Inside all the cells
Extracellular (ECF)~1/3 (about 20% of body weight)Outside the cells — split below
  · Interstitial~3/4 of the ECFIn the spaces between cells
  · Plasma~1/4 of the ECFThe fluid part of blood, inside vessels
TranscellularA small extra poolCSF, joint, eye, GI secretions

Same water, very different salt

What makes the compartments distinct is not the water but the solutes dissolved in it. The ICF and ECF have strikingly different ionic recipes, kept apart by the cell membrane and its pumps. The ECF is a salty sea, dominated by sodium (Na⁺) and chloride (Cl⁻), much like seawater. The ICF, by contrast, is rich in potassium (K⁺), magnesium, phosphate, and negatively charged proteins. The sodium-potassium pump in every cell membrane works around the clock to hold this difference — pushing Na⁺ out and pulling K⁺ in — and that stored gradient is what powers nerve impulses, muscle contraction, and much of the traffic across membranes.

Because water follows solute by osmosis, the amount of dissolved particle in each compartment (its osmolarity) sets where the water goes. Add salt to the ECF and it becomes concentrated, pulling water out of the cells so they shrink; dilute the ECF and water rushes in, swelling the cells. This is why sodium, the most abundant ECF particle, is the single biggest lever on fluid movement in the body.

The 60–40–20 rule. Water is ~60% of body weight; of that, ~40% of weight is intracellular and ~20% extracellular. Fat is essentially waterless, so the leaner the person, the higher the percentage — a useful thing to remember when a very lean athlete and a heavier patient of the same weight can hold very different amounts of water.

Tap each compartment in the interactive below to see where the water lives.

Interactive About 60% of your body is water, split into compartments separated by cell membranes — tap each to see where it lives. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Water is about 60% of body mass; leaner bodies and infants hold more, older adults less.
  • Two-thirds of body water is intracellular; one-third is extracellular, and the ECF splits into interstitial fluid and plasma.
  • ECF is high in Na⁺/Cl⁻; ICF is high in K⁺ and proteins — a difference held by the sodium-potassium pump.
  • Water follows solute by osmosis, so ECF sodium is the main control on where fluid moves.

Section 2Water in, water out

A ledger that must balance

Total body water stays remarkably steady from day to day, and the reason is simple bookkeeping: over any 24 hours, water in must equal water out. A typical adult moves about 2.5 liters across that ledger each day. On the intake side, most comes from what you drink (roughly 1.5 L), a good deal from the water hidden in food (about 0.7 L — fruit, vegetables, and even bread are surprisingly wet), and a small but real amount is manufactured inside you. Every time a cell burns glucose or fat for energy, water is a by-product of that reaction; this metabolic water adds roughly a quarter-liter a day without your drinking a drop.

The four ways out

Water leaves by four routes, and only one of them is something you can adjust at will. The kidneys make urine (about 1.5 L, the single largest and the only closely regulated output). The rest is insensible loss — water you shed without noticing: it evaporates continuously from your skin and leaves as vapor in every breath from your lungs (together close to 0.9 L). A modest amount leaves in the feces, and more pours out as sweat when you are hot or working hard. Fever, heat, exercise, vomiting, and diarrhea can all send these losses soaring, which is why they so quickly threaten balance.

DirectionRouteRough daily amount
InFluids you drink~1.5 L
InWater in food~0.7 L
InMetabolic water (from metabolism)~0.3 L
OutUrine (regulated)~1.5 L
OutSkin & lungs (insensible)~0.9 L
OutFeces~0.1 L

Thirst and the water-saving hormone

Because intake is behavioral and output is largely obligatory, the body regulates both ends. When you lose water, the ECF grows concentrated and its osmolarity rises. Tiny osmoreceptors in the hypothalamus detect this and do two things. First they trigger thirst, driving you to drink and raise intake. Second, they signal the posterior pituitary to release antidiuretic hormone (ADH), also called vasopressin. ADH travels to the kidney and tells the collecting ducts to install water channels (aquaporins) that reabsorb water back into the blood, producing a small volume of concentrated urine. Drink too much, and the reverse happens: osmolarity falls, ADH switches off, and the kidney lets a large volume of dilute urine flow. Between the pull of thirst and the throttle of ADH, total body water is held within about a percent from one day to the next.

The kidney can concentrate, but it cannot make water. Even in severe dehydration the kidney must pass a minimum obligatory urine volume (about half a liter) just to flush out the day's metabolic wastes. That is why fluids can never be shut off completely, and why a person deprived of water still keeps losing it through breath, skin, and this unavoidable urine.

Tap each route in the interactive to see how the body keeps water in and out equal.

Interactive Each day you take in about 2.5 L of water and lose the same amount — tap each route to see how the body keeps in and out equal. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Over a day, intake ≈ output ≈ 2.5 L; intake is drink + food + metabolic water.
  • Output is urine (the only tightly regulated route) plus insensible loss from skin and lungs, feces, and sweat.
  • Thirst raises intake; ADH (vasopressin) lowers output by making the kidney reabsorb water.
  • A minimum obligatory urine volume must always be excreted to clear metabolic waste.

Section 3The electrolytes

Charged particles that do the work

An electrolyte is any substance that breaks apart into charged ions when it dissolves, allowing the fluid to carry an electric current. They are the reason body fluids conduct at all, and they do far more than sit in solution: electrolyte gradients drive every nerve impulse, every muscle contraction, and every shift of water between compartments. Because these jobs are so sensitive, the body regulates electrolyte levels tightly and measures them in milliequivalents per liter (mEq/L), a unit that counts electrical charge rather than plain mass. Positively charged ions are cations (Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺); negatively charged ones are anions (Cl⁻, bicarbonate, phosphate).

The big four cations

Sodium (Na⁺) is the chief cation of the ECF and the master of fluid volume — where sodium goes, water follows. Its level (normally 135–145 mEq/L) is guarded chiefly by aldosterone, an adrenal hormone that tells the kidney to reabsorb sodium. Potassium (K⁺) is the chief cation of the ICF and sets the resting membrane potential of excitable cells; its narrow range (3.5–5.0 mEq/L) matters intensely to the heart, because too much or too little potassium can stop it. Calcium (Ca²⁺) — 99% stored in bone — runs muscle contraction, blood clotting, and nerve signaling, and its blood level is tuned by parathyroid hormone and vitamin D. Magnesium is a quieter partner, a cofactor for hundreds of enzymes and a stabilizer of nerve and muscle.

ElectrolyteMainly inKey jobMain regulator
Sodium (Na⁺)ECFECF volume & osmolarity; nerve/muscle signalsAldosterone, ADH, ANP
Potassium (K⁺)ICFResting membrane potential; heart rhythmAldosterone
Calcium (Ca²⁺)Bone / ECFMuscle contraction, clotting, nervesPTH, calcitriol, calcitonin
Chloride (Cl⁻)ECFFollows sodium; balances charge; stomach acidFollows Na⁺ handling
Bicarbonate (HCO₃⁻)ECFThe body's main pH bufferKidneys
PhosphateICF / boneATP, DNA, bone; bufferPTH

When the numbers drift

Because these ions are electrically active, small deviations cause outsized symptoms. Low sodium (hyponatremia) draws water into brain cells and brings confusion, headache, and seizures; high sodium dehydrates them. Potassium problems are dangerous mainly at the heart — both hyperkalemia and hypokalemia cause arrhythmias that can be fatal. Low calcium raises nerve excitability into tetany (muscle spasms and tingling), while high calcium blunts it into weakness and confusion. Reading electrolytes is a daily act of clinical medicine precisely because these are the numbers that keep hearts beating and neurons firing.

Aldosterone is the salt-retaining hormone: it keeps sodium and dumps potassium. One easy sentence unlocks a lot of physiology — when aldosterone rises, the kidney holds Na⁺ (and water follows, expanding volume and raising blood pressure) while secreting K⁺ into the urine. So conditions that raise aldosterone tend to lower potassium, and drugs that block it tend to raise it.

Tap each electrolyte in the interactive to see where it lives and what it does.

Interactive Electrolytes are charged ions dissolved in body fluids that drive nerve signals, muscle contraction, and fluid balance — tap each. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Electrolytes are ions (cations and anions) measured in mEq/L; they drive nerve, muscle, and fluid balance.
  • Sodium rules ECF volume; potassium sets membrane potential and is critical to the heart; calcium runs contraction, clotting, and nerves.
  • Aldosterone retains sodium and excretes potassium; PTH and vitamin D control calcium.
  • Small shifts cause big trouble — hyponatremia (brain), hyperkalemia (heart), hypocalcemia (tetany).

Section 4Blood pH and buffers

A razor-thin window

pH measures how acidic or basic a solution is on a scale from 0 to 14, where 7 is neutral; lower means more acidic (more free hydrogen ions, H⁺), higher means more basic. Arterial blood is held slightly alkaline, in the tiny band of 7.35 to 7.45. That is not a comfort zone but a survival zone: below 7.35 is acidemia, above 7.45 is alkalemia, and life becomes impossible outside roughly 6.8 to 7.8. The stakes are high because pH bends the shape of proteins — and since enzymes are proteins, a drifting pH slows or halts the chemistry of every cell at once. The trouble is that metabolism is relentlessly acidifying: burning fuel makes carbon dioxide (which becomes acid in water), and hard work or fasting adds lactic acid and ketoacids on top.

What a buffer does

The first defense against this acid tide is a buffer — a chemical pair that soaks up excess H⁺ when acid is added and releases H⁺ when base is added, blunting the swing in either direction. Buffers act in an instant, but they do not remove acid from the body; they merely tie it up temporarily until the lungs or kidneys can dispose of it. The body's most important buffer is the bicarbonate system, captured in one reaction worth memorizing:

CO₂ + H₂O ⇆ H₂CO₃ ⇆ H⁺ + HCO₃⁻

Read it both ways. When acid floods in, the extra H⁺ is captured by bicarbonate (HCO₃⁻), driving the reaction left toward carbonic acid and CO₂, which the lungs can exhale. When base is added, carbonic acid gives up an H⁺ to replace what was lost, driving the reaction right. What makes this buffer so powerful is that it is open at both ends: the lungs can blow off the CO₂ on the left, and the kidneys can adjust the bicarbonate on the right, so the body can shift the whole equation rather than simply exhaust the buffer.

Buffer systemWhere it worksNote
BicarbonateBlood / ECFThe main ECF buffer; linked to lungs and kidneys, so it can be regulated
PhosphateInside cells; urineImportant in the ICF and in acidifying urine
ProteinCells & plasmaLargest total capacity; hemoglobin buffers the CO₂ carried in blood
Blood pH tracks a 20-to-1 ratio. Normal pH 7.4 corresponds to about 20 parts bicarbonate for every 1 part carbonic acid (CO₂). It is the ratio, not the absolute amounts, that fixes the pH — so the kidney (which sets the bicarbonate on top) and the lungs (which set the CO₂ on the bottom) can each nudge pH by changing their side of that fraction.

Add acid or base in the interactive and watch the bicarbonate buffer fight to hold the line.

Interactive Blood pH must stay between 7.35 and 7.45 — add acid or base and watch the bicarbonate buffer fight to hold the line. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Arterial blood pH is held at 7.35–7.45; straying from it disables enzymes and threatens life.
  • A buffer soaks up or releases H⁺ to blunt pH swings instantly, but does not remove acid from the body.
  • The bicarbonate buffer — CO₂ + H₂O ⇆ H₂CO₃ ⇆ H⁺ + HCO₃⁻ — is central because the lungs control CO₂ and the kidneys control HCO₃⁻.
  • Phosphate and protein buffers back it up; proteins carry the largest total buffering capacity.

Section 5The three lines of defense

Three defenders, three clocks

The body guards its pH with three mechanisms that work on completely different timescales, each covering for the limits of the others. Think of them as an emergency response in stages: an instant reflex, a fast adjustment, and a slow but complete repair. The first is chemical buffering, which we just met — it acts within seconds but has limited capacity and only hides the acid rather than removing it. The second is respiratory control, and the third is renal control. Together they let the body absorb an acid load immediately and then actually clear it.

The lungs: fast and forceful

The lungs adjust pH by controlling how much CO₂ stays in the blood, and because CO₂ forms acid, breathing is a pH dial. When blood becomes too acidic, chemoreceptors sense the rising H⁺ and drive the respiratory center to breathe faster and deeper, blowing off CO₂ and pulling the buffer equation to the left — pH climbs back up within minutes. When blood turns too alkaline, breathing slows to retain CO₂ and let pH fall. This respiratory response is quick and remarkably powerful, but it has a built-in limit: it cannot fix a problem that the lungs themselves caused.

The kidneys: slow and complete

The final and most thorough defender is the kidney, which can actually add or subtract acid and base from the body. By secreting H⁺ into the urine and reabsorbing or generating bicarbonate back into the blood, the kidney resets the whole system — but it works over hours to days, not seconds. It is the slowest line of defense and the only one that can permanently correct an imbalance, which is why it is the ultimate authority on blood pH. Buffers buy time, the lungs buy more, and the kidneys settle the account.

Line of defenseSpeedHow it worksLimit
1. Chemical buffersSecondsBind or release H⁺ on the spotLimited capacity; only hides the acid
2. Respiratory (lungs)MinutesChange breathing to blow off or keep CO₂Cannot fix a lung-caused problem
3. Renal (kidneys)Hours to daysExcrete H⁺, reabsorb/make HCO₃⁻Slow — but the only complete fix
Fast but shallow, slow but deep. The lungs can correct a pH swing in a minute yet run out of reach on their own turf; the kidneys need a day but are the only system that truly removes acid from the body. When one is the source of the trouble, the other becomes the rescuer — the essence of compensation in the next section.

Tap each line of defense in the interactive to watch it act on its own timescale.

Interactive The body defends its pH with three systems acting on very different timescales — from instant to days. Tap each line of defense. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Three defenses in order of speed: chemical buffers (seconds), respiratory (minutes), renal (hours to days).
  • The lungs tune pH by exhaling more or less CO₂ — fast and strong, but blind to lung-caused problems.
  • The kidneys excrete H⁺ and adjust bicarbonate — slow but the only complete correction.
  • When one organ system causes the disturbance, the other compensates.

Section 6The four acid-base disorders

Two questions sort every case

When pH control fails, the result is always one of four core disorders, and you can name any of them by answering two questions. First, which direction did the pH go — too acidic (acidosis, pH below 7.35) or too basic (alkalosis, pH above 7.45)? Second, what is the cause — a lung problem changing CO₂ (respiratory) or a metabolic problem changing bicarbonate and everything else (metabolic)? Cross those two axes and you get a simple two-by-two grid with one disorder in each box.

DisorderPrimary changepHTypical causes
Respiratory acidosisCO₂ retained (hypoventilation)LowCOPD, opioid overdose, chest injury
Respiratory alkalosisCO₂ blown off (hyperventilation)HighAnxiety, high altitude, fever, pain
Metabolic acidosisHCO₃⁻ lost or acid gainedLowDiabetic ketoacidosis, lactic acidosis, diarrhea, kidney failure
Metabolic alkalosisHCO₃⁻ gained or acid lostHighVomiting (loss of stomach acid), diuretics, antacid excess

The body fights back: compensation

Whenever one system fails, the other steps in to blunt the damage — a process called compensation. If the trouble is metabolic, the lungs respond within minutes: in diabetic ketoacidosis the deep, rapid Kussmaul breathing is the lungs frantically blowing off CO₂ to counter the acid. If the trouble is respiratory, the kidneys respond over days, retaining or dumping bicarbonate to nudge pH back. Compensation can be partial (pH still abnormal) or, given time, nearly full (pH back inside range while the underlying numbers stay skewed). It never over-corrects past normal, and it never cures the cause — it only buys the patient time.

Reading the numbers

To interpret a blood-gas result, look at three values in order: the pH (which way is it off?), the CO₂ (the respiratory number), and the bicarbonate (the metabolic number). Then ask which of the two matches the pH. If the pH is low and the CO₂ is high, the lungs are the culprit — respiratory acidosis. If the pH is low and the bicarbonate is low, metabolism is to blame — metabolic acidosis. The value that moves in step with the disorder points to its cause.

ROME — Respiratory Opposite, Metabolic Equal. In a respiratory disorder, pH and CO₂ move in opposite directions (CO₂ up, pH down = acidosis). In a metabolic disorder, pH and bicarbonate move in the same direction (HCO₃⁻ down, pH down = acidosis). Four letters that let you name any acid-base picture at a glance.

Tap a box in the interactive to explore each of the four disorders by its cause and direction.

Interactive Four core disorders, sorted by cause (lungs or metabolism) and direction (too acid or too basic) — tap a box. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Four disorders come from two axes: direction (acidosis/alkalosis) × cause (respiratory/metabolic).
  • Respiratory problems change CO₂; metabolic problems change bicarbonate.
  • Compensation uses the opposite organ system — lungs for metabolic problems (Kussmaul breathing), kidneys for respiratory ones.
  • Interpret by ROME: respiratory moves pH and CO₂ opposite ways; metabolic moves pH and HCO₃⁻ the same way.

The chapter in one breath

  • Water is about 60% of body mass, split two-thirds intracellular and one-third extracellular (interstitial fluid plus plasma).
  • The compartments differ in solute: ECF is Na⁺/Cl⁻-rich, ICF is K⁺-rich, and water follows solute by osmosis.
  • Daily intake equals output at ~2.5 L; thirst raises intake and ADH lowers output to hold total body water steady.
  • Electrolytes drive nerve, muscle, and fluid balance — sodium rules volume, potassium rules the heart, calcium rules contraction; aldosterone keeps Na⁺ and dumps K⁺.
  • Blood pH is pinned at 7.35–7.45 by the bicarbonate buffer, CO₂ + H₂O ⇆ H₂CO₃ ⇆ H⁺ + HCO₃⁻.
  • Three defenses guard pH: buffers (seconds), lungs adjusting CO₂ (minutes), kidneys adjusting HCO₃⁻ (days).
  • Failure gives four disorders — respiratory vs metabolic, acidosis vs alkalosis — read with ROME and softened by compensation.

ReferenceWords worth knowing

A quick reference for the terms in this chapter — skim it now, or circle back whenever a word trips you up.

Acidosis
A condition in which blood pH falls below 7.35; called respiratory when caused by CO₂ retention, metabolic when caused by lost bicarbonate or gained acid.
Aldosterone
An adrenal hormone that makes the kidney reabsorb sodium (and water) while secreting potassium, raising ECF volume and blood pressure.
Alkalosis
A condition in which blood pH rises above 7.45; respiratory from excess CO₂ loss, metabolic from gained bicarbonate or lost acid.
Antidiuretic hormone (ADH)
A posterior-pituitary hormone (vasopressin) that makes the kidney reabsorb water, concentrating urine and conserving body water.
Bicarbonate (HCO₃⁻)
The major buffer anion of the ECF and the metabolic component of the bicarbonate buffer system, regulated by the kidneys.
Buffer
A chemical pair that binds excess H⁺ or releases it to resist changes in pH; acts instantly but does not remove acid from the body.
Compensation
The response of one organ system (lungs or kidneys) to counter a pH disturbance caused by the other, easing but not curing the imbalance.
Electrolyte
A substance that dissociates into charged ions in water, enabling fluids to conduct current and driving nerve, muscle, and fluid balance.
Extracellular fluid (ECF)
The roughly one-third of body water outside the cells, made of interstitial fluid and plasma and rich in sodium and chloride.
Insensible loss
Water lost unnoticed by evaporation from the skin and as vapor in exhaled breath.
Interstitial fluid
The extracellular fluid that fills the spaces between cells; the largest part of the ECF.
Intracellular fluid (ICF)
The roughly two-thirds of body water inside the cells, rich in potassium, magnesium, phosphate, and proteins.
Metabolic water
Water produced inside cells as a by-product of burning fuel for energy; a small daily source of intake.
Osmolarity
The concentration of dissolved particles in a fluid; the property that determines which way water moves by osmosis.
pH
A 0–14 measure of acidity based on hydrogen-ion concentration; blood is held at 7.35–7.45.
Potassium (K⁺)
The chief intracellular cation, which sets the resting membrane potential and is critical to normal heart rhythm.
Respiratory acidosis
A fall in pH caused by hypoventilation and CO₂ retention, as in COPD or opioid overdose.
Sodium (Na⁺)
The chief extracellular cation and master control of ECF volume and osmolarity; regulated mainly by aldosterone.
Thirst
The conscious drive to drink, triggered by hypothalamic osmoreceptors when the ECF becomes too concentrated.
Total body water
All the water in the body, about 60% of mass in an adult, divided among the intracellular and extracellular compartments.

Check yourself

1. About what fraction of body mass is water in a typical adult, and how is that water split between the intracellular and extracellular compartments?
Roughly 60% of body mass; about two-thirds is intracellular and one-third extracellular. The extracellular third is itself split into interstitial fluid (the larger part) and plasma. Leaner bodies and infants hold a higher percentage because fat carries almost no water.
2. Which ions dominate the extracellular fluid, and which dominate the intracellular fluid? What keeps the two so different?
ECF is rich in sodium (Na⁺) and chloride (Cl⁻); ICF is rich in potassium (K⁺), magnesium, phosphate, and proteins. The sodium-potassium pump in every cell membrane continuously pushes Na⁺ out and pulls K⁺ in, maintaining the gradient that powers nerve and muscle activity.
3. The ECF becomes more concentrated (its osmolarity rises). Which way does water move across the cell membranes, and what happens to cell volume?
Water moves out of the cells by osmosis, so the cells shrink. Water always follows solute toward the higher concentration; a concentrated ECF pulls water out, while a dilute ECF drives water in and swells the cells.
4. List the three sources of daily water intake and the four routes of water output.
Intake: fluids you drink, water in food, and metabolic water. Output: urine, skin/lung insensible loss, feces, and sweat. Intake and output each total about 2.5 L/day, and only urine is closely regulated.
5. A hiker becomes dehydrated. Trace the two-part response that raises intake and cuts output.
Hypothalamic osmoreceptors sense the concentrated ECF: they trigger thirst (raising intake) and release ADH (cutting output). ADH makes the kidney's collecting ducts insert aquaporins to reabsorb water, producing a small volume of concentrated urine.
6. Which electrolyte is the chief intracellular cation, what does it set, and why do its abnormalities most threaten the heart?
Potassium (K⁺); it sets the resting membrane potential of excitable cells. Because heart muscle depends on that potential to fire and reset, both hyperkalemia and hypokalemia disturb the electrical rhythm and can cause fatal arrhythmias.
7. Aldosterone rises. What happens to sodium and potassium levels, and to blood pressure?
Sodium is retained (and water follows, so volume and blood pressure rise); potassium is secreted into urine, so its level falls. Aldosterone is the salt-retaining hormone — it keeps Na⁺ and dumps K⁺.
8. Write the bicarbonate buffer equation and explain what happens to it when acid is added to the blood.
CO₂ + H₂O ⇆ H₂CO₃ ⇆ H⁺ + HCO₃⁻. Added acid (H⁺) is bound by bicarbonate, pushing the reaction left toward CO₂, which the lungs exhale. The system works because it is open at both ends — lungs control the CO₂ and kidneys control the bicarbonate.
9. Name the body's three lines of acid-base defense in order of speed, and give the timescale of each.
Chemical buffers (seconds), respiratory control by the lungs (minutes), and renal control by the kidneys (hours to days). Buffers hide the acid instantly, the lungs blow off CO₂ quickly, and only the kidneys actually remove acid to correct the imbalance completely.
10. A patient with a severe opioid overdose is breathing very slowly. Predict the acid-base disorder and explain the mechanism.
Respiratory acidosis. Hypoventilation traps CO₂ in the blood; the extra CO₂ forms carbonic acid, raising H⁺ and dropping pH below 7.35. The cause is the lungs (a respiratory number: high CO₂).
11. A patient in diabetic ketoacidosis develops deep, rapid Kussmaul breathing. What disorder do they have, and what is the breathing accomplishing?
Metabolic acidosis, with respiratory compensation. Ketoacids consume bicarbonate and drop the pH; the lungs respond by hyperventilating to blow off CO₂, pulling the buffer equation left and pushing pH back toward normal. Compensation eases but does not cure the acidosis.
12. Blood shows a low pH with a low bicarbonate. Using ROME, is this respiratory or metabolic, and which way did pH and the matching value move?
Metabolic acidosis — Metabolic means pH and bicarbonate move in the same direction (both down). ROME: Respiratory Opposite, Metabolic Equal. Because the bicarbonate (a metabolic number) fell along with the pH, the cause is metabolic, not respiratory.
Anatomy & Physiology · Chapter 27

The Reproductive System

Every other system in this book works to keep you alive. The reproductive system is the one that looks past you — its job is to make a new person. To do that, the body must accomplish something no other tissue attempts: it must build a special cell, a gamete, that carries exactly half of your genetic instructions, so that when a sperm meets an egg the full set is restored. This chapter walks both anatomies, the male path from testis to exit and the female path from ovary to uterus. It follows the two production lines — spermatogenesis and oogenesis — that turn stem cells into gametes, and it traces the hormonal tide of the menstrual cycle. Underneath all of it sits one elegant kind of cell division, meiosis, which we save for last because once you see it, the rest of the chapter clicks into place.

By the end of this chapter you can

  • Trace the path of sperm from the seminiferous tubules to the urethra, and name the glands that add fluid along the way.
  • Describe spermatogenesis — how a diploid stem cell becomes four haploid sperm — and where and how long it takes.
  • Identify the major female reproductive organs and give the function of the ovary, uterine tube, uterus, and their layers.
  • Explain oogenesis and how its unequal divisions and lifelong arrest differ from sperm production.
  • Sequence the phases of the ovarian and uterine cycles and match each to its dominant hormone (FSH, LH, estrogen, progesterone).
  • Distinguish meiosis from mitosis and explain how crossing over and independent assortment create genetic variety.
  • Define diploid and haploid and explain why halving the chromosome number is essential to sexual reproduction.

Section 1Male reproductive anatomy

Two jobs: make sperm, deliver sperm

The male system has a wonderfully simple mandate — produce sperm and deliver them, suspended in a nourishing fluid, to the female tract. Everything in the anatomy serves one of those two aims. Sperm are manufactured in the paired testes, which hang outside the body wall inside the scrotum. That external location is not an accident: sperm production needs a temperature a few degrees below core body heat, and the scrotum, with the help of a small muscle that raises and lowers the testes, acts as a thermostat. Inside each testis are tightly coiled seminiferous tubules, and it is along the walls of these tubules that sperm are born.

Following the road out

Once made, sperm are not yet able to swim or to fertilize. They travel first into the epididymis, a comma-shaped coil of tubing draped over the back of each testis, where over roughly two weeks they mature and gain motility and are stored. From there they enter the ductus (vas) deferens, a muscular tube that climbs out of the scrotum, loops over the bladder, and joins the duct of the seminal vesicle to form the ejaculatory duct. That short duct passes through the prostate and empties into the urethra, the final common passage that carries semen out through the penis. Notice that in the male the urethra is shared plumbing — it carries both urine and semen, though never at the same time.

StructureRole in the journeyNote
Seminiferous tubulesSite of sperm productionCoiled inside each testis
EpididymisSperm mature, gain motility, and are stored~6 m of coiled tube on the testis
Ductus (vas) deferensPropels sperm toward the urethraCut and tied in a vasectomy
Ejaculatory ductJoins sperm with seminal vesicle fluidPasses through the prostate
UrethraCarries semen (and urine) outShared passage in the male

The glands that make semen

Sperm make up only a small fraction of semen — most of the volume is fluid added by three accessory glands, and each fluid has a purpose. The paired seminal vesicles contribute the largest share: a thick, sugar-rich fluid whose fructose fuels the sperm's tails. The single prostate gland, which encircles the urethra just below the bladder, adds a milky, slightly alkaline fluid that helps sperm survive the acidic environment of the female tract. Finally, the small bulbourethral glands release a clear pre-ejaculate that lubricates and neutralizes traces of acidic urine in the urethra. Together these secretions activate the sperm, feed them, and protect them for the trip ahead.

Remember the road out with the classic mnemonic "SEVEN UP": Seminiferous tubules, Epididymis, Vas (ductus) deferens, Ejaculatory duct, Nothing, Urethra, Penis. However you remember it, the order is what matters: sperm are made, matured, propelled, mixed with fluid, and delivered.

Tap each structure in the interactive to trace the sperm's route from where it is made to the exit.

Interactive Follow the path sperm take from where they are made to the exit — tap each part. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Sperm are made in the seminiferous tubules of the testes, which sit in the scrotum to stay slightly cooler than the body.
  • The road out runs testis → epididymis → ductus deferens → ejaculatory duct → urethra.
  • Three glands add fluid: seminal vesicles (fructose), prostate (alkaline), and bulbourethral (lubricating).
  • The male urethra is shared plumbing for both urine and semen.

Section 2Spermatogenesis

Turning a stem cell into a swimmer

Spermatogenesis is the assembly line that converts a diploid stem cell into mature sperm, and it runs continuously along the walls of the seminiferous tubules from puberty onward. It begins with a spermatogonium, a stem cell carrying the full 46 chromosomes (the diploid, or 2n, number). One daughter of each division stays behind as a stem cell — which is why a male never runs out — while the other, now a primary spermatocyte, commits to becoming sperm. That primary spermatocyte enters meiosis, the special division that halves the chromosome count.

Two divisions, four cells

The math of spermatogenesis is clean. The primary spermatocyte completes meiosis I to become two secondary spermatocytes, each now carrying the haploid (n = 23) number. Each of those completes meiosis II, yielding a total of four spermatids. Unlike egg production, the divisions here are equal — every cell gets a fair share of cytoplasm, so one starting cell produces four usable products. The spermatids are round and immobile; they still must be remodeled into streamlined sperm in a final step called spermiogenesis, in which the cell sheds excess cytoplasm, packs its DNA into a compact head, caps it with an enzyme-filled acrosome, and grows the whip-like flagellum (tail) it will swim with. A midpiece stuffed with mitochondria supplies the energy for the trip.

StageChromosomesWhat happens
SpermatogoniumDiploid (2n)Stem cell; one daughter self-renews
Primary spermatocyteDiploid (2n)Enters and completes meiosis I
Secondary spermatocyteHaploid (n)Two cells; complete meiosis II
SpermatidHaploid (n)Four cells; round and non-motile
Sperm (spermatozoon)Haploid (n)Head + acrosome + midpiece + tail

Helped along by nurse cells

The developing cells do not fend for themselves. Tucked among them are tall Sustentacular (Sertoli) cells, sometimes called nurse cells, that nourish the maturing sperm and form the blood–testis barrier, a protective wall that keeps the immune system from attacking sperm as if they were foreign. Between the tubules sit the interstitial (Leydig) cells, which produce testosterone — the hormone that drives sperm production and the male secondary sex characteristics. The whole cycle, from spermatogonium to finished sperm, takes about 64 to 74 days, and a healthy male makes hundreds of millions of sperm every single day.

One in, four out — and the factory never closes. A single primary spermatocyte yields four functional sperm through two equal meiotic divisions, and because the stem cell renews itself, production runs nonstop from puberty into old age. Contrast this with the egg, where one starting cell yields just one egg — the great asymmetry of this chapter.

Step through each division in the interactive to watch a stem cell become four swimming sperm.

Interactive Watch stem cells become swimming sperm through meiosis — step through it. Open full ↑
Key points
  • A diploid spermatogonium yields four haploid sperm through two equal meiotic divisions.
  • Spermiogenesis remodels round spermatids into streamlined sperm with an acrosome, midpiece, and tail.
  • Sertoli cells nurse the sperm and form the blood–testis barrier; Leydig cells make testosterone.
  • The full cycle takes about 64–74 days, and production is continuous.

Section 3Female reproductive anatomy

A place to make an egg and a place to grow a baby

The female system does two things the male system does not: it not only makes the gamete, it also provides the place where a fertilized egg can implant and develop for nine months. The paired ovaries, each about the size of an almond, sit low in the pelvis and hold the lifetime supply of eggs. Once a month an ovary releases a mature egg in ovulation. Reaching toward each ovary is a uterine (fallopian) tube, whose fringed, finger-like end — the fimbriae — sweeps the released egg into the tube. Fertilization, when it happens, usually takes place here, in the outer third of the tube.

The uterus and its layers

The tube carries the egg toward the uterus, a muscular, pear-shaped organ built to house a growing fetus. Its wall has three layers worth knowing. The inner endometrium is the lining that thickens each month to receive an embryo and, if none arrives, is shed as the menstrual period. The thick middle myometrium is smooth muscle that will contract powerfully during labor. The thin outer perimetrium is the serous covering. The lower neck of the uterus, the cervix, opens into the vagina, the muscular canal that serves as the birth canal and receives sperm during intercourse.

OrganFunctionNote
OvaryProduces eggs and the hormones estrogen & progesteroneHolds all eggs from birth
Uterine (fallopian) tubeCarries the egg toward the uterus; usual site of fertilizationFimbriae sweep in the egg
UterusHouses and nourishes the developing fetusEndometrium / myometrium / perimetrium
CervixNeck of the uterus; opens into the vaginaDilates in labor
VaginaBirth canal; receives semenMuscular, distensible

The ovary is also a gland

It is easy to think of the ovary as just an egg store, but it is equally an endocrine organ. Each month a cluster of cells around the maturing egg — the follicle — pumps out estrogen, and after ovulation the leftover follicle transforms into the corpus luteum, which secretes progesterone. These two hormones drive the changes in the uterine lining and, working with the brain, run the whole monthly cycle we will meet in Section 5. So the ovary does double duty: it releases the gamete and orchestrates the tissue that will receive it.

The egg is never inside the tube's plumbing continuously. There is a tiny gap between the ovary and the fimbriae — the released egg briefly crosses the peritoneal space before the fringe sweeps it in. It is a reminder that the female tract is, unusually, open to the abdominal cavity, which is also why pelvic infections can spread inward.

Tap each structure in the interactive to explore the female organs from the front.

Interactive Explore the female organs and where a fertilized egg implants — tap each structure, front view. Open full ↑
Key points
  • The ovaries store eggs and act as glands, making estrogen and progesterone.
  • The uterine tube catches the ovulated egg with its fimbriae; fertilization usually occurs in its outer third.
  • The uterus wall has three layers: endometrium (shed lining), myometrium (labor muscle), perimetrium (serous cover).
  • The cervix opens into the vagina, the birth canal.

Section 4Oogenesis

The same tool, used very differently

Egg production, or oogenesis, uses the very same meiosis as sperm production, but the female body runs the process on a completely different schedule and with a completely different goal. Where the male makes four small sperm from one cell and does it endlessly, the female makes one large egg from one cell and does it on a strict, once-a-month timetable. The reason for the difference is investment: an egg must carry enough cytoplasm, nutrients, and organelles to launch a new organism, so the female strategy is to pour almost everything into a single winner.

Unequal divisions on purpose

Here is the twist. When a primary oocyte completes meiosis I, it does not split its cytoplasm evenly. Instead it divides unequally: one daughter, the secondary oocyte, keeps nearly all the cytoplasm, while the other becomes a tiny polar body that gets almost nothing and eventually degenerates. The same lopsided split happens again at meiosis II — but note that in humans this second division only completes if a sperm fertilizes the egg. The result of the whole process is one functional egg (ovum) plus two or three doomed polar bodies. One cell in, one usable egg out — the mirror image of the male's one-in-four.

FeatureOogenesis (egg)Spermatogenesis (sperm)
Products per starting cellOne egg (+ polar bodies)Four sperm
Cytoplasm splitUnequal — egg keeps it allEqual
TimingBegins before birth; one per cycleContinuous from puberty
Completion of meiosis IIOnly if fertilizedAlways completed
Lifetime output~400 eggs ovulatedTrillions of sperm

A process paused for decades

Oogenesis also has the strangest timeline in the body. A female is born with all the eggs she will ever have — roughly one to two million primary oocytes, already begun on meiosis I and then frozen partway through, arrested before birth. They wait, paused, for years. Beginning at puberty, one oocyte finishes meiosis I each month and is ovulated; the rest keep waiting. This means an egg ovulated at age forty has been sitting in suspended animation for four decades — a delay that helps explain why the chance of chromosomal errors, such as those causing Down syndrome, rises with maternal age. Supply dwindles over the reproductive years until, at menopause, the ovaries run low and the cycles stop.

Polar bodies are the price of a big egg. Meiosis normally makes four equal products. Oogenesis deliberately throws away three of them as polar bodies so that a single egg can inherit all the cytoplasm and stored nutrients it needs to nourish an embryo before implantation. The unequal split is a feature, not a flaw.

Step through the interactive to watch the unequal divisions concentrate everything into one egg.

Interactive See how the unequal divisions of meiosis make one egg keep almost everything — step through it. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Oogenesis makes one egg (plus polar bodies) per starting cell, through unequal cytoplasm divisions.
  • All eggs begin meiosis before birth and arrest; one finishes per cycle, and meiosis II completes only at fertilization.
  • A female is born with her lifetime egg supply, which declines to zero at menopause.
  • Long meiotic arrest helps explain the rising rate of chromosomal errors with maternal age.

Section 5The menstrual cycle

Two cycles running as one

The roughly 28-day menstrual cycle is really two synchronized cycles — one in the ovary and one in the uterus — conducted by four hormones. Two come from the brain's pituitary gland: follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) and luteinizing hormone (LH). Two come from the ovary itself: estrogen and progesterone. The ovarian cycle tracks what is happening to the egg and its follicle; the uterine cycle tracks the building and shedding of the endometrial lining. They are timed so that a fresh lining is ready exactly when an egg might arrive.

Follicular phase, ovulation, luteal phase

The cycle opens with menstruation — the shedding of last month's lining — on day 1. During the follicular phase that follows, FSH coaxes several follicles to grow; one becomes dominant and pours out estrogen, which rebuilds the endometrium. Rising estrogen eventually triggers a dramatic surge of LH around day 14, and that LH surge causes ovulation — the dominant follicle bursts and releases its egg. In the luteal phase afterward, the emptied follicle becomes the corpus luteum, which secretes progesterone to maintain and enrich the lining, readying it for a possible implantation. If no pregnancy occurs, the corpus luteum withers, progesterone and estrogen fall, and the unsupported lining sheds — and the next cycle begins.

Phase (days)Dominant hormoneWhat is happening
Menstruation (1–5)All lowOld endometrium is shed
Follicular (1–13)FSH, then estrogenA follicle ripens; lining rebuilds
Ovulation (~14)LH surgeFollicle bursts; egg released
Luteal (15–28)ProgesteroneCorpus luteum enriches the lining

Feedback that flips

What makes this cycle beautiful is that the same hormone can push in opposite directions depending on timing. For most of the cycle, estrogen exerts negative feedback, quietly restraining the pituitary. But as the dominant follicle matures, estrogen climbs to a high, sustained level, and at that threshold the feedback flips to positive — high estrogen now drives the pituitary to release the LH surge that triggers ovulation. It is one of the body's few examples of positive feedback, and it exists for exactly the reason positive feedback always does: to produce a decisive, one-time event. After ovulation, progesterone from the corpus luteum re-establishes negative feedback and calms everything down.

Follicles make estrogen; the corpus luteum makes progesterone. Tie each ovarian structure to its hormone and the cycle unlocks: a growing follicle → estrogen → lining rebuilds; the post-ovulation corpus luteum → progesterone → lining maintained. When the corpus luteum dies, progesterone falls and the period begins. This same drop in hormones is what most birth-control pills prevent by holding hormone levels artificially steady.

Tap a phase in the interactive to see the hormones, the ovary, and the lining move together.

Interactive Tap a phase to see the four hormones, the ovary, and the uterine lining across ~28 days. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Four hormones run the cycle: FSH and LH from the pituitary, estrogen and progesterone from the ovary.
  • The phases are menstruation → follicular → ovulation (LH surge, ~day 14) → luteal.
  • Follicles make estrogen (rebuild lining); the corpus luteum makes progesterone (maintain lining).
  • A high estrogen level flips to positive feedback to trigger the ovulatory LH surge.

Section 6Meiosis

Why half is the whole point

We have saved the engine for last. Every body cell you own is diploid — it carries 46 chromosomes, arranged as 23 pairs, one member of each pair from each of your parents. If a sperm with 46 chromosomes fertilized an egg with 46, the child would have 92, and the number would double every generation. Nature's solution is meiosis, a special division that makes haploid gametes with just 23 chromosomes. When a haploid sperm meets a haploid egg, the full diploid set of 46 is restored — and the count stays stable forever. Halving the chromosome number is not a side effect of gamete production; it is the reason meiosis exists.

Two divisions, not one

Meiosis reaches the haploid number through two back-to-back divisions after a single DNA copy. In meiosis I, the matched pairs of chromosomes (called homologous pairs) line up and are separated — this is the reduction step that takes the cell from diploid to haploid. In meiosis II, which looks much like an ordinary mitosis, the two copies of each chromosome finally split apart. The end result is four haploid cells (four sperm, or one egg plus polar bodies). Compare that with mitosis, the ordinary division that builds and repairs the body: mitosis is one division that makes two identical diploid cells.

FeatureMitosisMeiosis
PurposeGrowth and repairMake gametes
DivisionsOneTwo
Daughter cells24
Chromosome numberDiploid (2n) — unchangedHalved to haploid (n)
GeneticallyIdentical clonesUnique — all different

Shuffling the deck

Meiosis does more than halve — it reshuffles your genes so that no two gametes are alike. It does this two ways. In crossing over, homologous chromosomes swap matching segments while paired up in meiosis I, blending the versions you inherited from each parent. In independent assortment, each pair lines up and separates at random, so which grandparent's chromosome ends up in a given gamete is a coin flip — done 23 times over. Together these produce millions of genetically distinct gametes from one person, which is why siblings differ and why sexual reproduction generates the variety that evolution acts on. When something goes wrong here and a pair fails to separate — nondisjunction — a gamete ends up with the wrong number of chromosomes, the cause of conditions such as trisomy 21.

Meiosis I reduces; meiosis II divides. The single most-missed point on exams: it is meiosis I that separates the homologous pairs and drops the cell from diploid to haploid. Meiosis II merely splits the sister copies, like a mitosis on an already-haploid cell. If you can say which division does the halving, you understand meiosis.

Step through both divisions in the interactive to watch the chromosomes halve and the genes shuffle.

Interactive Two divisions halve the chromosomes and shuffle the genes — step through it. Open full ↑
Key points
  • Meiosis halves the chromosome number from diploid (46) to haploid (23) so fertilization can restore the full set.
  • It uses two divisions: meiosis I separates homologous pairs (the reduction step); meiosis II splits the sister copies.
  • Crossing over and independent assortment make every gamete genetically unique.
  • Nondisjunction — a pair failing to separate — gives a gamete the wrong chromosome number.

The chapter in one breath

  • The reproductive system exists to make a new person, which requires haploid gametes that combine to restore the full chromosome set.
  • In the male, sperm made in the seminiferous tubules travel epididymis → ductus deferens → ejaculatory duct → urethra, mixed with fluid from the seminal vesicles, prostate, and bulbourethral glands.
  • Spermatogenesis makes four sperm per stem cell through equal divisions, continuously, over ~64–74 days.
  • In the female, the ovary both stores eggs and makes hormones; the uterine tube catches the egg and the uterus (endometrium, myometrium, perimetrium) houses a pregnancy.
  • Oogenesis makes one egg plus polar bodies through unequal divisions, starts before birth, and completes meiosis II only at fertilization.
  • The menstrual cycle is run by FSH, LH, estrogen, and progesterone: follicles make estrogen, an LH surge triggers ovulation, and the corpus luteum makes progesterone.
  • Meiosis halves the chromosomes in two divisions — meiosis I reduces, meiosis II divides — and crossing over plus independent assortment make every gamete unique.

ReferenceWords worth knowing

A quick reference for the terms in this chapter — skim it now, or circle back whenever a word trips you up.

Acrosome
The enzyme-filled cap over a sperm's head that helps it penetrate the egg's outer coat.
Corpus luteum
The structure formed from the emptied follicle after ovulation; it secretes progesterone to maintain the uterine lining.
Diploid (2n)
Having the full set of chromosomes — 46 in humans, arranged as 23 pairs; the state of body cells.
Ductus (vas) deferens
The muscular tube that propels sperm from the epididymis toward the urethra; cut in a vasectomy.
Endometrium
The inner lining of the uterus that thickens each cycle to receive an embryo and is shed as menstruation if none implants.
Epididymis
The coiled tube on the back of the testis where sperm mature, gain motility, and are stored.
Estrogen
The ovarian hormone, made mainly by the growing follicle, that rebuilds the uterine lining and triggers the LH surge.
Follicle
The cluster of ovarian cells surrounding a developing egg; it secretes estrogen and releases the egg at ovulation.
FSH (follicle-stimulating hormone)
A pituitary hormone that stimulates follicle growth in the ovary and sperm production in the testis.
Gamete
A haploid reproductive cell — a sperm or an egg — that fuses with another at fertilization.
Haploid (n)
Having half the chromosome set — 23 in humans; the state of gametes.
LH (luteinizing hormone)
A pituitary hormone whose mid-cycle surge triggers ovulation and forms the corpus luteum.
Meiosis
The two-division process that halves the chromosome number to make haploid gametes and shuffles genes.
Menopause
The permanent end of menstrual cycles when the ovaries' egg supply is exhausted.
Nondisjunction
The failure of a chromosome pair to separate during meiosis, giving a gamete the wrong chromosome number.
Oogenesis
The production of eggs; unequal meiotic divisions yield one ovum and two or three polar bodies.
Ovulation
The release of a mature egg from the ovary, triggered by the mid-cycle LH surge.
Polar body
A small cell produced by the unequal divisions of oogenesis that receives little cytoplasm and degenerates.
Progesterone
The hormone made by the corpus luteum that maintains and enriches the uterine lining after ovulation.
Semen
The fluid ejaculated from the male tract, made of sperm plus secretions of the seminal vesicles, prostate, and bulbourethral glands.
Seminiferous tubules
The coiled tubes inside the testis where sperm are produced.
Spermatogenesis
The production of sperm; equal meiotic divisions yield four haploid sperm from one stem cell.

Check yourself

1. A sperm cell has just been made in the seminiferous tubules. List, in order, the structures it passes through on its way out of the body.
Seminiferous tubule → epididymis → ductus (vas) deferens → ejaculatory duct → urethra. Along the way, fluid from the seminal vesicles and prostate is added to make semen; the urethra is the shared final passage for both semen and urine.
2. Match each accessory gland to the fluid it contributes: seminal vesicles, prostate, bulbourethral glands.
Seminal vesicles → fructose-rich fluid (fuel); prostate → alkaline milky fluid (protection); bulbourethral → clear lubricating pre-ejaculate. The seminal vesicles provide most of the semen volume, and the alkalinity buffers the acidic female tract.
3. How many functional sperm result from one primary spermatocyte, and why are the divisions described as "equal"?
Four sperm, from two meiotic divisions that split the cytoplasm evenly. Because each division shares cytoplasm fairly, all four products are viable — unlike oogenesis, where the split is deliberately unequal.
4. What are Sertoli (sustentacular) cells and Leydig (interstitial) cells, and how do their jobs differ?
Sertoli cells nurse the developing sperm and form the blood–testis barrier; Leydig cells make testosterone. One supports and protects the sperm inside the tubule; the other, between the tubules, supplies the hormone that drives the whole process.
5. Name the three layers of the uterine wall from inside out, and give the function of each.
Endometrium (lining that thickens and sheds), myometrium (smooth muscle that contracts in labor), perimetrium (outer serous covering). The endometrium receives an embryo or is shed as the period; the myometrium powers delivery.
6. Where does fertilization usually occur, and how does the released egg get there from the ovary?
In the outer third of the uterine (fallopian) tube; the fimbriae sweep the ovulated egg into the tube. There is a small gap between ovary and tube, so the egg briefly crosses open peritoneal space before being caught.
7. Oogenesis and spermatogenesis both use meiosis. Give two ways their outcomes differ.
Oogenesis makes one egg (plus polar bodies) with unequal cytoplasm division; spermatogenesis makes four equal sperm. Oogenesis is also once-monthly and begins before birth, while spermatogenesis is continuous from puberty.
8. What is a polar body, and why does the female body make one at all?
A small cell that receives almost no cytoplasm during oogenesis and degenerates. The unequal split lets a single egg keep all the cytoplasm and nutrients it needs to nourish an early embryo; the polar bodies are the discarded remainder.
9. Put the phases of the menstrual cycle in order and name the dominant hormone of each: luteal, follicular, menstruation, ovulation.
Menstruation (all hormones low) → follicular (FSH, then estrogen) → ovulation (LH surge) → luteal (progesterone). Follicles secrete estrogen to rebuild the lining; the corpus luteum secretes progesterone to maintain it.
10. What event does the LH surge cause, and what happens to the follicle afterward?
The LH surge triggers ovulation; the emptied follicle becomes the corpus luteum. The corpus luteum then secretes progesterone; if no pregnancy occurs it degenerates, hormones fall, and the lining sheds.
11. A body cell has 46 chromosomes. How many does a human gamete have, and why must the number be halved before fertilization?
A gamete has 23 (haploid); halving is required so that sperm (23) + egg (23) restores 46. Without halving, the chromosome number would double every generation. This reduction is the entire purpose of meiosis.
12. Which meiotic division reduces the chromosome number, and name the two processes that make each gamete genetically unique.
Meiosis I is the reduction division (it separates homologous pairs); crossing over and independent assortment create variety. Meiosis II merely splits the sister copies. Crossing over swaps segments between homologs; independent assortment randomizes which chromosome of each pair goes to each gamete.
Anatomy & Physiology · Chapter 28

Development & Inheritance

Every human life begins as a single cell no bigger than the period at the end of this sentence — and yet folded inside it is a complete instruction book for building a person. This chapter follows that cell forward: the dramatic meeting of sperm and egg, the first week of frantic cell division and burrowing into the uterus, the moment three germ layers appear and quietly divide up the job of making every organ, and the long stretch of fetal growth that ends in birth. Then we turn the story around and ask how the instruction book itself is passed on — the rules of inheritance that let us predict, with a simple square grid, the odds that a child will have brown eyes or a particular disease. It is the most compressed miracle in all of physiology, and it follows rules you can learn.

By the end of this chapter you can

  • Describe the steps of fertilization, from the sperm crossing the egg's outer coats to the fusion that forms a zygote, and explain how the egg blocks fertilization by more than one sperm.
  • Trace the first week of development through cleavage, the morula, and the blastocyst, and describe how and where implantation occurs.
  • Explain gastrulation and name the three primary germ layers along with the major tissues each one gives rise to.
  • Distinguish the embryonic period from the fetal period and outline the milestones of the three trimesters.
  • Describe the structure and functions of the placenta, and list the three stages of labor.
  • Define allele, genotype, and phenotype, and contrast dominant with recessive inheritance.
  • Use a Punnett square to predict the genotype and phenotype ratios of offspring from a given cross.
  • Recognize why two unaffected parents can have a child with a recessive condition.

Section 1Fertilization

A race with one finisher

Fertilization is the fusion of a sperm and an oocyte (egg) to create a new, genetically unique cell. It usually happens not in the uterus but high up in the ampulla of the uterine tube, within roughly a day of ovulation, because the ovulated egg is only fertile for about 12 to 24 hours and sperm survive at most a few days. Hundreds of millions of sperm are deposited, but the journey is brutal: most are lost to the acidic vagina, the cervical mucus, and simply taking wrong turns. Only a few hundred reach the egg. Along the way the survivors undergo capacitation — a final chemical maturation in the female tract that makes their membranes ready to fuse. The egg they find is not naked. It is wrapped in a halo of follicle cells called the corona radiata and, beneath that, a thick glycoprotein shell called the zona pellucida.

Breaking in: the acrosome reaction

To reach the egg's plasma membrane, a sperm must get through both coats. Sitting like a cap over the sperm's head is the acrosome, a lysosome-like sac full of digestive enzymes. When the sperm contacts the zona pellucida, the acrosome ruptures and spills those enzymes — the acrosome reaction — digesting a narrow tunnel through the shell. Because it takes the combined effort of many sperm chipping away at the corona and zona, fertilization is very much a team effort with a single winner. The first sperm to reach the oocyte's plasma membrane binds to receptors there, and the two plasma membranes fuse. The sperm's nucleus, carrying its 23 chromosomes, slides into the egg's cytoplasm.

BarrierWhat it isHow the sperm passes
Corona radiataOuter halo of granulosa (follicle) cellsMany sperm push and enzymatically loosen the cells
Zona pellucidaThick glycoprotein shell around the eggThe acrosome reaction digests a path through it
Oocyte plasma membraneThe egg cell's own surfaceSperm receptors bind; the two membranes fuse

One sperm only: the blocks to polyspermy

Fertilization by two sperm — polyspermy — would give the embryo the wrong number of chromosomes and is lethal, so the egg has two safeguards that snap shut the instant one sperm fuses. The fast block is an immediate change in the electrical charge across the egg membrane that stops further sperm from binding. The slow block, or cortical reaction, follows seconds later: tiny vesicles just under the egg's surface release their contents into the zona pellucida, chemically hardening it into an impenetrable wall. Only now does the egg finish its own final division (meiosis II). The two sets of 23 chromosomes — one from each parent — come together, restoring the full human count of 46, and the fertilized egg is now a single diploid cell called the zygote. Its sex was decided at this exact moment, by whether the winning sperm carried an X or a Y.

Fertilization sets the clock and the sex.
